Table of Contents

    How to Use This Textbook

    This comprehensive textbook is designed as your complete companion for mastering DevOps Engineering and Cloud Infrastructure. Whether you're an instructor leading a course or a self-directed learner, this guide will help you maximize your learning experience.

    For Students

    • Before Each Week: Complete the pre-class reading assignments and exercises. These are designed to activate prior knowledge and prepare you for new concepts. Spend 2-3 hours on pre-class work.
    • During Class Time: Focus on the detailed teaching notes and lecture content. These sections provide comprehensive explanations with real-world examples. Take detailed notes and ask questions.
    • Hands-On Labs: Every lab must be completed. Follow the step-by-step instructions carefully, but also experiment and break things intentionally to deepen understanding. Allocate 4-6 hours per week for lab work.
    • Practice Questions: After completing each week's content, work through all 10 practice questions. These reinforce key concepts and identify knowledge gaps. Review the detailed answers provided.
    • Weekly Assessments: Complete the quiz (10 auto-gradable questions) and test (5 scenario-based questions) to gauge your understanding. These are designed for Google Classroom submission.
    • Weekly Assignment: Each week concludes with a comprehensive assignment that integrates all concepts. Use the provided rubric to self-assess before submission. Budget 3-4 hours for assignments.
    • Lab Solutions: Attempt labs independently first. Refer to solutions only after trying for at least 30 minutes. Solutions include troubleshooting guides for common issues.
    • Build Your Portfolio: Document all your work on GitHub with comprehensive README files. Your course projects become your professional portfolio.
    💡 Pro Tip: Time Management
    Plan for 15-20 hours per week: 2-3 hours pre-class work, 3-4 hours lecture study, 4-6 hours labs, 2-3 hours practice questions/quizzes, 3-4 hours assignments. This intensive program requires consistent dedication.

    For Instructors

    • Course Structure: This textbook provides complete lesson plans for a 20-week intensive program. Each week includes all materials needed: lecture content, labs, assessments, and solutions.
    • Pre-Class Assignments: Assign pre-class reading and exercises one week in advance. Use these to implement a flipped classroom approach where class time focuses on practice and problem-solving.
    • Teaching Notes: The detailed teaching notes in each chapter provide comprehensive content coverage. Use these as your lecture script or adapt them to your teaching style.
    • Lab Facilitation: Labs are designed for guided practice. Walk through the first few steps with students, then let them work independently. Circulate to provide support and troubleshoot issues.
    • Google Classroom Integration: All quizzes, tests, and assignments are formatted for easy copy-paste into Google Classroom. See Appendix B for detailed integration instructions and grading rubrics.
    • Assessment Strategy: Use quizzes for formative assessment (weekly knowledge checks), tests for summative assessment (applied scenarios), and assignments for project-based evaluation.
    • Customization: Feel free to adapt content to your context. Add local cloud provider examples, modify labs for your infrastructure, or extend assignments based on student interests.
    📚 Teaching Resources
    All assessment rubrics, answer keys, and grading criteria are provided in Appendix B. Lab solution videos and additional teaching resources are available at www.hexadigitall.com/instructor-resources.

    Textbook Structure

    Each weekly chapter follows a consistent, pedagogically-sound structure:

    • Chapter Introduction: Overview of the week's content, relevance to DevOps practice, and connection to previous/future topics.
    • Learning Objectives: Specific, measurable objectives mapped to Bloom's Taxonomy levels (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate, Create).
    • Pre-Class Exercises: Reading assignments and preparatory exercises (3-5 activities) to complete before studying the main content.
    • Detailed Teaching Notes: Comprehensive lecture content (8-15 pages) with concept explanations, diagrams, code examples, and real-world scenarios.
    • Hands-On Labs: Step-by-step practical exercises (3-5 labs per week) with detailed instructions, expected outputs, and verification steps.
    • Lab Solutions: Complete solutions with code, explanations, troubleshooting guides for common errors, and best practice notes.
    • Practice Questions: 10 questions covering key concepts with detailed answer explanations and concept reinforcement.
    • Weekly Quiz: 10 multiple-choice questions (auto-gradable) for knowledge assessment, formatted for Google Classroom.
    • Weekly Test: 5 scenario-based questions requiring applied problem-solving, with evaluation rubric.
    • Weekly Assignment: Comprehensive project-based assignment integrating all weekly concepts, with detailed rubric and submission guidelines.
    🎯 Learning Approach
    This textbook implements a constructivist learning approach where you build knowledge through active practice. Every concept is immediately applied in labs. Theory without practice is forgotten; practice without theory is dangerous. We combine both.

    Prerequisites & Preparation

    This is an advanced course with mandatory prerequisites. Before starting, you must have:

    • Linux Administration: Comfortable with command line, file systems, users, permissions, and shell scripting
    • Git & GitHub: Version control fundamentals, branching, merging, pull requests, and collaboration workflows
    • Programming/Scripting: Python or Bash scripting for automation tasks
    • Networking Basics: Understanding of TCP/IP, DNS, HTTP/HTTPS, and basic network troubleshooting
    • System Requirements: Computer with 16GB RAM minimum, AWS Free Tier account, GitHub account, Docker Desktop installed

    If you haven't completed these prerequisites, enroll in our foundational courses first: Linux Administration & Shell Scripting Pro and Git & GitHub Fundamentals.

    Success Strategies

    • Practice Daily: DevOps skills require consistent hands-on practice. Code every day.
    • Document Everything: Keep detailed notes, create runbooks, document your troubleshooting steps.
    • Build Real Projects: Don't just follow tutorials—deploy actual applications to production environments.
    • Embrace Failure: Break things intentionally. Understanding failure scenarios makes you a better engineer.
    • Join Communities: Engage with CNCF Slack, DevOps forums, local meetups. Learning is social.
    • Stay Current: DevOps tools evolve rapidly. Follow release notes, read blogs, watch conference talks.
    • Infrastructure as Code Always: Never manually configure anything. Automate everything from day one.
    🚀 Career Outcomes
    Upon completion, you'll be qualified for roles such as DevOps Engineer, Cloud Infrastructure Engineer, Kubernetes Administrator, Platform Engineer, and Site Reliability Engineer (SRE). Typical salaries: ₦4-12M+ annually (Nigeria) or $70-150K+ USD (global remote). Your portfolio from this course becomes your job application.

    Teaching Methodology & Learning Framework

    This textbook implements evidence-based pedagogical approaches designed to maximize learning effectiveness and skill retention. Our methodology combines educational theory with practical industry experience to create DevOps engineers who can immediately contribute to production environments.

    Core Pedagogical Principles

    🏗️

    Constructivist Learning

    Students build knowledge through active experimentation and reflection. Every concept is immediately applied in hands-on labs where learners construct their own understanding through practice.

    🔄

    Iterative Mastery

    Concepts are introduced, practiced, and revisited throughout the course. Each week builds on previous knowledge, reinforcing fundamentals while introducing new complexity.

    🎯

    Problem-Based Learning

    All content is taught through real-world problems and scenarios. Students learn by solving actual DevOps challenges they'll encounter in professional settings.

    🧪

    Experiential Practice

    Hands-on labs dominate the learning experience. Theory is taught in service of practice, not the reverse. Students spend 60% of their time doing, not just reading.

    📊

    Scaffolded Difficulty

    Complexity increases gradually. Early weeks provide extensive guidance and support. Later weeks require more independent problem-solving and system design.

    ✅

    Continuous Assessment

    Multiple assessment types (quizzes, tests, labs, projects) provide ongoing feedback. Students identify knowledge gaps early and can remediate before moving forward.

    🤝

    Collaborative Learning

    While individual mastery is essential, DevOps is fundamentally collaborative. Assignments encourage peer review, knowledge sharing, and team problem-solving.

    🔍

    Metacognitive Reflection

    Students are prompted to reflect on their learning process, troubleshooting strategies, and problem-solving approaches. "Thinking about thinking" deepens expertise.

    Bloom's Taxonomy in Action

    Learning objectives are mapped to Bloom's Taxonomy to ensure cognitive development from basic recall to creative problem-solving:

    1. Remember
    Recall key concepts, commands, and terminology. (Weeks 1-5 focus)
    2. Understand
    Explain concepts in your own words, describe how systems work. (Weeks 3-8 focus)
    3. Apply
    Use tools and techniques to solve defined problems. (Weeks 5-12 focus)
    4. Analyze
    Troubleshoot issues, compare approaches, identify patterns. (Weeks 10-15 focus)
    5. Evaluate
    Assess trade-offs, critique designs, recommend solutions. (Weeks 14-18 focus)
    6. Create
    Design complete systems, architect solutions, build original projects. (Weeks 18-20 focus)

    Assessment Philosophy

    Assessment serves learning, not just evaluation. Our multi-tiered assessment approach ensures comprehensive skill development:

    • Formative Assessments (Weekly Quizzes): Low-stakes knowledge checks that identify gaps and guide study focus. Auto-gradable for immediate feedback.
    • Applied Assessments (Weekly Tests): Scenario-based problems requiring analysis and application. Develop real-world problem-solving skills.
    • Project-Based Assessments (Weekly Assignments): Comprehensive projects that integrate multiple concepts. Build portfolio-worthy work.
    • Summative Assessment (Capstone Project): Demonstrates mastery through end-to-end system design and implementation.
    🎓 Educational Research Foundation
    Our methodology is grounded in research from cognitive science, educational psychology, and instructional design. Key influences include: Constructivism (Piaget, Vygotsky), Bloom's Taxonomy (Anderson & Krathwohl), Problem-Based Learning (Barrows), Experiential Learning Theory (Kolb), and Cognitive Load Theory (Sweller). We adapt these theories specifically for technical skill development in cloud and DevOps domains.

    Industry Alignment

    While pedagogically sound, this textbook is ultimately practical. Every topic, tool, and technique is actively used in production DevOps environments. We teach industry-standard practices, not academic exercises. Upon completion, you'll have:

    • Production-ready DevOps skills used by major tech companies
    • Portfolio of projects deployable to real infrastructure
    • Experience with tools that appear in 80%+ of DevOps job descriptions
    • Preparation for industry certifications (CKA, Terraform Associate, DCA)
    • Practical troubleshooting experience from intentional failure scenarios
    • Documentation and communication skills essential for team collaboration
    1

    DevOps Foundations & CI/CD Overview

    Understanding DevOps Culture, Principles, and Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment

    Welcome to your DevOps journey! This foundational week establishes the core principles, culture, and practices that define modern DevOps engineering. You'll understand why DevOps emerged, how it transforms software delivery, and master the fundamental concepts of continuous integration and deployment. By the end of this week, you'll have a complete mental model of DevOps practices and a working development environment ready for the intensive hands-on work ahead.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for DevOps Foundations & CI/CD Overview: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define DevOps, CI/CD, and related terminology. List the key principles of DevOps culture.

    Understand

    Explain how DevOps improves software delivery. Describe the relationship between CI/CD and DevOps.

    Apply

    Set up a development environment with essential DevOps tools. Configure AWS Free Tier account and install tooling.

    Analyze

    Compare different deployment strategies. Identify DevOps anti-patterns in case studies.

    Evaluate

    Assess the readiness of an organization for DevOps adoption. Critique deployment strategies for specific scenarios.

    Create

    Design a DevOps transformation roadmap. Propose CI/CD pipeline architecture for a sample application.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Complete these exercises before diving into the main teaching content. They activate prior knowledge and prepare your mind for new concepts. Budget 2-3 hours for this preparatory work.

    1Software Delivery Reflection

    Reflect on your experience (or research if you're new): How is software traditionally developed and deployed? Write 200-300 words describing the process from code writing to production deployment. What challenges or pain points exist in this process?

    💡 Reflection Prompt

    Think about: How long does it take to deploy a simple bug fix? How many people/teams are involved? What manual steps exist? What could go wrong?

    2DevOps Research

    Research and watch: "What is DevOps?" by major tech companies (search YouTube for videos from AWS, Microsoft Azure, or Google Cloud). Take notes on:

    • Key principles or values mentioned
    • Tools or practices highlighted
    • Benefits claimed
    • Any points that confuse you (bring questions to class!)

    3CI/CD Pipeline Visualization

    Search for "CI/CD pipeline diagram" images online. Study 3-5 different pipeline diagrams and sketch your own interpretation of what a CI/CD pipeline does. Label the stages you identify. Don't worry about accuracy—this exercise builds mental scaffolding for upcoming learning.

    4Tool Ecosystem Exploration

    Visit these websites and spend 5 minutes on each, reading their homepage and "What is..." sections:

    • Docker: docker.com - Container platform
    • Kubernetes: kubernetes.io - Container orchestration
    • Terraform: terraform.io - Infrastructure as Code
    • Jenkins: jenkins.io - CI/CD automation server

    Don't try to understand everything—just familiarize yourself with the names and general purpose.

    5Environment Checklist

    Verify you meet the course prerequisites:

    • ✓ Computer with 16GB RAM minimum
    • ✓ Linux command line proficiency (can navigate, create files, run commands)
    • ✓ Git basics (can clone repos, commit, push)
    • ✓ Basic programming/scripting (Python or Bash)
    • ✓ GitHub account created
    • ✓ Text editor or IDE installed (VS Code recommended)

    If any prerequisites are missing, complete our foundational courses before proceeding.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    1.1 The DevOps Revolution: Why We Needed Change

    To understand DevOps, we must first understand the problem it solves. For decades, software development followed a siloed approach:

    🎯 Traditional Software Delivery Problem

    Developers wrote code in isolated environments, optimizing for features and innovation. Operations teams managed production servers, optimizing for stability and uptime. These contradictory goals created an organizational chasm called "throwing code over the wall."

    This separation created predictable problems:

    • Slow deployment cycles: Months or even quarters between releases
    • Deployment failures: "It works on my machine" syndrome
    • Blame culture: Developers vs. Operations finger-pointing
    • Manual processes: Error-prone human interventions
    • Knowledge silos: Only specific people could deploy
    • Business agility: Inability to respond quickly to market needs

    The 2000s brought a perfect storm of changes: cloud computing (AWS launched 2006), agile methodology adoption, open-source infrastructure tools, and competitive pressure for faster innovation. Companies like Amazon, Netflix, and Google pioneered new approaches, deploying thousands of times per day while maintaining high availability.

    1.2 What is DevOps? Core Definition and Principles

    DevOps is not a tool, not a team, and not a job title—it's a cultural and technical movement that unifies development and operations to deliver software rapidly, reliably, and repeatedly.

    📖 DevOps Definition

    DevOps is a set of practices, cultural philosophies, and tools that increase an organization's ability to deliver applications and services at high velocity: evolving and improving products faster than organizations using traditional software development and infrastructure management processes.

    DevOps rests on several foundational principles, often remembered by the acronym CALMS:

    The CALMS Framework

    C - Culture

    Break down silos between development and operations. Foster collaboration, shared responsibility, and blameless post-mortems. "You build it, you run it" mentality where developers share on-call responsibilities.

    A - Automation

    Automate everything: testing, deployment, infrastructure provisioning, monitoring, and even recovery. Manual processes are slow, error-prone, and don't scale. Automation is the engine of DevOps velocity.

    L - Lean

    Apply Lean principles: eliminate waste, focus on value stream, optimize flow. Small batch sizes (frequent small changes) are safer than big bang releases. Continuous improvement through experimentation.

    M - Measurement

    "You can't improve what you don't measure." Track deployment frequency, lead time, mean time to recovery (MTTR), change failure rate. Use data to drive decisions, not opinions or politics.

    S - Sharing

    Share knowledge, tools, and responsibilities. Open communication channels. Document everything. Celebrate failures as learning opportunities. Build a culture of psychological safety.

    1.3 The Three Ways: DevOps Philosophy

    Gene Kim's "The Phoenix Project" articulates DevOps through The Three Ways:

    First Way: Systems Thinking (Flow)
    Optimize for the entire value stream, not individual silos. Understand that work flows from development → testing → operations → customer. Never pass defects downstream. Never allow local optimization to degrade global goals. Accelerate flow by reducing batch sizes and intervals of work.

    Second Way: Amplify Feedback Loops
    Create short, fast feedback loops from production back to development. Monitor everything, fail fast, and learn quickly. Telemetry and logging are not afterthoughts—they're first-class citizens. When something breaks in production, the team that wrote the code investigates (not just operations).

    Third Way: Culture of Experimentation & Learning
    Create a culture where risk-taking is rewarded, failure is seen as learning, and repetition leads to mastery. Schedule time for technical debt reduction. Hold blameless post-mortems. Practice chaos engineering (deliberately breaking things to build resilience).

    1.4 Continuous Integration (CI): The Foundation

    Continuous Integration is the practice of frequently integrating code changes into a shared repository, with each integration verified by automated builds and tests.

    🔄 CI Core Principle

    "Integrate early, integrate often." Instead of working in isolation for weeks or months, developers merge code multiple times per day. Automated tests run on every commit, catching integration problems immediately when they're cheap to fix.

    Why CI Matters:

    • Reduces integration risk: Small, frequent integrations are predictable and low-risk
    • Finds bugs early: Automated tests catch regressions within minutes
    • Reduces manual work: No more "integration week" nightmares
    • Improves collaboration: Team sees everyone's changes in real-time
    • Builds confidence: Green builds mean deployable code

    CI Workflow:

    1. Developer writes code on feature branch 2. Developer commits and pushes to version control (Git) 3. CI server detects new commit (via webhook or polling) 4. CI server checks out code 5. CI server runs build process (compile, package) 6. CI server runs automated tests (unit, integration) 7. CI server reports results (pass/fail) 8. If pass → code ready for merge If fail → developer notified immediately to fix
    💡 CI Best Practices
    • Commit frequently (multiple times per day)
    • Don't commit broken code
    • Fix broken builds immediately
    • Keep builds fast (< 10 minutes ideal)
    • Test in a clone of production environment
    • Make it easy to get latest code
    • Everyone can see build results
    • Automate deployment to test environments

    1.5 Continuous Deployment (CD): Automation to Production

    Continuous Deployment extends CI to automatically deploy every change that passes tests to production. Continuous Delivery (also CD) means code is always deployment-ready, but requires manual approval for production release.

    🚀 Continuous Deployment vs. Continuous Delivery
    • Continuous Delivery: Code is always in deployable state. Requires human decision to deploy to production.
    • Continuous Deployment: Every change automatically goes to production after passing automated tests. No human gate.

    Most organizations start with Continuous Delivery and progress to Continuous Deployment as confidence grows. Continuous Deployment requires:

    • Comprehensive test coverage: High confidence in automated tests
    • Monitoring and observability: Detect production issues immediately
    • Feature flags: Deploy code without exposing features
    • Rollback capability: Quick recovery from bad deployments
    • Cultural maturity: Team comfort with automated production changes

    CD Pipeline Stages (Typical):

    ┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ │ Commit │────▶│ Build │────▶│ Test │ │ (Git) │ │ (Compile)│ │ (Automated) │ └─────────────┘ └──────────┘ └─────────────┘ │ ┌───────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ │ Package │────▶│ Deploy │────▶│ Monitor │ │ (Artifact) │ │ (Prod) │ │ (Observe) │ └─────────────┘ └──────────┘ └─────────────┘

    1.6 Deployment Strategies: How to Ship Safely

    Deploying new code to production is risky. Different deployment strategies manage this risk in different ways.

    Common Deployment Strategies

    1. Recreate (Big Bang)

    Shut down old version, deploy new version. Simple but causes downtime.

    Use case: Development environments, maintenance windows acceptable

    2. Rolling Update

    Gradually replace old instances with new ones. No downtime, but mixed versions temporarily coexist.

    Use case: Standard production deployments, backward-compatible changes

    3. Blue-Green Deployment

    Run two identical environments (Blue=current, Green=new). Switch traffic instantly. Easy rollback.

    Use case: Zero-downtime requirement, need instant rollback capability

    4. Canary Deployment

    Release new version to small subset of users (5-10%) first. Monitor metrics. Gradually increase if healthy.

    Use case: High-risk changes, A/B testing, progressive rollout

    5. Feature Flags (Dark Launch)

    Deploy code but keep features disabled. Enable features via configuration without redeployment.

    Use case: Decouple deployment from release, testing in production

    ⚠️ Common Deployment Mistakes
    • Deploying on Friday afternoons (no weekend recovery)
    • No rollback plan or tested rollback procedure
    • Deploying multiple changes at once (hard to identify culprit)
    • Insufficient monitoring to detect issues
    • No communication to stakeholders
    • Skipping testing "just this once" because it's urgent

    1.7 DevOps Toolchain: Overview of the Ecosystem

    DevOps relies on a rich ecosystem of tools. No single tool "does DevOps"—rather, you combine tools into a toolchain that supports your workflows. Think of it like building a house: you need different specialized tools (hammer, saw, drill, etc.), and they work together to achieve the final result.

    DevOps Toolchain Lifecycle

    PLAN → CODE → BUILD → TEST → RELEASE → DEPLOY → OPERATE ↓ MONITOR & FEEDBACK ↓ (Loop back to PLAN for improvements)

    Essential Tool Categories:

    Source Control

    Git, GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket - Version control for code

    CI/CD Platforms

    Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI, Travis CI - Automate build and deploy pipelines

    Containerization

    Docker, containerd, Podman - Package applications and dependencies

    Orchestration

    Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, Amazon ECS - Manage and scale containers

    Infrastructure as Code

    Terraform, AWS CloudFormation, Pulumi - Define infrastructure in code

    Configuration Management

    Ansible, Chef, Puppet, SaltStack - Automate system configuration

    Monitoring & Metrics

    Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog, New Relic - Track system health and performance

    Logging & Observability

    ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana), Splunk - Centralized logging and analysis

    Cloud Providers

    AWS, Azure, Google Cloud Platform - Infrastructure hosting and services

    This course focuses on the most widely-adopted open-source and cloud-native tools: Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, GitHub Actions, Prometheus, and Grafana. These appear in 80%+ of DevOps job descriptions.

    1.8 Infrastructure as Code: Bridging Development and Operations

    Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is the management of infrastructure (servers, networks, databases, storage) through machine-readable code rather than manual configuration. This is a cornerstone of DevOps.

    🏗️ IaC Principle

    Infrastructure should be defined, versioned, tested, and deployed like application code. Version control, code review, testing, and automation should apply to infrastructure as much as to applications.

    Why IaC Matters:

    • Consistency: Identical environments every time (dev, test, prod)
    • Reproducibility: Disaster recovery becomes straightforward
    • Scalability: Deploy entire infrastructure with one command
    • Auditability: Git history shows exactly who changed what when
    • Automation: Infrastructure changes integrate with CI/CD pipelines

    IaC Tools Overview: Terraform (cloud-agnostic), CloudFormation (AWS-specific), ARM Templates (Azure-specific), and Pulumi (programmatic IaC) represent the major categories. Later weeks will dive deep into Terraform as the industry-standard choice.

    1.9 DevOps Metrics & Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)

    "You can't improve what you don't measure." DevOps teams use specific metrics to track progress and identify improvement areas. The industry has converged on four key metrics recommended by DORA (DevOps Research and Assessment):

    Four Key DevOps Metrics (DORA)

    1. Deployment Frequency

    How often is code deployed to production? (Daily, Weekly, Monthly, etc.)

    Why it matters: Frequent, small deployments are lower-risk than infrequent, large releases. Elite performers deploy multiple times per day.

    2. Lead Time for Changes

    How long from code commit to production deployment? (Minutes, Hours, Days, etc.)

    Why it matters: Shorter lead times mean faster feedback loops and quicker business value delivery. Elite teams deploy in minutes.

    3. Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)

    How long does it take to recover from production incidents? (Minutes, Hours, Days)

    Why it matters: Reliability isn't about preventing all failures—it's about recovering quickly. Good monitoring and automation enable fast recovery.

    4. Change Failure Rate

    Percentage of deployments that cause production incidents (0-15%, 16-30%, 31-45%, 45%+)

    Why it matters: Elite performers deploy frequently AND safely. Comprehensive testing and gradual rollouts enable this balance.

    Tracking these metrics creates a feedback loop that drives continuous improvement. Teams compare their metrics against industry benchmarks and identify optimization opportunities. Later weeks will explore monitoring tools (Prometheus, Grafana) that automatically capture these metrics.

    Hands-On Labs

    These labs establish the foundation for all subsequent weeks. Complete them sequentially in the order provided. Budget 4-6 hours for all labs this week. Each lab should take 1-1.5 hours including troubleshooting.

    Lab 1: AWS Account Setup & Essential Tools Installation

    Objective: Establish your cloud infrastructure foundation and install development tools that will be used throughout the course.

    Prerequisites: Valid email address, credit/debit card for AWS account verification (no charges for Free Tier)

    1. Create AWS Free Tier Account
      • Visit aws.amazon.com → Click "Create AWS Account"
      • Enter email, password, AWS account name (e.g., "devops-learning")
      • Provide contact information (personal address acceptable)
      • Enter billing address and payment method (no charges for 12 months of Free Tier)
      • Choose "Business" as account type; verify phone number
      • AWS will send verification email—click link to confirm
      • Choose "Basic Support Plan" (free)
      • You're now in the AWS Console—save your Account ID (visible in top-right menu)
    2. Enable MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication)
      • Click account name (top-right) → "Security credentials"
      • Under "Multi-factor authentication (MFA)", click "Activate MFA"
      • Choose "Authenticator app" option
      • Install Google Authenticator or Authy on your phone
      • Scan the QR code shown on screen
      • Enter two consecutive codes from the app to verify
      • Store backup codes in secure location
    3. Create IAM User for Development
      • Go to AWS Console → Search "IAM" → Click "Users" → "Create user"
      • Username: "devops-learning" (for personal development)
      • Check "Provide user access to AWS Management Console"
      • Choose "I want to create an IAM user" → Create custom password
      • Uncheck "Users must create new password on next sign-in"
      • Proceed to next step → Click "Attach policies directly"
      • Search and select "AdministratorAccess" policy (appropriate for learning environment)
      • Create user → Save login URL, username, and password securely
    4. Create Access Keys
      • Click the IAM user you just created → "Security credentials" tab
      • Under "Access keys", click "Create access key"
      • Choose "Command Line Interface (CLI)" use case
      • Check "I understand..." → Create access key
      • IMPORTANT: Download CSV file immediately—you can't retrieve the secret key later
      • Store this file securely (will use in next steps)
    5. Install AWS CLI
      • Windows: Download MSI from aws.amazon.com/cli → Run installer
      • Mac: brew install awscli
      • Linux: sudo apt-get install awscli (Ubuntu/Debian) or equivalent for your distro
      • Verify installation: aws --version
    6. Configure AWS CLI
      • Open terminal/command prompt
      • Run: aws configure
      • Enter AWS Access Key ID (from CSV file)
      • Enter AWS Secret Access Key (from CSV file)
      • Default region name: us-east-1
      • Default output format: json
      • Verify configuration: aws sts get-caller-identity
    7. Install Docker Desktop
      • Visit docker.com → Sign up for free account (if needed)
      • Windows/Mac: Download Docker Desktop → Follow installer
      • Linux: sudo apt-get install docker.io docker-compose
      • Start Docker daemon (automatic on Windows/Mac, manual on Linux: sudo systemctl start docker)
      • Verify installation: docker --version
      • Run test container: docker run hello-world
    8. Install kubectl
      • Visit kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/ for OS-specific instructions
      • Mac: brew install kubectl
      • Windows: Download binary from kubernetes.io or use Chocolatey
      • Linux: sudo apt-get install kubectl
      • Verify: kubectl version --client
    9. Install Terraform
      • Visit terraform.io → Download section
      • Mac: brew tap hashicorp/tap && brew install hashicorp/tap/terraform
      • Windows/Linux: Download binary, extract to directory in PATH
      • Verify: terraform version
    10. Final Verification
      • Test AWS CLI: aws s3 ls (lists S3 buckets, should show empty list initially)
      • Test Docker: docker run -it ubuntu bash (enter Ubuntu container, type "exit" to leave)
      • Test kubectl: kubectl version --client
      • Test Terraform: terraform version
      • Create local directory: mkdir -p ~/devops-learning
      • All tools should respond without errors
    💡 Troubleshooting Lab 1

    AWS CLI not found after install? Path may not be updated. Close and reopen terminal, or manually add AWS CLI directory to PATH.
    Docker permission denied? Add current user to docker group: sudo usermod -aG docker $USER (Linux only)
    AWS configure credentials rejected? Verify CSV file contains correct keys; regenerate if unsure.

    Lab 2: GitHub Repository Setup & Local Development Environment

    Objective: Create a professional GitHub repository that will house all your course work and become your DevOps portfolio.

    1. Create GitHub Account (if needed)
      • Visit github.com → Click "Sign up"
      • Enter email, password, choose username (make it professional—this becomes your portfolio)
      • Verify email address
      • Choose "Free" plan
    2. Generate SSH Key Pair
      • Open terminal: ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -C "your-email@example.com"
      • Press Enter to accept default location (~/.ssh/id_rsa)
      • Enter secure passphrase when prompted (remember it!)
      • View public key: cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub
      • Copy the entire output (starts with "ssh-rsa")
    3. Add SSH Key to GitHub
      • GitHub → Settings (click profile picture, top-right) → SSH and GPG keys
      • Click "New SSH key" → Title: "DevOps Learning"
      • Paste the public key content into "Key" field
      • Click "Add SSH key" → Authenticate with GitHub password
      • Verify: Terminal → ssh -T git@github.com → Should say "Hi [username]!"
    4. Create New Repository
      • GitHub home → Click "New" → Repository name: "devops-engineering-course"
      • Description: "Course work for Hexadigitall DevOps Engineering & Cloud Infrastructure (Core Track)"
      • Choose "Public" (for portfolio sharing) or "Private" (for privacy)
      • Check "Add a README file"
      • Add .gitignore: "Node" (we'll use Node for some tools)
      • License: "MIT"
      • Click "Create repository"
    5. Clone Repository Locally
      • Click green "Code" button → Select "SSH" tab
      • Copy the SSH URL (git@github.com:...)
      • Terminal: cd ~/devops-learning && git clone [PASTE URL]
      • Navigate into repo: cd devops-engineering-course
    6. Configure Git Locally
      • git config --global user.name "Your Name"
      • git config --global user.email "your-email@example.com"
      • Verify: git config --list
    7. Create Folder Structure
      • mkdir -p week-1/{notes,labs,solutions,assignments}
      • mkdir -p week-1/labs/{lab-1,lab-2,lab-3,lab-4}
      • Create placeholder file: touch week-1/README.md
    8. Update README.md
      • Open README.md in text editor
      • Replace with:
      # DevOps Engineering & Cloud Infrastructure ## Hexadigitall Technologies - Core Track This repository contains my coursework and projects from the intensive 20-week DevOps Engineering program. ### Course Structure - **Duration:** 20 weeks - **Level:** Advanced - **Prerequisites:** Linux, Git, Python/Bash, Networking basics ### Weekly Contents - Week 1: DevOps Foundations & CI/CD Overview - Week 2: Docker Fundamentals & Containerization - (Weeks 3-20 to be added) ### Lab Progress - [ ] Week 1: Lab 1 - AWS Setup - [ ] Week 1: Lab 2 - GitHub & Local Environment - [ ] Week 1: Lab 3 - GitHub Actions CI - [ ] Week 1: Lab 4 - Docker Basics ### Learning Resources - Official course textbook - Tool documentation (links below) - Community forums and resources **Course Instructor:** Hexadigitall Technologies **Contact:** info@hexadigitall.com
    9. Make First Commit
      • Terminal: git add .
      • git commit -m "Initial course setup: Week 1 folder structure and README"
      • git push origin main
      • Refresh GitHub page to confirm changes appeared
    10. Verify Setup
      • Repository visible on GitHub with proper structure
      • README displays nicely on repository home page
      • All three commits visible in commit history
      • SSH authentication working (no password prompts)
    💡 Lab 2 Troubleshooting

    SSH key not recognized? Ensure your .ssh/id_rsa.pub file is correctly added to GitHub.
    Git credentials keep asking for password? Switch to SSH authentication (URL should start with git@github.com, not https://)
    Git commit failing? Check that user.name and user.email are configured.

    Lab 3: First CI Pipeline with GitHub Actions

    Objective: Create your first CI/CD pipeline using GitHub Actions—the foundation for all automation work ahead.

    1. Create GitHub Actions Workflow Directory
      • In your local repo: mkdir -p .github/workflows
      • Create file: touch .github/workflows/basic-pipeline.yml
    2. Write Basic CI Pipeline Workflow
      • Open basic-pipeline.yml and add:
      name: Basic DevOps Pipeline on: push: branches: [ main, develop ] pull_request: branches: [ main ] jobs: build: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - name: Checkout code uses: actions/checkout@v3 - name: Print system info run: | echo "=== System Information ===" uname -a pwd echo "=== Git Information ===" git log -1 --pretty=%B - name: Run basic tests run: | echo "=== Running Validation ===" echo "Checking file structure..." if [ -f README.md ]; then echo "✓ README.md found" else echo "✗ README.md not found" exit 1 fi echo "✓ All basic checks passed!" - name: Success notification if: success() run: echo "🎉 Pipeline completed successfully!"
    3. Commit and Push Workflow
      • Terminal: git add .github/
      • git commit -m "Add basic CI pipeline with GitHub Actions"
      • git push origin main
    4. View Workflow Execution
      • GitHub repository → "Actions" tab
      • You should see "Basic DevOps Pipeline" running or completed
      • Click on workflow to see detailed execution
      • Expand "build" job to see each step's output
      • You should see green checkmarks for all steps
    5. Verify Pipeline Output
      • In the workflow output, you should see:
      • System information (Ubuntu version, file path)
      • Git commit message you just pushed
      • File validation output ("✓ README.md found")
      • Success message with emoji
    6. Trigger Pipeline with New Commit
      • Make any file change locally: echo "# Week 1 Notes" > week-1/notes.md
      • git add week-1/notes.md
      • git commit -m "Add Week 1 notes placeholder"
      • git push origin main
      • GitHub Actions → Watch new workflow execution
      • Confirm pipeline runs automatically on every push
    7. Document Learning
      • In week-1/notes.md, document what you learned:
      # Week 1 - GitHub Actions CI Pipeline ## What is GitHub Actions? GitHub Actions is GitHub's built-in CI/CD platform. It runs workflows automatically when triggered by repository events (push, pull request, etc.). ## Key Concepts - **Workflow:** Automated process defined in YAML - **Job:** Set of steps that run in sequence - **Step:** Individual task (checkout code, run command, etc.) - **Runner:** Virtual machine that executes the workflow ## Our First Pipeline - Triggers on: push to main/develop or pull request to main - Runs on: Ubuntu latest - Steps: Checkout, print system info, validate files, success notification ## Next Steps - Add more complex tests - Add Docker and Kubernetes steps - Integrate with deployment steps
    8. Push Documentation
      • git add week-1/notes.md
      • git commit -m "Document GitHub Actions learning"
      • git push origin main
    💡 Lab 3 Troubleshooting

    Workflow not triggering? YAML indentation must be perfect (GitHub Actions is very sensitive to whitespace). Use spaces, not tabs.
    Can't see Actions tab? Repository may have Actions disabled. Go to Settings → Actions and enable.
    Workflow failed with cryptic error? Click on failed step and read the full error output carefully.

    Lab 4: Local Container Setup & Docker Basics

    Objective: Get hands-on with Docker, the containerization technology that powers DevOps infrastructure.

    1. Verify Docker Installation
      • Terminal: docker --version (should show version 20.x or later)
      • docker run hello-world (downloads and runs test container)
      • Should print message: "Hello from Docker! This message shows that your installation appears to be working correctly."
    2. Explore Container Basics
      • List downloaded images: docker images
      • You should see "hello-world" image and possibly "ubuntu"
      • Run interactive Ubuntu container: docker run -it ubuntu:latest bash
    3. Explore Container Environment (inside container)
      • Once inside Ubuntu container, run: cat /etc/os-release (shows container OS info)
      • ps aux (shows running processes—very minimal)
      • whoami (shows current user—typically "root")
      • apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl (install curl tool)
      • curl https://www.hexadigitall.com (demonstrates network connectivity)
      • exit (leave container)
    4. Create Simple Dockerfile
      • Create directory: mkdir -p ~/devops-learning/docker-app && cd docker-app
      • Create Dockerfile: touch Dockerfile
      • Add content:
      FROM ubuntu:latest # Set working directory WORKDIR /app # Install dependencies RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \ curl \ wget \ git \ && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* # Create a simple script RUN echo '#!/bin/bash' > /app/greet.sh && \ echo 'echo "Hello from Docker Container! 🐳"' >> /app/greet.sh && \ echo 'echo "Current directory: $(pwd)"' >> /app/greet.sh && \ echo 'echo "User: $(whoami)"' >> /app/greet.sh && \ chmod +x /app/greet.sh # Run script by default CMD ["/app/greet.sh"]
    5. Build Docker Image
      • Terminal (in docker-app directory): docker build -t my-devops-app:1.0 .
      • Watch the build process (should take 1-2 minutes)
      • You should see:
      • Each line of Dockerfile executed
      • "Successfully built [IMAGE ID]"
      • Verify: docker images | grep my-devops-app
    6. Run Custom Container
      • docker run my-devops-app:1.0
      • Should output:
      Hello from Docker Container! 🐳 Current directory: /app User: root
    7. Run Container Interactively
      • docker run -it my-devops-app:1.0 bash
      • Now inside container shell: ls -la (see /app directory with greet.sh)
      • ./greet.sh (manually run the script)
      • exit
    8. Check Running Containers
      • docker ps -a (shows all containers, even stopped ones)
      • You should see multiple entries from your runs
      • Note: Container names are auto-generated if not specified
    9. Save Work to Repository
      • Copy Dockerfile to course repo: cp -r ~/devops-learning/docker-app ~/devops-learning/devops-engineering-course/week-1/labs/lab-4/
      • Create README for lab: touch ~/devops-learning/devops-engineering-course/week-1/labs/lab-4/README.md
      • Add content explaining what you did:
      # Lab 4: Docker Basics ## Objectives Completed - [x] Verified Docker installation - [x] Ran interactive container - [x] Created custom Dockerfile - [x] Built Docker image - [x] Ran custom container ## Key Learnings 1. Docker separates application from infrastructure 2. Containers are lightweight and fast to start 3. Dockerfiles define container configuration as code 4. Images are templates; containers are running instances ## Commands Reference - `docker build -t name:tag .` - Build image from Dockerfile - `docker run image` - Run container from image - `docker run -it image bash` - Run interactive bash shell - `docker images` - List downloaded images - `docker ps -a` - List all containers ## Next Steps - Learn Docker networking - Build multi-container applications - Push images to Docker registry
    10. Commit to GitHub
      • Terminal (in course repo): git add week-1/labs/lab-4/
      • git commit -m "Complete Lab 4: Docker basics - first custom image and container"
      • git push origin main
    💡 Lab 4 Troubleshooting

    Docker daemon not running? Start Docker Desktop (Mac/Windows) or run sudo systemctl start docker (Linux).
    Build fails with "permission denied"? Add user to docker group: sudo usermod -aG docker $USER (Linux)
    Image build very slow first time? Downloading base OS and packages takes time. Subsequent builds are faster due to caching.

    Lab Solutions & Troubleshooting Guides

    Lab 1 Solution: Expected Outputs & Common Issues

    Expected Output After Completing Lab 1:

    $ kubectl version --client Client Version: v1.27.0 $ terraform version Terraform v1.5.0 on linux_amd64

    Lab 1 Troubleshooting Reference:

    ⚠️ Common Issue: AWS CLI Configuration Failed

    Problem: aws sts get-caller-identity fails with "Unable to locate credentials"

    Solution: Verify credentials are correctly configured:

    • Run aws configure list to check current configuration
    • Verify CSV file from IAM has valid Access Key ID and Secret Key
    • Confirm credentials file exists: cat ~/.aws/credentials
    • If empty, run aws configure again and re-enter credentials

    Labs 2-4 Solutions Summary

    ✓ Lab 2: GitHub Repository & Local Environment

    Expected Outcome: GitHub repository created with proper folder structure (week-1/labs, week-1/solutions, etc.), SSH authentication working without password prompts, all initial commits pushed and visible on GitHub.

    ✓ Lab 3: GitHub Actions CI Pipeline

    Expected Outcome: GitHub Actions workflow automatically executes on every commit, displays green checkmarks for all steps, demonstrates working CI/CD automation with system info and file validation output.

    ✓ Lab 4: Docker Fundamentals

    Expected Outcome: Custom Docker image built successfully (my-devops-app:1.0), containers run with expected greeting output, Dockerfile and detailed README saved and committed to GitHub.

    Practice Questions & Answers

    Work through these 10 questions to test your understanding of Week 1 concepts. Answers are provided below—attempt each question before reviewing the answer.

    1. What is the primary difference between DevOps culture and traditional software development?

      Answer: DevOps breaks down silos between development and operations teams, creating shared responsibility for the entire software delivery lifecycle. In traditional approaches, developers handed off code to operations, creating inefficiency and blame culture. DevOps emphasizes "you build it, you run it" mentality, shared responsibility, and cross-functional collaboration.

    2. Explain the CALMS framework and give an example of how each principle improves software delivery.

      Answer: CALMS stands for Culture, Automation, Lean, Measurement, Sharing. Culture (collaboration) reduces handoff delays. Automation eliminates manual errors and speeds up deployments. Lean (small batches) reduces risk by deploying frequently. Measurement (data-driven) enables continuous improvement. Sharing (knowledge transfer) prevents knowledge silos.

    3. What are "The Three Ways" in DevOps philosophy, and how do they relate to continuous improvement?

      Answer: The Three Ways are Systems Thinking (optimize flow), Amplify Feedback Loops (learn quickly), and Culture of Experimentation (safe to fail). Together they create a cycle: optimize flow → see results → experiment with improvements → optimize further. This continuous feedback loop drives continuous improvement.

    4. Describe the CI/CD workflow. What are the benefits of each practice?

      Answer: CI (Continuous Integration) merges code frequently (multiple times daily) with automated testing. This catches integration bugs early. CD (Continuous Deployment) automates the path to production. Benefits: reduced integration risk, faster bug detection, faster deployment, reduced manual work, higher confidence in code quality.

    5. Compare the five deployment strategies. When would you use canary deployments over blue-green deployments?

      Answer: Blue-green runs two identical environments and switches traffic instantly—good for critical systems needing zero downtime. Canary gradually shifts traffic to new version—better for testing unknowns. Use canary when you have concerns about new features, as it limits blast radius. Use blue-green when you need instant rollback capability or have well-tested changes.

    6. What is Infrastructure as Code, and why is it essential to DevOps?

      Answer: Infrastructure as Code means defining infrastructure (servers, networks, databases) in code files that can be versioned, tested, and deployed automatically. It's essential because it enables: consistency (identical environments), reproducibility (disaster recovery), auditability (Git history), and integration with CI/CD pipelines (infrastructure changes trigger automated workflows).

    7. Explain the four key DevOps metrics (DORA). Which would you prioritize first if improving your organization's DevOps maturity?

      Answer: The four metrics are: Deployment Frequency (how often), Lead Time (how fast from commit to production), Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR, how quickly we recover from failures), and Change Failure Rate (what percentage of deployments cause incidents). I'd prioritize Lead Time first because it requires building the infrastructure and discipline (testing, monitoring, automation) that naturally improves the other metrics.

    8. Why do you think "blameless post-mortems" are important to DevOps culture?

      Answer: Blameless post-mortems create psychological safety—people are more likely to report incidents and discuss root causes honestly if they're not afraid of punishment. This leads to better learning, faster improvements, and stronger team trust. It shifts focus from "who failed" to "what failed and how can we prevent it"—enabling the "Culture of Experimentation" that the Third Way emphasizes.

    9. You're building a CI/CD pipeline for a financial services application. What concerns should guide your design?

      Answer: Financial services require: comprehensive automated testing (security, compliance), extensive monitoring and alerting, gradual rollout strategies (canary or blue-green), rapid rollback capability, audit trails of every change, compliance with regulatory requirements. You'd prioritize Change Failure Rate and MTTR over deployment frequency, as reliability matters more than speed in this domain.

    10. How would you convince a traditional Ops team that DevOps improves their work rather than threatening their jobs?

      Answer: DevOps elevates Ops from manual configuration to system design, automation, monitoring, and optimization. Instead of firefighting, Ops engineers become reliability engineers (SREs). Key message: automation handles repetitive toil; Ops teams focus on architecture, observability, incident response, and strategic improvements. This is more fulfilling work and commands higher salaries. Show how MTTR improvements and fewer incidents reduce stress.

    Weekly Quiz

    Format for Google Classroom: This quiz contains 10 multiple-choice questions. Copy and paste into Google Classroom for auto-grading. Answer key provided below.

    Week 1 DevOps Foundations Quiz

    1. What is DevOps primarily focused on?

    • A) Automating server management
    • B) Unifying development and operations to deliver software faster and more reliably
    • C) Replacing system administrators with automation tools
    • D) Using only open-source software for all infrastructure

    2. Which of the following is NOT part of the CALMS framework?

    • A) Automation
    • B) Scaling
    • C) Lean
    • D) Measurement

    3. What is the primary benefit of Continuous Integration?

    • A) Eliminates the need for testing
    • B) Catches integration problems early through frequent merging and automated testing
    • C) Makes manual deployments faster
    • D) Reduces the number of developers needed

    4. Which deployment strategy allows you to gradually shift traffic to a new version while monitoring for errors?

    • A) Blue-Green Deployment
    • B) Canary Deployment
    • C) Recreate Deployment
    • D) Rolling Update

    5. What does "you build it, you run it" mean in DevOps culture?

    • A) Developers should also handle customer support
    • B) Developers should be responsible for the code they write and its performance in production
    • C) Operations teams are no longer needed
    • D) Code must be built and run on the same machine

    6. Which of the "Four Key Metrics" measures how quickly you can recover from a production incident?

    • A) Deployment Frequency
    • B) Lead Time for Changes
    • C) Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)
    • D) Change Failure Rate

    7. What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?

    • A) Writing code that generates infrastructure (servers, networks) from configuration files
    • B) The programming language used to create infrastructure
    • C) A code review process for infrastructure changes
    • D) Using code to replace all system administrators

    8. What is the Third Way in DevOps philosophy primarily about?

    • A) Using three different deployment strategies
    • B) Creating a culture of experimentation and learning from failures
    • C) Deploying to three different cloud providers
    • D) Having three levels of management review

    9. Why are blameless post-mortems important to DevOps culture?

    • A) To avoid holding anyone responsible for failures
    • B) To focus on root cause analysis and learning rather than assigning blame
    • C) To make incidents seem less serious than they are
    • D) To eliminate the need for incident response procedures

    10. What is the main advantage of frequent deployments over infrequent large releases?

    • A) They're always faster
    • B) They have lower risk and allow faster recovery if problems occur
    • C) They require fewer developers
    • D) They eliminate all possibility of failures
    📋 Quiz Answer Key

    1. B | 2. B | 3. B | 4. B | 5. B | 6. C | 7. A | 8. B | 9. B | 10. B

    Weekly Test: Applied Scenarios

    These 5 scenario-based questions assess your ability to apply Week 1 concepts to real-world situations. Expect to write 1-2 paragraphs per question. These can be submitted as a Google Classroom short-answer assignment.

    1. Scenario 1 - DevOps Transformation: A software company currently releases new versions once every 6 months through a manual process involving multiple handoffs between teams. They experience frequent deployment failures and take 2-3 weeks to recover from production incidents. As a DevOps consultant, describe how you would transform their software delivery using DevOps principles.

      Sample Answer (Grading Focus): Should include: establishing CI/CD automation to reduce deployment frequency, implementing monitoring/alerting to improve MTTR, fostering collaboration culture, measuring baseline metrics, phased adoption strategy. Strong answers will reference CALMS framework, deployment strategies, and business benefits. Poor answers focus only on tools without cultural change.

    2. Scenario 2 - Deployment Strategy Selection: Your e-commerce company receives 50% of annual revenue during a 72-hour holiday shopping period. A critical bug is found 24 hours before peak sales. Would you use blue-green or canary deployment to fix this? Justify your choice, considering the context.

      Sample Answer: Blue-green deployment is better here because: (1) you need instant rollback capability if something goes wrong, (2) the timing is too critical for gradual canary rollout, (3) the fix is urgent and proven, (4) revenue impact is high so risk must be minimized. A good answer explains trade-offs (why not canary) and considers business context, not just technical factors.

    3. Scenario 3 - CI/CD Pipeline Failure: Your new CI/CD pipeline was working well for 2 weeks. Then developers started complaining that they're spending more time fixing broken builds than shipping features. The build failure rate is now 40%. What's likely happening, and how would you investigate?

      Sample Answer: Should identify possible causes: tests are flaky/unreliable, test coverage is inadequate for recent changes, CI environment doesn't match development environment, or developers aren't running tests locally before commit. Investigation should include: examine failing test results, analyze test history for patterns, survey developers about their CI workflow, measure test reliability. Good answers show systematic troubleshooting approach.

    4. Scenario 4 - Infrastructure Challenge: Your company manages infrastructure for 50 microservices across 5 environments (dev, staging, prod for 3 regions). Each service needs its own database, cache, load balancer. When engineers manually configure new environments, mistakes happen frequently and configuration drifts over time. How would Infrastructure as Code help solve this?

      Sample Answer: IaC would enable: defining all infrastructure in version-controlled code files, reproducing identical environments consistently, auditing all changes in Git history, automating environment provisioning (self-service), testing infrastructure changes before deployment. Good answers explain specific benefits (consistency, reproducibility, scalability, auditability) and mention tools like Terraform. Should acknowledge learning curve during transition.

    5. Scenario 5 - Post-Mortem Culture Problem: After a major production outage, your incident report shows "Developer A made a bad deployment decision" as the root cause. When management suggests terminating the developer, your DevOps lead says this violates DevOps principles. Explain why the DevOps lead is correct and what a blameless post-mortem should focus on instead.

      Sample Answer: A blameless post-mortem recognizes that individuals don't cause system failures; failures result from system and process deficiencies. Instead of blaming Developer A, ask: Why did our process allow bad deployments? Were safeguards missing (automated tests, approval process, monitoring, gradual rollout)? What training or tooling would have prevented this? How can we improve systems to prevent recurrence? Good answers mention psychological safety, continuous learning, systems thinking, and connection to "Third Way" culture of experimentation.

    Weekly Assignment: DevOps Transformation Proposal

    Objective: Synthesize Week 1 learning by creating a DevOps transformation proposal for a hypothetical organization.

    Scenario: Acme Corp is a 200-person software company with 4 development teams and a separate operations team. Current state: annual releases, 3-month recovery time from incidents, poor communication between teams, high turnover. Management wants to improve software delivery velocity and reliability.

    Assignment Requirements

    Part 1: Current State Assessment (15%)

    Analyze Acme Corp's current state using the Four Key Metrics and CALMS framework. For each metric, estimate current performance (e.g., "Deployment Frequency: Once annually"). Identify specific deficiencies in CALMS dimensions.

    Part 2: DevOps Vision (25%)

    Define what DevOps success looks like for Acme Corp. Set target metrics (e.g., "Deploy 20x per day, MTTR under 15 minutes"). Describe culture changes (breaking silos, shared responsibility, blameless post-mortems). Explain how you'd implement "you build it, you run it."

    Part 3: Implementation Roadmap (30%)

    Create a 6-month roadmap with specific initiatives: Phase 1 (Months 1-2): CI/CD pipeline for one team, cultural workshops. Phase 2 (Months 3-4): Expand CI/CD, implement IaC basics. Phase 3 (Months 5-6): Company-wide adoption, advanced monitoring. For each phase, list specific goals, owner team, success metrics.

    Part 4: Deployment Strategy (15%)

    Recommend which deployment strategies Acme Corp should adopt and why. Consider their service types, risk tolerance, and team maturity. Justify your choices with examples from their current challenges.

    Part 5: Risk Mitigation (15%)

    Identify potential risks to successful DevOps transformation (cultural resistance, tool complexity, initial slowdown). For each risk, propose mitigation strategies. Explain how quick wins in Phase 1 build momentum for later phases.

    Submission Format

    Submit as one of the following:

    • Document: 3-5 page Word/PDF document, 12pt font, 1.5 spacing
    • Slides: 15-20 slide presentation (Google Slides or PowerPoint)
    • GitHub Document: Well-formatted Markdown file in your course repository

    Grading Rubric

    Criteria Excellent (4) Good (3) Fair (2) Poor (1)
    Current State Assessment Accurately applies Four Key Metrics and CALMS framework; identifies specific root causes Applies frameworks; identifies most deficiencies Attempts framework application; misses some elements Vague or inaccurate assessment
    DevOps Vision Clear, measurable targets; comprehensive culture changes; explains team structure Good targets and culture changes; mostly clear Targets present but vague; limited culture consideration Unclear or incomplete vision
    Implementation Roadmap Realistic 6-month plan; phased approach; specific goals and owners; metrics defined Good roadmap with phases; mostly specific; most metrics defined Roadmap present but vague; unclear responsibilities or metrics Insufficient detail or unrealistic plan
    Deployment Strategy Well-justified recommendations; considers multiple factors; examples from scenario Good recommendations with justification; adequate examples Recommendations present; limited justification or examples Weak or unjustified recommendations
    Risk Mitigation Identifies 4+ risks; realistic mitigation strategies; explains momentum-building Identifies 3 risks; good mitigation strategies; mentions momentum Identifies 2 risks; basic mitigation strategies One or fewer risks identified; weak mitigation

    Submission Deadline: End of Week 1 (7 days from course start)

    Feedback: Assignments will be reviewed within 48 hours. You'll receive detailed feedback on your DevOps thinking and proposal quality to guide Week 2 learning.

    Mentorship Review for Week 1

    Mentorship students: your Week 1 work (DevOps Foundations & CI/CD Overview) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    2

    Docker Fundamentals & Containerization

    Understanding Container Architecture, Image Creation, Container Management, and Registry Operations

    This week marks your transition from DevOps principles to practical containerization. Docker revolutionized how we package and deploy applications by introducing lightweight, portable containers. You'll understand the architecture of containers, learn to build and manage images, run containers in various configurations, and prepare applications for production deployment. By week's end, you'll have built multiple Docker images, managed containerized applications, and understand how containers form the foundation for modern cloud-native development.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Docker Fundamentals & Containerization: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define container, image, registry, and related Docker terminology. List Docker components and their functions.

    Understand

    Explain how containers differ from virtual machines. Describe Docker architecture and the image layer system.

    Apply

    Build Docker images using Dockerfiles. Run containers with appropriate configurations. Manage volumes and networks.

    Analyze

    Troubleshoot container issues. Optimize Dockerfiles for performance and security. Compare image build strategies.

    Evaluate

    Assess image security vulnerabilities. Critique Dockerfile best practices. Evaluate registry options for different scenarios.

    Create

    Design multi-stage Dockerfiles. Create container orchestration strategies. Build custom images for production applications.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Complete these exercises before diving into the main teaching content. They prepare your understanding and activate prior knowledge. Budget 2-3 hours for this preparatory work.

    1Container vs Virtual Machine Comparison

    Research and write 200-300 words comparing containers to traditional virtual machines. Focus on: resource usage, startup time, isolation level, and use cases. Why might a container be preferable to a VM for certain scenarios?

    💡 Research Prompt

    Search for "containers vs virtual machines" and read 2-3 articles. Create a comparison table with at least 5 criteria.

    2Docker Installation & Verification

    Install Docker Desktop on your computer (Windows, Mac, or Linux version). Run these commands in your terminal to verify installation:

    docker --version docker run hello-world docker ps

    Take a screenshot showing successful output. Document any errors you encounter—we'll troubleshoot in class.

    3Docker Hub Exploration

    Visit hub.docker.com (Docker's official registry) and explore:

    • Official images (nginx, python, ubuntu, postgres)
    • Read the description and documentation for at least 3 images
    • Note the download/usage statistics—these are popular images for a reason
    • Find one image related to your interests and describe it

    Write 150 words summarizing what you learned about image versioning and tags.

    4Dockerfile Example Study

    Search GitHub for "Dockerfile examples" and study 2-3 Dockerfiles. Create an annotated version of one, explaining what each line does. Focus on understanding: FROM, RUN, COPY, WORKDIR, EXPOSE, CMD commands.

    💡 Learning Tip

    Don't worry if you don't understand everything—this exercise builds familiarity. We'll deep-dive into Dockerfile syntax in the teaching notes.

    5Real-World Container Applications

    Research and list 5 major technology companies or projects that use Docker/containers extensively (hint: Netflix, Spotify, Amazon, Google all use containers). For each, write 2-3 sentences explaining what they containerize and why it matters.

    This exercise contextualizes why container skills are highly valuable in industry.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    2.1 Container Architecture: Why Containers Matter

    Before Docker and containers, the primary unit of deployment was either: (1) bare metal servers (expensive, slow to provision), or (2) virtual machines (resource-heavy, each running a full OS). Containers revolutionized this by providing a lightweight alternative.

    What is a Container? A container is a standardized unit of software that packages code, runtime, system tools, libraries, and settings into an isolated, executable package. Unlike virtual machines that virtualize the entire hardware stack, containers virtualize only the operating system layer, sharing the host kernel.

    🐳 Containers vs Virtual Machines

    Virtual Machines: Full OS per VM (2-10GB each), minutes to start, slower. Containers: Share host kernel (MB per container), seconds to start, faster. Containers are lightweight; VMs are isolated.

    Key Benefits of Containers:

    • Consistency: "Builds once, runs anywhere" - identical environment from dev to production
    • Efficiency: Lightweight, fast startup, minimal resource overhead
    • Scalability: Run dozens or hundreds of containers on single machine
    • Isolation: Container processes isolated from each other and host
    • Portability: Container image works on any system with container runtime
    • DevOps Culture: Enables "you build it, you ship it" responsibility model

    2.2 Docker Architecture & Core Components

    Docker's architecture consists of several key components working together:

    Docker Architecture Components

    1. Docker Engine

    The core runtime that creates and manages containers. Runs as a daemon process on the host OS. Communicates via REST API.

    2. Docker Client

    Command-line interface (docker command) that sends instructions to Docker daemon. Primary tool for daily Docker interaction.

    3. Docker Images

    Read-only blueprints containing all code, dependencies, and configuration needed to run an application. Built from Dockerfile instructions.

    4. Docker Containers

    Running instances of images. Writable layer added to image. Containers are ephemeral - they can be created and destroyed quickly.

    5. Docker Registry

    Repository of images (Docker Hub public, private registries). Enables image sharing and distribution across teams and systems.

    6. Volumes & Networking

    Persistent storage (volumes), networking between containers and external systems (networks, ports).

    2.3 Docker Images & the Layer System

    Docker images are built in layers. Each instruction in a Dockerfile creates a layer. Understanding layers is crucial for optimizing image size and build time.

    📦 Image Layers Concept

    Docker images use a Union File System (typically aufs, overlay2, or btrfs). Each layer is a set of file differences from the layer below. Layers are read-only; only the running container has a writable layer. Layers are cached - if a layer hasn't changed, Docker reuses it from cache.

    Common Image Instructions (Dockerfile):

    FROM ubuntu:22.04 # Base layer - every image starts here RUN apt-get update && \ # Create layer with updates apt-get install -y python3 COPY app.py /app/ # Create layer with application files WORKDIR /app # Set working directory EXPOSE 8000 # Document exposed port CMD ["python3", "app.py"] # Default command when container starts

    Layer Caching & Optimization: Docker caches layers after first build. If you modify a layer, all subsequent layers must be rebuilt. Put frequently-changing instructions (like COPY app.py) near the end, stable instructions (like base OS) near the beginning. This maximizes cache hits and speeds up rebuilds.

    2.4 Docker Containers & Container Lifecycle

    A container is a running instance of an image. Understanding the container lifecycle is essential for managing containerized applications.

    Container States & Lifecycle

    Created

    Container created but not started. Use docker create or when pulling an image.

    Running

    Container actively executing. Started with docker run or docker start. Processes are active.

    Paused

    Container processes suspended. Use docker pause to freeze, docker unpause to resume.

    Stopped

    Container process exited. Container still exists with its filesystem. Use docker restart to start again.

    Removed

    Container and its filesystem deleted. Use docker rm. Cannot restart. Data is lost unless in volumes.

    Key Container Commands:

    docker run -d --name myapp ubuntu:22.04 # Create and start container docker ps # List running containers docker ps -a # List all containers (including stopped) docker logs myapp # View container output docker exec -it myapp bash # Execute command in running container docker stop myapp # Gracefully stop container docker kill myapp # Force stop container docker rm myapp # Remove stopped container

    2.5 Container Networking Basics

    Networking allows containers to communicate with each other and the outside world. Docker provides multiple networking options for different use cases.

    Docker Network Drivers

    Bridge (Default)

    Default network type. Containers on same bridge can communicate. Each container gets unique IP. Containers isolated from external network unless ports exposed.

    Host

    Container uses host's network directly. No IP isolation. Container ports directly map to host ports. Performance best, least isolated.

    None

    Container has no network. Only loopback interface. Use for completely isolated containers.

    Overlay (Swarm/Kubernetes)

    Multi-host networking. Containers on different hosts can communicate securely. Used in orchestration platforms.

    Port Mapping: Expose container ports to external access using -p flag:

    docker run -p 8080:3000 myapp # Map host port 8080 to container port 3000 docker run -p 127.0.0.1:8080:3000 myapp # Only accessible on localhost docker run -d -p 8080:3000 -p 8443:443 myapp # Multiple port mappings

    Container-to-Container Communication: Containers on the same network can communicate using container name as hostname. This enables microservice architectures where services discover each other by name.

    💡 Networking Best Practice

    Create custom bridges for multi-container applications. Use container names for DNS resolution instead of hardcoding IPs. This improves portability and resilience.

    2.6 Volumes & Data Persistence

    One of the most critical concepts to understand about containers is that their filesystem is ephemeral by default. When a container is removed, all data written to its filesystem is lost. For stateful applications (databases, file uploads, logs), this is unacceptable.

    💾 The Container Data Problem

    Containers are designed to be immutable and disposable. You should be able to destroy and recreate any container without losing critical data. This requires separating application logic (in the container) from application data (in volumes).

    Docker provides three mechanisms for data persistence:

    Docker Storage Options

    1. Volumes (Recommended)

    Managed by Docker, stored in Docker's storage location on the host. Completely managed by Docker, work on all platforms (Linux, Windows, Mac), can be shared between containers, and can be backed up or migrated easily. This is the preferred mechanism for persistence.

    2. Bind Mounts

    Map a specific host directory to a container directory. Useful for development (mount your code directly) but creates tight coupling between container and host filesystem. Less portable across environments. Use for development, not production.

    3. tmpfs Mounts (Linux Only)

    Store data in host memory, not on disk. Data is lost when container stops. Useful for sensitive information (passwords, tokens) that you don't want persisted to disk, or for high-performance temporary storage needs.

    Working with Named Volumes:

    # Create a named volume docker volume create my-data-volume # List all volumes docker volume ls # Inspect a volume (see mount point, metadata) docker volume inspect my-data-volume # Run container with volume mounted docker run -d --name my-app \ -v my-data-volume:/app/data \ ubuntu:22.04 # Volume persists even after container removal docker rm -f my-app docker volume ls # Volume still exists # Remove a volume (only when no containers use it) docker volume rm my-data-volume # Remove all unused volumes docker volume prune

    Volume Use Cases:

    • Database storage: MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB data directories
    • Application uploads: User-uploaded files, images, documents
    • Configuration persistence: Settings that survive container recreation
    • Log files: Application logs you need to analyze or ship to log aggregation
    • Shared data: Multiple containers reading/writing common data
    💡 Volume Best Practice

    Always use named volumes (not anonymous volumes) in production. Named volumes are easier to manage, backup, and migrate. Document which volumes each service needs in your docker-compose.yml or deployment manifests.

    2.7 Docker Registry & Image Management

    A Docker Registry is a storage and distribution system for Docker images. It's the "GitHub for Docker images"—a central repository where you push images for storage and pull images for deployment.

    📦 Registry Hierarchy

    Images are organized hierarchically: registry/namespace/repository:tag. For example: docker.io/library/nginx:1.25.3 where docker.io is the registry (Docker Hub), library is the namespace (official images), nginx is the repository, and 1.25.3 is the tag.

    Public vs. Private Registries:

    • Docker Hub: The default public registry. Free for public images, paid for private repos. Hosts official images (nginx, ubuntu, python) and millions of community images.
    • Amazon ECR: AWS's managed registry, tight integration with ECS/EKS, supports vulnerability scanning.
    • Google Container Registry (GCR): Google Cloud's registry, integrated with GKE and Cloud Build.
    • Azure Container Registry (ACR): Microsoft Azure's registry, geo-replication and security features.
    • Private Registry: Self-hosted using Docker's open-source registry or commercial solutions like Harbor, Artifactory.

    Image Naming & Tagging Strategy:

    # Full image reference format [registry]/[namespace]/[repository]:[tag] # Examples docker.io/library/nginx:1.25.3 # Docker Hub official mycompany.azurecr.io/backend:v2.3.1 # Azure Container Registry 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/frontend:latest # AWS ECR # Tagging strategies myapp:v1.2.3 # Semantic versioning (production) myapp:dev-abc123 # Development with git commit SHA myapp:latest # Always points to newest (dangerous in production!) myapp:stable # Current stable release myapp:2024-01-14 # Date-based tagging
    ⚠️ Avoid :latest in Production
