    Never use the :latest tag in production deployments. It's a moving target—today's "latest" is different from tomorrow's "latest," making rollbacks impossible and debugging nightmares. Always use specific version tags (semantic versioning or git SHAs) for reproducible deployments.

    Working with Registries:

    # Login to a registry docker login # Docker Hub (prompts for credentials) docker login myregistry.azurecr.io # Custom registry # Tag an image for pushing docker tag my-app:latest myregistry.io/my-app:v1.0.0 # Push image to registry docker push myregistry.io/my-app:v1.0.0 # Pull image from registry docker pull myregistry.io/my-app:v1.0.0 # Search Docker Hub for images docker search nginx # Logout from registry docker logout myregistry.azurecr.io

    Image Security Scanning:

    Modern registries provide vulnerability scanning—analyzing images for known security issues (CVEs). Docker Hub, ECR, GCR, and ACR all offer automated scanning. Always review scan results before deploying to production.

    🔒 Registry Security Best Practices
    • Use private registries for proprietary applications
    • Enable vulnerability scanning on all images
    • Implement image signing and verification (Docker Content Trust)
    • Use least-privilege access controls (IAM roles, service accounts)
    • Regularly update base images to patch vulnerabilities
    • Scan images in CI pipeline before deployment

    2.8 Docker Best Practices & Performance Optimization

    Building containers is easy. Building production-ready, secure, performant containers requires discipline and best practices. This section covers essential guidelines that separate hobbyist Docker usage from professional DevOps engineering.

    Dockerfile Optimization Strategies:

    Production Dockerfile Best Practices

    1. Use Official Base Images

    Start with official images (python:3.11, node:20, nginx:1.25) from Docker Hub's "library" namespace. They're maintained by dedicated teams, regularly updated for security patches, and optimized for size and performance. Avoid random community images.

    2. Minimize Layer Count

    Each RUN, COPY, ADD instruction creates a new layer. Combine related commands into single RUN statements using && to reduce layer count. Example: RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y package1 package2 && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

    3. Order Instructions by Changeability

    Place instructions that rarely change (base image, system dependencies) at the top. Place frequently changing instructions (COPY application code) at the bottom. This maximizes Docker's layer caching, making builds faster by reusing unchanged layers.

    4. Use .dockerignore

    Create a .dockerignore file to exclude unnecessary files from build context (node_modules, .git, logs, test files). Smaller build context = faster builds and smaller images. Works like .gitignore but for Docker.

    5. Don't Run as Root

    Containers run as root by default—a security risk. Create a non-root user in your Dockerfile and switch to it with USER instruction. If compromised, attacker has limited privileges instead of root access to container.

    6. Use Multi-Stage Builds (Advanced Preview)

    Build your application in one stage (with all build tools), then copy only the compiled artifact to a minimal runtime stage. Result: tiny production images without build dependencies. We'll explore this deeply in Week 3.

    Example: Optimized Python Application Dockerfile

    # Use official Python slim image (smaller than full Python) FROM python:3.11-slim # Set working directory WORKDIR /app # Install system dependencies (rarely change, cache this layer) RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \ gcc \ && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* # Copy requirements first (changes less often than code) COPY requirements.txt . # Install Python dependencies (cached if requirements.txt unchanged) RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt # Copy application code (changes frequently, goes last) COPY . . # Create non-root user and switch to it RUN useradd -m -u 1000 appuser && chown -R appuser:appuser /app USER appuser # Expose port EXPOSE 8000 # Run application CMD ["python", "app.py"]

    Container Resource Management:

    # Limit CPU and memory usage docker run -d \ --name myapp \ --cpus="1.5" \ # Limit to 1.5 CPU cores --memory="512m" \ # Limit to 512MB RAM --memory-swap="1g" \ # Total memory + swap = 1GB myapp:latest # Set CPU priority (relative weight) docker run -d \ --name high-priority-app \ --cpu-shares=1024 \ # Higher = more CPU time when contention myapp:latest # Monitor container resource usage docker stats # Real-time stats for all containers docker stats myapp # Stats for specific container
    ⚡ Performance Tips
    • Always set resource limits in production to prevent runaway containers
    • Use alpine-based images for minimal footprint (python:3.11-alpine vs python:3.11)
    • Clean up package manager caches in same RUN command that installs packages
    • Avoid installing "recommended" packages: apt-get install --no-install-recommends
    • Use specific version tags for reproducible builds
    • Regularly update base images for security patches and performance improvements

    Hands-On Labs

    Now it's time to apply what you've learned! These five labs build progressively from basic container operations to multi-container applications. Complete each lab in order, as later labs build on earlier concepts.

    ⚠️ Lab Prerequisites

    Before starting, ensure Docker Desktop is installed and running. Verify with docker --version and docker ps. You'll need a stable internet connection for pulling images from Docker Hub. Budget 4-6 hours to complete all five labs.

    Lab 1: Your First Docker Container

    Objective: Pull an official nginx image, run it as a container, access the web server, and understand basic container lifecycle commands.

    Learning Goals:

    • Understand image pull and container run workflow
    • Practice port mapping for accessing containerized services
    • Learn basic container management commands
    Search for nginx image on Docker Hub:
    docker search nginx # Identify the official nginx image (should be first result)
    Pull the official nginx image:
    docker pull nginx:latest # This downloads all layers of the nginx image

    Verify: Run docker images to see the downloaded image with its size and ID.

    Run nginx container with port mapping:
    docker run -d --name my-nginx -p 8080:80 nginx:latest # -d: Run in background (detached mode) # --name: Friendly name for the container # -p 8080:80: Map host port 8080 to container port 80
    Verify container is running:
    docker ps # Should show my-nginx container with STATUS "Up"
    Access the web server:

    Open your browser and navigate to http://localhost:8080. You should see the nginx welcome page.

    Alternatively, use curl: curl http://localhost:8080

    Inspect container logs:
    docker logs my-nginx # Shows nginx access logs and error logs
    Execute commands inside the running container:
    docker exec -it my-nginx bash # Opens an interactive bash shell inside the container # Inside the container, explore: ls /usr/share/nginx/html # Default nginx web root cat /etc/nginx/nginx.conf # Nginx configuration exit # Exit the container shell
    Stop and remove the container:
    docker stop my-nginx # Gracefully stop the container docker ps -a # Show all containers (including stopped) docker rm my-nginx # Remove the stopped container docker ps -a # Verify it's gone
    ✅ Lab Success Criteria

    You've completed this lab when: (1) You can access nginx welcome page at localhost:8080, (2) You can view container logs, (3) You can execute commands inside the running container, (4) You successfully stop and remove the container.

    Lab 2: Building Your First Custom Image

    Objective: Create a Dockerfile for a simple Python web application, build a custom Docker image, and run it as a container.

    Learning Goals:

    • Write a production-ready Dockerfile
    • Understand the image build process and layer caching
    • Practice image tagging and versioning
    Create project directory and application code:
    mkdir ~/docker-lab-app && cd ~/docker-lab-app # Create a simple Flask application cat > app.py << 'EOF' from flask import Flask import socket app=Flask(__name__) @app.route('/') def hello(): hostname=socket.gethostname() return f"

    Hello from Docker!

    Container hostname: {hostname}

    " if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000) EOF
    Create requirements.txt:
    cat > requirements.txt << 'EOF' flask==3.0.0 EOF
    Create a Dockerfile:
    cat > Dockerfile << 'EOF' # Use official Python slim image FROM python:3.11-slim # Set working directory WORKDIR /app # Copy requirements and install dependencies COPY requirements.txt . RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt # Copy application code COPY app.py . # Expose port EXPOSE 5000 # Run the application CMD ["python", "app.py" ] EOF
    Build the Docker image:
    docker build -t my-flask-app:v1.0 . # -t: Tag the image with name and version # .: Build context (current directory) # Watch the build process—each step creates a layer
    Verify the image was created:
    docker images | grep my-flask-app # Should show my-flask-app with tag v1.0
    Run a container from your custom image:
    docker run -d --name flask-container -p 5000:5000 my-flask-app:v1.0
    Test the application:
    curl http://localhost:5000 # Should display HTML with "Hello from Docker!" and container hostname

    Or open http://localhost:5000 in your browser.

    Rebuild to demonstrate layer caching:
    # Modify app.py slightly (change the HTML message) sed -i 's/Hello from Docker/Hello from Docker v2/g' app.py # Rebuild the image docker build -t my-flask-app:v1.1 . # Notice: Requirements layer is cached (USING CACHE), only COPY app.py layer rebuilds
    Clean up:
    docker stop flask-container docker rm flask-container docker rmi my-flask-app:v1.0 my-flask-app:v1.1
    ✅ Lab Success Criteria

    You've completed this lab when: (1) You can build a custom image from a Dockerfile, (2) Your containerized Flask app is accessible at localhost:5000, (3) You understand how layer caching speeds up rebuilds, (4) You can create multiple tagged versions of an image.

    Lab 3: Container Networking & Communication

    Objective: Create a custom Docker network, run multiple containers on that network, and demonstrate container-to-container communication using DNS.

    Learning Goals:

    • Create and manage custom Docker networks
    • Understand container DNS resolution
    • Practice multi-container communication patterns
    Create a custom bridge network:
    docker network create my-app-network
    Inspect the network:
    docker network inspect my-app-network # Shows network configuration, subnet, gateway
    Run a nginx container on the custom network:
    docker run -d --name web-server --network my-app-network nginx:latest
    Run a second container (alpine with networking tools) on the same network:
    docker run -it --name client --network my-app-network alpine sh # Inside the alpine container, install curl: apk add --no-cache curl # Test DNS resolution - container name resolves to IP: ping -c 3 web-server # Access nginx using container name as hostname: curl http://web-server # Should return nginx welcome page HTML exit
    Verify network isolation:
    # Run a container on the default bridge network docker run -d --name isolated-nginx nginx:latest # Try to access it from the client on my-app-network docker exec client ping -c 3 isolated-nginx # Should fail - containers on different networks can't communicate
    Connect a container to multiple networks:
    # Connect isolated-nginx to my-app-network docker network connect my-app-network isolated-nginx # Now client can reach it docker exec client curl http://isolated-nginx # Success! Container is now on both networks
    Clean up:
    docker stop web-server client isolated-nginx docker rm web-server client isolated-nginx docker network rm my-app-network
    ✅ Lab Success Criteria

    You've completed this lab when: (1) You can create custom networks, (2) Containers on the same network can communicate using container names as hostnames, (3) You understand network isolation (containers on different networks can't communicate by default), (4) You can connect a container to multiple networks.

    Lab 4: Data Persistence with Volumes

    Objective: Demonstrate container filesystem ephemerality, create named volumes, and persist data across container recreation.

    Learning Goals:

    • Understand why volumes are necessary for stateful applications
    • Create and manage named volumes
    • Practice data persistence patterns
    Demonstrate ephemeral filesystem (no volume):
    # Run a container and write data to its filesystem docker run --name test-ephemeral ubuntu:22.04 \ bash -c "echo 'This data will be lost' > /data.txt && cat /data.txt" # Remove the container docker rm test-ephemeral # Run a new container with same image - data is gone docker run --name test-ephemeral-2 ubuntu:22.04 cat /data.txt # Error: No such file or directory (as expected) docker rm test-ephemeral-2
    Create a named volume:
    docker volume create my-persistent-data
    Run container with volume mounted:
    # Write data to the volume docker run --name test-persistent -v my-persistent-data:/data ubuntu:22.04 \ bash -c "echo 'This data persists!' > /data/persistent.txt && cat /data/persistent.txt"
    Remove container and verify data persists:
    docker rm test-persistent # Run a NEW container with the SAME volume docker run --name test-persistent-2 -v my-persistent-data:/data ubuntu:22.04 \ cat /data/persistent.txt # Success! Data persisted across container recreation
    Practical example - MySQL database with persistent storage:
    # Create volume for MySQL data docker volume create mysql-data # Run MySQL with volume for database files docker run -d \ --name mysql-db \ -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=secret \ -v mysql-data:/var/lib/mysql \ mysql:8.0 # Wait for MySQL to initialize (20-30 seconds) sleep 30 # Create a test database docker exec mysql-db mysql -uroot -psecret \ -e "CREATE DATABASE testdb; USE testdb; CREATE TABLE users (id INT, name VARCHAR(50)); INSERT INTO users VALUES (1, 'Alice');" # Query the data docker exec mysql-db mysql -uroot -psecret \ -e "SELECT * FROM testdb.users;"
    Simulate container failure and recovery:
    # Stop and remove MySQL container docker stop mysql-db docker rm mysql-db # Start a NEW MySQL container with the SAME volume docker run -d \ --name mysql-db-recovered \ -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=secret \ -v mysql-data:/var/lib/mysql \ mysql:8.0 # Wait for startup sleep 20 # Query data - it's still there! docker exec mysql-db-recovered mysql -uroot -psecret \ -e "SELECT * FROM testdb.users;" # Returns: Alice (data survived container recreation)
    Clean up:
    docker stop mysql-db-recovered docker rm mysql-db-recovered test-persistent-2 docker volume rm my-persistent-data mysql-data
    ✅ Lab Success Criteria

    You've completed this lab when: (1) You understand that container filesystems are ephemeral by default, (2) You can create named volumes, (3) Data written to volumes survives container deletion and recreation, (4) You can run a stateful application (MySQL) with persistent storage.

    Lab 5: Multi-Container Application (Web + Database)

    Objective: Deploy a complete application stack with separate containers for web frontend and database backend, connected via custom network and using volumes for data persistence.

    Learning Goals:

    • Integrate networking and volume concepts
    • Understand multi-container application architecture
    • Practice real-world deployment patterns
    Create project infrastructure:
    # Create custom network for the application docker network create app-network # Create volume for database persistence docker volume create postgres-data
    Deploy PostgreSQL database:
    docker run -d \ --name app-database \ --network app-network \ -v postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \ -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=mypassword \ -e POSTGRES_DB=appdb \ postgres:15
    Create a simple web application that connects to the database:
    mkdir ~/multi-container-app && cd ~/multi-container-app cat > app.py << 'EOF' from flask import Flask import psycopg2 import os app=Flask(__name__) def get_db_connection(): return psycopg2.connect( host='app-database' , # Container name as hostname database='appdb' , user='postgres' , password='mypassword' ) @app.route('/') def index(): try: conn=get_db_connection() cur=conn.cursor() cur.execute('SELECT version();') db_version=cur.fetchone()[0] cur.close() conn.close() return f"

    Connected to Database!

    PostgreSQL version: {db_version}

    " except Exception as e: return f"

    Database connection failed

    {str(e)}

    " if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000) EOF cat > requirements.txt << 'EOF' flask==3.0.0 psycopg2-binary==2.9.9 EOF cat> Dockerfile << 'EOF' FROM python:3.11-slim WORKDIR /app COPY requirements.txt . RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt COPY app.py . EXPOSE 5000 CMD ["python", "app.py" ] EOF
    Build and deploy the web application:
    # Build the image docker build -t my-web-app:v1 . # Run the web container on the same network docker run -d \ --name app-web \ --network app-network \ -p 5000:5000 \ my-web-app:v1
    Test the application:
    # Wait a few seconds for containers to be fully ready sleep 10 # Access the web application curl http://localhost:5000 # Should display "Connected to Database!" with PostgreSQL version # Or open http://localhost:5000 in your browser
    Verify data persistence:
    # Create a test table and insert data docker exec app-database psql -U postgres -d appdb \ -c "CREATE TABLE visitors (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, visit_time TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW());" docker exec app-database psql -U postgres -d appdb \ -c "INSERT INTO visitors DEFAULT VALUES;" # Query the data docker exec app-database psql -U postgres -d appdb \ -c "SELECT * FROM visitors;" # Stop and remove database container docker stop app-database docker rm app-database # Start new database container with same volume docker run -d \ --name app-database \ --network app-network \ -v postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \ -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=mypassword \ -e POSTGRES_DB=appdb \ postgres:15 sleep 10 # Data still exists! docker exec app-database psql -U postgres -d appdb \ -c "SELECT * FROM visitors;"
    View logs and monitor the application:
    # View web application logs docker logs app-web # View database logs docker logs app-database # Monitor resource usage docker stats app-web app-database # Press Ctrl+C to exit
    Clean up:
    docker stop app-web app-database docker rm app-web app-database docker rmi my-web-app:v1 docker network rm app-network docker volume rm postgres-data
    ✅ Lab Success Criteria

    You've completed this lab when: (1) Web and database containers run on the same custom network, (2) Web container can connect to database using container name as hostname, (3) Database data persists across container recreation, (4) You can access the web application at localhost:5000 and see successful database connection message.

    🎯 Labs Completed - What's Next?

    Congratulations! You've completed all five Docker fundamentals labs. You now have hands-on experience with containers, images, networking, volumes, and multi-container applications. Next, review the lab solutions and troubleshooting guides below to deepen your understanding. Then complete the practice questions, quiz, test, and week-ending assignment to solidify your knowledge.

    Lab Solutions & Troubleshooting Guide

    This section provides complete solutions for all five labs, expected outputs at each step, and comprehensive troubleshooting guidance for common issues. Use these solutions after attempting labs independently—the learning happens in the struggle!

    💡 How to Use Lab Solutions

    Try each lab step on your own first. If stuck for more than 15-20 minutes, consult the solution. Don't just copy commands—understand why each command works. The troubleshooting section addresses real errors you'll likely encounter.

    Lab 1 Solution: Your First Docker Container

    Expected Outputs

    Step 2 - After pulling nginx:

    latest: Pulling from library/nginx a2abf6c4d29d: Pull complete a9edb18cadd1: Pull complete 589b7251471a: Pull complete Digest: sha256:4c0fdaa8b6341bfdeca5f18f7837462c80cff90527ee35ef185571e1c327beac Status: Downloaded newer image for nginx:latest docker.io/library/nginx:latest

    Step 3 - After running container (docker ps):

    CONTAINER ID IMAGE COMMAND CREATED STATUS PORTS NAMES abc123def456 nginx:latest "/docker-entrypoint.…" 5 seconds ago Up 4 seconds 0.0.0.0:8080->80/tcp my-nginx

    Step 5 - Browser access to localhost:8080:

    You should see the standard nginx welcome page with "Welcome to nginx!" heading and configuration instructions.

    Step 6 - Container logs (docker logs my-nginx):

    /docker-entrypoint.sh: /docker-entrypoint.d/ is not empty, will attempt to perform configuration /docker-entrypoint.sh: Looking for shell scripts in /docker-entrypoint.d/ /docker-entrypoint.sh: Launching /docker-entrypoint.d/10-listen-on-ipv6-by-default.sh 10-listen-on-ipv6-by-default.sh: info: Getting the checksum of /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf /docker-entrypoint.sh: Configuration complete; ready for start up 172.17.0.1 - - [14/Jan/2026:10:30:45 +0000] "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 615 "-" "Mozilla/5.0..."

    Common Issues & Solutions

    ❌ Error: "port is already allocated"

    Symptom: docker: Error response from daemon: driver failed programming external connectivity on endpoint my-nginx: Bind for 0.0.0.0:8080 failed: port is already allocated.

    Cause: Another process (or container) is already using port 8080 on your host.

    Solution: Either stop the conflicting process, or use a different host port: docker run -d --name my-nginx -p 8081:80 nginx:latest (then access at localhost:8081)

    ❌ Error: "name is already in use"

    Symptom: docker: Error response from daemon: Conflict. The container name "/my-nginx" is already in use by container "abc123".

    Cause: A container named "my-nginx" already exists (running or stopped).

    Solution: Remove the existing container first: docker rm -f my-nginx, then run your command again.

    ❌ Problem: Can't access localhost:8080 in browser

    Troubleshooting steps:

    • Verify container is running: docker ps (status should be "Up")
    • Check port mapping is correct: Look for "0.0.0.0:8080->80/tcp" in docker ps output
    • Test with curl first: curl http://localhost:8080 (bypasses browser cache issues)
    • If using WSL2 on Windows: Try accessing from Windows browser at the WSL IP, not localhost
    • Check firewall isn't blocking port 8080
    • Check container logs for errors: docker logs my-nginx

    Best Practices & Insights

    • Always name your containers: --name flag makes containers easier to manage than using random IDs
    • Use -d for services: Detached mode keeps containers running in background
    • Port mapping syntax: -p HOST_PORT:CONTAINER_PORT - always in that order
    • Logs are your friend: docker logs is the first troubleshooting step for any container issue
    • Clean up regularly: Remove stopped containers to avoid name conflicts and save disk space

    Lab 2 Solution: Building Custom Images

    Expected Outputs

    Step 4 - During docker build:

    [+] Building 12.5s (10/10) FINISHED => [internal] load build definition from Dockerfile => [internal] load .dockerignore => [internal] load metadata for docker.io/library/python:3.11-slim => [1/5] FROM docker.io/library/python:3.11-slim => [internal] load build context => [2/5] WORKDIR /app => [3/5] COPY requirements.txt . => [4/5] RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt => [5/5] COPY app.py . => exporting to image => => naming to docker.io/library/my-flask-app:v1.0

    Step 7 - Testing the application (curl localhost:5000):

    <h1>Hello from Docker!</h1><p>Container hostname: a3f8c9d12e45</p>

    Step 8 - Layer caching demonstration:

    [+] Building 2.1s (10/10) FINISHED => [internal] load build definition from Dockerfile => [1/5] FROM docker.io/library/python:3.11-slim => [2/5] WORKDIR /app => [3/5] COPY requirements.txt . => [4/5] RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt <-- CACHED => [5/5] COPY app.py . <-- REBUILT => exporting to image

    Notice: Build time reduced from 12.5s to 2.1s because requirements layer was cached!

    Common Issues & Solutions

    ❌ Error: "no such file or directory" during COPY

    Symptom: COPY failed: file not found in build context

    Cause: The file you're trying to COPY doesn't exist in the build context (current directory).

    Solution: Verify files exist: ls -la. Make sure you're in the correct directory when running docker build. The . at the end of docker build command specifies build context.

    ❌ Error: Flask module not found when running container

    Symptom: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'flask' in container logs

    Cause: Either requirements.txt wasn't copied correctly, or pip install failed during build.

    Solution: Check your Dockerfile has COPY requirements.txt . before the RUN pip install line. Review build output for pip errors. Rebuild with --no-cache: docker build --no-cache -t my-flask-app:v1.0 .

    ❌ Problem: Container exits immediately after starting

    Symptom: docker ps shows no running container, but docker ps -a shows it with "Exited" status

    Cause: Application crashed on startup, likely due to syntax error in app.py or missing dependency.

    Solution: Check container logs: docker logs flask-container. This will show the Python error. Common issues: typos in app.py, incorrect indentation, missing imports. Fix the error, rebuild the image, and try again.

    Best Practices & Insights

    • Order matters in Dockerfile: Put rarely-changing instructions (dependencies) before frequently-changing ones (code)
    • Use specific version tags: python:3.11-slim not python:latest for reproducible builds
    • Slim/alpine images: python:3.11-slim is 50MB vs 300MB for full Python image
    • Clean up in same RUN: Combine install + cleanup: RUN pip install ... && rm -rf ...
    • Layer caching optimization: Changing app.py doesn't invalidate pip install cache, speeding up iteration
    • Build context matters: Everything in current directory is sent to Docker daemon—use .dockerignore to exclude large files

    Lab 3 Solution: Container Networking

    Expected Outputs

    Step 2 - Network inspect:

    [ { "Name": "my-app-network", "Id": "8f3c4d2e1a9b7c6d5e4f3a2b1c0d9e8f7", "Driver": "bridge", "Scope": "local", "IPAM": { "Config": [ { "Subnet": "172.18.0.0/16", "Gateway": "172.18.0.1" } ] }, "Containers": {} } ]

    Step 4 - Inside alpine container (ping web-server):

    PING web-server (172.18.0.2): 56 data bytes 64 bytes from 172.18.0.2: seq=0 ttl=64 time=0.123 ms 64 bytes from 172.18.0.2: seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.089 ms 64 bytes from 172.18.0.2: seq=2 ttl=64 time=0.095 ms

    The container name "web-server" automatically resolves to its IP address via Docker's built-in DNS!

    Step 4 - curl http://web-server from alpine:

    <!DOCTYPE html> <html> <head> <title>Welcome to nginx!</title> ... </html>

    Common Issues & Solutions

    ❌ Error: "bad address 'web-server'"

    Symptom: ping: bad address 'web-server' or curl: Could not resolve host: web-server

    Cause: Containers are on different networks, or web-server container isn't running.

    Solution: Verify both containers are on same network: docker network inspect my-app-network. Check "Containers" section lists both. If not, you likely started containers on wrong network. Remove and recreate with --network my-app-network.

    ❌ Problem: Default bridge network doesn't provide DNS

    Symptom: Containers on default bridge can't resolve each other by name, only by IP.

    Cause: Docker's default bridge network doesn't include automatic DNS resolution.

    Solution: Always create custom bridge networks for multi-container apps: docker network create mynet. Only user-defined bridges provide DNS. This is why we don't use the default bridge in production.

    ❌ Error: "network already exists"

    Symptom: Error response from daemon: network with name my-app-network already exists

    Cause: Network wasn't cleaned up from previous lab attempt.

    Solution: Either use the existing network (continue lab), or delete it first: docker network rm my-app-network. If that fails with "network has active endpoints," stop and remove all containers on that network first.

    Best Practices & Insights

    • User-defined networks required: For DNS resolution, always create custom networks, never use default bridge
    • Network isolation by default: Containers on different networks can't communicate—this is a security feature
    • Container names as hostnames: Use container names in connection strings instead of IPs for flexibility
    • Multiple networks per container: Containers can connect to multiple networks (frontend + backend separation)
    • Network drivers: Bridge (single host), Overlay (multi-host Swarm/K8s), Host (direct host network), None (no networking)
    • Port publishing still needed: -p flag only needed for external access, not container-to-container

    Lab 4 Solution: Data Persistence with Volumes

    Expected Outputs

    Step 1 - Ephemeral filesystem demonstration:

    This data will be lost docker: Error response from daemon: open /data.txt: no such file or directory

    This proves containers lose data when removed—filesystem is ephemeral!

    Step 4 - Data persists across containers:

    This data persists!

    Same data retrieved from new container using same volume—data survived container deletion!

    Step 5 - MySQL data query after container recreation:

    +----+-------+ | id | name | +----+-------+ | 1 | Alice | +----+-------+ 1 row in set (0.00 sec)

    Database data persisted through container destruction and recreation—production-ready persistence!

    Common Issues & Solutions

    ❌ Error: "volume is in use"

    Symptom: Error response from daemon: remove mysql-data: volume is in use

    Cause: A container is still using the volume (even if stopped).

    Solution: Find containers using the volume: docker ps -a --filter volume=mysql-data. Remove those containers first: docker rm container_name, then remove volume.

    ❌ Problem: MySQL won't start after recreating container

    Symptom: Container exits immediately, logs show: [ERROR] [MY-010735] Can't open the mysql.plugin table

    Cause: Volume was corrupted, or MySQL version mismatch between original and recreated container.

    Solution: If testing: delete volume and start fresh. In production: use same MySQL version tag (e.g., mysql:8.0.35 not mysql:8.0). For corrupted volumes, restore from backup or run MySQL repair.

    ❌ Problem: "Cannot connect to MySQL server"

    Symptom: docker exec commands fail with ERROR 2002 (HY000): Can't connect to MySQL server

    Cause: MySQL hasn't finished initializing yet. First startup takes 20-30 seconds.

    Solution: Wait longer! Check logs: docker logs mysql-db. Look for message: [Server] /usr/sbin/mysqld: ready for connections. Only then is MySQL ready for queries. Use sleep 30 or implement proper health checks.

    Best Practices & Insights

    • Always use volumes for stateful apps: Databases, file uploads, logs—anything you need to keep
    • Named volumes over anonymous: -v my-volume:/data not -v /data (latter creates anonymous volume)
    • Volume backup strategy: Volumes are in Docker's data directory—back them up regularly
    • Bind mounts for development: -v $(pwd):/app lets you edit code without rebuilding image
    • Never run databases without volumes: Without volumes, database data is lost on container restart
    • Volume drivers: Local (default), NFS, cloud storage (AWS EFS, Azure Files) for distributed systems

    Lab 5 Solution: Multi-Container Application

    Expected Outputs

    Step 5 - Web application response (curl localhost:5000):

    <h1>Connected to Database!</h1><p>PostgreSQL version: PostgreSQL 15.5 (Debian 15.5-1.pgdg120+1) on x86_64-pc-linux-gnu, compiled by gcc (Debian 12.2.0-14) 12.2.0, 64-bit</p>

    Step 6 - After creating table and inserting data:

    id | visit_time ----+---------------------------- 1 | 2026-01-14 10:45:23.123456 (1 row)

    Step 6 - After container recreation, data still exists:

    id | visit_time ----+---------------------------- 1 | 2026-01-14 10:45:23.123456 (1 row)

    Perfect! This demonstrates a production-ready multi-container application with persistent data.

    Common Issues & Solutions

    ❌ Error: "Database connection failed" in web app

    Symptom: Web page shows "Database connection failed" with psycopg2 error

    Possible causes and solutions:

    • Wrong network: Verify both containers on same network: docker network inspect app-network
    • Postgres not ready: Wait 10 seconds after starting postgres before starting web app
    • Wrong hostname: Ensure app.py uses host='app-database' (container name), not 'localhost' or IP
    • Wrong credentials: Password in app.py must match POSTGRES_PASSWORD environment variable
    • Database doesn't exist: Ensure you set -e POSTGRES_DB=appdb when starting postgres
    ❌ Error: psycopg2 build failed during docker build

    Symptom: error: command 'gcc' failed when building Python image

    Cause: psycopg2 (not the -binary version) requires build tools to compile from source.

    Solution: Use psycopg2-binary in requirements.txt, not psycopg2. Binary version includes pre-compiled libraries, no gcc needed. For production, install build dependencies or use psycopg2-binary.

    ❌ Problem: Port 5000 already in use

    Symptom: Error starting userland proxy: listen tcp4 0.0.0.0:5000: bind: address already in use

    Cause: Another service (possibly previous lab) is using port 5000.

    Solution: Find and stop the conflicting process: docker ps | grep 5000. Remove old containers: docker rm -f $(docker ps -aq). Or use a different port: -p 5001:5000 and access at localhost:5001.

    Architecture Insights

    🏗️ Multi-Container Application Patterns

    This lab demonstrates fundamental microservices architecture patterns:

    • Separation of concerns: Web tier (stateless) vs data tier (stateful) in separate containers
    • Service discovery via DNS: Web app connects to database using container name, not hardcoded IP
    • Network isolation: Application network isolates services from external access (only web port published)
    • Data persistence layer: Volume ensures database survives container failures/updates
    • Horizontal scalability: Could run multiple web containers (load balanced) sharing one database
    • Rolling updates: Can update web container independently without touching database

    Best Practices & Insights

    • Infrastructure first: Always create network and volumes before starting containers
    • Start order matters: Database must be ready before application starts (implement health checks in production)
    • Don't expose database port: Only web container needs external access (-p flag), database stays internal
    • Use environment variables: For production, use -e or env files, never hardcode passwords in code
    • Container restart policies: In production, add --restart=unless-stopped for automatic recovery
    • This pattern scales: Same architecture works for complex microservices (just more containers on same network)
    • Next level: Docker Compose: This multi-container setup is why Docker Compose exists (Week 3 topic!)
    🎓 Lab Solutions Summary

    You've now seen complete solutions for all five Docker fundamentals labs. Key takeaways:

    • Containers are disposable - Always design for failure and recreation
    • Networks enable service discovery - User-defined bridges provide DNS resolution
    • Volumes provide persistence - Never run stateful apps without volumes
    • Multi-container patterns - Real apps need multiple specialized containers working together
    • Troubleshooting is systematic - Check logs, verify networking, validate volumes, inspect configs

    Next, test your understanding with the practice questions, quiz, and comprehensive week-ending assignment!

    Practice Questions

    These 10 practice questions reinforce key Docker concepts from this week. Work through each question independently, then review the detailed answers to identify any knowledge gaps. These questions progress from basic recall to applied problem-solving.

    Explain the fundamental difference between containers and virtual machines. Why are containers considered more lightweight?
    Answer: Virtual machines virtualize hardware and run a complete guest operating system on top of a hypervisor. Each VM includes the full OS, consuming gigabytes of disk space and significant memory/CPU overhead. Containers, in contrast, virtualize the operating system and share the host kernel. They package only the application and its dependencies, not a full OS. This makes containers typically 10-100MB vs. several GB for VMs, start in seconds vs. minutes, and use far less memory. Containers achieve isolation through Linux namespaces and cgroups, not hardware virtualization. The trade-off: VMs provide stronger isolation (each has its own kernel), while containers offer better density and faster startup.
    What is a Docker image layer, and why does Docker use a layered filesystem? Describe how layer caching improves build performance.
    Answer: A Docker image layer is a read-only filesystem change created by each instruction in a Dockerfile. For example, `FROM ubuntu:20.04` creates a base layer, `RUN apt-get update` creates another layer with the package cache, and `COPY app.py /app/` creates a layer with your application file. Layers are stacked using a union filesystem to appear as a single filesystem to the container. Docker uses layers for three key reasons: (1) Storage efficiency - common layers (like base images) are shared across images, stored only once. (2) Build caching - if a layer hasn't changed, Docker reuses the cached layer instead of rebuilding, dramatically speeding up builds. (3) Distribution efficiency - when pulling images, Docker downloads only layers you don't already have. Layer caching improves build performance by skipping unchanged layers; if you rebuild an image and only the final COPY command changed, all previous layers (FROM, RUN apt-get, RUN pip install) are reused from cache, reducing a 5-minute build to 2 seconds.
    A developer complains: "My container works fine on my laptop but crashes immediately in production." List four possible causes and how you would investigate each.
    Answer: (1) Environment variables missing - Production may lack required env vars (API keys, database URLs). Investigate with `docker logs` to see error messages about missing config. Fix by ensuring all env vars are passed via `docker run -e` or environment files. (2) Port conflicts - Production may have services already using required ports. Check logs for "address already in use" errors, verify with `netstat -tulpn` on host, change ports if needed. (3) Resource constraints - Production containers may have memory/CPU limits that laptop doesn't. Check with `docker stats` and `docker inspect` for resource limits. Container may be OOMKilled. Increase limits or optimize application. (4) Dependencies on host filesystem - Developer may be using bind mounts to host files that don't exist in production. Check Dockerfile and run command for `-v` mounts. Fix by copying required files into image with `COPY` instruction rather than mounting. Investigation strategy: Always start with `docker logs ` to see application errors, then `docker inspect ` to compare configurations between environments.
    Why should you never use the `:latest` tag in production? What tagging strategy would you recommend instead?
    Answer: The `:latest` tag is dangerous in production because it's mutable and unpredictable. When you pull `nginx:latest` today, you get one version; tomorrow you get a different version with potentially breaking changes. This violates infrastructure reproducibility - you can't reliably recreate an environment. It also makes rollbacks impossible (which "latest" version should you roll back to?) and debugging difficult (which version caused the issue?). Recommended tagging strategy: (1) Semantic versioning - Use specific versions like `nginx:1.21.6` or your own images as `myapp:2.3.1`. This ensures consistency. (2) Git commit SHA - Tag images with the commit they were built from: `myapp:a7f39c2`. This provides exact traceability. (3) Combined approach - Use both: `myapp:2.3.1-a7f39c2`. This gives human-readable versions plus exact commit reference. (4) Environment-specific tags - Maintain tags like `myapp:production` that you update deliberately after testing, never automatically. Always specify exact versions in production manifests/compose files. Save `:latest` for local development only.
    Explain the difference between named volumes and bind mounts. When would you use each?
    Answer: Named volumes are Docker-managed storage created with `docker volume create mydata` or automatically via `docker run -v mydata:/data`. Docker stores them in a dedicated location (/var/lib/docker/volumes on Linux) and manages their lifecycle. Benefits: portable across hosts, can be backed up as a unit, work on any platform including Windows/Mac, Docker manages permissions. Bind mounts map a specific host directory into the container: `docker run -v /home/user/project:/app`. The container sees the exact host directory; changes are immediately reflected on the host. When to use each: (1) Named volumes for production data - Use for databases, application state, any data that should outlive containers. They're safer (Docker manages them), more portable, and easier to backup. Example: PostgreSQL data directory. (2) Bind mounts for development - Use when you want live code reloading; edit files on host and see changes instantly in container without rebuilding. Example: mounting your source code directory. (3) Bind mounts for configuration - Use when injecting specific host files/configs into containers. Example: mounting SSL certificates or application config files. Rule of thumb: Production = named volumes, Development = bind mounts.
    What is the purpose of user-defined bridge networks? Why can't containers on the default bridge network communicate by container name?
    Answer: User-defined bridge networks enable automatic DNS-based service discovery between containers. When you create a network with `docker network create mynetwork` and attach containers to it, Docker runs an embedded DNS server (at 127.0.0.11) that resolves container names to IP addresses. This means container "web" can ping container "database" by name, without knowing its IP. The default bridge network (what containers join when you don't specify `--network`) deliberately lacks this DNS functionality for backward compatibility reasons. On the default bridge, containers can only communicate by IP address, which is impractical since IPs change with each restart. Why user-defined networks matter: (1) Service discovery - Use container names as hostnames in connection strings: `postgres://database:5432` instead of hardcoded IPs. (2) Network isolation - Containers on different networks can't communicate, providing security boundaries. (3) Better control - You can attach/detach containers from networks without restarting them. (4) Link replacement - Modern alternative to deprecated `--link` flag. Best practice: Never use the default bridge for multi-container applications; always create user-defined networks. Default bridge is only acceptable for single standalone containers.
    You're optimizing a Python application Dockerfile that's currently slow to build. The Dockerfile installs system packages, Python dependencies, and copies application code. Propose a layer ordering strategy to maximize cache effectiveness.
    Answer: Order Dockerfile instructions from least frequently changed to most frequently changed to maximize cache hits. Optimal layer order: (1) `FROM python:3.11-slim` - Base image, rarely changes. (2) `RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y ...` - System dependencies, change occasionally. (3) `COPY requirements.txt /app/requirements.txt` - Copy only requirements file first. (4) `RUN pip install -r requirements.txt` - Install Python dependencies. This is the expensive operation (can take minutes) but only rebuilds when requirements.txt changes, not on every code change. (5) `COPY . /app` - Copy application code last, as it changes most frequently. Why this works: Docker caches each layer. When you modify application code, only the final COPY layer invalidates; all previous layers (base image, system packages, Python dependencies) are reused from cache. Without this optimization, copying code early would invalidate the pip install cache on every code change, forcing a full dependency reinstall each build (5 minutes) instead of a quick copy (2 seconds). Additional optimizations: Use `.dockerignore` to exclude files not needed in the image (tests, docs, .git) - smaller context means faster COPY. Use multi-stage builds to separate build dependencies from runtime. Pin dependency versions in requirements.txt to avoid cache busting from version changes.
    Describe the container lifecycle states (Created, Running, Paused, Stopped, Removed). What happens to container data in each state transition?
    Answer: (1) Created - Container exists but hasn't started. Created with `docker create` or `docker run` before starting. The container filesystem is initialized (writable layer created on top of image layers), but no processes are running. Data: Filesystem exists but is empty of runtime data. (2) Running - Container processes are executing. Entered with `docker start` or `docker run`. Main process (PID 1) is running; container consumes CPU/memory. Data: Application can read/write to the container filesystem and mounted volumes. (3) Paused - Container processes are frozen using cgroups freezer. Entered with `docker pause`. Processes stop executing but remain in memory. Used for resource management or suspending workloads temporarily. Data: Filesystem and memory state preserved exactly; no data loss. (4) Stopped - Container processes have exited. Entered with `docker stop` (SIGTERM then SIGKILL) or when main process exits. Processes are terminated, but filesystem still exists. Data: Critical distinction - Container filesystem persists (data in non-volume directories is preserved), but memory state is lost. Volumes remain unchanged. (5) Removed - Container is deleted entirely. Entered with `docker rm`. Container filesystem is deleted. Data: Filesystem is destroyed - any data written to the container filesystem (not volumes) is permanently lost. Volumes persist unless explicitly removed with `-v` flag. Key insight: Only "Removed" destroys data. Stopped containers retain their filesystem, which is why you can inspect logs and files from stopped containers. This is why volumes are critical for persistent data - they survive container removal.
    A container needs to persist data across restarts and be accessible from the host for backup purposes. Compare the approaches: (1) named volume, (2) bind mount, (3) writing to container filesystem. Which is most appropriate and why?
    Answer: Best choice: Named volume (with host access via `docker cp` or volume backup strategies). Here's why each approach works or fails: (1) Named volume ✅ - Persists data across container deletion/recreation (survives restarts and even `docker rm`). Managed by Docker with proper permissions. Host can access via `docker volume inspect myvolume` to find mount point (though direct host access is platform-dependent). Proper backup strategy: Use `docker run --rm -v myvolume:/data -v $(pwd):/backup ubuntu tar czf /backup/backup.tar.gz /data` to create backups. This is production-appropriate, portable, and safe. (2) Bind mount ⚠️ - Persists data (it's on the host filesystem) and is easily accessible from host, which seems ideal. However, it has drawbacks: Breaks portability (path must exist on every host), permission issues (container user may not match host user), and ties container to specific host filesystem structure. Acceptable for development, risky for production. (3) Container filesystem ❌ - Data is lost when container is removed (`docker rm`). Survives restarts (`docker stop/start`) but not recreation. Not accessible from host without `docker cp`. This violates the stateless container principle. Never rely on container filesystem for persistent data. Verdict: Named volume is most appropriate. It persists data properly, works across platforms, follows Docker best practices, and supports backup strategies. If host accessibility is critical, bind mount is acceptable but document the path requirements. Never use container filesystem for data that must persist.
    You need to deploy a web application with a PostgreSQL database. Design the Docker architecture: What networks do you need? What volumes? How should containers communicate? Describe the complete setup.
    Answer: Complete Architecture: Networks: Create a user-defined bridge network `docker network create app-network`. Both containers join this network for DNS-based communication. Do NOT use default bridge. Optionally, create a separate `backend-network` if you have multiple services (web connects to both networks, database only to backend) for network isolation. Volumes: Create a named volume for PostgreSQL data: `docker volume create postgres-data`. Mount to PostgreSQL container at `/var/lib/postgresql/data`. This ensures database data survives container restarts/removal. Do NOT rely on container filesystem or bind mounts for database storage in production. Container Setup: (1) Database container: `docker run -d --name database --network app-network -v postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=securepass postgres:15`. Note: NOT exposing ports to host (no `-p 5432:5432`) because web container accesses it via internal network. This is more secure. (2) Web container: `docker run -d --name web --network app-network -p 80:5000 -e DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:securepass@database:5432/mydb myapp:latest`. Note DATABASE_URL uses container name `database` as hostname (DNS resolution). Port 80 exposed to host for external access. Communication: Web connects to database using hostname `database` (container name) on port 5432. DNS resolves this to database container's IP automatically. No hardcoded IPs needed. Best Practices Applied: (1) Named volume for persistence, (2) User-defined network for DNS, (3) Environment variables for configuration, (4) Database not exposed to host (security), (5) Web app exposed for user access, (6) Container names used as hostnames (maintainability). Production Enhancement: Use Docker Compose to define this architecture as code, add health checks, implement restart policies, use secrets for passwords instead of environment variables, add resource limits.

    Weekly Quiz: Docker Fundamentals

    This quiz contains 10 auto-gradable multiple-choice questions. Answer each question and check your responses against the answer key. In Google Classroom, these questions can be configured for automatic grading. Aim for 80%+ to demonstrate solid understanding.

    Question 1: Container Definition

    What is a Docker container?

    A. A virtual machine with a complete operating system
    B. A running instance of a Docker image with its own isolated filesystem and processes
    C. A compressed archive of application files
    D. A server dedicated to running Docker workloads

    ✓ Correct Answer: B

    Question 2: Dockerfile Instructions

    Which Dockerfile instruction defines the base image to build from?

    A. BASE
    B. FROM
    C. IMAGE
    D. PULL

    ✓ Correct Answer: B

    Question 3: Port Mapping

    What does the command `docker run -p 8080:80 nginx` accomplish?

    A. Maps container port 8080 to host port 80
    B. Maps host port 8080 to container port 80
    C. Creates a network bridge between ports 8080 and 80
    D. Configures nginx to listen on port 8080 instead of 80

    ✓ Correct Answer: B (Format: -p HOST:CONTAINER)

    Question 4: Layer Caching

    Why should you copy requirements.txt separately before copying all application code in a Dockerfile?

    A. To reduce image size by excluding unnecessary files
    B. To comply with Docker security policies
    C. To leverage layer caching and avoid reinstalling dependencies when only code changes
    D. Requirements files must always be copied before other files

    ✓ Correct Answer: C

    Question 5: Volume Persistence

    What happens to data in a named volume when you remove the container with `docker rm`?

    A. Data is deleted along with the container
    B. Data persists and can be attached to a new container
    C. Data is archived and must be restored manually
    D. Data is moved to the host filesystem

    ✓ Correct Answer: B (Volumes survive container deletion)

    Question 6: Network Communication

    On a user-defined bridge network, how can one container communicate with another?

    A. Only by IP address
    B. By container name using automatic DNS resolution
    C. By creating explicit links between containers
    D. Containers on the same network cannot communicate directly

    ✓ Correct Answer: B

    Question 7: Image Tagging

    Why is using the `:latest` tag discouraged in production environments?

    A. The latest tag is slower to download
    B. Latest tag images are not properly tested
    C. The latest tag is mutable and can point to different image versions over time
    D. Latest tag is only for development environments by Docker policy

    ✓ Correct Answer: C (Unpredictable and not reproducible)

    Question 8: Container Filesystem

    Where should you store database data for a containerized PostgreSQL database?

    A. In the container's writable layer (/var/lib/postgresql/data without a volume)
    B. In a bind mount to the host's home directory
    C. In a named Docker volume mounted to the container's data directory
    D. Database data should not be stored in containers

    ✓ Correct Answer: C

    Question 9: Docker Registry

    What is Docker Hub?

    A. A tool for managing Docker containers locally
    B. A public registry for storing and sharing Docker images
    C. A graphical interface for Docker Desktop
    D. A container orchestration platform

    ✓ Correct Answer: B

    Question 10: Default Bridge Network Limitation

    What is the primary limitation of the default bridge network that user-defined networks solve?

    A. Default bridge is slower than user-defined networks
    B. Default bridge has a maximum of 10 containers
    C. Default bridge does not provide automatic DNS resolution between containers
    D. Default bridge cannot be used with volumes

    ✓ Correct Answer: C (Must use IP addresses on default bridge)

    📊 Quiz Scoring Guide

    10/10 (100%): Excellent mastery of Docker fundamentals
    8-9/10 (80-90%): Strong understanding with minor gaps
    6-7/10 (60-70%): Basic competency, review weak areas before Week 3
    Below 6/10: Significant gaps - schedule office hours and review teaching notes

    Weekly Test: Applied Docker Scenarios

    This test contains 5 scenario-based questions that require applied problem-solving and analysis. These questions assess your ability to use Docker concepts in realistic situations. Provide detailed answers demonstrating your understanding. Use the rubric to guide your responses.

    Scenario 1: Troubleshooting Container Networking

    You have deployed a Flask web application container and a PostgreSQL database container on the same Docker host. The web application cannot connect to the database, showing error: psycopg2.OperationalError: could not translate host name "database" to address. Both containers are running (verified with docker ps). Diagnose the problem and propose a solution with specific commands.

    📝 Evaluation Rubric (20 points)
    • Correct diagnosis (8 pts): Identifies that containers are likely on the default bridge network which lacks DNS resolution
    • Solution approach (6 pts): Proposes creating user-defined network and connecting both containers to it
    • Specific commands (4 pts): Provides exact docker network commands with proper syntax
    • Explanation quality (2 pts): Explains why user-defined networks provide DNS resolution

    Scenario 2: Dockerfile Optimization

    A development team complains that their Node.js application takes 8 minutes to build every time they change a single line of code. Here's their current Dockerfile:

    FROM node:18 COPY . /app WORKDIR /app RUN npm install EXPOSE 3000 CMD ["node", "server.js"]

    Rewrite this Dockerfile to optimize build times using layer caching. Explain each change and why it improves performance.

    📝 Evaluation Rubric (20 points)
    • Correct layer ordering (8 pts): Copies package.json before copying all code, runs npm install on dependency file only
    • Optimization explanation (6 pts): Explains how layer caching works and why ordering matters
    • Additional improvements (4 pts): Mentions .dockerignore, multi-stage builds, or slim base images
    • Syntax correctness (2 pts): Dockerfile is valid and would build successfully

    Scenario 3: Data Persistence Strategy

    Your company is deploying a containerized WordPress application with MySQL database. The requirements are: (1) Database data must survive container restarts and updates, (2) WordPress uploads directory must persist, (3) The solution must work across different server environments, (4) Backups should be possible. Design the complete volume strategy with justification for each choice.

    📝 Evaluation Rubric (20 points)
    • MySQL volume strategy (6 pts): Recommends named volume for /var/lib/mysql with justification
    • WordPress uploads strategy (6 pts): Recommends named volume for wp-content/uploads
    • Backup approach (4 pts): Describes backup strategy using docker run with volume mounts or backup containers
    • Portability consideration (4 pts): Explains why named volumes work across environments vs. bind mounts

    Scenario 4: Production Deployment Problem

    A container runs perfectly in development but immediately crashes in production with exit code 137. Logs show the application started successfully but stopped abruptly with "Killed" message. The application is a Python data processing script that loads a large dataset into memory. What is likely causing this issue, how would you confirm your diagnosis, and what are three possible solutions?

    📝 Evaluation Rubric (20 points)
    • Correct diagnosis (6 pts): Identifies exit code 137 as OOMKilled (out of memory)
    • Diagnostic steps (6 pts): Describes using docker stats, docker inspect for memory limits, checking host memory
    • Solutions (6 pts): Proposes at least 3 valid solutions (increase memory limit, optimize application, add swap, chunk processing)
    • Prevention strategy (2 pts): Mentions testing with production-like resource constraints

    Scenario 5: Multi-Container Application Architecture

    Design a complete Docker architecture for an e-commerce application with these components: (1) React frontend served by Nginx, (2) Node.js REST API, (3) Python background job processor, (4) PostgreSQL database, (5) Redis cache. Specify: networks needed, which containers connect to which networks, volume requirements, port exposures, and how containers discover each other. Draw or describe the architecture clearly.

    📝 Evaluation Rubric (20 points)
    • Network design (6 pts): Creates appropriate networks (frontend-backend, backend-database) with proper segmentation
    • Service communication (5 pts): Correctly identifies which containers need to communicate and how (DNS names)
    • Volume strategy (4 pts): Identifies data persistence needs (PostgreSQL, Redis optionally)
    • Port exposure (3 pts): Correctly exposes only Nginx to host, keeps backend services internal
    • Security considerations (2 pts): Mentions network isolation, minimal port exposure
    📋 Test Submission Guidelines

    Format: Submit as a PDF or Google Doc with each scenario answered separately. Include command examples where applicable. Aim for 200-400 words per scenario.
    Resources: Open book - you may reference Docker documentation, but answers must be in your own words.
    Time: Allow 90-120 minutes for thoughtful responses.
    Grading: Total 100 points (20 per scenario). Passing grade: 70/100.

    Week-Ending Assignment: Deploy a Production-Ready Containerized Application

    🎯 Assignment Objective

    Integrate all Week 2 concepts by building and deploying a complete multi-container application with production-quality Dockerfiles, networking, persistence, and documentation. This assignment simulates real-world DevOps work and becomes a portfolio piece.

    Assignment Requirements

    You will build a Task Management API with the following architecture:

    Application Components

    1. REST API (Flask/Python or Express/Node.js): Provides CRUD endpoints for tasks (create, read, update, delete). Must connect to PostgreSQL database and Redis cache.
    2. PostgreSQL Database: Stores task data persistently (id, title, description, completed status, created_at timestamp).
    3. Redis Cache: Caches frequently accessed tasks to reduce database load (implement GET /tasks endpoint with caching).
    4. Web Interface (Bonus - Optional): Simple HTML/JavaScript frontend that calls the API.

    Technical Requirements

    • ✅ Write production-quality Dockerfiles for each service with multi-layer caching optimization
    • ✅ Create user-defined bridge network for service communication
    • ✅ Implement named volumes for PostgreSQL data persistence
    • ✅ Use environment variables for configuration (database URL, Redis host)
    • ✅ Tag images with semantic versioning (e.g., taskapi:1.0.0)
    • ✅ Push images to Docker Hub (create free account)
    • ✅ API must expose only necessary ports; database and Redis should not be accessible from host
    • ✅ Include health check endpoints in API (GET /health returns {"status": "healthy"})

    Deliverables

    1. GitHub Repository: Create public repo named "docker-task-api" with all code
    2. Dockerfiles: One Dockerfile for the API service (in root or api/ directory)
    3. docker-compose.yml (Preview): Define all services, networks, volumes (we'll cover Compose in Week 3, but use documentation to create basic version)
    4. Architecture Diagram: Draw architecture showing containers, networks, volumes, port mappings (use draw.io, Lucidchart, or hand-drawn and photographed)
    5. README.md: Must include:
      • Project overview and architecture description
      • Prerequisites (Docker, Docker Compose)
      • Step-by-step deployment instructions
      • API endpoint documentation (list all endpoints with examples)
      • Testing instructions (how to verify it works)
      • Troubleshooting section (common issues you encountered and solutions)
      • Docker Hub links to your images
    6. Deployment Documentation: Separate DEPLOYMENT.md file with:
      • All docker commands used (network create, volume create, docker build, docker run)
      • Explanation of why each command is necessary
      • Screenshots of running containers (docker ps output)
      • Screenshot of successful API test (curl or Postman)
    ⚠️ Common Pitfalls to Avoid
    • Don't use default bridge network - create user-defined network
    • Don't store database data in container filesystem - use named volumes
    • Don't expose PostgreSQL/Redis ports to host in production architecture
    • Don't copy entire project directory early in Dockerfile - optimize layer caching
    • Don't hardcode IPs - use container names for service discovery
    • Don't commit secrets to Git - use environment variables
    💡 Bonus Challenges (Extra Credit)
    • +5 points: Implement multi-stage Dockerfile to reduce final image size
    • +5 points: Add Nginx reverse proxy container in front of API
    • +5 points: Implement health checks in docker-compose.yml with proper restart policies
    • +5 points: Add automated tests that run in container during build
    • +5 points: Create simple web UI (HTML/JS) that calls the API

    Grading Rubric

    Category Points Criteria
    Dockerfiles Quality 25 Excellent (23-25): Multi-layer caching optimized, uses slim base images, proper instruction ordering, includes .dockerignore, runs as non-root user
    Good (20-22): Layer caching implemented, proper COPY ordering, valid syntax
    Fair (15-19): Dockerfile builds successfully but suboptimal (copies all code early, no caching optimization)
    Poor (<15): Dockerfile doesn't build or uses very poor practices
    Architecture & Networking 25 Excellent (23-25): User-defined network, proper service isolation, only API exposed to host, containers communicate by name, architecture diagram clear and accurate
    Good (20-22): User-defined network used, containers communicate correctly, minor architecture issues
    Fair (15-19): Default bridge network used OR all services exposed to host
    Poor (<15): Networking doesn't work or hardcoded IPs used
    Data Persistence 15 Excellent (14-15): Named volumes for PostgreSQL, data survives container deletion, proper volume mount points
    Good (12-13): Volumes used but minor issues (anonymous volumes, suboptimal mount points)
    Fair (9-11): Bind mounts used instead of volumes
    Poor (<9): No persistence; data lost on container restart
    Functionality 20 Excellent (18-20): All CRUD endpoints work, caching implemented, health check present, thorough testing demonstrated
    Good (15-17): All endpoints work, minor bugs or caching not fully implemented
    Fair (10-14): Basic functionality works but significant features missing
    Poor (<10): Application doesn't work or major features broken
    Documentation 15 Excellent (14-15): README comprehensive with all sections, clear instructions, architecture diagram professional, DEPLOYMENT.md detailed with explanations
    Good (12-13): README covers essentials, deployment instructions clear, diagram present
    Fair (9-11): Basic documentation but missing key sections or unclear instructions
    Poor (<9): Minimal or confusing documentation
    Best Practices 10 Environment variables used, images tagged properly, pushed to Docker Hub, no secrets in code, proper .gitignore, code organized well, troubleshooting section shows learning from errors
    Bonus Challenges +25 Up to 25 extra credit points for implementing bonus features (multi-stage builds, Nginx proxy, health checks, tests, web UI)
    📤 Submission Instructions

    Due Date: End of Week 2 (before Week 3 class begins)
    Submission Method: Google Classroom

    Submit:
    1. GitHub repository URL (ensure it's public or add instructor as collaborator)
    2. Docker Hub image URLs (e.g., yourusername/taskapi:1.0.0)
    3. Brief video (2-3 minutes, optional but recommended) demonstrating your application running and showing key features

    🎓 Learning Outcomes

    This assignment integrates all Week 2 concepts: Dockerfile optimization, multi-container orchestration, networking, data persistence, image management, and production best practices. Successfully completing this project demonstrates you can design and deploy containerized applications—a core DevOps skill. The documentation you create showcases your ability to communicate technical work, equally important in professional settings. This is portfolio-worthy work. After completion, you'll have a project you can show in job interviews and discuss in technical conversations.

    🎯 Week 2 Complete - What's Next?

    Congratulations on completing Docker Fundamentals! You now understand container architecture, image building, networking, persistence, and multi-container applications. In Week 3: Advanced Docker, we'll explore Docker Compose for declarative multi-service orchestration, multi-stage builds for production optimization, container security scanning, advanced networking with custom drivers, and production deployment patterns. Your Week 2 assignment will serve as the foundation—we'll enhance it with Compose in Week 3. Take time to review this week's concepts; they're the building blocks for everything ahead.

    Mentorship Review for Week 2

    Mentorship students: your Week 2 work (Docker Fundamentals & Containerization) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    3

    Advanced Docker & Multi-Container Applications

    Docker Compose, Multi-Stage Builds, Security, and Production Deployment Patterns

    Building on Week 2's Docker fundamentals, this week elevates your containerization skills to production-grade expertise. You'll master Docker Compose for declarative multi-service orchestration, implement multi-stage builds that dramatically reduce image sizes, secure containers with vulnerability scanning and best practices, configure advanced networking patterns, and deploy production-ready architectures. By week's end, you'll orchestrate complex applications with the same tools used by Fortune 500 companies, transforming the manual docker run commands from Week 2 into elegant, version-controlled infrastructure definitions.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Advanced Docker & Multi-Container Applications: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate advanced Docker competencies across all Bloom's Taxonomy levels:

    Remember

    Define Docker Compose, multi-stage builds, and container security concepts. List Docker Compose commands and YAML structure.

    Understand

    Explain how Docker Compose simplifies multi-container orchestration. Describe multi-stage build optimization strategies.

    Apply

    Write docker-compose.yml files for multi-service applications. Implement multi-stage Dockerfiles to reduce image sizes by 70%+.

    Analyze

    Compare Docker Compose vs manual container orchestration. Assess container vulnerabilities using security scanning tools.

    Evaluate

    Critique docker-compose.yml configurations for production readiness. Recommend security hardening strategies for containerized applications.

    Create

    Design complete multi-tier application stacks with Docker Compose. Architect production deployment strategies with health checks and rollback capabilities.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Complete these exercises before studying the main content. They prepare you for advanced Docker concepts and activate prior knowledge from Week 2. Budget 2-3 hours for this work.

    1Week 2 Multi-Container Reflection

    Review your Week 2 assignment (Task Management API with PostgreSQL + Redis). Write 200-300 words reflecting on:

    • How many docker commands did you need to remember?
    • What happens if you restart your computer? Do you need to recreate networks and volumes manually?
    • If you wanted to share your project with a teammate, how would they replicate your exact setup?
    • What pain points did you experience with manual container orchestration?
    💡 Reflection Goal

    This reflection helps you understand why Docker Compose exists. The friction you experienced is exactly what Compose solves.

    2Docker Compose Quick Start

    Visit docs.docker.com/compose and read the "Overview" and "Get Started" pages (spend 20-30 minutes). Don't code yet—just familiarize yourself with:

    • What problem does Docker Compose solve?
    • What is a docker-compose.yml file?
    • What are the basic commands: docker-compose up, docker-compose down?
    • How does Compose handle networks and volumes?

    Take notes on any confusing terminology—we'll clarify in teaching notes.

    3Multi-Stage Build Research

    Search for "Docker multi-stage builds" and study 2-3 blog posts or tutorials. Look for examples showing before/after image sizes. Answer:

    • What is a multi-stage build?
    • Why do images become smaller with multi-stage builds?
    • What kinds of files/dependencies get excluded from final images?
    • When would you not use multi-stage builds?
    ⚠️ Don't Worry About Syntax Yet

    Focus on understanding the concept. We'll master the syntax together in labs.

    4Container Security Awareness

    Read about container security vulnerabilities. Search for "Docker security best practices" and identify at least 5 security concerns when running containers in production. Consider:

    • Running containers as root user
    • Using images from untrusted sources
    • Exposing unnecessary ports
    • Outdated base images with known CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)
    • Secrets in environment variables or image layers

    Write a brief summary (150-200 words) of what you learned about container security risks.

    5Production Deployment Patterns

    Research how companies deploy Docker containers to production. Look for information about:

    • Health checks: How do systems know if a container is healthy?
    • Restart policies: What happens when a container crashes?
    • Resource limits: How do you prevent one container from consuming all host resources?
    • Logging strategies: Where do container logs go in production?

    Don't try to implement anything yet—just build awareness of production concerns beyond basic docker run.

    📚 Recommended Reading

    Optional but valuable: Browse the official Docker Compose documentation (docs.docker.com/compose/compose-file) to see the full range of configuration options. Don't memorize—just get a sense of what's possible. We'll reference this extensively during labs.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    3.1 Docker Compose Introduction: Simplifying Multi-Container Orchestration

    In Week 2's assignment, you experienced the pain of manually orchestrating multiple containers. Remember writing long docker run commands with dozens of flags? Creating networks and volumes separately? Managing container startup order? Docker Compose eliminates all of that complexity.

    🎯 What is Docker Compose?

    Docker Compose is a tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications using a simple YAML configuration file. Instead of typing multiple docker commands, you declare your entire application stack in docker-compose.yml and bring it up with a single command: docker-compose up.

    The Problem Compose Solves:

    Think back to your Week 2 Task Management API assignment. To run it, you had to:

    # Step 1: Create a custom network docker network create task-api-network # Step 2: Create volumes docker volume create postgres-data docker volume create redis-data # Step 3: Start PostgreSQL docker run -d \ --name task-db \ --network task-api-network \ -e POSTGRES_USER=taskuser \ -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=taskpass \ -e POSTGRES_DB=taskdb \ -v postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \ -p 5432:5432 \ postgres:15-alpine # Step 4: Wait for database to be ready... # Step 5: Start Redis docker run -d \ --name task-cache \ --network task-api-network \ -v redis-data:/data \ -p 6379:6379 \ redis:7-alpine # Step 6: Start the API application docker run -d \ --name task-api \ --network task-api-network \ -e DATABASE_URL=postgresql://taskuser:taskpass@task-db:5432/taskdb \ -e REDIS_URL=redis://task-cache:6379 \ -p 3000:3000 \ --depends-on task-db task-cache \ my-task-api:latest

    That's 50+ lines of imperative commands that must be run in the correct order. If you restart your computer, you have to remember all of this. If a colleague joins your team, they need documentation. If you deploy to staging, you manually recreate everything. This doesn't scale.

    With Docker Compose, this entire stack becomes:

    version: '3.8' services: task-db: image: postgres:15-alpine environment: POSTGRES_USER: taskuser POSTGRES_PASSWORD: taskpass POSTGRES_DB: taskdb volumes: - postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data networks: - task-api-network ports: - "5432:5432" task-cache: image: redis:7-alpine volumes: - redis-data:/data networks: - task-api-network ports: - "6379:6379" task-api: image: my-task-api:latest environment: DATABASE_URL: postgresql://taskuser:taskpass@task-db:5432/taskdb REDIS_URL: redis://task-cache:6379 depends_on: - task-db - task-cache networks: - task-api-network ports: - "3000:3000" volumes: postgres-data: redis-data: networks: task-api-network:

    Now, to start the entire application: docker-compose up. To stop it: docker-compose down. To rebuild and restart: docker-compose up --build. One command instead of six complex commands.

    💡 Key Benefits of Docker Compose
    • Declarative Configuration: Describe what you want, not how to achieve it
    • Single Source of Truth: Your entire stack is documented in one file
    • Version Controlled: Commit docker-compose.yml to Git with your application code
    • Environment Parity: Dev, staging, and production use identical configurations
    • Automatic Networking: Compose creates networks and DNS resolution automatically
    • Dependency Management: Control startup order with depends_on
    • Single-Command Lifecycle: Start, stop, rebuild entire stacks with one command

    When to Use Docker Compose:

    • Local Development: Run your entire application stack on your laptop
    • Testing Environments: Spin up temporary environments for testing branches
    • CI/CD Pipelines: Integration tests that require multiple services
    • Single-Host Deployments: Small projects on a single server (not production-scale)
    • Documentation: Even if you don't use it, docker-compose.yml documents your architecture
    ⚠️ When NOT to Use Docker Compose

    Docker Compose is NOT a production orchestrator for multi-host, scaled applications. For production, you need Kubernetes (covered in Weeks 5-9). Compose works on a single Docker host—it doesn't distribute containers across multiple servers, doesn't provide high availability, and doesn't auto-scale. Think of Compose as "development/testing tool" and Kubernetes as "production orchestrator."

    3.2 Docker Compose File Structure: YAML Anatomy

    Docker Compose uses YAML (YAML Ain't Markup Language), a human-readable data serialization format. If you've never used YAML, don't worry—it's simpler than JSON with less punctuation.

    📖 YAML Basics
