    Secrets are similar to ConfigMaps but are intended to hold sensitive information, such as passwords, OAuth tokens, and SSH keys.

    🔒 Secrets vs. ConfigMaps
    • Encoding: Secrets are stored as Base64-encoded strings in your YAML files (to handle binary data), whereas ConfigMaps store plain text. Note: Base64 is NOT encryption!
    • Handling: Kubernetes treats Secrets with extra care. They are stored in tmpfs (RAM) on the worker nodes, never written to disk, to prevent data leakage.
    • Encryption at Rest: In a production cluster, you must enable "Encryption at Rest" for etcd to ensure Secrets are encrypted specifically within the cluster database.
    # Secret Example apiVersion: v1 kind: Secret metadata: name: db-credentials type: Opaque data: # 'username' is base64 encoded "admin" username: YWRtaW4= # 'password' is base64 encoded "secret123" password: c2VjcmV0MTIz
    💡 Secret Management in Production

    Checking Secret manifests (even Base64 encoded) into Git is a security risk ("Secret Sprawl"). In advanced DevOps (Week 12/18), we use tools like Sealed Secrets, External Secrets Operator (connecting to AWS Secrets Manager/HashiCorp Vault), or SOPS to handle secrets securely in GitOps workflows.

    6.7 Architecture: Bringing It All Together

    A robust microservice architecture typically combines these elements:

    • A Deployment manages the stateless application logic (Frontend/API).
    • A StatefulSet manages the persistence layer (Database/Cache), ensuring stable storage.
    • ConfigMaps inject non-sensitive environment configuration (URLs, feature flags).
    • Secrets inject credentials (DB passwords, API keys).
    • A Service creates a stable network interface for the Deployment to talk to the StatefulSet.

    Hands-On Labs

    Theory is essential, but execution is what builds careers. In these five labs, you will move beyond simple stateless pods. You will configure applications dynamically, secure sensitive data, manage rolling updates for deployments, orchestrate stateful databases, and schedule batch jobs.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    Ensure your local cluster (Minikube/Kind) is running and kubectl is configured. Create a clean namespace for these labs to avoid clutter:
    kubectl create namespace week6
    kubens week6 (or kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=week6)

    Lab 1: Decoupling Config with ConfigMaps

    Objective: Deploy a Python application that reads configuration from the environment. You will inject this configuration using Kubernetes ConfigMaps instead of hardcoding it in the image.

    Create the ConfigMap (Imperative):

    First, let's create a simple configuration object imperatively.

    kubectl create configmap app-settings \ --from-literal=APP_COLOR=blue \ --from-literal=APP_MODE=production \ --from-literal=MAX_RETRIES=5
    Inspect the ConfigMap:
    kubectl get configmap app-settings -o yaml

    Notice how the data is stored as plain text key-value pairs.

    Create a Pod Manifest consuming the ConfigMap:

    Create a file named config-pod.yaml. We will use a basic image that prints environment variables.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: config-test-pod spec: containers: - name: test-container image: busybox command: [ "sh", "-c", "env | grep APP_ && sleep 3600" ] envFrom: - configMapRef: name: app-settings
    Apply and Verify:
    kubectl apply -f config-pod.yaml # Wait for it to start sleep 5 # Check logs to see injected variables kubectl logs config-test-pod
    APP_COLOR=blue APP_MODE=production
    Update Configuration (Hot Reload Simulation):
    # Edit the ConfigMap kubectl edit configmap app-settings # Change APP_COLOR to "green"

    Observation: Check the logs again. Did the value change?
    Spoiler: No. Environment variables are injected at startup. To pick up changes, you must restart the Pod. (We will see how Volumes solve this in Lab 4).

    Lab 2: Managing Sensitive Data with Secrets

    Objective: Store database credentials securely using Kubernetes Secrets and mount them into an application Pod securely.

    Create a Secret (Imperative):

    We will create a generic secret for database credentials.

    kubectl create secret generic db-creds \ --from-literal=username=admin \ --from-literal=password=SuperSecretPass123!
    Inspect the Secret (Decoding):

    Secrets are base64 encoded, not encrypted (by default in kubectl view).

    kubectl get secret db-creds -o yaml

    Copy the base64 string for password and decode it:

    echo "U3VwZXJTZWNyZXRQYXNzMTIzIQ==" | base64 --decode
    Create a Declarative Secret Manifest:

    Often you need to commit secrets (bad idea) or manage them via GitOps. Let's see the YAML format. Create secret.yaml:

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Secret metadata: name: api-key type: Opaque data: # "my-api-key" base64 encoded api-token: bXktYXBpLWtleQ==
    kubectl apply -f secret.yaml
    Mount Secret as a Volume:

    Applications often expect secrets as files (e.g., /etc/secrets/token). Create secret-vol-pod.yaml:

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: secret-test-pod spec: containers: - name: app image: nginx:alpine volumeMounts: - name: secret-volume mountPath: "/etc/secret-volume" readOnly: true volumes: - name: secret-volume secret: secretName: db-creds
    Verify Secret Injection:
    kubectl apply -f secret-vol-pod.yaml # Execute into the pod to see the file kubectl exec -it secret-test-pod -- ls /etc/secret-volume kubectl exec -it secret-test-pod -- cat /etc/secret-volume/password
    💡 Security Note

    Notice that inside the pod, the file contains the decoded plain text. Kubernetes handles the decoding automatically when mounting.

    Lab 3: Zero-Downtime Deployments

    Objective: Perform a rolling update of a web application from version 1 to version 2 without interrupting service, then perform a rollback.

    Create the Deployment (v1):

    Create nginx-deploy.yaml using the nginx 1.19 image.

    apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: nginx-prod spec: replicas: 3 selector: matchLabels: app: nginx-prod template: metadata: labels: app: nginx-prod spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:1.19.0 ports: - containerPort: 80
    kubectl apply -f nginx-deploy.yaml kubectl get pods -l app=nginx-prod
    Perform Rolling Update:

    Update the image to 1.21.0. We will use kubectl set image for speed, though editing YAML is better for git history.

    # Open a second terminal window to watch the update kubectl get pods -l app=nginx-prod -w # In original window, trigger update kubectl set image deployment/nginx-prod nginx=nginx:1.21.0

    Watch the "Terminating" and "ContainerCreating" dance. The Deployment ensures minimal downtime.

    Verify Rollout Status:
    kubectl rollout status deployment/nginx-prod
    Introduce a Bug & Rollback:

    Let's break it by setting a non-existent image.

    kubectl set image deployment/nginx-prod nginx=nginx:999.999 # Check status - it will hang or error kubectl get pods

    You will see ImagePullBackOff or ErrImagePull. The old pods (v1.21) are still running! The deployment stopped the rollout because the new pods failed.

    Execute Rollback:
    # View history kubectl rollout history deployment/nginx-prod # Undo the last change kubectl rollout undo deployment/nginx-prod # Verify recovery kubectl get pods

    Lab 4: Running Stateful Workloads

    Objective: Deploy a MySQL database using a StatefulSet. Understand the difference in pod naming and ordered creation compared to Deployments.

    Create Headless Service:

    StatefulSets require a "Headless Service" (ClusterIP: None) to control the network identity of the pods. Create mysql-svc.yaml:

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: mysql labels: app: mysql spec: ports: - port: 3306 name: mysql clusterIP: None selector: app: mysql
    Create StatefulSet Manifest:

    Create mysql-statefulset.yaml.

    apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: StatefulSet metadata: name: mysql spec: selector: matchLabels: app: mysql serviceName: "mysql" replicas: 2 template: metadata: labels: app: mysql spec: containers: - name: mysql image: mysql:5.7 env: - name: MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD value: "secretpassword" volumeMounts: - name: mysql-store mountPath: /var/lib/mysql volumeClaimTemplates: - metadata: name: mysql-store spec: accessModes: [ "ReadWriteOnce" ] resources: requests: storage: 1Gi
    Apply and Observe Ordered Creation:
    kubectl apply -f mysql-svc.yaml kubectl apply -f mysql-statefulset.yaml # Watch the ordered creation kubectl get pods -l app=mysql -w

    Unlike Deployments which start all pods seemingly at once, you will see mysql-0 start. Only after mysql-0 is Running will mysql-1 begin initializing. This strict ordering is critical for database replication setup.

    Verify Stable Network Identity:
    # Run a temporary pod to test DNS kubectl run -it --rm --image=busybox:1.28 dns-test --restart=Never -- nslookup mysql-0.mysql

    You should see a resolved IP. The DNS name mysql-0.mysql is stable. If you delete the pod, it comes back with the same name.

    Lab 5: Batch Processing with CronJobs

    Objective: Schedule a task that runs periodically (like a database backup or report generator) using Kubernetes CronJobs.

    Create CronJob Manifest:

    Create periodic-job.yaml that runs every minute.

    apiVersion: batch/v1 kind: CronJob metadata: name: hello-cron spec: schedule: "*/1 * * * *" # Run every minute jobTemplate: spec: template: spec: containers: - name: hello image: busybox imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent command: - /bin/sh - -c - date; echo "Hello from Kubernetes CronJob" restartPolicy: OnFailure
    Apply and Wait:
    kubectl apply -f periodic-job.yaml # Wait 60-90 seconds for the first run kubectl get cronjobs kubectl get jobs
    Inspect the Results:

    CronJobs create Jobs, and Jobs create Pods. You need to find the pod to see the logs.

    # Find the pod created by the job kubectl get pods # Check logs of the completed pod kubectl logs hello-cron-xxxxx-xxxxx
    Cleanup:
    # Deleting the CronJob deletes all child Jobs and Pods kubectl delete cronjob hello-cron

    Lab Solutions & Troubleshooting Guide

    This section allows you to verify your work from the hands-on labs. It delves into the "why" behind the commands, explaining the architectural mechanics of how Kubernetes handles configuration injection, stateful identities, and batch scheduling.

    Lab 1 Solution: Decoupling Config

    Expected Behavior Analysis

    Step: Verify Env Vars

    APP_COLOR=blue APP_MODE=production MAX_RETRIES=5

    Insight: Using `envFrom` is cleaner than listing every variable individually. It injects all key-value pairs from the ConfigMap as environment variables.

    Step: The Hot Reload Test

    🔥 Why didn't the variable update?

    When you edited the ConfigMap to change APP_COLOR to "green", the logs inside the Pod still showed "blue".

    Reason: Environment variables are injected only when the process starts. Kubernetes updates the ConfigMap object immediately, but the running container process has no way of knowing about this change.

    Solution: To apply configuration changes when using Env Vars, you must restart the Pods (usually by triggering a Rolling Update of the Deployment).

    Lab 2 Solution: Secrets Management

    Decoding Analysis

    echo "U3VwZXJTZWNyZXRQYXNzMTIzIQ==" | base64 --decode # Output: SuperSecretPass123!

    This proves that anyone with "View" access to Secrets in your namespace can decode them. RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) is essential to restrict who can get secrets.

    Volume Mount Verification

    /etc/secret-volume/api-token

    Mechanism: When you mount a Secret as a volume, Kubernetes creates a specific file for each key in the Secret data. The filename is the key, and the content is the decoded value.

    💡 Secret Volume Updates

    Unlike Environment Variables, Volumes DO update automatically. If you edit the Secret, Kubernetes will eventually (within a minute or so) update the file inside the container. Many modern applications use a "file watcher" to reload their config when this file changes, enabling true hot-reloading without restarts.

    Lab 3 Solution: Deployments & Rollouts

    Rolling Update Mechanics

    When you updated the image, you observed pods terminating and creating.

    nginx-prod-v1-xxxxx Running nginx-prod-v1-yyyyy Terminating nginx-prod-v2-zzzzz ContainerCreating

    Under the hood: The Deployment controller created a new ReplicaSet for v2. It scaled the new ReplicaSet up by 1, and the old ReplicaSet down by 1, repeating until the transfer was complete. This ensures traffic is always served.

    Rollback Analysis

    When you introduced the bad image nginx:999.999, the rollout paused. Kubernetes prevents a total outage by requiring new pods to become "Ready" before killing old ones. Since the new pods stuck in ImagePullBackOff never became ready, the old pods remained running, keeping your app online despite the bad config.

    Lab 4 Solution: StatefulSets

    Ordered Creation

    mysql-0 Running mysql-1 ContainerCreating

    This strict ordering (`0` then `1`) allows the database logic to say "If I am `mysql-1`, I should look for `mysql-0` and treat it as the Master/Primary node to replicate data from." A Deployment creates pods in parallel (`xc9df`, `a87bf`), making this logic impossible.

    Headless Service DNS

    The command nslookup mysql-0.mysql worked because we set clusterIP: None.

    • Standard Service: DNS resolves to a single Virtual IP (VIP). Traffic is load-balanced randomly.
    • Headless Service: DNS resolves directly to the Pod IPs. This allows direct peer-to-peer communication between database nodes.

    Lab 5 Solution: CronJobs

    Hierarchy

    The CronJob doesn't run containers. It creates Jobs based on the schedule. The Job creates Pods to do the work.

    CronJob (hello-cron) -> Job (hello-cron-16094850) -> Pod (hello-cron-16094850-x8d9f)
    ⚠️ Cleanup Hygiene

    By default, Kubernetes keeps completed Jobs (and their logs) around so you can inspect them. However, if you run a job every minute, you will have thousands of dead pods after a few days. Always set .spec.successfulJobsHistoryLimit in production to clean up old metadata.

    Practice Questions

    Test your knowledge of Workload Controllers and Configuration. These scenarios are typical of what you will encounter in day-to-day DevOps work and interviews.

    You need to deploy a logging agent that runs on every single node in the cluster, including new nodes added in the future. Which controller should you use?
    A. Deployment
    B. StatefulSet
    C. DaemonSet
    D. ReplicaSet
    Answer: C. DaemonSets ensure that all (or some matching) Nodes run a copy of a Pod. As nodes are added to the cluster, Pods are added to them. Typical use cases: logging (fluentd), monitoring (node-exporter).
    What is the primary security limitation of Kubernetes Secrets stored in etcd by default?
    A. They are plain text
    B. They are Base64 encoded, not encrypted
    C. They cannot be mounted as volumes
    D. They expire after 24 hours
    Answer: B. Base64 is an encoding scheme, not encryption. Anyone with access to the API server or etcd can decode the secrets. In production, you must enable Encryption at Rest configuration in the API server.
    Your application requires a persistent identifier (e.g., `app-0`, `app-1`) and stable storage that survives pod restarts. Which controller is appropriate?
    A. Deployment
    B. StatefulSet
    C. DaemonSet
    D. Job
    Answer: B. StatefulSets provide sticky identities and stable persistent storage. If a pod dies, the replacement gets the exact same name and re-attaches to the same PersistentVolume.
    You updated a ConfigMap that is injected into a Pod as an environment variable. The Pod is running. Does the application see the new value?
    A. Yes, immediately
    B. Yes, after a short delay
    C. No, environment variables are only set at startup
    D. No, ConfigMaps cannot be updated
    Answer: C. Environment variables are loaded by the container runtime when the process starts. To update them, the process must restart (i.e., the Pod must be recreated).
    What type of Service is typically required for a StatefulSet to allow pods to discover each other by name (e.g., `mysql-0.mysql`)?
    A. NodePort
    B. LoadBalancer
    C. Headless (ClusterIP: None)
    D. ExternalName
    Answer: C. A Headless Service does not allocate a single VIP or load balance. Instead, it returns A records for the IPs of the backend pods directly, allowing direct addressing of specific pods.
    Which update strategy does a Deployment use by default?
    A. Recreate (Kill all, then start new)
    B. RollingUpdate (Start new, then kill old incrementally)
    C. Blue/Green
    D. Canary
    Answer: B. RollingUpdate is the default. It ensures zero downtime by incrementally replacing old pods with new ones, respecting `maxUnavailable` and `maxSurge` constraints.
    You want to run a database migration script that executes once and then stops. Which workload resource should you use?
    A. Deployment
    B. Pod
    C. Job
    D. Service
    Answer: C. A Job creates one or more Pods and will continue to retry execution of the Pods until a specified number of them successfully terminate. Deployments are for long-running services.
    What is the "Twelve-Factor" recommended way to store configuration?
    A. Hardcoded in the code
    B. In a text file inside the git repo
    C. In the environment (Env Vars)
    D. In a database table
    Answer: C. Storing config in the environment allows strictly separating config from code. Ideally, this is injected via ConfigMaps/Secrets in Kubernetes.
    If a StatefulSet pod `web-0` crashes and is restarted on a different node, what happens to its storage?
    A. It gets a fresh empty disk
    B. It re-attaches to the same PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) it had before
    C. It copies data from `web-1`
    D. It fails to start
    Answer: B. This is the defining feature of StatefulSets. The mapping between the Pod identity (`web-0`) and the PVC (`www-web-0`) is static. The storage follows the pod.
    How do you imperatively creating a generic secret from a literal value on the command line?
    A. kubectl create configmap ...
    B. kubectl apply -f secret.yaml
    C. kubectl create secret generic my-secret --from-literal=key=value
    D. kubectl run secret ...
    Answer: C. `kubectl create secret generic` is the command. `--from-literal` allows creating key-value pairs without a file.

    Week-Ending Assignment: Project "IronVault" (Stateful Architecture)

    🎯 Assignment Objective

    You have mastered the individual components of Kubernetes workloads. Now, you must architect a complete, production-grade Stateful Application Stack.

    Your mission: Deploy "IronVault," a secure document storage system. Unlike previous stateless web apps, this requires persistent storage for a database, secure credential management for access, and dynamic configuration injection. You will prove your ability to choose the right controller (StatefulSet vs. Deployment) for the right task.

    Architecture Overview

    The application stack consists of three distinct layers that must work in unison:

    🗄️

    1. The Database (Stateful)

    Controller: StatefulSet
    Image: postgres:15-alpine
    Requirement: Must use a PersistentVolumeClaim to ensure data survives pod restarts. Must have a stable network identity.

    ⚙️

    2. The API (Stateless)

    Controller: Deployment
    Image: nginx:alpine (Simulating an App)
    Requirement: Must scale to 3 replicas. Must read database credentials from a Secret and config from a ConfigMap.

    🛡️

    3. The Agent (Daemon)

    Controller: DaemonSet
    Image: busybox
    Requirement: A mock "security agent" that must run on every node in the cluster to monitor logs.

    Technical Requirements

    1. Namespace Isolation

    Create a namespace called ironvault-prod. All resources must be deployed here.

    2. Configuration & Secrets

    • Create a Secret named db-creds containing username=vaultadmin and password=fortknox123.
    • Create a ConfigMap named vault-config containing log_level=verbose and environment=production.

    3. The Database (StatefulSet)

    • Must use StatefulSet controller.
    • Must utilize a Headless Service for stable network identity.
    • Must inject the db-creds Secret as environment variables (POSTGRES_USER, POSTGRES_PASSWORD).
    • Must use volumeClaimTemplates to request 1Gi of storage.

    4. The App (Deployment)

    • Must use Deployment controller with 3 replicas.
    • Must inject vault-config as Environment Variables.
    • Must mount the db-creds Secret as a Volume at /etc/secrets.

    Step-by-Step Implementation Guide

    Phase 1: Foundation

    Create the namespace manifest. Create your Secret (remember to base64 encode values!) and ConfigMap manifests.

    Phase 2: The Persistence Layer

    Define the Headless Service and the StatefulSet for PostgreSQL. Ensure the volume claim template is correct. Apply and verify that postgres-0 starts before postgres-1 (if replicas > 1).

    Phase 3: The Application Layer

    Define the Deployment. Use envFrom for the ConfigMap and volumes for the Secret. This ensures the app can "see" the database credentials securely.

    Phase 4: The Node Agent

    Define a DaemonSet using the busybox image. Use a command like sleep infinity to keep it running. Verify it runs on all available nodes (in Minikube, this will be just 1 node).

    Phase 5: Validation

    Exec into an App pod and verify:

    # Check Env Vars env | grep log_level # Check Secret Volume cat /etc/secrets/password

    📋 Student Submission Checklist

    Before submitting your assignment, verify you have completed all items:

    Teacher's Solution Guide

    Use this section to grade student submissions. These manifests represent the "Golden Path" solution. Key things to check: correct usage of Headless Service for StatefulSet, correct Volume Mount syntax for Secrets, and proper Base64 encoding.

    Solution: IronVault Manifests

    1. Namespace & Configuration

    # 00-namespace.yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Namespace metadata: name: ironvault-prod --- # 01-secret.yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Secret metadata: name: db-creds namespace: ironvault-prod type: Opaque data: # username=vaultadmin (base64) username: dmF1bHRhZG1pbg== # password=fortknox123 (base64) password: Zm9ydGtub3gxMjM= --- # 02-config.yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: ConfigMap metadata: name: vault-config namespace: ironvault-prod data: log_level: "verbose" environment: "production"

    2. Database Layer (StatefulSet)

    Check: Look for clusterIP: None (Headless) and volumeClaimTemplates.

    # 03-db-statefulset.yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: postgres namespace: ironvault-prod labels: app: postgres spec: ports: - port: 5432 name: postgres clusterIP: None # HEADLESS SERVICE CRITICAL FOR STATEFULSETS selector: app: postgres --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: StatefulSet metadata: name: postgres namespace: ironvault-prod spec: selector: matchLabels: app: postgres serviceName: "postgres" replicas: 1 template: metadata: labels: app: postgres spec: containers: - name: postgres image: postgres:15-alpine ports: - containerPort: 5432 name: postgres env: - name: POSTGRES_USER valueFrom: secretKeyRef: name: db-creds key: username - name: POSTGRES_PASSWORD valueFrom: secretKeyRef: name: db-creds key: password volumeMounts: - name: postgres-data mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data volumeClaimTemplates: - metadata: name: postgres-data spec: accessModes: [ "ReadWriteOnce" ] resources: requests: storage: 1Gi

    3. Application Layer (Deployment)

    Check: Look for envFrom (ConfigMap) and volumes (Secret).

    # 04-app-deployment.yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: vault-app namespace: ironvault-prod spec: replicas: 3 selector: matchLabels: app: vault-app template: metadata: labels: app: vault-app spec: containers: - name: app image: nginx:alpine envFrom: - configMapRef: name: vault-config volumeMounts: - name: secret-vol mountPath: "/etc/secrets" readOnly: true volumes: - name: secret-vol secret: secretName: db-creds

    4. System Agent (DaemonSet)

    # 05-agent-daemonset.yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: DaemonSet metadata: name: security-agent namespace: ironvault-prod spec: selector: matchLabels: name: security-agent template: metadata: labels: name: security-agent spec: containers: - name: agent image: busybox command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "echo 'Security Agent Scanning'; sleep infinity"]

    5. Validation Commands

    # 1. Apply all kubectl apply -f . # 2. Verify StatefulSet Storage kubectl get pvc -n ironvault-prod # Should see postgres-data-postgres-0 # 3. Verify Secret Mount kubectl exec -n ironvault-prod deploy/vault-app -- cat /etc/secrets/password # Should output: fortknox123 # 4. Verify DaemonSet kubectl get daemonset -n ironvault-prod
    🚀 Week 6 Complete

    Congratulations! You have successfully managed the entire lifecycle of a stateful application. You understand how to inject configuration securely, how to persist database data using StatefulSets, and how to deploy system-wide agents.

    Next Week (Week 7): With our applications running, we need to connect them to the outside world and manage their storage at scale. We will master Services (LoadBalancers, NodePorts), Ingress Controllers, and StorageClasses.

    Mentorship Review for Week 6

    Mentorship students: your Week 6 work (Kubernetes Workloads & Configuration) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    7

    Kubernetes Networking & Storage

    Connecting Applications and Persisting Data at Scale

    Welcome to Week 7. In Week 6, you deployed complex applications, but they lived in somewhat isolated bubbles. You used basic Services and Volumes, but likely treated them as "magic black boxes." This week, we tear open those boxes.

    Networking in Kubernetes is notoriously complex because it imposes a unique set of constraints: every pod must be able to reach every other pod without NAT. We will dissect the Pod Network, explore how CNI (Container Network Interface) plugins make this possible, and master Services and Ingress to route external traffic to your pods. We will also secure this traffic using Network Policies—the firewall of the cloud-native world.

    Storage is equally critical. Containers are ephemeral, but data must be eternal. We will move beyond simple Docker volumes to the robust Kubernetes Storage API: PersistentVolumes (PVs), PersistentVolumeClaims (PVCs), and StorageClasses. You will learn how to dynamic provisioning allows developers to request storage on-demand without waking up storage administrators.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Kubernetes Networking & Storage: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Concepts: Define the roles of CNI (networking) and CSI (storage) plugins. List the four types of Kubernetes Services (ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, ExternalName) and the access modes for PersistentVolumes (RWO, RWX, ROX).

    Understand

    Explain Mechanics: Explain the Kubernetes "Flat Network" model (IP-per-Pod). Describe the binding process between a PVC and a PV, and how StorageClasses automate the creation of physical disks.

    Apply

    Execute Configuration: Implement an Ingress Controller to route HTTP/HTTPS traffic based on domains. Configure Dynamic Volume Provisioning to automatically create AWS EBS or local storage volumes for a database.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Issues: Troubleshoot CoreDNS failures (Service Discovery issues). Analyze "Pending" PVCs to identify StorageClass misconfigurations or capacity constraints.

    Evaluate

    Assess Strategies: Compare different storage backends (NFS vs. Block Storage) for specific workload requirements. Evaluate the security implications of open pod-to-pod communication versus restricting traffic with Network Policies.

    Create

    Build Architecture: Design a secured multi-tier application where the frontend communicates with the backend via Service Discovery, the database uses persistent storage, and Network Policies block unauthorized access.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Networking and Storage rely heavily on the underlying infrastructure. These exercises verify that your local cluster supports these advanced features. Budget approximately 3-4 hours.

    1The "Flat Network" Research

    Kubernetes Networking is different from standard Docker networking.

    Task: Research the "Kubernetes Networking Model" and answer the following:

    • Does Kubernetes use NAT (Network Address Translation) when Pod A talks to Pod B on a different node? (Hint: No)
    • What is a CNI Plugin? Name three popular ones (e.g., Calico, Flannel, Cilium).
    • If you use Minikube or Kind, which CNI are you using by default?
    💡 Why this matters

    In Docker, you had to link containers or map ports explicitly. In Kubernetes, every pod gets its own IP address and can talk to any other pod by default. This "flat network" assumption simplifies app development but complicates infrastructure setup.

    2Prepare Network Debugging Tools

    Debugging networking issues usually requires tools like dig, nslookup, or curl, which are missing from slim production images.

    Task: Pull the nicolaka/netshoot image locally. This is the "Swiss Army Knife" of container networking. We will use it extensively in the labs.

    docker pull nicolaka/netshoot

    Test it: Run a temporary pod to explore your cluster's DNS.

    kubectl run tmp-shell --rm -i --tty --image nicolaka/netshoot -- /bin/bash # Inside the pod, try resolving the internal DNS service dig kube-dns.kube-system.svc.cluster.local

    3Storage Class Verification

    Storage behavior depends entirely on where your cluster is running (AWS vs. Laptop).

    Task: Check what StorageClasses are available in your local cluster.

    kubectl get storageclass
    NAME PROVISIONER RECLAIMPOLICY VOLUMEBINDINGMODE ALLOWVOLUMEEXPANSION AGE standard (default) k8s.io/minikube-hostpath Delete Immediate false 2d

    Analysis: If you see (default) next to a name, that's the class used when you don't specify one. On Minikube, it uses hostpath (folders on your laptop). On AWS, it would be gp2 (EBS volumes).

    4Enable Ingress Addon

    We will be doing advanced Ingress routing this week. Ensure your controller is ready.

    # If using Minikube minikube addons enable ingress # If using Kind kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/main/deploy/static/provider/kind/deploy.yaml

    Task: Locate the Ingress Controller pod.

    kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx # Ensure status is 'Running'

    5Reset Your Environment

    Clean up any leftover resources from Week 6 (IronVault project) to prevent conflicts.

    kubectl delete namespace ironvault-prod kubectl delete namespace week6 kubectl delete pv --all kubectl delete pvc --all

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    If Kubernetes has a "final boss" for beginners, it is Networking. In Docker, you explicitly linked containers or mapped ports. In Kubernetes, networking is designed to be "flat," meaning every Pod can talk to every other Pod without Network Address Translation (NAT). This simplifies application logic (apps just talk to IPs) but complicates the infrastructure required to make that happen.

    Similarly, Storage in Kubernetes requires a mental shift from "writing to disk" to "claiming storage resources." We abstract the physical hard drive (EBS, SSD, NFS) into logical resources that developers can request without knowing the underlying hardware.

    7.1 The "Flat Network" Assumption

    Kubernetes imposes a specific set of networking requirements on any cluster implementation. This is often referred to as the "IP-per-Pod" model.

    🌐 The 3 Networking Rules
    1. Pod-to-Pod: All Pods can communicate with all other Pods without NAT. (If Pod A at 10.1.1.1 pings Pod B at 10.1.2.1, Pod B sees the source as 10.1.1.1).
    2. Node-to-Pod: All Nodes can communicate with all Pods (and vice-versa) without NAT.
    3. IP Identity: The IP that a Pod sees itself as is the same IP that others see it as.

    How is this achieved? CNI Plugins.
    Kubernetes itself does not implement the network. It offloads this to CNI (Container Network Interface) plugins. Common plugins include:

    • Calico: Uses BGP routing for high performance and advanced network policies.
    • Flannel: A simple overlay network (VXLAN) that encapsulates packets. Great for beginners.
    • Cilium: Uses eBPF for extremely high-performance filtering and observability at the kernel level.
    • AWS VPC CNI: Assigns real AWS VPC IP addresses to Pods (allowing them to talk to RDS/EC2 natively).

    7.2 Service Networking (East-West Traffic)

    Pods are ephemeral. Their IPs change. To solve this, we use Services. But how does a Service actually work? It is not a process. It is a stable Virtual IP (VIP) and a set of packet forwarding rules.

    The Magic of Kube-Proxy

    A Service IP (e.g., 10.96.0.1) does not exist on any network interface. It is a "ghost" IP.

    1. You create a Service my-service (IP: 10.96.0.1).
    2. kube-apiserver notifies kube-proxy (running on every node) about the new Service.
    3. kube-proxy writes iptables (or IPVS) rules on the Node's kernel.
    4. The Rule: "If a packet is destined for 10.96.0.1:80, DNAT (Destination NAT) it to one of the Pod IPs (e.g., 10.1.1.5:80)."

    This creates a client-side load balancer in the kernel of every node.

    7.3 Ingress Networking (North-South Traffic)

    Services handle traffic inside the cluster (East-West). To get traffic from the internet into the cluster (North-South), we typically use an Ingress Controller.

    Unlike a Service (which is Layer 4 TCP/UDP), Ingress operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS). It can make intelligent routing decisions:

    • Host Routing: api.example.com goes to the API service; www.example.com goes to the Web service.
    • Path Routing: example.com/v1 goes to Legacy API; example.com/v2 goes to New API.
    • TLS Termination: The Ingress Controller handles the SSL certificates, decrypting traffic before passing it to the pods (which saves CPU on the pods).

    7.4 Securing the Network

    By default, Kubernetes is a "soft shell." Any pod can talk to any other pod, even across namespaces. This is a security risk. To lock this down, we use Network Policies.

    A Network Policy is like a firewall rule for pods. It allows you to say: "Only the Frontend pods are allowed to talk to the Backend pods on port 8080. Block everything else."

    ⚠️ Requirement

    Network Policies require a CNI plugin that supports them (like Calico or Cilium). Flannel does not support Network Policies; creating them on Flannel will simply do nothing.

    7.5 Storage Architecture: The PV Subsystem

    Containers write to a writable layer that disappears when the container dies. For databases, we need data to survive pod restarts, node failures, and even cluster upgrades.

    Kubernetes decouples storage users (developers) from storage providers (admins/cloud) using three API objects:

    The Storage Trinity

    1. PersistentVolume (PV)

    The physical piece of storage in the infrastructure. It is a cluster-level resource (like a Node). It represents an actual EBS volume, NFS share, or local disk.
    Analogy: A physical USB drive plugged into the server.

    2. PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC)

    A request for storage by a user. It lives in a Namespace. A PVC claims a specific size and access mode (e.g., "I need 10GB ReadWriteOnce").
    Analogy: A voucher or ticket redeemable for storage.

    3. StorageClass (SC)

    The "Provisioner." Instead of creating PVs manually, the SC listens for PVCs and automatically calls the cloud provider API to create the volume (e.g., AWS EBS) and creates the PV object to bind to the PVC. This is called Dynamic Provisioning.

    7.6 Access Modes & Volume Types

    Not all storage is created equal. You must choose the right type for your workload.

    # Access Modes ReadWriteOnce (RWO): Mounted by a single Node (Block Storage) ReadWriteMany (RWX): Mounted by multiple Nodes simultaneously (File Storage) ReadOnlyMany (ROX): Mounted by multiple Nodes, read-only
    • Block Storage (RWO): AWS EBS, Azure Disk, GCE PD. High performance, low latency. Can only attach to ONE node at a time. Used for Databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL).
    • File Storage (RWX): AWS EFS, Azure Files, NFS. Slower, but can attach to multiple nodes. Used for shared content (CMS uploads, configuration files shared across web servers).

    7.7 The Binding Lifecycle

    Understanding how PVCs find PVs is crucial for debugging "Pending" storage.

    1. Provisioning: Administrator creates a PV (Static) or StorageClass creates one (Dynamic).
    2. Binding: User creates a PVC. Kubernetes looks for a PV that matches the request (Size, Access Mode, StorageClass). If found, they are bound together (1-to-1 mapping).
    3. Using: Pod references the PVC as a volume. Kubelet tells the Node to mount the physical device associated with the PV.
    4. Reclaiming: When user deletes PVC, the PV is either Retained (saved) or Deleted (wiped), depending on the ReclaimPolicy.
    💡 Production Tip: Retain Policy

    Default StorageClasses usually have reclaimPolicy: Delete. This means if you accidentally delete a PVC (e.g., `helm uninstall`), your database volume is deleted from the cloud provider immediately. For production databases, always set `reclaimPolicy: Retain` or use Volume Snapshots.

    Hands-On Labs

    These labs are designed to break the "magic" of Kubernetes networking and storage. You will manually trace a packet across the cluster, configure an Ingress load balancer, lock down traffic with firewalls (Network Policies), and provision persistent disk storage that survives pod destruction.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    Ensure your local cluster is running.
    Network Tools: We will use the nicolaka/netshoot image for debugging. It contains tools like dig, nslookup, curl, and tcpdump.
    Namespace: Create a clean namespace: kubectl create ns week7 and switch to it.

    Lab 1: Internal DNS & Service Discovery

    Objective: Prove that Kubernetes provides a stable internal DNS system. You will deploy two pods and make them communicate using hostnames, bypassing unstable IP addresses.

    Deploy a Backend Service:

    First, create a simple Nginx deployment and expose it as a Service.

    # Create Deployment kubectl create deployment backend --image=nginx --replicas=2 --port=80 # Expose it as a ClusterIP Service kubectl expose deployment backend --name=backend-svc --port=80 --target-port=80
    Deploy a "Netshoot" Client:

    Run an interactive pod to act as our network troubleshooter.

    kubectl run client --rm -it --image=nicolaka/netshoot -- /bin/bash

    You are now inside the container's shell.

    Test 1: DNS Resolution:

    Inside the client pod, query the internal DNS server.

    # Lookup the service by name dig backend-svc.week7.svc.cluster.local +short
    10.96.134.55 <-- This is the Service VIP (Virtual IP)
    Test 2: Connectivity via Name:

    Verify that you can reach the backend using the DNS name.

    curl http://backend-svc

    You should see the "Welcome to nginx!" HTML. This confirms Service Discovery is working. The application code doesn't need to know IPs; it just needs the name backend-svc.

    exit # Leave the pod

    Lab 2: Exposing Apps via Ingress

    Objective: Configure an Ingress Resource to route traffic based on a hostname (week7.local) to your backend service.

    Verify Ingress Controller:
    # If using Minikube kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx # If using Kind/Cloud kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx

    Ensure the controller pod is Running. Without this, Ingress resources are just ignored text files.

    Define Ingress Resource:

    Create ingress.yaml.

    apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: Ingress metadata: name: week7-ingress annotations: nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: / spec: ingressClassName: nginx rules: - host: week7.local http: paths: - path: / pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: backend-svc port: number: 80
    kubectl apply -f ingress.yaml
    Wait for Address:
    kubectl get ingress -w
    NAME CLASS HOSTS ADDRESS PORTS AGE week7-ingress nginx week7.local 192.168.49.2 80 30s

    Wait until the ADDRESS column gets an IP. If it stays empty for >2 minutes, check your Ingress Controller logs.

    Configure Local DNS (Spoofing):

    You don't own the domain week7.local, but you can trick your computer using curl headers or /etc/hosts.

    # Get the Ingress IP (Minikube users: 'minikube ip') INGRESS_IP=$(kubectl get ingress week7-ingress -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}') # Send request with Host Header curl -H "Host: week7.local" http://$INGRESS_IP
    ✅ Success Criteria

    You should see the Nginx welcome page. This proves the Ingress Controller received the request, checked the Host header, matched it to your rule, and forwarded traffic to the backend Service.

    Lab 3: Implementing a "Default Deny" Firewall

    Objective: Lock down the namespace so pods cannot communicate unless explicitly allowed. This is a critical security practice (Zero Trust).

    ⚠️ CNI Requirement

    This lab requires a CNI that supports Network Policies (Calico, Cilium, Antrea). Minikube's default CNI (kicbase) often does NOT support this. If you are using standard Minikube, these policies might create successfully but have no effect. To verify support, start minikube with --cni=calico.

    Verify Connectivity (The "Before" State):

    Using your backend-svc from Lab 1.

    # Run a hacker pod kubectl run hacker --rm -it --image=alpine -- sh # Install curl apk add curl # Access backend (Should Succeed) curl --connect-timeout 2 http://backend-svc exit
    Apply "Default Deny" Policy:

    Block ALL ingress traffic to all pods in the namespace.

    apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: NetworkPolicy metadata: name: default-deny-all spec: podSelector: {} # Selects all pods policyTypes: - Ingress
    kubectl apply -f default-deny.yaml
    Verify Blocking:

    Try the hacker pod again.

    kubectl run hacker --rm -it --image=alpine -- sh apk add curl # Should TIMEOUT or Fail curl --connect-timeout 2 http://backend-svc
    Allow Specific Access:

    Allow only the Ingress Controller to talk to the backend.

    apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: NetworkPolicy metadata: name: allow-ingress-only spec: podSelector: matchLabels: app: backend # Only applies to backend pods ingress: - from: - namespaceSelector: matchLabels: kubernetes.io/metadata.name: ingress-nginx # Assuming controller is here

    Note: Selector labels depend on your specific Ingress Controller deployment labels.

    Lab 4: Manual Storage Provisioning

    Objective: Understand the relationship between persistent hardware (PV) and storage requests (PVC) by manually creating both.

    Create the PersistentVolume (PV):

    This represents the physical disk. We use hostPath for simulation.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: PersistentVolume metadata: name: task-pv-volume labels: type: local spec: storageClassName: manual capacity: storage: 1Gi accessModes: - ReadWriteOnce hostPath: path: "/mnt/data"
    kubectl apply -f pv.yaml
    Create the PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC):

    This is the "ticket" requesting storage.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: PersistentVolumeClaim metadata: name: task-pv-claim spec: storageClassName: manual accessModes: - ReadWriteOnce resources: requests: storage: 500Mi
    kubectl apply -f pvc.yaml
    Verify Binding:
    kubectl get pv task-pv-volume kubectl get pvc task-pv-claim

    Status must be Bound. If it stays Available or Pending, check that storageClassName matches exactly.

    Mount to Pod:
    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: task-pv-pod spec: containers: - name: task-pv-container image: nginx volumeMounts: - mountPath: "/usr/share/nginx/html" name: task-pv-storage volumes: - name: task-pv-storage persistentVolumeClaim: claimName: task-pv-claim
    kubectl apply -f pod.yaml
    Test Persistence:
    # Write data kubectl exec task-pv-pod -- sh -c "echo 'Hello Persisted World' > /usr/share/nginx/html/index.html" # Delete Pod kubectl delete pod task-pv-pod # Recreate Pod (apply yaml again) kubectl apply -f pod.yaml # Verify data exists kubectl exec task-pv-pod -- cat /usr/share/nginx/html/index.html

    Lab 5: Dynamic Provisioning (The "Easy Button")

    Objective: Use a StorageClass to automatically provision storage without manually creating PVs. This is how 99% of cloud storage is handled.

    Check Default StorageClass:
    kubectl get sc

    Note the class marked (default) (e.g., standard, gp2, hostpath).

    Create a PVC (No PV!):

    Create dynamic-pvc.yaml. Do not specify storageClassName—it will use the default.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: PersistentVolumeClaim metadata: name: dynamic-claim spec: accessModes: - ReadWriteOnce resources: requests: storage: 1Gi
    kubectl apply -f dynamic-pvc.yaml
    Observe the Magic:
    kubectl get pvc dynamic-claim kubectl get pv
    ✨ Automation

    You should see the PVC is Bound.
    You should also see a new PV named pvc-xxxx-xxxx... that was automatically created by the Storage Provisioner. You didn't have to define the physical volume yourself!

    Lab Solutions & Troubleshooting Guide

    This section provides the "answer key" for the networking and storage labs. It dissects the results, explains the architectural reasons behind the behaviors you observed, and helps you troubleshoot common infrastructure issues like CNI failures and pending storage claims.

    Lab 1 Solution: DNS & Service Discovery

    DNS Resolution Analysis

    Step: `dig backend-svc...`

    ; <<>> DiG 9.16.1-Ubuntu <<>> backend-svc.week7.svc.cluster.local +short ;; global options: +cmd 10.96.134.55

    Architectural Insight: The IP returned (10.96.x.x) is the Service VIP (Virtual IP). It is stable and will not change even if all backend pods die and are replaced. This VIP is virtual—it doesn't exist on any interface. Kube-proxy intercepts traffic destined for this IP and NATs it to a healthy Pod IP.

    Connectivity Verification

    Running curl http://backend-svc worked because the container's /etc/resolv.conf points to the CoreDNS server (usually 10.96.0.10). CoreDNS resolves the service name to the ClusterIP.

    Lab 2 Solution: Ingress Traffic

    The Host Header Mechanism

    The command curl -H "Host: week7.local" http://$INGRESS_IP works because:

    • The HTTP request arrives at the Ingress Controller (Nginx).
    • Nginx reads the Host: week7.local header.
    • It checks its internal configuration (generated from your YAML).
    • It finds a match and proxies the traffic to the backend-svc.
    ❌ Common Error: 404 Not Found

    If you access the Ingress IP directly without the Host header (or hosts file entry), Nginx returns a 404. This is "Default Backend" behavior. It means "I received your request, but I have no rule for this domain/IP."

    Lab 3 Solution: Network Policies

    Verification of Blocking

    If curl --connect-timeout 2 http://backend-svc timed out, your policy worked! The traffic was dropped silently by the CNI plugin (iptables drop rule).

    ⚠️ "It didn't block anything!"

    If you could still access the service after applying the "Default Deny" policy, your cluster is likely running a CNI that does not support Network Policies (e.g., standard Flannel or Minikube's default bridge). Network Policies are an API specification; if the underlying network driver doesn't implement them, they are silently ignored.

    Allowing Ingress

    The policy allow-ingress-only explicitly whitelists traffic from the Ingress Controller. This creates a "Zero Trust" zone where only the load balancer can talk to the backend, preventing unauthorized lateral movement from compromised pods.

    Lab 4 Solution: Manual Storage

    PV/PVC Binding

    Step: Verify Binding

    NAME STATUS VOLUME CAPACITY ACCESS MODES STORAGECLASS task-pv-claim Bound task-pv-volume 1Gi RWO manual

    Analysis: The status Bound confirms the handshake. The PVC requested "1Gi, RWO, class 'manual'". Kubernetes found a PV that satisfied these requirements ("1Gi, RWO, class 'manual'") and linked them.

    Persistence Test

    When you deleted the Pod, the PVC and PV remained. When you recreated the Pod, it requested the same PVC (by name). The Kubelet mounted the same host path (/mnt/data), so the index.html file was instantly available to the new container.

    Lab 5 Solution: Dynamic Provisioning

    The "Easy Button"

    In Lab 4, you wrote ~30 lines of YAML for a PV and PVC. In Lab 5, you wrote ~10 lines for just a PVC.

    NAME STATUS VOLUME CAPACITY ACCESS MODES STORAGECLASS dynamic-claim Bound pvc-8d7f9a2e-1b3c-4d5e-9f0a-1a2b3c4d5e6f 1Gi RWO standard

    What happened?
    1. You created a PVC asking for 1Gi from the "default" StorageClass.
    2. The Provisioner (e.g., `k8s.io/minikube-hostpath` or `kubernetes.io/aws-ebs`) saw the Pending claim.
    3. It provisioned the actual storage resource (a folder or EBS volume).
    4. It automatically created a PV object representing that resource and bound it to your PVC.

    Practice Questions

    These questions test your grasp of the complex Kubernetes networking and storage models.

    What is the "Flat Network" requirement in Kubernetes?
    A. All pods must share the same IP address
    B. Every pod must be able to communicate with every other pod without NAT
    C. All pods must run on the same node
    D. Network policies are disabled by default
    Answer: B. Kubernetes mandates that pods can talk to each other directly across nodes without Network Address Translation. This gives every pod a unique, routable IP within the cluster.
    Which Service type makes an application accessible on a static port on every Node's IP address?
    A. ClusterIP
    B. NodePort
    C. LoadBalancer
    D. ExternalName
    Answer: B. NodePort opens a specific port (e.g., 30007) on every node in the cluster. Traffic hitting any node on that port is forwarded to the Service.
    You create a Network Policy that selects `role: db` pods but defines NO ingress rules. What is the result?
    A. All traffic is allowed (default allow)
    B. All ingress traffic to those pods is blocked (default deny)
    C. Only traffic from the same namespace is allowed
    D. The policy is invalid
    Answer: B. Selecting pods with a Network Policy switches them from "Default Allow" to "Default Deny." If no allow rules are specified, all incoming traffic is dropped.
    What does a StorageClass primarily automate?
    A. Backups of data
    B. Dynamic provisioning of PersistentVolumes (PVs)
    C. Deletion of unused data
    D. Binding of PVCs to Pods
    Answer: B. A StorageClass defines a "provisioner" (like AWS EBS). When a user creates a PVC referencing the class, the provisioner automatically creates the storage volume and the corresponding PV object.
    Which access mode allows a volume to be mounted by multiple nodes for read-write access simultaneously?
    A. ReadWriteOnce (RWO)
    B. ReadWriteMany (RWX)
    C. ReadOnlyMany (ROX)
    D. WriteOnceReadMany (WORM)
    Answer: B. RWX allows multiple nodes to write to the volume at the same time. This typically requires a file-based storage backend like NFS, AWS EFS, or Azure Files. Block storage (EBS) usually creates RWO volumes.
    Why does Ingress operate at "Layer 7"?
    A. It uses port 7
    B. It makes routing decisions based on HTTP content (Host headers, Paths)
    C. It routes based on MAC addresses
    D. It routes based on IP addresses only
    Answer: B. Layer 7 is the Application layer. Ingress inspects the HTTP protocol data (Host header: `api.example.com`, Path: `/users`) to decide which Service receives the request.
    If a Pod tries to access a Service using the DNS name my-service, which component answers the DNS query?
    A. etcd
    B. kube-apiserver
    C. CoreDNS (or kube-dns)
    D. The upstream DNS server (8.8.8.8)
    Answer: C. CoreDNS is the internal DNS server for the cluster. It watches the API for new Services and creates DNS records (A records) for them automatically.
    What is a "Headless Service"?
    A. A service without a selector
    B. A service with `clusterIP: None` that returns Pod IPs directly
    C. A service that is down
    D. A service used only for Ingress
    Answer: B. A Headless Service doesn't provide load balancing or a single VIP. Instead, DNS lookups return the list of IPs of all backing Pods. This is crucial for stateful apps (databases) where you need to talk to a specific instance (e.g., the primary).
    You delete a PVC, but the underlying PV persists and its status becomes "Released". Why wasn't the data deleted?
    A. The StorageClass failed
    B. The PV's ReclaimPolicy is set to `Retain`
    C. The volume is still mounted
    D. Kubernetes never deletes data
    Answer: B. The ReclaimPolicy determines what happens when a PVC is deleted. `Delete` (default for cloud) wipes the volume. `Retain` keeps the volume and data, requiring manual cleanup or recovery.
    Which component is responsible for setting up the networking routes on each Node?
    A. kube-scheduler
    B. CoreDNS
    C. The CNI Plugin (e.g., Calico, Flannel)
    D. The Container Runtime
    Answer: C. The Container Network Interface (CNI) plugin is responsible for allocating IP addresses to pods and configuring the network bridges/routes on the host to ensure connectivity.

    Week-Ending Assignment: The "Fortified CMS" Project

    🎯 Assignment Objective

    You have learned the individual components of networking and storage. Now, you must combine them to build a Production-Grade Content Management System.

    Your mission: Deploy a highly secure, persistent blogging platform (using Ghost or WordPress). Unlike previous weeks, this deployment requires Zero Trust Networking (Network Policies) and Persistent Storage that survives pod failures. You will prove that you can route external traffic in (Ingress), route internal traffic securely (Policies), and save data permanently (PVCs).

    Architecture Overview

    The stack consists of a frontend application and a backend database, secured by a network firewall.

    🗄️

    1. The Database (MySQL)

    Storage: Must use a PersistentVolumeClaim (1Gi) mapped to /var/lib/mysql.
    Network: ClusterIP Service (Internal only).
    Security: Must accept traffic only from the Frontend pods.

    📝

    2. The App (Ghost CMS)

    Config: Connects to DB via Service DNS name.
    Network: ClusterIP Service.
    Public Access: Exposed via Ingress Controller on cms.local.

    🔒

    3. The Firewall

    Policy: A "Default Deny" policy for the namespace, plus specific "Allow" rules for Ingress->App and App->DB traffic.

    Technical Requirements

    1. Storage Persistence

    Create a PersistentVolumeClaim named mysql-pvc.

    • Size: 1Gi
    • Access Mode: ReadWriteOnce
    • StorageClass: default (or standard)

    2. Database Layer

    Deploy MySQL (use image mysql:8.0).

    • Mount the PVC to /var/lib/mysql.
    • Define MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD via a Secret.
    • Define MYSQL_DATABASE as ghost_db.
    • Expose port 3306 via a ClusterIP Service named mysql.

    3. Application Layer

    Deploy Ghost (use image ghost:5-alpine).

    • Configure Env Vars to connect to the MySQL service (refer to Ghost Docker Hub docs for env var names: database__client, database__connection__host, etc.).
    • Create an Ingress resource routing cms.local to the Ghost service port 2368.

    4. Network Security

    Implement NetworkPolicies:

    • Policy 1: Deny All Ingress traffic in the namespace.
    • Policy 2: Allow traffic from Ingress Controller to Ghost.
    • Policy 3: Allow traffic from Ghost Pods to MySQL Pods on port 3306.

    Step-by-Step Implementation Guide

    Phase 1: Setup

    Create a namespace cms-prod. Create a Secret db-creds storing the database password.

    Phase 2: Storage & Database

    Create the PVC. Create the MySQL Deployment using that PVC. Create the MySQL Service.
    Checkpoint: Run a temporary pod and check if you can resolve mysql.cms-prod.svc.cluster.local.

    Phase 3: The Frontend

    Create the Ghost Deployment and Service. Ensure the environment variables point to the mysql service hostname.
    Checkpoint: Check Ghost logs (`kubectl logs`) to confirm it connected to the DB and migrated tables.

    Phase 4: Ingress

    Create the Ingress routing cms.local to the Ghost service. Update your hosts file.
    Checkpoint: Visit http://cms.local in your browser. You should see the Ghost setup page.

    Phase 5: Lockdown (The Firewall)

    Apply the Network Policies.
    Validation: Run a "hacker" pod in the same namespace. Try to `curl` the MySQL IP. It should timeout (blocked). Try to `curl` the Ghost IP. It should timeout (blocked). Only the Ingress and Ghost-to-DB traffic should flow.

    📋 Student Submission Checklist

    Before submitting your assignment, verify you have completed all items:

    Teacher's Solution Guide

    Use this reference to grade submissions. These manifests represent the "Golden Path" solution. Pay close attention to the NetworkPolicy selectors.

    Solution: Fortified CMS Manifests

    1. Storage & Secret

    # 01-persistence.yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Secret metadata: name: db-creds namespace: cms-prod type: Opaque data: password: cGFzc3dvcmQxMjM= # "password123" --- apiVersion: v1 kind: PersistentVolumeClaim metadata: name: mysql-pvc namespace: cms-prod spec: accessModes: - ReadWriteOnce resources: requests: storage: 1Gi

    2. Database (MySQL)

    # 02-mysql.yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: mysql namespace: cms-prod labels: app: mysql spec: selector: matchLabels: app: mysql template: metadata: labels: app: mysql spec: containers: - name: mysql image: mysql:8.0 env: - name: MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD valueFrom: secretKeyRef: name: db-creds key: password - name: MYSQL_DATABASE value: ghost_db volumeMounts: - name: mysql-storage mountPath: /var/lib/mysql volumes: - name: mysql-storage persistentVolumeClaim: claimName: mysql-pvc --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: mysql namespace: cms-prod spec: ports: - port: 3306 selector: app: mysql

    3. Application (Ghost)

    # 03-ghost.yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: ghost namespace: cms-prod labels: app: ghost spec: selector: matchLabels: app: ghost template: metadata: labels: app: ghost spec: containers: - name: ghost image: ghost:5-alpine env: - name: database__client value: mysql - name: database__connection__host value: mysql # Service Discovery DNS - name: database__connection__user value: root - name: database__connection__password valueFrom: secretKeyRef: name: db-creds key: password - name: database__connection__database value: ghost_db - name: url value: http://cms.local ports: - containerPort: 2368 --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: ghost namespace: cms-prod spec: ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 2368 selector: app: ghost

    4. Ingress & Security

    # 04-access.yaml apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: Ingress metadata: name: ghost-ingress namespace: cms-prod spec: ingressClassName: nginx rules: - host: cms.local http: paths: - path: / pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: ghost port: number: 80 --- # 05-policies.yaml apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: NetworkPolicy metadata: name: default-deny namespace: cms-prod spec: podSelector: {} policyTypes: - Ingress --- apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: NetworkPolicy metadata: name: allow-ghost-to-db namespace: cms-prod spec: podSelector: matchLabels: app: mysql ingress: - from: - podSelector: matchLabels: app: ghost ports: - protocol: TCP port: 3306 --- apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: NetworkPolicy metadata: name: allow-ingress-to-ghost namespace: cms-prod spec: podSelector: matchLabels: app: ghost ingress: - from: # Allow traffic from all sources (the Internet/Ingress Controller) # Refine this selector in production to match Ingress Controller labels only - namespaceSelector: {} ports: - protocol: TCP port: 2368
    🚀 Week 7 Complete

    Congratulations! You have successfully built a "Fortified" Kubernetes application. You have moved beyond simple deployments to master the critical infrastructure components: Networking (DNS, Service Discovery, Ingress), Storage (PVC/PV), and Security (Network Policies).

    Next Week (Week 8): We will explore Advanced Kubernetes Patterns, including Autoscaling (HPA/VPA), Probes (Liveness/Readiness), and Role-Based Access Control (RBAC).

    Mentorship Review for Week 7

    Mentorship students: your Week 7 work (Kubernetes Networking & Storage) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    8

    Advanced Kubernetes Patterns

    Autoscaling, Health Probes, Scheduling, and Access Control

    Welcome to Week 8. By now, you can deploy applications, expose them to the internet, and persist their data. But is your cluster "Production Ready"? A production cluster isn't just about running containers; it's about resilience under load, self-healing capabilities, and security.

    This week, we implement the patterns that make Kubernetes truly powerful. We will configure Liveness and Readiness Probes so your applications only receive traffic when they are actually ready. We will implement Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (HPA) to automatically add more pods during traffic spikes. We will dive into Scheduling nuances (Taints, Tolerations, Affinity) to control exactly where workloads run. Finally, we will lock down the cluster using RBAC (Role-Based Access Control), moving away from the "admin-has-all-power" model to the principle of least privilege.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Advanced Kubernetes Patterns: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Mechanisms: Define the three types of probes (Liveness, Readiness, Startup), the components of RBAC (Role, RoleBinding, ServiceAccount), and the metric sources for Autoscaling.

    Understand

    Explain Logic: Explain how HPA calculates replica counts based on CPU utilization. Describe the difference between Taints (repelling pods) and Affinity (attracting pods).

    Apply

    Execute Configuration: Implement robust health checks to prevent broken deploys. Configure HPA to scale a web application from 1 to 10 pods under load. Create a ServiceAccount with restricted permissions.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Constraints: Analyze why a Pod is stuck in "Pending" due to Taints or insufficient resources. Debug 502 errors caused by failing Readiness probes.

    Evaluate

    Assess Strategies: Evaluate the trade-offs between Vertical Scaling (VPA) and Horizontal Scaling (HPA). Determine the appropriate QoS class (Guaranteed vs. BestEffort) for critical workloads.

    Create

    Build Policies: Author a complete RBAC policy that grants a specific user "read-only" access to a specific namespace while denying access to Secrets.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Advanced features like Autoscaling rely on specific cluster addons (Metrics Server) that are not installed by default. Budget approximately 3-4 hours.

    1Install the Metrics Server

    Kubernetes cannot scale your applications if it doesn't know how much CPU/RAM they are using. The Metrics Server is the component that collects these stats. It is required for the HPA lab.

    For Minikube Users:

    minikube addons enable metrics-server

    For Kind Users:

    kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml # Patch for Kind (allow insecure TLS for local testing) kubectl patch deployment metrics-server -n kube-system --type='json' -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/args/-", "value": "--kubelet-insecure-tls"}]'

    Verification: Run kubectl top nodes. If you see CPU/Memory usage numbers, you are ready. If you see "metrics API not available," troubleshoot before proceeding.

    2Resource Limits Refresher

    Autoscaling and Scheduling depend entirely on Resource Requests and Limits.

    Task: Research and write a concise explanation of:

    • Request: What does the Scheduler do with this number? (Hint: Scheduling / Bin-packing).
    • Limit: What does the Container Runtime do with this number? (Hint: Throttling / OOMKilled).
    • QoS Classes: What are Guaranteed, Burstable, and BestEffort classes? Which one gets evicted first when the node runs out of RAM?
    💡 Critical Concept

    If you don't set Requests, the HPA cannot calculate utilization percentages, and Autoscaling will fail.

    3The "Thundering Herd" Problem

    Imagine you have a Java application that takes 45 seconds to start up (load classes, connect to DB).

    Thought Experiment: You deploy an update. Kubernetes kills the old pod and starts the new one immediately. The new container process starts (PID 1 exists), so Kubernetes sends traffic to it instantly.

    Question: What happens to the user requests sent during those first 45 seconds? How does a Readiness Probe solve this? Write down your hypothesis.

    4RBAC Roleplay

    Scenario: You are the Lead DevOps Engineer. You hired a "Junior Developer" named Alice.

    Task: List the permissions Alice needs. Does she need to delete Nodes? (No). Does she need to read Secrets in the production namespace? (Probably not). Does she need to view Pod logs in Dev? (Yes).

    Sketch a permission matrix:

    Action Namespace Alice's Access
    Get/List Pods All Allow
    Read Secrets Production Deny
    Delete Deployments Dev Allow

    5Load Generator Tool

    To test Autoscaling, we need to stress our cluster.

    Task: We will rely on a simple busybox loop in the labs, but for robust testing, install Hey (a modern HTTP load generator replacement for Apache Bench).

    # Mac/Linux (Homebrew) brew install hey # Or download binary from https://github.com/rakyll/hey

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    In the previous weeks, we focused on "Day 1" operations: getting applications deployed, networked, and persisted. Week 8 focuses on "Day 2" operations: Reliability, Scalability, and Security.

    A cluster isn't production-ready until it can handle traffic spikes without crashing (Autoscaling), detect and fix zombie processes (Probes), intelligently place workloads on appropriate hardware (Scheduling), and prevent unauthorized access (RBAC).

    8.1 Application Health: Probes

    By default, Kubernetes assumes an application is "healthy" as long as the container process (PID 1) is running. However, a Java app might be running but stuck in a deadlock, or a Node.js app might be running but disconnected from the database.

    Kubernetes solves this with Probes. Kubelet performs these diagnostic checks periodically.

    The Three Guardians

    1. Liveness Probe (The Defibrillator)

    Question: "Are you alive?"
    Action if Failed: Restart the container.
    Use Case: Deadlocks, infinite loops, frozen applications that cannot recover without a restart.

    2. Readiness Probe (The Traffic Cop)

    Question: "Are you ready to serve traffic?"
    Action if Failed: Remove the Pod's IP from the Service endpoints (stop sending traffic). Do not restart.
    Use Case: App is initializing, loading large cache files, or waiting for a database connection.

    3. Startup Probe (The Patient Waiter)

    Question: "Have you finished starting up?"
    Action: Disables Liveness/Readiness checks until this succeeds.
    Use Case: Legacy monoliths that take 5 minutes to boot. Without this, the Liveness probe would kill the app before it ever finished starting.

    # Example: Java App Configuration apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod spec: containers: - name: java-app image: my-java-app:v1 # 1. Wait for slow startup startupProbe: httpGet: path: /health port: 8080 failureThreshold: 30 periodSeconds: 10 # Allow 30 * 10s = 5 mins to boot # 2. Check if ready for traffic readinessProbe: httpGet: path: /ready port: 8080 initialDelaySeconds: 5 periodSeconds: 5 # 3. Check for deadlocks livenessProbe: httpGet: path: /health port: 8080 initialDelaySeconds: 10 periodSeconds: 20
    ⚠️ The Liveness Trap

    Do not make your Liveness probe depend on external services (like the Database). If the DB goes down, the Liveness probe fails, and K8s restarts your Web App. Restarting the Web App won't fix the DB. This creates a cascading failure loop. Liveness should only check internal app state. Readiness can check external dependencies.

    8.2 Autoscaling: HPA

    Static replica counts (replicas: 3) are inefficient. Traffic fluctuates. The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) automatically updates a workload resource (Deployment or StatefulSet) to scale the number of Pods up or down based on observed CPU utilization or custom metrics.

    📈 The HPA Algorithm

    The HPA controller loop runs every 15 seconds (default). It queries the Metrics Server.

    DesiredReplicas = ceil[CurrentReplicas * ( CurrentMetricValue / DesiredMetricValue )]

    Example: You have 2 pods. Target CPU is 50%. Current CPU is 100%.
    2 * (100 / 50) = 4 Replicas.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v2 kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler metadata: name: php-apache spec: scaleTargetRef: apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment name: php-apache minReplicas: 1 maxReplicas: 10 metrics: - type: Resource resource: name: cpu target: type: Utilization averageUtilization: 50
    💡 Cluster Autoscaler Connection

    HPA scales Pods. If you scale from 10 to 100 pods, your Nodes might run out of capacity. The Cluster Autoscaler (managed by cloud providers like AWS/GCP) watches for "Pending" pods due to resource shortage and provisions new physical Nodes (EC2 instances).

    8.3 Advanced Scheduling: Placing Workloads

    The Scheduler's default behavior is "bin packing"—put the pod on the least busy node. Sometimes, you need more control.

    1. Taints and Tolerations (The Repellent)

    Taints are applied to Nodes. They repel pods.
    Tolerations are applied to Pods. They allow (but do not require) a pod to schedule onto a tainted node.

    Use Case: You have expensive GPU nodes. You Taint them gpu=true:NoSchedule so regular web apps don't waste the space. Only AI workloads with the matching Toleration can land there.

    2. Node Affinity (The Magnet)

    Affinity allows you to constrain which nodes your pod can be scheduled on based on node labels.

    • requiredDuringScheduling: Hard rule. If no node matches, Pod stays Pending. (e.g., "Must run on Intel CPU").
    • preferredDuringScheduling: Soft rule. "Try to run on Zone A, but Zone B is okay if A is full."

    3. Pod Affinity/Anti-Affinity

    Rules based on other pods running on the node.

    • Anti-Affinity: "Do not run this Web Pod on a node that already has a Web Pod." (Spreads replicas for High Availability).
    • Affinity: "Run this Cache Pod on the same node as the Web Pod." (Reduces network latency).

    8.4 Managing Resources & QoS

    When you define resources, you assign a Quality of Service (QoS) class to your pod. This determines who gets killed first when the node runs out of memory (OOM).

    QoS Classes

    Class Definition OOM Kill Priority
    Guaranteed Requests == Limits (for both CPU & RAM) Last to be killed (Gold Class)
    Burstable Requests < Limits Medium priority
    BestEffort No Requests or Limits set First to be killed (Trash Class)

    8.5 Security: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

    Kubernetes is an API. RBAC controls who can hit that API and what they can do. It follows a "Default Deny" model.

    🛡️ The RBAC Triad

    1. Subject (Who): User (Human), Group (Devs), or ServiceAccount (Application/Bot).
    2. Resource (What): Pods, Services, Deployments, Secrets.
    3. Verb (Action): Get, List, Watch, Create, Update, Delete.

    Binding it together:

    • Role: Defines permissions within a specific Namespace (e.g., "Can read pods in 'dev'").
    • RoleBinding: Connects a Subject to a Role (e.g., "User 'Alice' gets the 'read-pods' Role in 'dev'").
    • ClusterRole / ClusterRoleBinding: Same as above, but cluster-wide (e.g., "Can read Nodes" - nodes are not namespaced).
    # 1. Define the Role apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 kind: Role metadata: namespace: dev name: pod-reader rules: - apiGroups: [""] resources: ["pods", "pods/log"] verbs: ["get", "watch", "list"] --- # 2. Bind the Role to a User apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 kind: RoleBinding metadata: name: read-pods namespace: dev subjects: - kind: User name: alice apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io roleRef: kind: Role name: pod-reader apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

    Hands-On Labs

    In these labs, you will push Kubernetes to its limits. You will intentionally deploy slow applications to test Probes, flood your cluster with traffic to trigger Autoscaling, restrict scheduling with Taints, and lock down permissions with RBAC.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    Metrics Server: Labs 2 and 5 require the Metrics Server to be running. Verify with kubectl top nodes.
    Namespace: Create a fresh namespace: kubectl create ns week8 and switch to it.

    Lab 1: Configuring Liveness & Readiness Probes

    Objective: Deploy an application that takes time to start up and occasionally "freezes." Configure probes to handle startup gracefully and restart the container when it freezes.

    Create the Manifest:

    Create probes.yaml. We simulate a "freeze" by creating a file /tmp/healthy and deleting it after 30 seconds.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: liveness-demo spec: containers: - name: liveness image: busybox args: - /bin/sh - -c - touch /tmp/healthy; sleep 30; rm -f /tmp/healthy; sleep 600 # 1. Liveness Probe (Checks file existence) livenessProbe: exec: command: - cat - /tmp/healthy initialDelaySeconds: 5 periodSeconds: 5 # 2. Readiness Probe (Simulates startup check) readinessProbe: exec: command: - cat - /tmp/healthy initialDelaySeconds: 2 periodSeconds: 5
    Apply and Watch:
    kubectl apply -f probes.yaml # Watch the status change kubectl get pod liveness-demo -w
    Analyze the Lifecycle:

    0-30s: The file exists. Probes succeed. Pod is Running and Ready (1/1).
    30s: The command deletes the file.
    35s+ : Liveness probe fails. Kubernetes kills the container.
    Observation: You will see RESTARTS count increment to 1.

    Verify Events:
    kubectl describe pod liveness-demo
    Warning Unhealthy ... Liveness probe failed: cat: can't open '/tmp/healthy': No such file or directory Normal Killing ... Container liveness failed liveness probe, will be restarted

    Lab 2: Autoscaling Under Load

    Objective: Deploy a CPU-intensive PHP application and configure HPA to scale it from 1 to 5 replicas when load increases.

    Deploy the Target App:

    We use a custom image designed to burn CPU on every request.

    apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: php-apache spec: selector: matchLabels: run: php-apache replicas: 1 template: metadata: labels: run: php-apache spec: containers: - name: php-apache image: registry.k8s.io/hpa-example ports: - containerPort: 80 resources: limits: cpu: 500m requests: cpu: 200m --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: php-apache spec: ports: - port: 80 selector: run: php-apache
    kubectl apply -f php-apache.yaml
    Create the HPA:
    # Autoscale when CPU > 50% kubectl autoscale deployment php-apache --cpu-percent=50 --min=1 --max=5 # Verify HPA state kubectl get hpa
    NAME REFERENCE TARGETS MINPODS MAXPODS REPLICAS AGE php-apache Deployment/php-apache 0%/50% 1 5 1 15s
    Generate Load:

    Run a separate pod acting as a load generator loop.

    kubectl run -it --rm load-generator --image=busybox -- /bin/sh # Inside the pod, run this infinite loop: while true; do wget -q -O- http://php-apache; done
    Observe Scaling:

    Open a new terminal window to watch the cluster.

    kubectl get hpa -w

    Within 1-2 minutes, you will see TARGETS spike (e.g., 250%/50%) and REPLICAS increase to 5.

    Cooldown:

    Stop the load generator (Ctrl+C). Watch the HPA. After a cooldown period (default 5 minutes), it will scale back down to 1.

    Lab 3: Controlling Scheduler Placement

    Objective: Simulate a "Dedicated GPU" node by Tainting it, then deploy a Pod that tolerates that taint.

    Taint the Node:

    We will apply a Taint to your single Minikube/Kind node. This usually prevents new pods from scheduling unless they tolerate it.

    # Get node name kubectl get nodes # Apply taint (replace 'minikube' with your node name) kubectl taint nodes minikube specialized=true:NoSchedule
    Attempt Normal Deployment:
    kubectl run normal-pod --image=nginx # Check status kubectl get pod normal-pod
    NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE normal-pod 0/1 Pending 0 10s

    The pod is Pending. kubectl describe pod normal-pod will show "1 node(s) had untolerated taint {specialized: true}".

    Deploy Tolerant Pod:

    Create tolerant.yaml with the matching toleration.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: tolerant-pod spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx tolerations: - key: "specialized" operator: "Equal" value: "true" effect: "NoSchedule"
    kubectl apply -f tolerant.yaml kubectl get pod tolerant-pod

    This pod should transition to Running.

    Cleanup (Important!):

    Remove the taint so your cluster returns to normal.

    # The minus sign removes the taint kubectl taint nodes minikube specialized- # Delete the pending pod kubectl delete pod normal-pod

    Lab 4: Creating a Restricted "View-Only" User
