    Objective: Create a ServiceAccount, bind it to a Role that allows viewing Pods but denies deleting them, and verify the permissions using impersonation.

    Create ServiceAccount:
    kubectl create serviceaccount intern
    Define the Role:

    Create role.yaml allowing read-only access to pods.

    apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 kind: Role metadata: namespace: week8 name: pod-viewer rules: - apiGroups: [""] resources: ["pods", "pods/log"] verbs: ["get", "watch", "list"]
    kubectl apply -f role.yaml
    Bind Role to Account:
    kubectl create rolebinding intern-binding \ --role=pod-viewer \ --serviceaccount=week8:intern
    Verify Permissions (Can-I):

    Use the auth can-i command to check permissions for the 'intern' account.

    # Can intern list pods? kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=system:serviceaccount:week8:intern # Output: yes # Can intern delete pods? kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as=system:serviceaccount:week8:intern # Output: no # Can intern read secrets? kubectl auth can-i get secrets --as=system:serviceaccount:week8:intern # Output: no
    Test Real Access:

    Try to delete the pod from Lab 3 as the intern.

    kubectl delete pod tolerant-pod --as=system:serviceaccount:week8:intern
    Error from server (Forbidden): pods "tolerant-pod" is forbidden: User ... cannot delete resource "pods" in API group "" in the namespace "week8"

    Lab 5: Managing Resources & OOMKilled

    Objective: Constrain a Pod's memory usage and intentionally trigger an Out Of Memory (OOM) Kill to understand how Kubernetes handles resource exhaustion.

    Create a Memory-Hungry Pod:

    Create oom-pod.yaml. We will use the polinux/stress image to consume 150MB of RAM, but we will limit the container to 100MB.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: memory-demo spec: containers: - name: stress image: polinux/stress resources: limits: memory: "100Mi" requests: memory: "50Mi" command: ["stress"] args: ["--vm", "1", "--vm-bytes", "150M", "--vm-hang", "1"]
    Apply and Monitor:
    kubectl apply -f oom-pod.yaml # Watch status kubectl get pod memory-demo -w
    Observe the Crash:

    The pod status will change from Running to OOMKilled, and then to CrashLoopBackOff.

    NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS memory-demo 0/1 OOMKilled 1 (5s ago)
    Inspect the Cause:

    Use describe to see the exact reason.

    kubectl describe pod memory-demo
    State: Waiting Reason: CrashLoopBackOff Last State: Terminated Reason: OOMKilled Exit Code: 137
    💡 Exit Code 137

    Exit code 137 = 128 + 9 (SIGKILL). This means the Linux kernel sent a SIGKILL signal to the process because it tried to exceed the cgroup memory limit set by Kubernetes.

    Lab Solutions & Troubleshooting Guide

    This section provides the "answer key" for the advanced pattern labs. It dissects the behaviors you observed—why the pod restarted, why the autoscaler triggered, and why the scheduler refused to place your workload. Understanding these mechanics is what separates a Kubernetes user from an administrator.

    Lab 1 Solution: Probes & Health Checks

    Lifecycle Analysis

    Step: Watch Status Change

    NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE liveness-demo 1/1 Running 0 10s liveness-demo 0/1 Running 1 45s

    Architectural Insight:
    1. 0-30s: The file /tmp/healthy exists. Both Liveness and Readiness probes pass (exit code 0). The Pod is marked Ready.
    2. 30s: The command deletes the file.
    3. 35s: The Liveness probe runs (period=5s), executes cat /tmp/healthy, and receives exit code 1 (Fail).
    4. Reaction: The Kubelet sees the failure. It executes the restart policy. It kills the container and starts a new one. The RESTARTS counter increments.

    💡 Liveness vs. Readiness Failure

    If we had only configured a Readiness Probe, the pod would not have restarted. It would simply have transitioned to 0/1 Running (Not Ready), and the Service would have stopped sending traffic to it. Because we used a Liveness Probe, the remedy was a hard restart.

    Lab 2 Solution: Autoscaling (HPA)

    The Scaling Math

    When you ran the load generator, the CPU usage spiked.

    TARGETS: 250%/50%

    Calculation:
    DesiredReplicas = ceil[CurrentReplicas * ( CurrentMetric / DesiredMetric )]
    DesiredReplicas = ceil[1 * ( 250 / 50 )] = 5
    The HPA controller calculated that to bring the average load down to 50%, it needed 5 times the capacity.

    Troubleshooting HPA

    ❌ Common Error: TARGETS <unknown>/50%

    Symptom: HPA shows "unknown" for current usage.
    Cause 1: Metrics Server is not installed or running.
    Cause 2: The Deployment Pods do not have resources.requests.cpu defined. HPA cannot calculate a percentage without a request value.

    Lab 3 Solution: Taints & Tolerations

    Scheduler Logic

    Scenario 1: Normal Pod

    Warning FailedScheduling ... 0/1 nodes are available: 1 node(s) had untolerated taint {specialized: true}.

    The Scheduler filters nodes. It saw the node had a taint specialized=true:NoSchedule. It checked the Pod for a matching toleration. Found none. Result: Cannot schedule.

    Scenario 2: Tolerant Pod

    The tolerant pod had the "magic key" (the toleration) matching the taint. The Scheduler allowed the pod to land on the tainted node. Note: Tolerations allow scheduling, but they do not force it. To force scheduling, you would need Node Affinity.

    Lab 4 Solution: RBAC

    Permission Verification

    Step: Deletion Attempt

    Error from server (Forbidden): pods "tolerant-pod" is forbidden: User "system:serviceaccount:week8:intern" cannot delete resource "pods" in API group "" in the namespace "week8"

    Analysis: This error message is essentially an RBAC audit log.
    1. Who: system:serviceaccount:week8:intern
    2. Action: delete
    3. Resource: pods
    4. Result: No RoleBinding found that grants this specific combination. Access Denied.

    💡 The "Can-I" Tool

    kubectl auth can-i is the best way to debug permissions without actually switching users. You can impersonate any user or service account to verify your policies before locking yourself out.

    Lab 5 Solution: OOMKilled

    Resource Contention

    Step: Observe Crash

    Last State: Terminated Reason: OOMKilled Exit Code: 137

    The Mechanism:
    1. You set a limit of 100Mi.
    2. Kubernetes configured the Linux cgroup for that container to enforce a hard ceiling of 100MB RAM.
    3. The application (stress) requested 150MB.
    4. The Linux Kernel (OOM Killer) noticed the cgroup violation. It instantly killed the process to protect the rest of the node.
    5. Kubernetes saw the process die and reported OOMKilled.

    ⚠️ Requests vs. Limits

    Requests: Used for Scheduling. "Find me a node with 50MB free."
    Limits: Used for enforcement. "Kill me if I exceed 100MB."
    The gap between Request and Limit is "Burstable" capacity. If you set them equal, you get "Guaranteed" QoS.

    Practice Questions

    These questions mimic the Certified Kubernetes Application Developer (CKAD) exam format.

    Which probe should you configure to prevent a slow-starting application from being killed by the Liveness probe during boot?
    A. Readiness Probe
    B. Startup Probe
    C. Liveness Probe (with higher timeout)
    D. InitContainer
    Answer: B. The Startup Probe disables all other probes until it succeeds. This is specifically designed for legacy apps with long initialization times, preventing the Liveness probe from premature killing.
    You apply a Taint `env=prod:NoSchedule` to a Node. Which statement is true?
    A. All existing pods on the node are evicted immediately
    B. Only pods with the label `env=prod` can schedule there
    C. No new pods can schedule there unless they have a matching Toleration
    D. The node is disabled
    Answer: C. `NoSchedule` affects new pods only. Existing pods keep running. To evict existing pods, you would use `NoExecute`. Taints repel pods that lack tolerations.
    What is the prerequisite for Horizontal Pod Autoscaling based on CPU usage?
    A. The Cluster Autoscaler must be enabled
    B. Pods must use the Burstable QoS class
    C. The Metrics Server must be running and Pods must have CPU requests defined
    D. You must use a StatefulSet
    Answer: C. HPA relies on the Metrics API. Furthermore, HPA calculates utilization as `current / requested`. If `requests` are not defined in the Pod, the math is impossible (division by zero/null).
    You want to grant a user permission to list Pods, but only in the `dev` namespace. Which resource do you create?
    A. ClusterRoleBinding
    B. RoleBinding (referencing a Role)
    C. ServiceAccount
    D. SecurityContext
    Answer: B. A `RoleBinding` applies permissions within a specific namespace. A `ClusterRoleBinding` grants permissions cluster-wide (all namespaces).
    A container exits with code 137. What is the most likely cause?
    A. Syntax error in the application code
    B. OOMKilled (Out of Memory)
    C. Liveness probe failed
    D. Node failure
    Answer: B. 137 indicates the process received `SIGKILL` (signal 9) + 128. This is almost exclusively used by the OOM Killer when a container exceeds its memory limit.
    What happens if a Readiness Probe fails?
    A. The container restarts
    B. The Pod's IP is removed from the Service endpoints
    C. The Pod is deleted
    D. The Node is tainted
    Answer: B. Readiness checks if the app can handle traffic. Failure stops traffic flow (by removing it from the load balancer/service) but keeps the container running so it can potentially recover or process background tasks.
    Which QoS class is assigned to a Pod if it has NO requests or limits set?
    A. Guaranteed
    B. Burstable
    C. BestEffort
    D. LowPriority
    Answer: C. BestEffort pods are the first to be evicted when a Node runs out of resources.
    You want to ensure a Pod is scheduled onto a node with the label `disk=ssd`. Which feature should you use?
    A. Taints
    B. Node Affinity (or nodeSelector)
    C. Pod Affinity
    D. Topology Spread Constraints
    Answer: B. Node Affinity attracts pods to nodes with specific labels. Taints repel pods.
    Which component enforces RBAC policies?
    A. kube-apiserver
    B. etcd
    C. kube-controller-manager
    D. kubelet
    Answer: A. The API Server intercepts every request and passes it through the Authorization modules (RBAC, ABAC, Node) before executing it.
    Can a Pod have both a Liveness Probe and a Readiness Probe?
    A. Yes, they serve different purposes and run in parallel
    B. No, they conflict
    C. Only if they check the same endpoint
    D. Only in StatefulSets
    Answer: A. It is best practice to have both. Liveness handles "dead" apps (restart needed), Readiness handles "busy" apps (stop traffic needed).

    Week-Ending Assignment: Project "Overwatch" (Resilient Scaling)

    🎯 Assignment Objective

    Your "Day 2" skills are now required. A critical monitoring application called "Overwatch" has been failing in production. It crashes during traffic spikes, routes traffic to broken pods during startup, and runs on the wrong hardware nodes.

    Your mission: Re-architect the deployment manifest to be Production Ready. You must implement Autoscaling (HPA) to handle load, robust Probes to ensure zero-downtime updates, Scheduling rules to pin workloads to specific nodes, and RBAC to allow a junior developer to view logs without deleting deployments.

    Architecture Overview

    The application stack is simple, but the operational requirements are complex.

    📈

    1. The Dashboard (Frontend)

    Controller: Deployment
    Image: nginx:alpine
    Requirement: Must scale automatically based on CPU usage (HPA). Must not accept traffic until fully initialized (Readiness Probe).

    🧠

    2. The Processor (Backend)

    Controller: Deployment
    Image: busybox (simulating work)
    Requirement: Must run on "High Performance" nodes only (Taints/Tolerations). Must restart if deadlocked (Liveness Probe).

    👮

    3. The Auditor (RBAC)

    Subject: ServiceAccount auditor
    Role: Read-Only
    Requirement: Can list Pods and view Logs, but cannot Delete resources or view Secrets.

    Technical Requirements

    1. Namespace & Setup

    Create a namespace called overwatch-prod. All resources must be deployed here. Ensure Metrics Server is running.

    2. Frontend Scalability (HPA + Probes)

    • Create a Deployment dashboard using php-apache (or any cpu-stress image).
    • Configure Resources: Requests: 200m CPU, Limits: 500m CPU.
    • Configure Readiness Probe: Check path / on port 80. Initial delay 5s.
    • Configure HPA: Scale from 2 to 10 replicas when CPU > 50%.

    3. Backend Scheduling (Taints/Affinity)

    • Simulate a high-perf node by tainting your node: tier=high-perf:NoSchedule.
    • Create a Deployment processor.
    • Add a Toleration so it can bypass the taint.
    • Add Node Affinity (preferred) to attract it to nodes labeled tier=high-perf.
    • Configure Liveness Probe: Exec command cat /tmp/healthy.

    4. Access Control (RBAC)

    • Create ServiceAccount auditor-sa.
    • Create Role log-viewer allowing get, list, watch on pods and pods/log.
    • Create RoleBinding binding the SA to the Role.
    • Verify they cannot delete pods using auth can-i.

    Step-by-Step Implementation Guide

    Phase 1: Cluster Prep

    Create the namespace. Taint your node (if using Minikube single-node, this effectively tests tolerations immediately). Enable Metrics Server.

    Phase 2: The Frontend (HPA)

    Define the Deployment with resource requests (CRITICAL for HPA). Define the HPA.
    Validation: Use the load generator from Lab 2 to stress the service and screenshot the HPA status scaling up to >2 replicas.

    Phase 3: The Backend (Scheduling)

    Define the Deployment with Tolerations. Apply it.
    Validation: Without the toleration, the pod should be Pending. With it, it should be Running. Screenshot the kubectl describe pod showing successful scheduling.

    Phase 4: Security (RBAC)

    Create the RBAC manifests.
    Validation: Run kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as=system:serviceaccount:overwatch-prod:auditor-sa -n overwatch-prod. It must return "no".

    📋 Student Submission Checklist

    Before submitting your assignment, verify you have completed all items:

    Teacher's Solution Guide

    Use this reference to grade submissions. These manifests represent the "Golden Path" solution.

    Solution: Overwatch Manifests

    1. Frontend with HPA & Probes

    # 01-dashboard.yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: dashboard namespace: overwatch-prod spec: replicas: 2 selector: matchLabels: app: dashboard template: metadata: labels: app: dashboard spec: containers: - name: php-apache image: registry.k8s.io/hpa-example ports: - containerPort: 80 # CRITICAL: Requests required for HPA resources: limits: cpu: 500m requests: cpu: 200m # CRITICAL: Probes required for reliability readinessProbe: httpGet: path: / port: 80 initialDelaySeconds: 5 periodSeconds: 5 --- # 02-hpa.yaml apiVersion: autoscaling/v2 kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler metadata: name: dashboard-hpa namespace: overwatch-prod spec: scaleTargetRef: apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment name: dashboard minReplicas: 2 maxReplicas: 10 metrics: - type: Resource resource: name: cpu target: type: Utilization averageUtilization: 50

    2. Backend with Taints/Tolerations

    Note: Assumes node was tainted with kubectl taint nodes minikube tier=high-perf:NoSchedule

    # 03-processor.yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: processor namespace: overwatch-prod spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: processor template: metadata: labels: app: processor spec: # Bypass the taint tolerations: - key: "tier" operator: "Equal" value: "high-perf" effect: "NoSchedule" # (Optional) Prefer this node if possible affinity: nodeAffinity: preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution: - weight: 100 preference: matchExpressions: - key: tier operator: In values: - high-perf containers: - name: busybox image: busybox args: [/bin/sh, -c, 'touch /tmp/healthy; sleep 3600'] livenessProbe: exec: command: - cat - /tmp/healthy initialDelaySeconds: 5 periodSeconds: 5

    3. RBAC Policy

    # 04-rbac.yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: auditor-sa namespace: overwatch-prod --- apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 kind: Role metadata: name: log-viewer namespace: overwatch-prod rules: - apiGroups: [""] resources: ["pods", "pods/log"] verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"] --- apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 kind: RoleBinding metadata: name: auditor-binding namespace: overwatch-prod subjects: - kind: ServiceAccount name: auditor-sa namespace: overwatch-prod roleRef: kind: Role name: log-viewer apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

    4. Validation Commands

    # 1. Verify HPA kubectl get hpa -n overwatch-prod # Should show TARGETS % # 2. Verify Scheduling kubectl get pod -l app=processor -n overwatch-prod -o wide # Should be Running. If pending, check taint/toleration match. # 3. Verify RBAC kubectl auth can-i delete deployment --as=system:serviceaccount:overwatch-prod:auditor-sa -n overwatch-prod # Must return "no"
    🚀 Week 8 Complete

    Congratulations! You have transformed your basic Kubernetes knowledge into Operational Excellence. You can now build systems that scale automatically, heal themselves, utilize specific hardware, and enforce security policies.

    Next Week (Week 9): We stop writing YAML by hand. We introduce Helm, the Package Manager for Kubernetes, which allows you to template, version, and distribute your applications like a pro.

    Mentorship Review for Week 8

    Mentorship students: your Week 8 work (Advanced Kubernetes Patterns) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    9

    Helm & Kubernetes Package Management

    Templating, Packaging, and Managing Complex Releases

    Welcome to Week 9. Up to this point, you have been crafting Kubernetes manifests by hand—writing static YAML files for Deployments, Services, and ConfigMaps. While this is excellent for learning the API, it creates significant friction in real-world scenarios. How do you deploy the same application to Development (1 replica), Staging (2 replicas), and Production (10 replicas) without maintaining three nearly identical files? How do you share your complex application stack with other teams?

    Enter Helm, the Package Manager for Kubernetes. Helm allows you to define, install, and upgrade even the most complex Kubernetes application. It introduces the concept of Charts (packages) and Templates (dynamic configuration). By the end of this week, you will stop copying-and-pasting YAML and start building reusable, version-controlled infrastructure packages that can be deployed anywhere with a single command.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Helm & Kubernetes Package Management: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Core Concepts: Define the three pillars of Helm: Charts (packages), Config (values), and Releases (running instances). Identify the standard directory structure of a Helm chart.

    Understand

    Explain Mechanics: Explain how Helm merges the values.yaml file with templates to generate valid Kubernetes manifests. Describe the lifecycle of a Helm Release (Install → Upgrade → Rollback).

    Apply

    Execute Operations: Install applications from public repositories (e.g., Bitnami). Perform atomic upgrades and rollbacks of releases. Use helm lint and --dry-run to validate changes.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Failures: Troubleshoot template rendering errors. Analyze failed releases using helm status and deployment history. Identify when configuration drift occurs between values and running state.

    Evaluate

    Assess Approaches: Evaluate the trade-offs between using "Off-the-shelf" charts vs. building "Library" charts. Critique the security implications of using Tiller (Helm v2) vs. the client-only architecture of Helm v3.

    Create

    Build Artifacts: Author a production-ready Helm Chart from scratch, including dynamic templates, dependency management (Subcharts), and helper functions in _helpers.tpl.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Helm is a new CLI tool that requires specific setup and familiarity with templating logic. Budget approximately 2-3 hours.

    1Install Helm CLI

    Helm is a single binary executable. Install it on your workstation.

    # macOS (Homebrew) brew install helm # Windows (Chocolatey) choco install kubernetes-helm # Linux/Unix (Script) curl -fsSL -o get_helm.sh https://raw.githubusercontent.com/helm/helm/main/scripts/get-helm-3 chmod 700 get_helm.sh ./get_helm.sh

    Verification: Run helm version. Ensure you are running v3.x.x (Helm v2 is deprecated and insecure).

    2The Go Template Primer

    Helm uses the Go templating language. It looks like {{ .Values.image }}. If you haven't seen this syntax before, it can be confusing.

    Task: Research "Go Template Syntax" specifically for Helm. Write down the syntax for:

    • Inserting a value.
    • Setting a default value if one is missing.
    • Looping over a list (range).
    • Conditionals (if/else).
    💡 Why this matters

    In the labs, you will be writing logic inside YAML files. Understanding the difference between {{ .Values.foo }} and {{ .Release.Name }} is critical.

    3Explore Artifact Hub

    Artifact Hub is the central registry for public Helm charts.

    Task: Visit artifacthub.io.
    1. Search for "MySQL".
    2. Find the chart maintained by Bitnami.
    3. Click "Default Values". Scroll through the configuration. This file (`values.yaml`) is the API documentation for the chart.

    4Add Common Repositories

    Configure your local Helm client with the most popular chart repositories.

    # Bitnami (Database & Infrastructure) helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami # Prometheus Community (Monitoring) helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts # Update your local cache helm repo update

    5Cluster Prep

    Ensure your local Kubernetes cluster (Minikube/Kind) is running and healthy. Clean up any lingering resources from Week 8 to ensure a blank canvas.

    kubectl delete namespace overwatch-prod kubectl delete namespace week8 # Verify clean state kubectl get all --all-namespaces

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    If Kubernetes is the operating system of the cloud, Helm is its package manager. Just as you wouldn't compile every piece of software on your laptop from source code, you shouldn't write every Kubernetes manifest from scratch. Helm solves the problem of Complexity Management by treating a collection of Kubernetes resources as a single logical unit.

    9.1 The "Parameterization" Problem

    In Week 8, you likely copied `deployment.yaml` to create `deployment-prod.yaml`, changing only the `replicas` and `image` tag. This is the "Parameterization Problem."

    Without Helm: You maintain static files. To change the image tag across 50 microservices, you must edit 50 files.
    With Helm: You maintain one Template. You inject the image tag as a Value at runtime.

    📦 The Three Pillars of Helm

    1. The Chart (Package): A bundle of information necessary to create an instance of a Kubernetes application. It contains templates and default configuration. (Analogy: A Docker Image or an `.msi` file).

    2. The Config (Values): Configuration information that is merged into the chart to create a releasable object. This contains your specific settings like environment variables, resource limits, and secrets.

    3. The Release (Instance): A running instance of a Chart, combined with a specific Config. You can install the same Chart five times to create five different Releases (e.g., `mysql-dev`, `mysql-staging`, `mysql-prod`).

    9.2 Helm Architecture (v3 vs v2)

    It is important to understand the architecture, especially if you encounter older tutorials.

    Helm v2 (Legacy): Relied on a server-side component called Tiller running inside the cluster. Tiller had "root" access to the cluster, which was a massive security risk.
    Helm v3 (Current): Client-only architecture. The Helm binary on your laptop talks directly to the Kubernetes API server using your local `kubeconfig` credentials. It uses Kubernetes Secrets to store the state of releases.

    9.3 Anatomy of a Chart

    A Chart is a directory with a strict structure.

    Directory Structure

    wordpress/ Chart.yaml # A YAML file containing information about the chart LICENSE # OPTIONAL: A plain text file containing the license for the chart README.md # OPTIONAL: A readme file values.yaml # The default configuration values for this chart charts/ # A directory containing any charts upon which this chart depends templates/ # A directory of templates that, when combined with values, # will generate valid Kubernetes manifest files. templates/NOTES.txt # OPTIONAL: A plain text file containing short usage notes
    values.yaml (The Interface)

    This file defines the API of your chart. If a setting is in `values.yaml`, users can change it easily. If it's hardcoded in `templates/`, they cannot.

    templates/_helpers.tpl

    A place to put shared logic and partials. For example, a standard way to generate the "app name" label so it's consistent across your Deployment, Service, and Ingress.

    9.4 The Templating Engine

    Helm uses the Go Template language. It works by injecting values into placeholders wrapped in double curly braces {{ }}.

    🧩 The Dot (.) Context

    In Helm templates, you will frequently see .Values or .Release. The leading dot . represents the Root Context (the scope). It contains several top-level objects:
    .Values: Access to `values.yaml` and `--set` flags.
    .Release: Metadata about the deployment (Name, Namespace, Revision).
    .Chart: Metadata from `Chart.yaml` (Version, Maintainers).
    .Capabilities: Info about the Kubernetes cluster (Version, API availability).

    Example: Dynamic Replica Count

    Input (values.yaml):

    replicaCount: 3 image: repository: nginx tag: 1.21

    Template (deployment.yaml):

    apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment spec: replicas: {{ .Values.replicaCount }} template: spec: containers: - image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }}"

    Functions and Pipelines: Helm includes hundreds of functions (like `upper`, `quote`, `b64enc`) that can be chained using pipes |.

    # Example: Quoting a value to ensure it's a string key: {{ .Values.someValue | quote }} # Example: Default value if missing port: {{ .Values.service.port | default 80 }}

    9.5 Release Lifecycle Management

    Helm tracks the history of every release. This allows for transactional updates.

    The 3-Way Merge

    When you run `helm upgrade`, Helm performs a "3-Way Merge" patch to determine what to do. It compares:

    1. The Old Chart: The manifest currently installed.
    2. The New Chart: The manifest you want to install.
    3. The Live State: The actual state of the cluster (what `kubectl get` shows).

    Why Live State? If you manually scaled a deployment to 5 replicas via kubectl, but your new chart says 3, Helm needs to decide whether to respect your manual change or overwrite it. (Helm generally overwrites manual changes to match the desired state).

    💡 Secret Management

    Helm stores its own state (release history) in Kubernetes Secrets in the namespace where the release is installed. You can view them: kubectl get secrets -l owner=helm. This is how Helm knows what version 1 looked like when you ask to rollback from version 2.

    9.6 Dependency Management (Subcharts)

    Modern applications rarely stand alone. Your API might need Redis and PostgreSQL. Instead of installing three separate charts manually, you can define them as Dependencies.

    Chart.yaml:

    dependencies: - name: redis version: 17.3.x repository: https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami condition: redis.enabled

    When you run helm dependency update, Helm downloads the Redis chart into your charts/ directory. You can then configure Redis from your parent `values.yaml` by nesting the config:

    # Parent values.yaml redis: enabled: true auth: password: secretpassword
    ⚠️ "Condition" Logic

    The condition: redis.enabled line in Chart.yaml creates a toggle. If you set redis.enabled: false in your values, Helm will completely ignore the Redis subchart during installation. This is useful for optional features.

    Hands-On Labs

    Theory is useful, but Helm is a tool you learn by doing. In these labs, you will progress from a consumer of public charts to an author of complex, multi-component packages. You will master the template syntax, manage release history, and integrate third-party dependencies.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    Ensure helm is installed (v3+) and your cluster is running.
    Create a clean namespace: kubectl create ns week9 and switch to it using kubens week9 (or kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=week9).

    Lab 1: Installing Public Charts

    Objective: Deploy a production-ready Nginx server using the Bitnami repository. Understand how to search, install, and override values without editing source files.

    Add the Repository:

    Helm needs to know where to look for charts.

    helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami helm repo update
    Search and Inspect:
    helm search repo bitnami/nginx # View the default values without downloading the chart helm show values bitnami/nginx > default-values.yaml

    Open default-values.yaml in your editor. This is your documentation. Notice the vast number of configurable options (resources, ingress, autoscaling, etc.).

    Install with Overrides (The CLI Way):

    We will install the chart but force it to use 2 replicas and a NodePort service.

    helm install my-webserver bitnami/nginx \ --set replicaCount=2 \ --set service.type=NodePort
    Verify the Release:
    helm list kubectl get pods -l app.kubernetes.io/instance=my-webserver
    NAME NAMESPACE REVISION STATUS CHART APP VERSION my-webserver week9 1 deployed nginx-15.x.x 1.25.x
    Cleanup:
    helm uninstall my-webserver

    Lab 2: Scaffolding and Basic Templating

    Objective: Use the helm create scaffolder to build a custom chart structure and understand how values.yaml injects data into templates/.

    Generate the Scaffold:
    helm create mychart ls -R mychart

    Helm creates a "Best Practice" chart for Nginx. It includes Ingress, ServiceAccount, and Autoscaling templates automatically.

    Modify default values:

    Open mychart/values.yaml. Change the image repository to something lightweight for testing.

    image: repository: stefanprodan/podinfo pullPolicy: IfNotPresent # Overrides the image tag whose default is the chart appVersion. tag: "6.4.0" replicaCount: 3 service: type: ClusterIP port: 9898 # podinfo listens on 9898
    Debug with Dry-Run:

    This is the most important command in Helm. It generates the YAML without sending it to the cluster.

    helm install debug-release ./mychart --dry-run --debug

    Scroll up the output. Look for the Deployment section. Verify that replicas: 3 and image: stefanprodan/podinfo... are present. If you see them, the template engine is working.

    Install the Custom Chart:
    helm install my-custom-app ./mychart

    Lab 3: Loops, Conditionals, and Ranges

    Objective: Modify your chart templates to support dynamic environment variables using Go Template loops (`range`) and conditional logic (`if`).

    Update values.yaml:

    Add a new section to mychart/values.yaml for custom environment variables.

    # Add this to the bottom of values.yaml env: - name: APP_COLOR value: "blue" - name: APP_MODE value: "production" enableFeatureX: true
    Update Deployment Template:

    Edit mychart/templates/deployment.yaml. Find the containers: section and add the loop logic.

    containers: - name: {{ .Chart.Name }} image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag | default .Chart.AppVersion }}" # --- ADD THIS BLOCK --- env: {{- range .Values.env }} - name: {{ .name }} value: {{ .value | quote }} {{- end }} {{- if .Values.enableFeatureX }} - name: FEATURE_X value: "enabled" {{- end }} # ----------------------
    Upgrade the Release:

    Apply the changes to your running release.

    helm upgrade my-custom-app ./mychart
    Verify Injection:
    # Get the pod name export POD_NAME=$(kubectl get pods -l app.kubernetes.io/instance=my-custom-app -o jsonpath="{.items[0].metadata.name}") # Check environment variables inside the pod kubectl exec $POD_NAME -- env | grep APP_
    APP_COLOR=blue APP_MODE=production

    Lab 4: Upgrades and Rollbacks

    Objective: Simulate a bad deployment (breaking change) and recover the application state using helm rollback.

    Check History:
    helm history my-custom-app
    REVISION STATUS DESCRIPTION 1 deployed Install complete 2 deployed Upgrade complete
    Deploy a "Broken" Version:

    Let's upgrade the chart with a non-existent image tag to cause a crash.

    helm upgrade my-custom-app ./mychart --set image.tag="99.99.99-broken"
    Verify Failure:
    kubectl get pods # Status will be ImagePullBackOff or ErrImagePull
    Perform Rollback:

    We need to go back to Revision 2 (the last working state).

    helm rollback my-custom-app 2

    Helm re-applies the manifest exactly as it was in Revision 2. The pods should recover to Running status.

    Lab 5: Managing Dependencies

    Objective: Add a Redis database to your application by defining it as a chart dependency, rather than writing Redis YAML manually.

    Edit Chart.yaml:

    Add the dependencies block to mychart/Chart.yaml.

    dependencies: - name: redis version: 18.x.x repository: https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami condition: redis.enabled
    Download Dependencies:
    helm dependency update ./mychart

    You will see a .tgz file appear in the mychart/charts/ directory. This is the downloaded Redis chart.

    Configure Redis in values.yaml:

    Enable the subchart and configure it from the parent values.

    # Add to mychart/values.yaml redis: enabled: true auth: enabled: false # Disable password for lab simplicity architecture: standalone
    Upgrade to Deploy Redis:
    helm upgrade my-custom-app ./mychart
    Verify Architecture:
    kubectl get pods kubectl get svc

    You should now see your my-custom-app pods AND a my-custom-app-redis-master pod. Helm deployed both the app and the database together as a single unit.

    Lab Solutions & Troubleshooting Guide

    This section provides the "answer key" for the Helm labs. It explains the mechanics of templating, how Helm manages state via Secrets, and why dependency management is the "killer feature" for complex stacks.

    Lab 1 Solution: Consuming Public Charts

    Understanding Overrides

    Command: helm install my-webserver bitnami/nginx --set replicaCount=2

    Architectural Insight:
    When you run this, Helm downloads the chart archive (tgz). It reads the built-in values.yaml. Then, it takes your --set flags and merges them on top.

    Priority Order (Lowest to Highest):
    1. Chart's default values.yaml
    2. Values file passed via -f my-values.yaml
    3. Individual values passed via --set

    💡 Debugging Tip

    If you are unsure what values are currently running in a release, use:
    helm get values my-webserver (shows user-supplied overrides)
    helm get values my-webserver --all (shows computed values including defaults)

    Lab 2 Solution: Custom Chart Basics

    The Dry-Run Debug

    The --dry-run --debug output is critical for developers. It shows the raw YAML generated by the template engine.

    # Source: mychart/templates/deployment.yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment spec: replicas: 3 template: spec: containers: - name: mychart image: "stefanprodan/podinfo:6.4.0"

    Verification:
    1. Replicas: Matches replicaCount: 3 from values.yaml.
    2. Image: Matches the override logic. Note that if you hadn't quoted "6.4.0" in values.yaml, YAML parsers might interpret 6.40 as 6.4, dropping the zero. Helm best practice is to always quote version strings.

    Lab 3 Solution: Advanced Templating

    Logic Analysis

    {{- range .Values.env }} - name: {{ .name }} value: {{ .value | quote }} {{- end }}

    The Dash (`-`): Did you notice the {{- syntax? This is whitespace control.
    If you write {{ range }} (no dash), Helm leaves a blank newline in the YAML output for that logic line.
    If you write {{- range }} (with dash), Helm consumes the previous whitespace/newline, keeping the generated YAML compact and valid.

    The Scope Change: Inside the range loop, the "dot" . no longer refers to the Root Context. It refers to the current item in the loop. That is why we use {{ .name }} (local scope) instead of {{ .Values.name }}.

    Lab 4 Solution: Rollbacks

    State Management

    When you executed helm rollback my-custom-app 2, Helm did the following:

    1. Looked up the Kubernetes Secret named sh.helm.release.v1.my-custom-app.v2 in the namespace.
    2. Decoded the gzipped payload inside that secret to retrieve the exact chart and config used in Revision 2.
    3. Created a new Revision (let's say Revision 4) that is a clone of Revision 2.
    4. Applied the difference to the cluster.
    ⚠️ History Limit

    By default, Helm keeps only the last 10 revisions to save space in etcd. You can configure this via --history-max during install/upgrade.

    Lab 5 Solution: Dependencies

    The Subchart Pattern

    Structure: After running helm dep update, your folder looked like this:

    mychart/ charts/ redis-18.x.x.tgz <-- The dependency

    Value Inheritance:
    In your parent values.yaml, you defined a redis: block. Helm automatically detects that this block matches the name of a subchart (Redis). It passes only that block down to the subchart as its root values.

    This allows you to control complex dependencies (like setting Redis passwords or disabling persistence) entirely from the top-level values.yaml without ever touching the Redis chart code.

    Practice Questions

    These questions test your understanding of Helm mechanics, templating, and command-line operations.

    Which Helm command generates the template YAML and prints it to the console without installing anything?
    A. helm lint
    B. helm install ... --dry-run --debug
    C. helm get manifest
    D. helm verify
    Answer: B. The combination of `--dry-run` (simulate) and `--debug` (print output) is the standard way to inspect rendered templates. `helm get manifest` retrieves YAML from an already installed release.
    Where does Helm v3 store the state of releases (history, current config)?
    A. In a local SQLite database on your laptop
    B. Inside the Tiller pod
    C. As Secrets (or ConfigMaps) in the Kubernetes namespace
    D. In a git repository
    Answer: C. Helm v3 uses Kubernetes Secrets (default) to store release data. This utilizes Kubernetes' own RBAC and storage backend (etcd) for security and reliability.
    You want to override a nested value `image.tag` during installation without creating a file. Which syntax is correct?
    A. --set image-tag=v1
    B. --set image.tag=v1
    C. --override image.tag=v1
    D. --value image.tag=v1
    Answer: B. The `--set` flag uses dot notation to traverse the values object. `image.tag` targets the `tag` key inside the `image` map.
    In a Helm template, what does `{{ .Release.Name }}` refer to?
    A. The name of the Chart defined in Chart.yaml
    B. The name given to the instance (Release) by the user at install time
    C. The Kubernetes namespace
    D. The name of the generated Pod
    Answer: B. `.Release.Name` is the name you type after `helm install` (e.g., `my-webserver`). It is widely used to ensure resource names are unique (e.g., `metadata: name: {{ .Release.Name }}-nginx`).
    What file is required in a directory for it to be recognized as a Helm Chart?
    A. values.yaml
    B. Chart.yaml
    C. templates/deployment.yaml
    D. requirements.yaml
    Answer: B. `Chart.yaml` contains the metadata (name, version, apiVersion) and is the only strictly mandatory file for Helm to recognize the directory as a package.
    How do you define a dependency on another chart (Subchart)?
    A. Put the chart manually in the templates folder
    B. List it in the `dependencies` section of Chart.yaml and run `helm dep update`
    C. Use `kubectl apply` before installing the parent chart
    D. It is not possible in Helm v3
    Answer: B. You define the name, version, and repository URL in `Chart.yaml`, then run the dependency update command to fetch the archives.
    What does the syntax `{{- if .Values.enabled }}` signify compared to `{{ if .Values.enabled }}`?
    A. It creates a loop
    B. It is a syntax error
    C. It removes whitespace/newlines before the block
    D. It negates the condition
    Answer: C. The dash `-` trims whitespace. Without it, Helm templates often generate large gaps of empty lines in the resulting YAML, which might remain valid YAML but looks messy.
    You installed a chart named `db` into namespace `prod`. How do you list it?
    A. helm list
    B. helm list -n prod
    C. kubectl get charts
    D. helm ls --all
    Answer: B. Helm is namespace-scoped. `helm list` only shows releases in your current active namespace context. To see releases in `prod`, you must specify `-n prod`.
    What happens if you modify a template file but do not bump the `version` in Chart.yaml?
    A. The upgrade fails
    B. The upgrade succeeds, but it is bad practice
    C. Helm automatically increments the version
    D. The changes are ignored
    Answer: B. Helm will apply the changes to the cluster. However, chart versioning should follow Semantic Versioning. If the logic changes, the version should change so users know an update is available.
    Which function allows you to output a set of values as a YAML block, often used for adding labels?
    A. `quote`
    B. `upper`
    C. `toYaml`
    D. `default`
    Answer: C. `toYaml` is frequently used to dump a whole map of values (like `resources:` or `labels:`) directly into the template without writing out every key manually.

    Week-Ending Assignment: The "Universal" Microservice Chart

    🎯 Assignment Objective

    You are now the Lead DevOps Engineer for a startup. Your developers are creating new microservices every week (Auth service, Billing service, Frontend UI).

    The Problem: Currently, they copy-paste a folder of raw YAML manifests for every new service. This is unmaintainable. If you need to change a standard label or add a security context, you have to edit 50 files.

    Your Mission: Create a single, highly flexible Helm Chart named universal-app. This single chart must be capable of deploying any standard stateless microservice simply by feeding it a different values.yaml file. It must support dynamic environment variables, optional Ingress, and automatic rollouts when configuration changes.

    Architecture Overview

    Your chart will be the "template" for the entire company. It needs to handle the following conditional logic:

    🎛️

    1. Dynamic Config

    Requirement: The Deployment must loop through a list of environment variables defined in values.
    Bonus: If the ConfigMap changes, the Pods must restart automatically (Checksum pattern).

    🌐

    2. Optional Ingress

    Requirement: Not all services are public. The Ingress resource should only be created if ingress.enabled is set to true.

    🏷️

    3. Standard Labels

    Requirement: Every resource (Service, Deployment, Ingress) must share a common set of labels generated in _helpers.tpl.

    Technical Requirements

    1. Chart Structure

    Create universal-app using helm create, but strip out the bloat.

    • Clean up `templates/` to only contain Deployment, Service, Ingress, and Helpers.
    • Ensure Chart.yaml has your name as maintainer.

    2. The Values Interface

    Your values.yaml must support this structure:

    env: - name: DB_HOST value: "localhost" ingress: enabled: true host: "myapp.local"

    3. The Deployment Template

    • Replica Count: Dynamic from values.
    • Env Loop: Must iterate over .Values.env using range.
    • Annotation Checksum: Add an annotation to the Pod Template that hashes the ConfigMap/Values to trigger restarts on change. (Research: `sha256sum` function in Helm).

    4. Conditional Ingress

    Wrap the entire ingress.yaml content in an {{- if .Values.ingress.enabled }} block.

    Step-by-Step Implementation Guide

    Phase 1: Scaffolding

    Run helm create universal-app. Delete serviceaccount.yaml and hpa.yaml to simplify the scope for now.

    Phase 2: Defining the Interface

    Edit values.yaml. Define a list of environment variables. Define the ingress toggle.

    Phase 3: The Deployment Logic

    Edit templates/deployment.yaml. Implement the `range` loop for environment variables.
    Challenge: Implement the checksum annotation: checksum/config: {{ include (print $.Template.BasePath "/configmap.yaml") . | sha256sum }} (Optional but recommended).

    Phase 4: Testing Dry-Run

    Run helm install test ./universal-app --dry-run --debug. Check if the environment variables are rendering correctly in the output YAML.

    Phase 5: Deploying Two Apps

    Deploy two "different" apps using the same chart:
    1. Frontend: helm install frontend ./universal-app --set ingress.enabled=true
    2. Backend: helm install backend ./universal-app --set ingress.enabled=false

    📋 Student Submission Checklist

    Before submitting your assignment, verify you have completed all items:

    Teacher's Solution Guide

    Use this reference to grade submissions. These manifests represent the "Golden Path" solution for a reusable Library Chart.

    Solution: Universal Chart

    1. values.yaml (The API)

    replicaCount: 1 image: repository: nginx pullPolicy: IfNotPresent tag: "" service: type: ClusterIP port: 80 ingress: enabled: false className: "nginx" host: "chart-example.local" # Custom list of env vars env: [] # Example usage: # env: # - name: LOG_LEVEL # value: "debug"

    2. templates/deployment.yaml

    Key Check: Look for the range loop and the checksum annotation.

    apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: {{ include "universal-app.fullname" . }} labels: {{- include "universal-app.labels" . | nindent 4 }} spec: replicas: {{ .Values.replicaCount }} selector: matchLabels: {{- include "universal-app.selectorLabels" . | nindent 6 }} template: metadata: annotations: # This triggers a rolling update if values.yaml changes! checksum/config: {{ toYaml .Values.env | sha256sum }} labels: {{- include "universal-app.selectorLabels" . | nindent 8 }} spec: containers: - name: {{ .Chart.Name }} image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag | default .Chart.AppVersion }}" ports: - name: http containerPort: {{ .Values.service.port }} protocol: TCP env: {{- range .Values.env }} - name: {{ .name }} value: {{ .value | quote }} {{- end }}

    3. templates/ingress.yaml

    Key Check: The entire file content must be wrapped in the `if` block.

    {{- if .Values.ingress.enabled -}} apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: Ingress metadata: name: {{ include "universal-app.fullname" . }} labels: {{- include "universal-app.labels" . | nindent 4 }} spec: ingressClassName: {{ .Values.ingress.className }} rules: - host: {{ .Values.ingress.host | quote }} http: paths: - path: / pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: {{ include "universal-app.fullname" . }} port: number: {{ .Values.service.port }} {{- end }}

    4. Validation Logic

    To verify the "Universal" nature, run these two commands. They should produce valid YAML with distinct configurations from the same chart.

    # 1. Frontend Config helm template frontend ./universal-app \ --set ingress.enabled=true \ --set env[0].name=ROLE --set env[0].value=UI # 2. Backend Config helm template backend ./universal-app \ --set ingress.enabled=false \ --set env[0].name=DB_HOST --set env[0].value=10.0.0.1
    🚀 Week 9 Complete

    Congratulations! You have moved from "manual YAML writer" to "Platform Engineer." You can now build abstractions that allow developers to deploy applications without needing to be Kubernetes experts.

    Next Week (Week 10): We step outside the cluster to manage the infrastructure itself. We will introduce Terraform (Infrastructure as Code) to provision the actual servers (AKS, EKS, GKE) that your charts run on.

    Mentorship Review for Week 9

    Mentorship students: your Week 9 work (Helm & Kubernetes Package Management) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    10

    Infrastructure as Code with Terraform

    Provisioning, Managing, and Versioning Cloud Infrastructure

    Welcome to Week 10. For the past 9 weeks, you have operated inside the cluster. You managed Pods, Services, and Ingress rules. But where did the cluster come from? Who created the Virtual Machines, the VPC networks, the Load Balancers, and the Database instances?

    Until now, you might have created these resources manually by clicking buttons in a web console (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud). This is fine for a hobby project, but in a professional environment, manual clicking is forbidden. It is slow, unrepeatable, and prone to human error.

    This week, we introduce Terraform, the industry standard for Infrastructure as Code (IaC). Terraform allows you to define your entire data center in text files (HCL). You can version control your infrastructure (Git), review changes before they happen (Plan), and deploy identical environments (Dev, Stage, Prod) with a single command. By the end of this week, you will stop being a "ClickOps" operator and become a Cloud Engineer.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Infrastructure as Code with Terraform: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Core Concepts: Define Infrastructure as Code (IaC), Immutable Infrastructure, Terraform Providers, Resources, Data Sources, and the State File.

    Understand

    Explain Mechanics: Explain the Terraform Workflow (`init` -> `plan` -> `apply`). Describe how the State file maps real-world resources to your configuration and why locking is critical.

    Apply

    Execute Provisioning: Write HCL code to provision an AWS EC2 instance (or Docker container). Use `terraform plan` to predict changes and `terraform destroy` to clean up.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Drift: Analyze the output of `terraform plan` to detect "Drift" (when manual changes in the console conflict with your code). Debug dependency errors in resource creation order.

    Evaluate

    Assess Strategies: Evaluate the benefits of using Remote State (S3 + DynamoDB) vs. Local State. Critique the security risks of committing `.tfstate` files to Git.

    Create

    Build Architecture: Architect a modular Terraform project that deploys a complete VPC network with public/private subnets and a web server, using variables for reusability.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Terraform requires credentials to interact with cloud providers. Budget approximately 3-4 hours.

    1Install Terraform CLI

    Terraform is a single binary tool.

    # macOS (Homebrew) brew tap hashicorp/tap brew install hashicorp/tap/terraform # Windows (Chocolatey) choco install terraform # Linux # Follow instructions at: https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/downloads

    Verification: Run terraform version. Ensure you are on v1.5.0 or higher.

    2Prepare a Cloud Provider

    Terraform needs a target. Ideally, we will use AWS Free Tier. If you cannot use AWS, we will use the Docker Provider as a local fallback.

    Option A: AWS (Recommended)

    • Create an AWS Free Tier account.
    • Create an IAM User with AdministratorAccess (for learning only).
    • Generate Access Keys (Access Key ID and Secret Access Key).
    • Install AWS CLI and run aws configure to save these credentials.

    Option B: Docker (Fallback)

    • Ensure Docker Desktop is running.
    • We will use the kreuzwerker/docker provider in the labs to create containers instead of VMs.

    3Declarative vs. Imperative

    Research Task: Write a short paragraph explaining the difference between:
    1. Imperative (Ansible/Bash): "Make a sandwich. Step 1: Get bread. Step 2: Get cheese..."
    2. Declarative (Terraform/Kubernetes): "I want a cheese sandwich."

    Why is Declarative safer when you run the script a second time? (Hint: Idempotency).

    4The "State" File Mystery

    Terraform creates a file called terraform.tfstate.

    Task: Read the HashiCorp documentation on "State". Answer this: If you manually delete an EC2 instance in the AWS Console, but the terraform.tfstate file still thinks it exists, what will happen when you run terraform plan?

    5Install VS Code Extension

    Writing HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language) is hard without syntax highlighting.

    Task: Install the official "HashiCorp Terraform" extension for VS Code. It provides autocompletion for AWS resources, which is a lifesaver.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is the practice of managing and provisioning computer data centers through machine-readable definition files, rather than physical hardware configuration or interactive configuration tools.

    Terraform by HashiCorp is the dominant tool in this space. Unlike scripts (Bash/Python) which describe how to build something ("run this command, then that command"), Terraform allows you to describe what you want ("I want three servers"). Terraform figures out the "how" for you.

    10.1 The HCL Language (HashiCorp Configuration Language)

    Terraform code is written in HCL. It is designed to be human-readable. A standard project is composed of several blocks.

    Anatomy of a Resource Block

    resource "aws_instance" "web_server" { ami = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0" instance_type = "t2.micro" tags = { Name = "MyFirstServer" } }
    • Block Type (`resource`): Tells Terraform we want to create something.
    • Provider Type (`aws_instance`): The specific resource type defined by the AWS Provider.
    • Local Name (`web_server`): How you refer to this resource inside your Terraform code (e.g., to link a load balancer to it). This name is NOT known to AWS.
    • Arguments: The configuration (AMI, Size, Tags).

    10.2 The Core Workflow

    Terraform has a strict, repeatable workflow that prevents errors. You will repeat this cycle thousands of times in your career.

    1

    terraform init

    Initialization. Terraform scans your code to see which Providers you are using (e.g., AWS, Docker). It downloads the plugin binaries into a hidden `.terraform` folder. You run this once per project.

    2

    terraform plan

    The Dry Run. Terraform compares your code (Desired State) against the Real World (Current State). It calculates the "Diff" and tells you exactly what it would do if you applied.
    Output: "Plan: 1 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy."

    3

    terraform apply

    Execution. Terraform calls the cloud APIs to create/update/delete resources to match the Plan. It then updates its State File.

    10.3 The State File (The "Brain")

    This is the most critical concept to understand. Terraform maintains a file called terraform.tfstate.

    The Problem: You declare `resource "aws_instance" "web"`. Terraform creates an EC2 instance with ID `i-012345abcdef`. How does Terraform remember that `web` = `i-012345abcdef` the next time you run it?
    The Solution: The State File maps your local code names to real-world cloud IDs.

    ⚠️ The Golden Rule of State

    NEVER edit the state file manually. Also, never touch resources manually in the AWS Console if they are managed by Terraform. This causes Drift.

    If you delete a server in the AWS Console, Terraform's state file still thinks it exists. When you run `terraform plan`, Terraform will see it's missing and attempt to recreate it (Healing).

    10.4 Variables and Outputs

    Hardcoding values (like AMIs or Regions) makes code brittle. We use Input Variables to make modules reusable and Output Values to display important info (like IP addresses) after deployment.

    # variables.tf variable "region" { description = "AWS Region" default = "us-east-1" type = string } # main.tf provider "aws" { region = var.region } # outputs.tf output "server_ip" { value = aws_instance.web.public_ip }

    10.5 Data Sources (Read-Only)

    Sometimes you need to configure your infrastructure based on resources that already exist (and aren't managed by you), or look up dynamic information (like the latest Ubuntu AMI ID).

    A Data Source is a read-only query to the provider.

    # Ask AWS for the default VPC ID data "aws_vpc" "default" { default = true } # Use that ID in a resource resource "aws_security_group" "sg" { name = "allow_http" vpc_id = data.aws_vpc.default.id <-- Dynamic Reference }

    10.6 Remote State & Locking

    When working alone, a local `terraform.tfstate` file is fine. In a team, it is disastrous.

    • Consistency: If Developer A applies changes, Developer B's local state file is now outdated.
    • Security: The state file stores sensitive data (like database passwords) in plain text. You should not commit this to GitHub.
    • Race Conditions: What if Alice and Bob run `terraform apply` at the exact same time?

    The Solution: Remote Backends. We configure Terraform to store the state file in a shared location (like an AWS S3 Bucket) and use a database (like DynamoDB) to create a "Lock". When Alice runs `apply`, Terraform locks the table. If Bob tries to run `apply`, Terraform says "Error: State is locked by Alice."

    Hands-On Labs

    The only way to learn Terraform is to destroy infrastructure (intentionally). In these labs, you will start by provisioning a local Docker container to master the workflow without cloud costs. Then, you will graduate to AWS to build a real web server with security groups and dynamic IP addressing. Finally, you will simulate a "disaster" and watch Terraform heal the infrastructure.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    1. Terraform Installed: Verify with terraform version.
    2. AWS Credentials: (For Labs 2-5) Ensure aws configure is run and you have a profile with Admin permissions.
    3. Docker Running: (For Lab 1) Ensure Docker Desktop is active.
    4. VS Code Extension: Ensure "HashiCorp Terraform" is installed for syntax highlighting.

    Lab 1: The "Hello World" of IaC

    Objective: Learn the core lifecycle (`init`, `plan`, `apply`, `destroy`) using a safe, local resource (a Docker container) before touching the cloud.

    Setup Workspace:
    mkdir terraform-labs cd terraform-labs mkdir lab1-docker cd lab1-docker touch main.tf
    Write the Configuration:

    Open main.tf and define the Docker provider and an Nginx container resource.

    terraform { required_providers { docker = { source = "kreuzwerker/docker" version = "~> 3.0.1" } } } provider "docker" {} resource "docker_image" "nginx" { name = "nginx:latest" keep_locally = false } resource "docker_container" "nginx" { image = docker_image.nginx.image_id name = "tutorial" ports { internal = 80 external = 8000 } }
    Step 1: Init:

    Initialize the project. This downloads the kreuzwerker/docker plugin.

    terraform init

    Success looks like: "Terraform has been successfully initialized!"

    Step 2: Plan:
    terraform plan
    Plan: 2 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.

    Terraform calculates it needs to download the Image AND start the Container.

    Step 3: Apply:
    terraform apply # Type 'yes' when prompted
    Verify:

    Open your browser to http://localhost:8000. You should see the Nginx welcome page. The container is running!

    docker ps # You will see a container named "tutorial"
    Step 4: Destroy:

    Clean up your workspace.

    terraform destroy # Type 'yes'

    Lab 2: Your First Cloud Resource

    Objective: Provision a real virtual machine on AWS. Learn how to map a local resource block to a cloud instance.

    Setup Workspace:
    cd .. mkdir lab2-aws cd lab2-aws touch main.tf
    Write the Provider Block:

    Tell Terraform we are talking to AWS in the us-east-1 region (N. Virginia).

    provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" }
    Define the EC2 Instance:

    Append this to main.tf. We are hardcoding the AMI ID for Ubuntu 20.04 (this ID is specific to us-east-1).

    resource "aws_instance" "web_server" { ami = "ami-0c7217cdde317cfec" # Ubuntu 22.04 LTS (us-east-1) instance_type = "t2.micro" # Free Tier eligible tags = { Name = "Terraform-Lab-2" } }
    Apply:
    terraform init terraform apply -auto-approve
    💡 -auto-approve

    This flag skips the interactive "yes" prompt. Use carefully in production!

    Verify in Console:

    Log into the AWS Console > EC2. You should see an instance named "Terraform-Lab-2" initializing.

    Lab 3: Smarter Infrastructure

    Objective: Remove hardcoded values. Use Data Sources to dynamically find the latest AMI and Outputs to print the public IP address.

    Create variables.tf:

    Define variables to make the code reusable.

    variable "instance_name" { description = "Value of the Name tag for the EC2 instance" type = string default = "My-Dynamic-Server" }
    Update main.tf (Use Data Source):

    Replace the hardcoded AMI with a dynamic lookup.

    provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" } # 1. Ask AWS for the latest Ubuntu AMI data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" { most_recent = true filter { name = "name" values = ["ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-jammy-22.04-amd64-server-*"] } filter { name = "virtualization-type" values = ["hvm"] } owners = ["099720109477"] # Canonical (Ubuntu Creator) } # 2. Use the result in the resource resource "aws_instance" "web" { ami = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id instance_type = "t2.micro" tags = { Name = var.instance_name } }
    Create outputs.tf:

    Tell Terraform to print the IP address after creation.

    output "instance_id" { description = "ID of the EC2 instance" value = aws_instance.web.id } output "public_ip" { description = "Public IP address of the EC2 instance" value = aws_instance.web.public_ip }
    Apply with Variable Override:
    terraform apply -var="instance_name=Production-Server"
    Apply complete! Resources: 1 added, 0 changed, 1 destroyed. Outputs: instance_id = "i-0123456789abcdef0" public_ip = "54.1.2.3"

    Terraform destroyed the old instance (because we changed the TF configuration structure significantly or renamed resources in state) and created a new one with the latest AMI.

    Lab 4: Detecting Drift & Healing

    Objective: Manually sabotage the infrastructure to see how Terraform reacts. This demonstrates the "Healing" capability of Declarative IaC.

    The Sabotage:

    1. Go to the AWS Console.
    2. Find your "Production-Server".
    3. Terminate it manually.
    4. Wait for the state to show "Terminated".

    ⚠️ The Reality

    In a script (Imperative), if you ran "create server", it would blindly create a second server. Terraform is smarter.

    Run Plan (Drift Detection):
    terraform plan
    Terraform used the selected providers to generate the following execution plan. Resource actions are indicated with the following symbols: + create Terraform will perform the following actions: # aws_instance.web will be created + resource "aws_instance" "web" { ... } Plan: 1 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.

    Analysis: Terraform refreshed its state (API lookup), realized the instance ID in its state file no longer exists in AWS, and decided the only way to match your desired state (`resource "aws_instance"`) is to create a new one.

    The Healing:
    terraform apply -auto-approve

    Your infrastructure is restored to the correct state.

    Lab 5: Building a Web Server with Security Groups

    Objective: Deploy a web server that is actually accessible over the internet by configuring Security Groups (Firewalls) and User Data (Startup Scripts).

    Create `security.tf`:

    Define a security group allowing HTTP (80) and SSH (22).

    resource "aws_security_group" "web_sg" { name = "allow_web_traffic" description = "Allow Web inbound traffic" ingress { description = "HTTP" from_port = 80 to_port = 80 protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } ingress { description = "SSH" from_port = 22 to_port = 22 protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } egress { from_port = 0 to_port = 0 protocol = "-1" # Allow all outbound cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } }
    Update `main.tf` with User Data:

    Modify your instance resource to use the security group and install Apache on boot.

    resource "aws_instance" "web" { ami = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id instance_type = "t2.micro" # Attach the Security Group vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web_sg.id] # Startup Script (Bash) user_data = <<-EOF #!/bin/bash sudo apt-get update sudo apt-get install -y apache2 echo "

    Deployed via Terraform

    " | sudo tee /var/www/html/index.html sudo systemctl start apache2 sudo systemctl enable apache2 EOF tags = { Name = "Terraform-Web-Server" } }
    Apply and Test:
    terraform apply -auto-approve

    Wait 2 minutes for the instance to boot and run the script.
    Copy the public_ip from the outputs.
    Paste it into your browser: http://54.x.x.x

    You should see "Deployed via Terraform".

    Final Cleanup (Crucial):

    Do not skip this. You will be billed if you leave resources running.

    terraform destroy -auto-approve

    Lab Solutions & Troubleshooting Guide

    This section provides the "answer key" for the Terraform labs. It dissects the behaviors you observed—why Terraform detected drift, how it resolved dependencies between security groups and instances, and what actually happens during initialization.

    Lab 1 Solution: The Docker Workflow

    Why `terraform init`?

    If you skipped terraform init, the plan command would have failed with "provider not found".

    Architectural Insight: Terraform Core (the CLI binary) knows nothing about Docker, AWS, or Azure. It is just a state management engine.
    When you run init, it reads required_providers, reaches out to the Terraform Registry, downloads the kreuzwerker/docker binary, and saves it to .terraform/providers/. This plugin architecture is what allows Terraform to support 2,000+ different APIs.

    The "2 to add" Logic

    Terraform saw dependencies. The docker_container resource referenced docker_image.nginx.image_id.
    Therefore, the graph logic determined: "I must create the Image first to get the ID, then I can create the Container." It handles this ordering automatically.

    Lab 2 Solution: AWS Provisioning

    Authentication Magic

    You didn't put your Access Keys in the .tf file (and you shouldn't!). How did it work?

    The AWS Provider automatically looks for credentials in standard locations, in this order:
    1. Environment Variables (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID)
    2. The ~/.aws/credentials file (created by aws configure)
    3. EC2 Instance Metadata (if running on an EC2 server)

    ⚠️ Common Error: InvalidAMIID.NotFound

    Symptom: "The image id '[ami-xxx]' does not exist".
    Cause: AMI IDs are Region Specific. An Ubuntu AMI in us-east-1 has a completely different ID in us-west-2. If you changed the region in the provider block but kept the hardcoded AMI ID from the lab instructions, it will fail.

    Lab 3 Solution: Dynamic Data

    Data Source Mechanics

    data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" { ... }

    When you run terraform plan, Terraform makes a real-time API call to AWS: "Give me the list of AMIs owned by Canonical, named ubuntu-22.04, virtualization hvm."
    It returns a list, sorts by date (`most_recent = true`), picks the top one, and injects that ID into your configuration. This ensures you always deploy the latest security patches without editing code.

    Output Values

    Outputs are extracted from the State File. After the apply finishes, the AWS API returns the new instance's metadata (Private IP, Public IP, DNS Name). Terraform saves this all to terraform.tfstate. The output block simply queries that JSON file and prints the requested field.

    Lab 4 Solution: Drift Detection

    The Refresh Step

    Step: Plan after manual deletion

    Every plan or apply starts with a hidden step called Refresh.
    1. Terraform looks at the state file: "I think instance i-0123abcd exists."
    2. It calls AWS: "Describe instance i-0123abcd."
    3. AWS responds: "InstanceTerminated" or "NotFound".
    4. Terraform updates the state file to remove that ID.
    5. It compares the (now empty) state against your code (`resource "aws_instance"`).
    6. Conclusion: "I need to create this."

    Lab 5 Solution: Web Server & User Data

    Dependency Graph

    You referenced the Security Group ID in the Instance:
    vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web_sg.id]

    Terraform implicitly understood that the Security Group must be created first. If you look at the logs, it created the SG, got its ID (e.g., `sg-0xyz...`), and only then launched the EC2 instance with that ID.

    User Data (Cloud-Init)

    The script provided in user_data is passed to the cloud provider. On first boot, the cloud-init service on Ubuntu reads this script and executes it as root.
    Troubleshooting: If the web server doesn't show up:
    1. Check Security Group (Is port 80 open to 0.0.0.0/0?).
    2. SSH into the box and check `/var/log/cloud-init-output.log` to see if the script failed (e.g., apt-get update failed).

    Practice Questions

    These questions are aligned with the HashiCorp Certified: Terraform Associate exam.

    What is the primary purpose of `terraform init`?
    A. To authenticate with AWS
    B. To download the necessary provider plugins and set up the backend
    C. To validate the syntax of .tf files
    D. To create the state file
    Answer: B. `init` initializes the working directory. It reads the configuration to determine which providers (AWS, Docker, etc.) are needed and downloads their binaries to `.terraform`.
    Which command would you use to check for syntax errors and internal consistency without accessing any cloud APIs?
    A. terraform fmt
    B. terraform validate
    C. terraform plan
    D. terraform init
    Answer: B. `terraform validate` checks the code for syntax errors, missing arguments, and type mismatches. It does not contact the cloud provider.
    You manually deleted an EC2 instance that Terraform manages. What will happen when you run `terraform plan`?
    A. Terraform will error and exit
    B. Terraform will update the state to remove the instance
    C. Terraform will detect the drift and propose creating a new instance
    D. Nothing, Terraform only adds resources
    Answer: C. Terraform detects the resource is missing (Drift) during the refresh phase. Since your code still says resource "aws_instance", the Plan will propose re-creating it to match the desired state.
    Which file stores the mapping between your real-world resources and your configuration?
    A. main.tf
    B. terraform.tfstate
    C. terraform.tfvars
    D. .terraform/
    Answer: B. The state file is the "source of truth" for Terraform. It maps the configuration (aws_instance.web) to the provider ID (i-12345678).
    You want to query the ID of an existing AWS VPC that was created manually, not by Terraform. Which block should you use?
    A. resource
    B. data
    C. variable
    D. output
    Answer: B. data sources allow Terraform to fetch information (read-only) about external resources that define specific criteria.
    What does `terraform fmt` do?
    A. Deletes all resources
    B. Validates the code
    C. Rewrites configuration files to a canonical format and style
    D. Updates the state file
    Answer: C. It automatically fixes indentation and spacing in your HCL files. It is best practice to run this before committing code.
    Which command destroys infrastructure managed by Terraform?
    A. terraform delete
    B. terraform destroy
    C. terraform kill
    D. terraform rm
    Answer: B. `terraform destroy` is essentially `terraform apply` with a plan to remove all resources.
    Why is it recommended to use a Remote Backend (like S3) instead of a local state file?
    A. It is faster
    B. It supports state locking and team collaboration
    C. It is required by AWS
    D. Local state files are deleted after 24 hours
    Answer: B. Remote backends allow multiple developers to work on the same infrastructure by locking the state during writes. It also keeps sensitive data off developer laptops.
    If you rename a resource block in `main.tf` (e.g., from `aws_instance.web` to `aws_instance.app`), what will Terraform do by default?
    A. Rename the instance in AWS
    B. Destroy the old instance and create a new one
    C. Update the state file in place
    D. Error out
    Answer: B. Terraform tracks resources by their address (`aws_instance.web`). If you change the name, it sees "web" as deleted and "app" as new. To fix this without downtime, you must use the `terraform state mv` command.
    What does the `auto-approve` flag do?
    A. Automatically fixes syntax errors
    B. Skips the interactive approval of the plan
    C. Automatically retries failed applies
    D. Approves the provider license
    Answer: B. It bypasses the "Do you want to perform these actions?" prompt. Essential for CI/CD pipelines, dangerous for manual use.

    Week-Ending Assignment: Project "SkyLaunch" (VPC & Compute)

    🎯 Assignment Objective

    You have learned how to provision individual resources. Now, you must architect a complete, production-grade Network & Compute Stack from scratch.

    The Scenario: Your company needs a dedicated environment for a new marketing campaign. You cannot use the "Default VPC" because of security compliance. You must build a custom Virtual Private Cloud (VPC), configure the networking plumbing (Subnets, Gateways, Routes), and launch a web server that auto-configures itself upon boot.

    Your Mission: Write a modular Terraform project that deploys a Custom VPC, a Public Subnet, an Internet Gateway, and an Apache Web Server. It must use Variables for reusability and Outputs to display the website URL.

    Architecture Overview

    The infrastructure consists of two distinct layers: The Network Layer (plumbing) and the Compute Layer (server).

    🌐

    1. The Network (VPC)

    Resource: aws_vpc
    CIDR: 10.0.0.0/16
    Components: Internet Gateway (IGW) for connectivity, Route Table for traffic routing, and one Public Subnet.

    🛡️

    2. The Firewall (SG)

    Resource: aws_security_group
    Rules: Allow HTTP (80) from anywhere (0.0.0.0/0) and SSH (22) from anywhere.

    💻

    3. The Server (EC2)

    Resource: aws_instance
    Config: Must run in the custom subnet. Must use user_data to install Apache and create a custom index.html.

    Technical Requirements

    1. Variable Usage

    Hardcoding is forbidden for these values. They must be defined in variables.tf:

    • aws_region (Default: us-east-1)
    • vpc_cidr (Default: 10.0.0.0/16)
    • instance_type (Default: t2.micro)
    • project_name (Default: SkyLaunch)

    2. Dynamic AMI Lookup

    Do not hardcode the AMI ID. Use a data source to find the latest "Amazon Linux 2023" or "Ubuntu 22.04" image automatically.

    3. Bootstrapping (User Data)

    The server must serve a custom HTML page immediately upon creation.
    Content: "

    Welcome to SkyLaunch - Deployed by [Your Name]

    "

    4. Outputs

    After `terraform apply`, the terminal must display:

    • vpc_id: The ID of the created VPC.
    • website_url: The full URL (e.g., http://54.123.45.67).

    Step-by-Step Implementation Guide

    Phase 1: Project Structure

    Create a folder `skylaunch`. Create empty files: `main.tf`, `network.tf`, `variables.tf`, `outputs.tf`, `providers.tf`.

    Phase 2: Networking (The Hard Part)

    In `network.tf`:
    1. Create aws_vpc.
    2. Create aws_internet_gateway attached to that VPC.
    3. Create aws_subnet (use CIDR 10.0.1.0/24). Crucial: Set map_public_ip_on_launch = true.
    4. Create aws_route_table pointing 0.0.0.0/0 to the Internet Gateway.
    5. Create aws_route_table_association to link the Subnet to the Route Table.

    Phase 3: Security & Compute

    In `main.tf`:
    1. Create the Security Group (allow port 80).
    2. Define the `data "aws_ami"` block.
    3. Create the `aws_instance`. Ensure `subnet_id` points to your custom subnet created in Phase 2. Pass the `user_data` script.

    Phase 4: Variables & Outputs

    Fill in `variables.tf` and `outputs.tf` to make the code clean and the result visible.

    Phase 5: Execution

    Run terraform init, terraform fmt, terraform validate, and finally terraform apply.
    Verification: Click the output URL.

    📋 Student Submission Checklist

    Before submitting your assignment, verify you have completed all items:

    Teacher's Solution Guide

    Use this reference to grade submissions. This represents the "Golden Path" solution.

    Solution: SkyLaunch Infrastructure

    1. variables.tf

    variable "aws_region" { default = "us-east-1" } variable "project_name" { default = "SkyLaunch" } variable "vpc_cidr" { default = "10.0.0.0/16" }

    2. network.tf (The VPC)
