    resource "aws_vpc" "main" { cidr_block = var.vpc_cidr enable_dns_hostnames = true tags = { Name = "${var.project_name}-vpc" } } resource "aws_internet_gateway" "gw" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id } resource "aws_subnet" "public" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24" map_public_ip_on_launch = true # Critical for public access availability_zone = "${var.aws_region}a" } resource "aws_route_table" "public" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id route { cidr_block = "0.0.0.0/0" gateway_id = aws_internet_gateway.gw.id } } resource "aws_route_table_association" "public" { subnet_id = aws_subnet.public.id route_table_id = aws_route_table.public.id }

    3. main.tf (Compute)

    provider "aws" { region = var.aws_region } # Find latest Amazon Linux 2023 data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux" { most_recent = true owners = ["amazon"] filter { name = "name" values = ["al2023-ami-2023.*-x86_64"] } } resource "aws_security_group" "web" { name = "${var.project_name}-sg" description = "Allow HTTP" vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id ingress { from_port = 80 to_port = 80 protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } # Allow outbound to install packages egress { from_port = 0 to_port = 0 protocol = "-1" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } } resource "aws_instance" "web" { ami = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id instance_type = "t2.micro" subnet_id = aws_subnet.public.id vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web.id] user_data = <<-EOF #!/bin/bash yum update -y yum install -y httpd systemctl start httpd systemctl enable httpd echo "

    Welcome to SkyLaunch - Deployed by Terraform

    " > /var/www/html/index.html EOF tags = { Name = "${var.project_name}-server" } }

    4. outputs.tf

    output "vpc_id" { value = aws_vpc.main.id } output "website_url" { value = "http://${aws_instance.web.public_ip}" }
    🚀 Week 10 Complete

    Congratulations! You are no longer clicking around the AWS Console. You have successfully defined a custom network and compute stack entirely in code. You understand State, Providers, and Resources.

    Next Week (Week 11): We will take Terraform to the next level. We will learn about Modules (reusable components), Remote State (team collaboration), and provisioning full Kubernetes clusters (EKS/AKS) via Terraform.

    Mentorship Review for Week 10

    Mentorship students: your Week 10 work (Infrastructure as Code with Terraform) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    11

    Advanced Terraform & Cloud Provisioning

    Modules, State Manipulation, and Production Architectures

    Welcome to Week 11. In Week 10, you learned the vocabulary of Terraform: Resources, Variables, and the basic Plan/Apply workflow. You built a single server. But in the real world, "Infrastructure" is rarely a single server. It is a complex web of Load Balancers, Auto Scaling Groups, Databases, and Networks, replicated across multiple environments (Dev, Staging, Production).

    This week, we move from "writing scripts" to "architecting systems." We will stop copying and pasting code and start building reusable Modules. We will tackle the hardest problems in Terraform: State Management (Remote Backends, Locking, and Importing existing resources), Control Logic (Loops, Conditionals, and Dynamic Blocks), and Refactoring live infrastructure without downtime. By the end of this week, you will be able to provision a complete, highly available 3-tier architecture with a single command.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Advanced Terraform & Cloud Provisioning: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Constructs: Define the syntax for `for_each`, `count`, and `dynamic` blocks. Identify the components required for a secure Remote Backend (State Storage + State Locking).

    Understand

    Explain Mechanics: Explain the difference between `local` values and `variables`. Describe how `terraform import` reconciles existing cloud resources into the state file.

    Apply

    Execute Refactoring: Refactor a monolithic `main.tf` into a modular directory structure. Use `for_each` to provision multiple subnets dynamically from a map variable.

    Analyze

    Diagnose State Issues: Analyze a "State Lock" error and resolve it safely. Debug a "Circular Dependency" error between two modules.

    Evaluate

    Assess Strategies: Evaluate the trade-offs between Terraform Workspaces vs. Directory-based environment separation. Critique the use of `provisioner "local-exec"` versus using `user_data`.

    Create

    Build Architecture: Architect a reusable "Web Server Cluster" module that includes an Application Load Balancer (ALB), Target Groups, and Auto Scaling Groups (ASG), parameterized for any environment.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Advanced Terraform requires setting up cloud storage for state and understanding control flow logic. Budget approximately 2-3 hours.

    1The "Chicken and Egg" Backend

    To use Terraform in a team, you need a Remote Backend (S3 + DynamoDB). You cannot easily use Terraform to manage the resources that hold Terraform's state (it creates a circular dependency). You must create them manually or via script.

    Task: Run these AWS CLI commands (or perform the equivalent in the AWS Console):

    # 1. Create S3 Bucket (Must be globally unique name) aws s3api create-bucket --bucket my-advanced-tf-state-[YOURNAME] --region us-east-1 # 2. Enable Versioning (CRITICAL for state recovery) aws s3api put-bucket-versioning --bucket my-advanced-tf-state-[YOURNAME] --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled # 3. Create DynamoDB Table for Locking aws dynamodb create-table \ --table-name terraform-locks \ --attribute-definitions AttributeName=LockID,AttributeType=S \ --key-schema AttributeName=LockID,KeyType=HASH \ --provisioned-throughput ReadCapacityUnits=1,WriteCapacityUnits=1
    ⚠️ Why Versioning?

    If your state file gets corrupted, S3 Versioning allows you to "Undo" and roll back to the previous state file. Without this, a corrupted state file can require a total infrastructure rebuild.

    2The "Import" Concept

    Scenario: Your boss manually created an S3 bucket named `legacy-data-bucket` via the AWS Console 3 years ago. They now want you to manage it with Terraform.

    Task: Create a dummy S3 bucket manually in the AWS Console. Then, research the `terraform import` command. Write down the syntax required to bring this existing bucket into a Terraform configuration without deleting and recreating it.

    3Logic Primer: Count vs. For_Each

    Terraform isn't a standard programming language, but it has loops.

    Research Task: Read the HashiCorp documentation on `count` and `for_each`.
    Scenario: You have a list of subnets: `["subnet-a", "subnet-b", "subnet-c"]`.
    Question: If you use `count` to create resources, what happens if you remove `subnet-b` from the middle of the list? (Hint: The index shifts). Why is `for_each` safer for lists that might change?

    4Install `tfenv` (Recommended)

    In professional environments, different projects use different versions of Terraform. Managing binaries manually is painful.

    Task: Install a Terraform version manager.

    # MacOS brew install tfenv # Linux git clone https://github.com/tfutils/tfenv.git ~/.tfenv echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.tfenv/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc

    Run tfenv install 1.9.0 and tfenv use 1.9.0 to standardize your version for this week.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    Moving from basic Terraform to advanced usage requires a shift in mindset. You are no longer writing a linear script; you are building a software application where the "compiler" is the Terraform Core and the "executable" is your Cloud Infrastructure.

    This section covers the four pillars of Advanced Terraform: Modularization (encapsulation), State Management (team safety), Control Logic (dynamic resource generation), and Brownfield Adoption (importing existing resources).

    11.1 The Module Architecture

    A Module is a container for multiple resources that are used together. Modules allow you to create abstractions. Instead of defining 5 resources to build a VPC (subnet, route table, gateway, association, vpc), you can define a single `module` block that handles it all.

    📦 Anatomy of a Module

    Every directory with `.tf` files is a module.
    1. Root Module: The directory where you run `terraform apply`.
    2. Child Module: A module called by another module.

    Data Flow:
    - Inputs: Data flows down into the child module via `variables`.
    - Outputs: Data flows up to the root module via `outputs`.

    # Directory Structure root/ ├── main.tf # Calls the module └── modules/ └── web_server/ # The Child Module ├── main.tf # Defines aws_instance, aws_security_group ├── variables.tf # Defines input: instance_type, ami_id └── outputs.tf # Defines output: public_ip # Calling the Module (in root/main.tf) module "frontend" { source = "./modules/web_server" instance_type = "t2.micro" # Passing input ami_id = "ami-0c55b159" } # Accessing the Output output "app_url" { value = module.frontend.public_ip }

    11.2 Remote State & Collaboration

    In Week 10, your state file (`terraform.tfstate`) lived on your laptop. In a team, this is disastrous. If your laptop dies, the state is lost. If your colleague applies changes, your state is outdated.

    The Solution: Remote Backends. We move the state file to a shared, durable storage location (AWS S3) and use a database (DynamoDB) to manage State Locking.

    The Locking Workflow

    1. Developer A runs `terraform apply`.
    2. Terraform calculates a checksum and writes a "Lock" item to the DynamoDB table.
    3. Developer B runs `terraform apply` at the same time.
    4. Terraform checks DynamoDB, sees the Lock, and aborts with:
      Error: State blob is already locked by Developer A.
    5. Developer A finishes. Terraform removes the Lock.
    6. Developer B can now proceed.

    11.3 Control Logic: Loops & Dynamics

    Terraform is declarative, but sometimes you need procedural logic (loops).

    1. Count vs. For_Each

    Both create multiple instances of a resource, but `for_each` is safer.

    🔢

    Count

    Uses an Array Index (0, 1, 2).
    resource "aws_instance" "web" { count = 3 }
    Risk: If you delete item #1, item #2 becomes #1. Terraform will destroy and recreate the wrong servers to match the new index order.

    🔠

    For_Each

    Uses a Map Key ("web", "api", "db").
    for_each = toset(["web", "api"])
    Benefit: Resources are identified by unique string keys. Adding/removing items does not shift the others.

    2. Dynamic Blocks

    Sometimes you need to loop inside a resource (e.g., creating multiple Ingress rules in a Security Group).

    resource "aws_security_group" "sg" { name = "dynamic-sg" # Loop over a list of ports dynamic "ingress" { for_each = [80, 443, 8080] content { from_port = ingress.value to_port = ingress.value protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } } }

    11.4 Dealing with Brownfield (Importing)

    Rarely do you start with a blank slate. Usually, there is existing infrastructure created manually. Terraform allows you to adopt these resources using `terraform import`.

    ⚠️ Import is Two Steps

    Running `terraform import` only updates the state file. It does not generate the configuration code (`main.tf`). You must write the `resource` block manually to match the real-world resource, or Terraform will try to destroy it on the next run.

    11.5 Managing Environments

    How do you deploy to Dev, Staging, and Prod using the same code?

    Approach A: Workspaces (The Terraform Native Way)
    CLI commands switch the backend state file.
    terraform workspace new dev -> `s3://bucket/env:/dev/tfstate`
    Pros: Easy setup. Cons: Easy to make mistakes (apply to prod while thinking you are in dev).

    Approach B: Directory Structure (The Industry Standard)
    Separate folders for each environment, referencing the same modules.
    /envs/dev/main.tf -> `module "app" { source = "../../modules/app" }`
    /envs/prod/main.tf -> `module "app" { source = "../../modules/app" }`
    Pros: Explicit separation. Cons: More boilerplate code.

    Hands-On Labs

    In these labs, you will perform the tasks of a Senior DevOps Engineer. You will not just "create infrastructure"; you will refactor existing code, secure state files against corruption, automate repetitive tasks with loops, and reconcile legacy infrastructure that wasn't created by Terraform.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    1. AWS Credentials: Ensure your terminal is authenticated (`aws configure` or env vars).
    2. Pre-Class Setup: You MUST have the S3 Bucket and DynamoDB Table created from the Pre-Class exercises.
    3. Workspace: Create a new directory `week11-labs` and cd into it.

    Lab 1: The "Module Refactor"

    Objective: Take a monolithic `main.tf` and split it into a reusable `web_server` module.

    Create Directory Structure:
    mkdir -p lab1/modules/web_server cd lab1 touch main.tf touch modules/web_server/{main.tf,variables.tf,outputs.tf}
    Step 1: Define the Child Module:

    We are creating a template for a standard web server.

    modules/web_server/variables.tf

    variable "server_name" { type = string } variable "instance_type" { type = string }

    modules/web_server/main.tf

    data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux" { most_recent = true owners = ["amazon"] filter { name = "name" values = ["al2023-ami-2023.*-x86_64"] } } resource "aws_instance" "web" { ami = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id instance_type = var.instance_type tags = { Name = var.server_name } }

    modules/web_server/outputs.tf

    output "public_ip" { value = aws_instance.web.public_ip }
    Step 2: Define the Root Module:

    Now, use the module to create two servers in lab1/main.tf.

    provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" } module "marketing_server" { source = "./modules/web_server" server_name = "marketing-prod" instance_type = "t2.micro" } module "finance_server" { source = "./modules/web_server" server_name = "finance-internal" instance_type = "t2.micro" } output "marketing_ip" { value = module.marketing_server.public_ip }
    Step 3: Init and Apply:
    terraform init # Note: "Initializing modules..." terraform apply -auto-approve

    Terraform creates 2 instances. Notice how you didn't have to duplicate the AMI lookup logic.

    Lab 2: Migrating to Remote Backend

    Objective: Move your local state file to S3 and verify that DynamoDB locking prevents concurrent modifications.

    Configure the Backend:

    Add this block to lab1/main.tf. Replace the bucket name with the one you created in Pre-Class exercises.

    terraform { backend "s3" { bucket = "my-advanced-tf-state-[YOURNAME]" # UPDATE THIS key = "lab1/terraform.tfstate" region = "us-east-1" dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks" encrypt = true } }
    Migrate State:
    terraform init
    Do you want to copy existing state to the new backend? Enter a value: yes

    Terraform uploads your local `terraform.tfstate` to S3. You can now delete the local file.

    Verify Locking (Simulation):

    We will simulate a collision.

    # Open Terminal Tab 1: terraform apply # (Wait at the "Enter a value" prompt. Do NOT type yes.) # Open Terminal Tab 2 (same directory): terraform plan
    Error: State blob is already locked Lock Info: ID: ...-uuid-... Operation: OperationTypeApply Who: user@hostname

    Result: Terraform protected you from corrupting the state.

    Cleanup:

    Cancel the apply in Tab 1 (`Ctrl+C`). Run terraform destroy -auto-approve.

    Lab 3: Advanced Control Logic

    Objective: Use `for_each` to create multiple IAM users and `dynamic` blocks to generate security group rules programmatically.

    Setup:
    mkdir ../lab3 cd ../lab3 touch main.tf
    The `for_each` User Loop:

    Create main.tf. We will create 3 IAM users from a Map.

    provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" } locals { users = { "alice" = "developer" "bob" = "manager" "charlie" = "intern" } } resource "aws_iam_user" "staff" { for_each = local.users name = each.key tags = { Role = each.value } }
    The `dynamic` Security Group:

    Append this to main.tf. We iterate over a list of ports to create ingress rules.

    variable "ingress_ports" { type = list(number) default = [80, 443, 8080, 22] } resource "aws_security_group" "web" { name = "dynamic-sg" dynamic "ingress" { for_each = var.ingress_ports content { from_port = ingress.value to_port = ingress.value protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } } }
    Apply and Inspect:
    terraform init && terraform apply -auto-approve

    Go to the AWS Console > IAM. You will see Alice, Bob, and Charlie. Go to EC2 > Security Groups. You will see "dynamic-sg" with 4 inbound rules.

    Cleanup:
    terraform destroy -auto-approve

    Lab 4: Importing "Brownfield" Infrastructure

    Objective: Simulate a "Legacy" resource created manually, and bring it under Terraform control without deleting/recreating it.

    Create a "Legacy" Resource:

    Use the CLI to create a Security Group manually (simulating a ClickOps engineer).

    aws ec2 create-security-group \ --group-name legacy-sg \ --description "Created manually" \ --region us-east-1
    { "GroupId": "sg-0123456789abcdef0" }

    Copy that GroupId. You will need it.

    Write the Terraform Config:

    Create `../lab4/main.tf`. We must define a resource block that matches the existing resource.

    # ../lab4/main.tf provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" } resource "aws_security_group" "legacy" { name = "legacy-sg" description = "Created manually" # Note: We haven't imported it yet, so Terraform thinks this is new. }
    The Import Command:
    cd ../lab4 terraform init # Syntax: terraform import
    terraform import aws_security_group.legacy sg-0123456789abcdef0
    Import successful!
    Verify Sync (Plan):
    terraform plan
    No changes. Your infrastructure matches the configuration.
    💡 What just happened?

    Terraform pulled the real-world configuration of `sg-xxxx` and wrote it into `terraform.tfstate`. It then compared `main.tf` to the state. Since the names matched, it reported "No changes". You have successfully adopted the resource.

    Cleanup:
    terraform destroy -auto-approve

    Lab Solutions & Troubleshooting Guide

    This section provides the "answer key" for the Advanced Terraform labs. It dissects the behaviors you observed—why the lock prevented your apply, how the module passed data back to the root, and why `for_each` is preferred over `count` for infrastructure stability.

    Lab 1 Solution: Module Refactoring

    The Module Contract

    In Lab 1, you successfully decoupled the implementation logic (the child module) from the configuration logic (the root module).

    # Root Module Call module "marketing_server" { source = "./modules/web_server" server_name = "marketing-prod" # <-- Input } # Root Output output "ip" { value = module.marketing_server.public_ip # <-- Output }

    Architectural Insight:
    Think of a module as a "Black Box." The Root module does not know that the child module is looking up an AMI ID or creating a Security Group. It only knows: "I give you a name, you give me an IP." This encapsulation allows you to swap out the implementation later (e.g., switching from Ubuntu to Amazon Linux) without changing the Root module's code.

    Lab 2 Solution: Remote State Locking

    The Mechanics of Locking

    Observation: When you ran `terraform plan` in the second tab, it failed immediately.

    Error: State blob is already locked Who: user@hostname ID: 7e6a5b4c-3d2e-1f0a...

    What happened?
    1. Tab 1 sent a `PutItem` request to DynamoDB creating a record with key `lab1/terraform.tfstate-md5`.
    2. Tab 2 attempted to acquire the lock but saw the record existed.
    3. This proves that Terraform operations are Mutually Exclusive (Atomic) when using a proper backend. This prevents "Race Conditions" where two engineers corrupt the infrastructure by modifying it simultaneously.

    ⚠️ Force Unlock

    If Terraform crashes mid-apply (e.g., your laptop runs out of battery), the lock might stay in DynamoDB forever. You can remove it with terraform force-unlock <LOCK_ID>. Only do this if you are absolutely sure no other process is running.

    Lab 3 Solution: Control Logic

    Why `for_each` Wins

    We used for_each = local.users (a Map).
    If we had used count with a List `["alice", "bob", "charlie"]`:
    - Alice = index 0
    - Bob = index 1
    - Charlie = index 2

    If we delete "Bob":
    - Alice = index 0 (No change)
    - Charlie = index 1 (CHANGED!)

    Terraform would see that Index 1 changed from Bob to Charlie. It might destroy Bob's resource and create Charlie's there. With for_each, resources are keyed by string ("bob"), so removing Bob only affects Bob.

    Dynamic Blocks

    The dynamic "ingress" block saved us from writing the `ingress { ... }` stanza 4 times. This keeps code DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself), but use it sparingly. Excessive dynamic blocks can make code hard to read.

    Lab 4 Solution: Importing Resources

    The State vs. Config Gap

    After running terraform import, your terraform.tfstate file contained all the details of the Security Group (ID, VPC ID, Rules).

    However, your main.tf only contained:

    resource "aws_security_group" "legacy" { name = "legacy-sg" }

    If you ran terraform plan immediately after import, Terraform might say: "Plan: 0 to add, 1 to change."
    Why? Because the real SG has a description ("Created manually"), but your code didn't specify one. Terraform defaults description to "Managed by Terraform". It would try to overwrite the description.
    The Lesson: Import is just step 1. Step 2 is manually updating your code to match the state exactly so that `terraform plan` shows "No Changes".

    Practice Questions

    These questions mimic the format of the HashiCorp Certified: Terraform Associate exam, specifically targeting advanced concepts.

    When using `for_each` to create multiple resources, which variable type is supported?
    A. String
    B. Integer
    C. Map or Set of Strings
    D. Boolean
    Answer: C. `for_each` requires a collection with unique keys (Map) or unique values (Set). It cannot iterate over a simple List directly unless you convert it with `toset()`.
    You have an existing S3 bucket not managed by Terraform. You want to bring it under management without deleting it. Which command do you use?
    A. terraform adopt
    B. terraform import
    C. terraform refresh
    D. terraform state push
    Answer: B. `terraform import [ADDRESS] [ID]` maps a real-world resource ID to a resource block in your configuration state.
    Which block is used to configure settings for the Terraform behavior itself, such as the required version or backend?
    A. provider {}
    B. resource {}
    C. terraform {}
    D. config {}
    Answer: C. The `terraform` block is where you configure the backend (S3), required_providers (AWS, Docker), and required_version (CLI version).
    How do you refer to the current key in a `dynamic` block loop?
    A. item.value
    B. [BLOCK_NAME].value (e.g., ingress.value)
    C. this.value
    D. self.value
    Answer: B. Inside `dynamic "ingress"`, the iterator object is named `ingress`. You access the current item via `ingress.value` or `ingress.key`.
    What is the primary risk of using `local-exec` provisioners?
    A. They are slow
    B. They break idempotency and state tracking
    C. They require AWS credentials
    D. They cannot run shell scripts
    Answer: B. Terraform does not track what happens inside a script. If the script fails halfway, Terraform might still mark the resource as "Created". It makes the infrastructure state unpredictable. Use `user_data` or configuration management (Ansible) instead.
    You want to define a value that is calculated inside the module and reused in multiple places, but NOT exposed to the user. What do you use?
    A. Variable
    B. Local Value (locals { ... })
    C. Output
    D. Data Source
    Answer: B. Locals are like private variables or constants within a module. They help reduce repetition and simplify complex expressions.
    Your team uses S3 for remote state. What must you enable on the S3 bucket to allow recovery from accidental state corruption?
    A. Static Website Hosting
    B. Versioning
    C. Transfer Acceleration
    D. Public Access Logging
    Answer: B. S3 Versioning keeps a history of every write. If a bad `terraform apply` corrupts the state file, you can restore the previous version from the S3 console.
    What happens if you define a variable in a child module but do not pass a value for it in the root module, and it has no default?
    A. Terraform uses an empty string
    B. Terraform ignores the variable
    C. Terraform errors and stops
    D. Terraform asks the user interactively
    Answer: C. (Assuming non-interactive run). If running interactively, it might prompt, but in CI/CD or automation, it is a hard error. Variables without defaults are mandatory arguments.
    Which file should be added to `.gitignore` to prevent secret leakage?
    A. main.tf
    B. variables.tf
    C. terraform.tfstate (and .tfvars if it contains secrets)
    D. outputs.tf
    Answer: C. The state file contains all resource data (including passwords/keys) in plain text. `terraform.tfvars` often contains sensitive inputs. Neither should be committed to version control.
    What is the purpose of the `.terraform.lock.hcl` file?
    A. It locks the state file
    B. It locks the versions of the providers used
    C. It prevents other users from running plan
    D. It stores API keys
    Answer: B. Introduced in Terraform 0.14, this file records the exact SHA256 hashes of the provider binaries used. It ensures that every team member uses the exact same plugin version, preventing "works on my machine" issues.

    Week-Ending Assignment: Project "Titan" (3-Tier Architecture)

    🎯 Assignment Objective

    You have been tasked with building the infrastructure for "Project Titan," a high-traffic web application. Single servers are no longer sufficient. The requirement is High Availability and Scalability.

    Your Mission: Architect a reusable Terraform codebase that deploys a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) and an Auto Scaling Group (ASG) behind an Application Load Balancer (ALB).

    The critical constraint: You must build this using Modules for the VPC and Compute layers, and you must manage the state remotely to prevent corruption.

    Architecture Overview

    The system handles failure by distributing traffic across two Availability Zones.

    🗂️

    1. The Modules

    Networking Module: Deploys VPC, Internet Gateway, and 2 Public Subnets using `for_each`.
    Compute Module: Deploys Launch Template, ASG, and ALB.

    🔐

    2. The State

    Remote Backend: Configuration must point to your S3 Bucket and DynamoDB Table. Local state is forbidden.

    🔁

    3. The Logic

    Control Flow: Use dynamic blocks for Security Group rules and data sources for AMI lookups.

    Technical Requirements

    1. Project Structure

    Your repository must follow this clean structure:

    project-titan/ ├── main.tf # The Root Module (calls the others) ├── variables.tf # Global variables ├── backend.tf # S3 configuration └── modules/ ├── networking/ # Child Module 1 └── compute/ # Child Module 2

    2. The Networking Module

    • Input: vpc_cidr, public_subnets (Map of CIDRs).
    • Logic: Use for_each to create subnets based on the input Map.
    • Output: vpc_id, public_subnet_ids.

    3. The Compute Module

    • Input: vpc_id, subnet_ids.
    • Resources:
      - Security Group: Allow HTTP (80) from anywhere.
      - Launch Template: T2.micro, Amazon Linux 2, User Data installing Apache.
      - Auto Scaling Group: Min 2, Max 4 instances.

    Step-by-Step Implementation Guide

    Phase 1: Foundation

    Create the directories. Setup backend.tf with your bucket details. Run terraform init.

    Phase 2: Networking Module

    Write modules/networking/main.tf.
    Key Challenge: Ensure your `aws_subnet` resource uses for_each and availability_zone properly.
    Export: You must output the Subnet IDs so the Compute module can place servers in them.

    Phase 3: Compute Module

    Write modules/compute/main.tf.
    Use a data "aws_ami" block to find the latest image.
    Define the aws_launch_template with a script that writes "Hello from Titan" to `index.html`.
    Define the aws_autoscaling_group pointing to the subnets passed in from variables.

    Phase 4: Wiring the Root

    In the root `main.tf`, call the modules.
    Pass the Output of Networking as the Input of Compute.
    Example: subnet_ids = module.networking.public_subnets.

    Phase 5: Validation

    Run terraform plan. Verify it proposes creating ~10 resources.
    Run terraform apply.
    Check the AWS Console: Do you see 2 Instances? Are they in different AZs?

    📋 Student Submission Checklist

    Before submitting your assignment, verify you have completed all items:

    Teacher's Solution Guide

    Use this reference to grade submissions.

    Solution: Project Titan

    1. Networking Module (modules/networking/main.tf)

    resource "aws_vpc" "main" { cidr_block = var.vpc_cidr tags = { Name = "titan-vpc" } } resource "aws_internet_gateway" "igw" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id } resource "aws_subnet" "public" { for_each = var.public_subnets # Map: { "us-east-1a" = "10.0.1.0/24", ... } vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id cidr_block = each.value availability_zone = each.key map_public_ip_on_launch = true } # Output Subnet IDs for Compute Module output "public_subnet_ids" { value = [for s in aws_subnet.public : s.id] } output "vpc_id" { value = aws_vpc.main.id }

    2. Compute Module (modules/compute/main.tf)

    resource "aws_launch_template" "web" { name_prefix = "titan-web-" image_id = var.ami_id instance_type = "t2.micro" user_data = base64encode(<<-EOF #!/bin/bash yum install -y httpd systemctl start httpd echo "Hello from Titan" > /var/www/html/index.html EOF ) vpc_security_group_ids = [var.security_group_id] } resource "aws_autoscaling_group" "bar" { desired_capacity = 2 max_size = 4 min_size = 2 vpc_zone_identifier = var.subnet_ids # List from Networking launch_template { id = aws_launch_template.web.id version = "$Latest" } }

    3. Root Module (main.tf)

    # Backend terraform { backend "s3" { bucket = "my-bucket" key = "titan/tfstate" region = "us-east-1" } } provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" } # Call Networking module "network" { source = "./modules/networking" vpc_cidr = "10.0.0.0/16" public_subnets = { "us-east-1a" = "10.0.1.0/24" "us-east-1b" = "10.0.2.0/24" } } # Security Group (Global) resource "aws_security_group" "web" { name = "titan-sg" vpc_id = module.network.vpc_id ingress { from_port = 80 to_port = 80 protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } egress { from_port = 0 to_port = 0 protocol = "-1" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } } # Call Compute module "compute" { source = "./modules/compute" vpc_id = module.network.vpc_id subnet_ids = module.network.public_subnet_ids security_group_id = aws_security_group.web.id ami_id = "ami-0c7217cdde317cfec" # Amazon Linux 2023 }
    🚀 Week 11 Complete

    Congratulations! You have mastered the "Infrastructure Architect" skillset. You know how to encapsulate logic into modules, secure state files, and deploy complex, multi-tiered applications programmatically.

    Next Week (Week 12): We reach the final frontier: GitOps & Advanced CI/CD. We will combine Docker, Kubernetes, and Terraform into a unified pipeline where infrastructure and applications are deployed automatically by pushing to Git.

    Mentorship Review for Week 11

    Mentorship students: your Week 11 work (Advanced Terraform & Cloud Provisioning) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    12

    GitOps & Advanced CI/CD

    The Grand Finale: Automating the Entire Lifecycle

    Welcome to the final week of the DevOps curriculum. Over the last 11 weeks, you have mastered the individual tools of the trade: Linux, Docker, Kubernetes, Helm, and Terraform. You know how to package an app, how to define infrastructure as code, and how to orchestrate containers.

    But currently, these are disjointed skills. You manually run `terraform apply`. You manually run `helm install`. You manually build Docker images. In a high-performing organization, humans do not deploy software; pipelines do.

    This week, we introduce the modern paradigm of GitOps. We will combine everything you have learned into a unified, self-healing system where Git is the single source of truth. If code is committed to the repository, it is deployed to the cluster automatically. If someone manually changes the cluster, the system detects the "Drift" and heals itself. You will finish this course by building a fully automated "Commit-to-Cloud" pipeline.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for GitOps & Advanced CI/CD: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Core Principles: Define the 4 Principles of GitOps: Declarative, Versioned, Automated Delivery, and Continuous Reconciliation.

    Understand

    Explain Architectures: Contrast "Push-based" CD (Jenkins/GitHub Actions scripting `kubectl apply`) vs. "Pull-based" CD (ArgoCD/Flux watching Git).

    Apply

    Execute Deployment: Install ArgoCD into a Kubernetes cluster and configure it to synchronize an application from a GitHub repository automatically.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Sync Issues: Analyze synchronization failures (e.g., "Out of Sync," "Pruning errors") and debug connectivity issues between the Cluster and the Git Repo.

    Evaluate

    Assess Security: Evaluate the security benefits of Pull-based CD (no cluster credentials stored in CI) compared to Push-based CD.

    Create

    Build the Pipeline: Construct a complete CI/CD workflow: GitHub Actions builds the image -> Updates the Helm Chart version in Git -> ArgoCD detects the change -> Deploys to Production.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. This week requires a running Kubernetes cluster and a GitHub account. Budget approximately 2 hours.

    1Prepare the Cluster

    You need a Kubernetes cluster to install the GitOps operator (ArgoCD). You can use Minikube, Docker Desktop, or the EKS cluster you built in Week 11 (if you are watching costs carefully).

    Task: Ensure your `kubectl` context is active and you have admin rights.

    # Recommended for cost savings: Minikube minikube start --cpus 4 --memory 6144 # Verify access kubectl cluster-info

    2The Sample Repository

    GitOps requires a Git repository to act as the "Source of Truth."

    Task:
    1. Create a new public GitHub repository named gitops-cert-manager.
    2. We will use this repo to store Kubernetes manifests that ArgoCD will watch.
    3. Initialize it with a `README.md`.

    3Install ArgoCD CLI

    While ArgoCD has a UI, the CLI is useful for automation and debugging.

    # MacOS brew install argocd # Linux curl -sSL -o argocd-linux-amd64 https://github.com/argoproj/argo-cd/releases/latest/download/argocd-linux-amd64 sudo install -m 555 argocd-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/argocd rm argocd-linux-amd64

    4Research: Push vs. Pull

    Context: Traditional CI/CD (Jenkins, GitLab CI) uses a "Push" model. The CI server runs a script that executes `kubectl apply`. GitOps uses a "Pull" model.

    Thought Experiment: If your Jenkins server is hacked, and it has `kubectl` admin credentials stored in it, what can the attacker do?
    Contrast this with ArgoCD running inside the cluster, pulling read-only public data from GitHub. Which is more secure?

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    GitOps is the operational framework that takes DevOps best practices used for application development (version control, collaboration, compliance, CI tools) and applies them to infrastructure automation.

    In simple terms: Git is the single source of truth. If it isn't in Git, it shouldn't exist in the cluster.

    12.1 The 4 Principles of GitOps

    GitOps isn't just "storing YAML in Git." To truly practice GitOps, you must adhere to these four standards defined by the OpenGitOps working group:

    📜 The GitOps Manifesto
    • 1. Declarative: The entire system must be described declaratively (Kubernetes YAML, Helm, Terraform). No imperative scripts ("run this, then that").
    • 2. Versioned & Immutable: The desired state is stored in a version control system (Git). Changes are immutable versions (commits).
    • 3. Automated Delivery: Approved changes can be automatically applied to the system.
    • 4. Software Agents: Software agents (like ArgoCD or Flux) run continuously to ensure the current state matches the desired state. If they drift, the agent attempts to reconcile (heal) them.

    12.2 Architecture: Push vs. Pull

    Understanding the difference between Traditional CI/CD (Push) and GitOps (Pull) is critical for architectural decisions.

    👉

    Push-Based (Traditional)

    Mechanism: The CI tool (Jenkins/GitHub Actions) executes a script: kubectl apply -f manifest.yaml.
    Security Risk: The CI server requires "God Mode" credentials (Kubeconfig) to the Production Cluster. If Jenkins is hacked, Production is compromised.
    Drift: "Fire and Forget." If someone manually changes a deployment 1 hour later, Jenkins has no idea.

    👈

    Pull-Based (GitOps)

    Mechanism: An operator (ArgoCD) lives inside the cluster. It polls the Git Repo. If it sees a change, it pulls it down and applies it.
    Security Benefit: No cluster credentials exist outside the cluster. The cluster only needs read-only access to GitHub.
    Drift: Continuous. If someone manually changes the cluster, ArgoCD detects it instantly and marks the app as "OutOfSync" (and can auto-revert it).

    12.3 ArgoCD Fundamentals

    ArgoCD is a Kubernetes controller. It uses a Custom Resource Definition (CRD) called Application to manage deployments.

    apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1 kind: Application metadata: name: guestbook namespace: argocd spec: project: default # 1. The Source (Git) source: repoURL: https://github.com/argoproj/argocd-example-apps.git targetRevision: HEAD path: guestbook # 2. The Destination (Cluster) destination: server: https://kubernetes.default.svc namespace: guestbook # 3. Automation Policy syncPolicy: automated: prune: true # Delete resources if they are removed from Git selfHeal: true # Revert manual changes in the cluster
    💡 "Pruning"

    By default, if you delete a file from Git, ArgoCD might not delete the resource from the cluster (for safety). Enabling prune: true ensures that file deletion in Git equals resource deletion in K8s, making Git a true 1:1 mirror of reality.

    12.4 The Complete CI/CD Pipeline

    In a GitOps world, "CI" and "CD" are decoupled.
    CI (Continuous Integration): Run tests, Build Docker Image, Push to Registry.
    CD (Continuous Delivery): Update the Kubernetes Manifest in Git to reference the new image tag.

    The "Image Updater" Workflow

    1. Developer: Pushes code change to app-repo.
    2. GitHub Actions (CI):
      - Runs Unit Tests.
      - Builds Docker Image myapp:v1.2.
      - Pushes Image to Docker Hub.
    3. GitHub Actions (CD Handoff):
      - Clones the config-repo (where YAMLs live).
      - Uses sed or yq to update deployment.yaml from image: myapp:v1.1 to image: myapp:v1.2.
      - Commits and Pushes to config-repo.
    4. ArgoCD: Detects the new commit in config-repo.
    5. ArgoCD: Syncs the cluster. K8s performs a Rolling Update to pull the new image.

    12.5 Managing Secrets (The "Elephant in the Room")

    You cannot commit `secret.yaml` with DB passwords to a public Git repo. But GitOps says "Everything in Git." How do we solve this paradox?

    Solution 1: Bitnami Sealed Secrets
    You encrypt the secret locally using a public key (`kubeseal`). The result is a `SealedSecret` CRD safe to commit to Git. Only the controller running inside the cluster (which has the private key) can decrypt it.

    Solution 2: External Secrets Operator (Enterprise)
    You store secrets in AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault. You commit an `ExternalSecret` object that says "Go fetch the DB password from AWS Vault X". The operator fetches the value and injects it as a Kubernetes Secret.

    Hands-On Labs

    This is the capstone of your training. You will install the ArgoCD operator, creating a "GitOps Control Plane" inside your Kubernetes cluster. You will then deploy applications not by running `kubectl`, but by pushing code to GitHub. Finally, you will simulate a full CI/CD pipeline where a code change automatically triggers a rollout.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    1. Kubernetes Cluster: A running cluster (Minikube/EKS/Docker Desktop).
    2. GitHub Repo: The gitops-cert-manager repo created in Pre-Class exercises.
    3. CLI Tools: `kubectl` and `argocd` installed.

    Lab 1: Bootstrap the GitOps Engine

    Objective: Install ArgoCD manifests into the cluster and access the Web UI.

    Create Namespace & Install:

    ArgoCD is just a set of Kubernetes resources (Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps). We install them from the official manifest.

    kubectl create namespace argocd kubectl apply -n argocd -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/argoproj/argo-cd/stable/manifests/install.yaml
    Verify Installation:
    kubectl get pods -n argocd

    Wait until all pods (server, repo-server, application-controller, dex, redis) are Running.

    Access the UI (Port Forward):

    Since we don't have an Ingress Controller set up yet, we will tunnel to the service.

    kubectl port-forward svc/argocd-server -n argocd 8080:443

    Leave this terminal open. Open a new terminal for the next steps.

    Login:

    Open https://localhost:8080 in your browser. Accept the security risk (self-signed cert).

    Default Username: admin

    Get Initial Password:

    kubectl -n argocd get secret argocd-initial-admin-secret -o jsonpath="{.data.password}" | base64 -d; echo

    Lab 2: The "Application" Manifest

    Objective: Instead of clicking buttons in the UI, we will define our deployment requirement as a Kubernetes object (IaC).

    Prepare the Git Repo:

    Clone your gitops-cert-manager repo. Create a folder `dev` and add a simple Nginx deployment.

    mkdir dev # Create dev/deployment.yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: my-app spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: my-app template: metadata: labels: app: my-app spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:1.14.2 ports: - containerPort: 80 --- # Create dev/service.yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: my-app spec: selector: app: my-app ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 80

    Commit and Push these files to GitHub.

    Define the Argo Application:

    Create a file argocd-app.yaml locally. Replace the repoURL with YOUR GitHub URL.

    apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1 kind: Application metadata: name: my-gitops-app namespace: argocd spec: project: default source: repoURL: https://github.com/[YOUR_USERNAME]/gitops-cert-manager.git targetRevision: main path: dev destination: server: https://kubernetes.default.svc namespace: default syncPolicy: automated: prune: true selfHeal: true
    Apply the Application:
    kubectl apply -f argocd-app.yaml

    Check the ArgoCD UI. You should see "my-gitops-app" appear. It will initially be "OutOfSync", then "Syncing", then "Healthy" (green).

    Verify in Kubernetes:
    kubectl get pods

    You will see the my-app pod running. ArgoCD created it for you.

    Lab 3: Testing Self-Healing

    Objective: Manually sabotage the cluster state and watch ArgoCD fix it automatically.

    The Sabotage:

    Manually delete the service via kubectl (Simulating an accidental deletion by a junior engineer).

    kubectl delete service my-app
    The Reaction:

    1. Quickly switch to the ArgoCD UI.
    2. You might briefly see the status turn yellow (OutOfSync).
    3. Because we set selfHeal: true, ArgoCD immediately re-applies the manifest from Git.

    Verify Healing:
    kubectl get service my-app
    NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE my-app ClusterIP 10.96.100.100 80/TCP 5s

    Notice the AGE is only a few seconds. It was resurrected.

    Lab 4: Simulating the Pipeline

    Objective: Simulate the workflow of a developer updating the application version. We will update the image tag in Git, and verify the rolling update in the cluster.

    Watch the Pods:

    Open a terminal to watch the rollout.

    kubectl get pods -w
    Update Git (The Trigger):

    Edit dev/deployment.yaml in your local repo.
    Change image: nginx:1.14.2 to image: nginx:1.21 (a newer version).

    git add . git commit -m "Upgrade Nginx to 1.21" git push origin main
    Observe Synchronization:

    By default, ArgoCD polls Git every 3 minutes. To speed it up, click "Refresh" in the UI.

    In your terminal: You will see the old pod Terminating and a new pod ContainerCreating.

    Verify Version:
    # Get the image name of the running pod kubectl get pod -l app=my-app -o jsonpath="{.items[0].spec.containers[0].image}"
    nginx:1.21

    Lab Solutions & Troubleshooting Guide

    This section provides the "answer key" for the GitOps labs. It explains the architectural decisions behind ArgoCD, how the synchronization loop works under the hood, and why "Self-Healing" is both a superpower and a potential danger.

    Lab 1 Solution: ArgoCD Architecture

    The Components

    When you installed the manifest, you deployed several distinct microservices:

    • argocd-server: The API server and Web UI. This is what you port-forwarded to.
    • argocd-repo-server: A background worker that clones Git repositories and generates manifests (running helm template or kustomize build internally).
    • argocd-application-controller: The "Brain." It compares the live state (K8s) vs. the desired state (Git) and executes the reconciliation.
    • argocd-dex-server: An identity provider for handling SSO (Single Sign-On).

    Why Port Forward?

    We used kubectl port-forward because we hadn't set up an Ingress Controller or LoadBalancer service for ArgoCD. In production, you would expose ArgoCD via an Ingress resource (e.g., argocd.example.com) secured with TLS, likely configured in the install.yaml values.

    Lab 2 Solution: The Application CRD

    Destination Logic

    destination: server: https://kubernetes.default.svc namespace: default

    Why that URL? ArgoCD is running inside the cluster. https://kubernetes.default.svc is the internal DNS name for the Kubernetes API server. This tells ArgoCD: "Deploy this application to the same cluster you are running in."
    If you were managing a multi-cluster setup (e.g., ArgoCD in a "Management" cluster deploying to "Prod" and "Staging" clusters), this URL would point to the external API endpoints of those downstream clusters.

    Lab 3 Solution: Drift & Healing

    The Reconciliation Loop

    When you deleted the service my-app manually:
    1. The Application Controller (polling K8s API) noticed the Service was missing.
    2. It checked the Git Repo cache and saw service.yaml still exists.
    3. Status: Changed to OutOfSync (Drift detected).
    4. Action: Because selfHeal: true is set, it immediately ran kubectl apply to restore the missing object.

    ⚠️ The Danger of Self-Heal

    In Production, some teams disable selfHeal. Why?
    Imagine you have a bug in production and you need to quickly hotfix it by manually editing a ConfigMap or scaling down a deployment to 0. If selfHeal is on, ArgoCD will fight you, instantly reverting your emergency fix. It is common to leave it off for Prod or to use "Maintenance Windows" where sync is paused.

    Lab 4 Solution: Pipeline Flow

    Git as the Trigger

    Traditional CD pipelines rely on a "Webhook" from the CI server to say "Deploy Now!"
    GitOps relies on Polling (or Webhooks to refresh the cache). The deployment wasn't triggered by a "command"; it was triggered by a "fact." The fact was: "The desired state of the image tag is now 1.21."

    This provides an excellent audit trail. If the deployment broke, you can look at the Git log:
    commit a1b2c3d: Upgrade Nginx to 1.21
    You know exactly who did it, when, and why. To rollback, you simply run git revert.

    Practice Questions

    These questions test your understanding of GitOps principles, security implications, and ArgoCD mechanics.

    Which of the following defines the "Source of Truth" in a GitOps architecture?
    A. The Kubernetes Etcd database
    B. The Git Repository
    C. The CI Server (Jenkins)
    D. The Docker Registry
    Answer: B. In GitOps, the desired state is versioned in Git. The cluster (Etcd) is merely the actual state which tries to converge to match Git.
    What is the primary security advantage of "Pull-based" CD (ArgoCD) over "Push-based" CD (Jenkins)?
    A. It is faster
    B. Cluster credentials are not stored outside the cluster
    C. It supports Helm charts
    D. It uses SSH keys
    Answer: B. In Push-based CD, the external CI server needs admin access to the cluster (a high-value target for attackers). In Pull-based CD, the operator is already inside the cluster and only needs read-access to Git.
    What happens if prune: true is NOT enabled in the sync policy?
    A. ArgoCD cannot sync at all
    B. Resources deleted from Git are NOT deleted from the cluster
    C. Resources are deleted immediately
    D. ArgoCD deletes the Git repo
    Answer: B. Without pruning, ArgoCD behaves additively. If you remove a Service from your Git repo, it becomes an "Orphaned Resource" in the cluster but stays running. Enabling pruning ensures strict 1:1 mirroring.
    You want to roll back a deployment that broke production. What is the "GitOps way" to do this?
    A. Run kubectl rollout undo manually
    B. Click "Rollback" in the ArgoCD UI
    C. Revert the commit in Git (`git revert`) and push
    D. Delete the namespace
    Answer: C. While you can click buttons in the UI (Option B), that drift will be overwritten by the next sync if Git hasn't changed. The correct, persistent fix is to revert the configuration in the Source of Truth (Git).
    How does ArgoCD detect "Drift"?
    A. It receives an email from AWS
    B. It periodically compares the live K8s manifest against the manifest generated from Git
    C. It relies on `kubectl diff` running on your laptop
    D. It doesn't; you must check manually
    Answer: B. The Application Controller runs a continuous loop (default every 3 mins) fetching the Git state and the K8s state, calculating the difference.
    Why can you not commit raw `Secret` YAML files (with passwords) to a public Git repository?
    A. Git does not support YAML
    B. They are base64 encoded, not encrypted, so anyone can decode them
    C. Kubernetes rejects them
    D. ArgoCD cannot read Secrets
    Answer: B. Base64 is encoding, not encryption. `echo "cGFzc3dvcmQ=" | base64 -d` reveals the password instantly. You must use tools like Sealed Secrets or External Secrets to encrypt data at rest in Git.
    What is the "App of Apps" pattern?
    A. An app that monitors other apps
    B. A single ArgoCD Application that points to a folder containing other Application YAMLs
    C. A microservice architecture
    D. A mobile app for ArgoCD
    Answer: B. This pattern allows you to bootstrap an entire cluster (50 microservices) by deploying just one "Root Application." That root app deploys the child apps, which deploy the actual services. It makes cluster bootstrapping recursive and automated.
    If you manually edit a Deployment in the cluster to increase replicas from 2 to 5, and selfHeal is disabled, what does ArgoCD show?
    A. Healthy
    B. OutOfSync
    C. Synced
    D. Error
    Answer: B. It detects the drift (Live=5, Git=2) and marks it OutOfSync, but it will not revert it automatically unless you click "Sync" or enable self-healing.
    Which tool generates the manifests if your Git repo contains a Helm Chart?
    A. Helm CLI installed on your laptop
    B. argocd-repo-server
    C. Tiller
    D. Docker
    Answer: B. The repo-server component inside the ArgoCD pod handles the rendering logic (running `helm template` or `kustomize build`) to convert high-level templates into raw YAML for comparison.
    What is a "Sync Wave"?
    A. A hand gesture
    B. A network protocol
    C. An ordering mechanism to deploy resources in a specific sequence
    D. A failure mode
    Answer: C. By annotating resources with `argocd.argoproj.io/sync-wave`, you can control order. Wave -1 deploys before Wave 0, which deploys before Wave 1. This is useful for deploying a Database (Wave 0) before the App (Wave 1) that connects to it.

    Week-Ending Assignment: The "GitOps Loop" Pipeline

    🎯 Assignment Objective

    You have mastered the individual components of the DevOps toolkit. Now, you must connect them into a unified, automated system.

    The Scenario: Your development team writes code in a `source-repo`. They do not have access to the cluster. They do not know Kubectl. Your job is to build a pipeline where a Developer pushes a commit to `main`, and within 2 minutes, that code is running in Production without human intervention.

    Your Mission: Connect GitHub Actions (CI) to ArgoCD (CD) using the "Config Repo" pattern. This assignment bridges the gap between your CI build server and your GitOps operator.

    Architecture Overview

    To achieve true GitOps, you need two separate repositories to separate "App Code" from "Infrastructure Config."

    🏗️

    1. The Source Repo

    Contains Python/Go/Node code + Dockerfile.
    CI Job: Build Docker Image -> Push to Registry -> Trigger Update in Config Repo.

    📜

    2. The Config Repo

    Contains Kubernetes YAMLs (Deployment/Service).
    State: This repo holds the "Desired State" (e.g., image: myapp:v1.0).

    🐙

    3. The GitOps Operator

    ArgoCD: Watches the Config Repo. Syncs changes to the Cluster.

    Technical Requirements

    1. Repository Setup

    • Repo A (Source): Simple HTML app ("Hello World v1").
    • Repo B (Config): Kubernetes Deployment YAML.

    2. The CI Script (GitHub Actions)

    Create a workflow .github/workflows/ci.yaml in Repo A.
    Logic:
    1. Build Docker Image tagging it with the Git SHA (`myapp:sha-123abc4`).
    2. Push to Docker Hub/GHCR.
    3. Check out Repo B.
    4. Use `sed` or `yq` to update the tag in `deployment.yaml`.
    5. Commit and Push change to Repo B.

    3. ArgoCD Configuration

    Deploy an ArgoCD `Application` pointing to Repo B.
    Enable automated.selfHeal and automated.prune.

    Step-by-Step Implementation Guide

    Phase 1: Manual Prep

    Create the two repos. Manually push a `deployment.yaml` to Repo B. Configure ArgoCD to sync it. Ensure the app is running.

    Phase 2: The CI Connection

    In Repo A, create the GitHub Action. You will need a Personal Access Token (PAT) stored as a Secret (`GIT_PAT`) to allow Repo A's workflow to push commits to Repo B.

    Phase 3: The Trigger

    Change "Hello World v1" to "Hello World v2" in Repo A. Push to main.

    Phase 4: Verification

    1. Check GitHub Actions: Did the build pass?
    2. Check Repo B: Did the commit appear "Update image to sha-xxxx"?
    3. Check ArgoCD: Did it sync?
    4. Check Browser: Does the app say "Hello World v2"?

    📋 Student Submission Checklist

    Before submitting your assignment, verify you have completed all items:

    Teacher's Solution Guide

    Solution: The "Update Image" Action

    The hardest part of this assignment is the yq update logic in GitHub Actions. Below is a production-ready workflow pattern.

    name: Build and Deploy on: push: branches: [ main ] jobs: build-and-push: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v2 # 1. Build Docker Image - name: Login to DockerHub uses: docker/login-action@v1 with: username: ${{ secrets.DOCKERHUB_USERNAME }} password: ${{ secrets.DOCKERHUB_TOKEN }} - name: Build and push uses: docker/build-push-action@v2 with: push: true tags: myuser/myapp:${{ github.sha }} # 2. Update Config Repo - name: Checkout Config Repo uses: actions/checkout@v2 with: repository: myuser/my-config-repo token: ${{ secrets.GIT_PAT }} # Critical: Needs write access path: config-repo - name: Update Image Tag run: | cd config-repo # Install yq (YAML Processor) wget https://github.com/mikefarah/yq/releases/download/v4.16.1/yq_linux_amd64 -O /usr/bin/yq && chmod +x /usr/bin/yq # Update YAML in place yq -i '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image = "myuser/myapp:${{ github.sha }}"' deployment.yaml # Commit and Push git config user.name "CI Bot" git config user.email "ci@example.com" git add deployment.yaml git commit -m "Update image to ${{ github.sha }}" git push
    🚀 Deployment Phase Complete

    You have now mastered the core "Day 1" skill set: Deployment. You can package code, build infrastructure, and automate releases using GitOps.

    What's Next? (Weeks 13-20): We now enter the "Advanced & Operations" phase. Real engineering happens after deployment. Starting next week, we will tackle Monitoring, Security, Service Meshes, and Production Optimization to turn you from a DevOps Engineer into a Platform Architect.

    Mentorship Review for Week 12

    Mentorship students: your Week 12 work (GitOps & Advanced CI/CD) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    13

    Monitoring, Logging & Observability

    Moving from "It runs" to "It works"

    Welcome to the Advanced Track. In the previous 12 weeks, your focus was on Day 0 (Design) and Day 1 (Deployment). Now we enter Day 2: Operations.

    Deployment is easy; keeping things running is hard. When a user reports "The site is slow," how do you know if it's the database, the network, or a code bug? If a pod crashes at 3 AM, do you have the logs?

    This week, we implement the "Three Pillars of Observability": Metrics, Logs, and Traces. We will deploy the industry-standard stack (Prometheus and Grafana) to visualize cluster health, and we will learn the difference between "Monitoring" (telling you the system is down) and "Observability" (telling you why).

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Monitoring, Logging & Observability: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define the Pillars: Define Metrics (aggregatable numbers), Logs (discrete events), and Traces (request lifecycle). Recall the "Four Golden Signals" (Latency, Traffic, Errors, Saturation).

    Understand

    Explain Architecture: Explain the Pull-based architecture of Prometheus (scraping targets) versus Push-based systems. Describe how AlertManager groups and routes alerts.

    Apply

    Execute Installation: Deploy the kube-prometheus-stack using Helm. Configure a Grafana dashboard to visualize Pod memory usage.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Performance: Analyze a spike in 99th percentile latency on a Grafana graph and correlate it with CPU throttling metrics.

    Evaluate

    Assess Tooling: Evaluate the trade-offs between self-hosted logging (ELK/Loki) vs. SaaS (Datadog/Splunk) regarding cost, maintenance, and data sovereignty.

    Create

    Build Alerts: Construct a Prometheus alerting rule that triggers PagerDuty only when error rates exceed 5% for more than 5 minutes (preventing flapping).

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. This week requires a Kubernetes cluster with significant resources (Observability stacks are heavy). Budget approximately 1 hour.

    1Cluster Sizing

    Prometheus and Grafana require more memory than a standard Nginx pod.

    Task: Ensure your local cluster (Minikube/Docker) has at least 2 CPUs and 4GB RAM.

    # Minikube users: minikube delete minikube start --cpus 2 --memory 4096

    2Install Helm (Refresher)

    We will use the community-maintained `kube-prometheus-stack` Helm chart. It is too complex to install via raw YAML.

    helm version # If not installed, please review Week 9. # Add the Prometheus Repo helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts helm repo update

    3Research: The 4 Golden Signals

    Task: Read the Google SRE Book chapter on "Monitoring Distributed Systems" (available online for free).
    Write down: What are the 4 Golden Signals? Why is "Saturation" different from "Traffic"?

    4Port Forwarding Primer

    Grafana runs inside the cluster. To view the dashboard, we will need to forward ports.

    Command Refresher: kubectl port-forward svc/service-name local_port:container_port.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    In the modern cloud-native ecosystem, systems are ephemeral. Pods die, nodes autoscale, and IPs change. Old-school monitoring (checking if a server responds to ping) is dead. Instead, we need Observability: the ability to understand the internal state of a system based purely on its external outputs.

    13.1 Monitoring vs. Observability

    These terms are often used interchangeably, but they are distinct concepts in Site Reliability Engineering (SRE).

    📟

    Monitoring

    "Is the system healthy?"
    Monitoring tells you when something is wrong based on known failure modes. It is dashboard-centric.
    Example: "CPU usage is at 95%." "The API is returning 500 errors."

    🔬

    Observability

    "Why is the system behaving this way?"
    Observability allows you to ask arbitrary questions about unknown failure modes. It is data-centric.
    Example: "Show me all requests from User ID 123 that took longer than 2s and hit the checkout service."

    13.2 The Three Pillars of Observability

    To achieve observability, we need three distinct types of data.

    📊 1. Metrics (Aggregatable)

    Metrics are numerical data measured over time. They are cheap to store and fast to query.
    Use Case: "How much traffic are we getting?" "What is the average latency?"
    Tool: Prometheus.
    Structure: http_requests_total{status="200", method="POST"} = 450

    📝 2. Logs (Discrete Events)

    Logs are text records of specific events. They provide high-fidelity context but are expensive to store at scale.
    Use Case: "Why did this specific transaction fail?" "What was the stack trace?"
    Tool: Loki, ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana), Fluentd.
    Structure: [ERROR] Database connection failed: Timeout at 12:00:01

    🕸️ 3. Traces (Request Lifecycle)

    Traces track a single request as it hops between microservices.
    Use Case: "The checkout is slow. Is it the frontend, the payment gateway, or the database lock?"
    Tool: Jaeger, Tempo, OpenTelemetry.
    Structure: Span A (50ms) -> Span B (200ms) -> Span C (Error).

    13.3 Deep Dive: Prometheus Architecture

    Prometheus is the de facto standard for Kubernetes monitoring. Its architecture is unique because it is Pull-based.

    • Scraping: Instead of apps sending data to Prometheus (Push), Prometheus visits the app's /metrics endpoint every 15 seconds to "scrape" data.
    • Time Series Database (TSDB): Prometheus stores data locally on disk in a highly optimized custom format. It does not use SQL.
    • Service Discovery: In Kubernetes, pods change IPs constantly. Prometheus asks the Kubernetes API server: "Where are all the pods?" and updates its scrape targets automatically.
    • PromQL: A powerful query language. Example: rate(http_requests_total[5m]) calculates the per-second request rate averaged over 5 minutes.

    13.4 The 4 Golden Signals of SRE

    When building dashboards, don't just graph random things. Google SRE recommends tracking these four signals for every user-facing system:

    The Signals

    1. Latency: Time taken to serve a request. (Distinguish between successful requests and errors—an error might be very fast!).
    2. Traffic: Demand placed on the system (e.g., HTTP requests/sec or I/O bytes/sec).
    3. Errors: The rate of requests that fail (HTTP 500s, non-zero exit codes).
    4. Saturation: How "full" is the system? (e.g., Memory usage, Thread pool capacity, CPU quotas). Saturation predicts imminent failure.

    Hands-On Labs

    Theory is useless without visibility. In these labs, you will deploy the industry-standard "Kube-Prometheus-Stack" (formerly Prometheus Operator). You will go from a blind cluster to a fully instrumented environment where you can visualize CPU throttling, memory leaks, and disk saturation.

    ⚠️ Resource Warning

    The full observability stack is heavy. It spins up Prometheus, AlertManager, Grafana, Node-Exporter (DaemonSet), and Kube-State-Metrics.
    Requirement: Ensure your cluster has at least 4GB of RAM allocated. If using Minikube: minikube start --memory 4096 --cpus 2.

    Lab 1: The "One-Click" Observability Suite

    Objective: Deploy Prometheus, Grafana, and AlertManager using Helm. Understand what components are installed.

    Create Namespace:

    Isolate monitoring tools from your application workloads.

    kubectl create namespace monitoring
    Install via Helm:

    We use the community stack, which includes pre-configured dashboards for Kubernetes.

    helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts helm repo update helm install monitoring prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack \ --namespace monitoring \ --set grafana.adminPassword="admin"

    Note: We set a fixed password for simplicity in this lab.

    Verify Installation:
    kubectl get pods -n monitoring

    You should see several pods:
    • alertmanager-... (Handles alerts)
    • grafana-... (The UI)
    • prometheus-... ( The Metric Collector)
    • kube-state-metrics-... (Converts K8s objects to metrics)
    • node-exporter-... (One per node, reads hardware stats)

    Lab 2: Visualizing Cluster Health

    Objective: Access the Grafana dashboard and analyze the "Cluster Compute Resources" to understand CPU/Memory pressure.

    Port Forwarding:

    Expose Grafana to your localhost.

    kubectl port-forward svc/monitoring-grafana 8080:80 -n monitoring

    Keep this terminal open. Open a new terminal for other commands.

    Login:

    1. Open browser to http://localhost:8080.
    2. Username: admin
    3. Password: admin (Set in Lab 1).

    Explore Default Dashboards:

    1. Click the Dashboards icon (four squares) in the left sidebar.
    2. Go to Browse -> General or Kubernetes folder.
    3. Open the dashboard named "Kubernetes / Compute Resources / Namespace (Pods)".

    Analyze Data:

    Select datasource: Prometheus and namespace: kube-system.
    Look at the "CPU Usage" graph. This is real-time data scraped from your actual cluster components (CoreDNS, Etcd, API Server).

    Lab 3: Writing PromQL Queries

    Objective: Don't rely just on pre-made dashboards. Learn to ask Prometheus specific questions using PromQL.

    Open Explore Mode:

    In Grafana, click the Compass Icon (Explore) on the left sidebar.

    Query 1: Instant Vector:

    Type this query and hit Shift + Enter:

    kube_pod_status_phase{phase="Running"}

    This returns a list of all running pods with a value of 1. It tells you what is running right now.

    Query 2: Rate (The most important function):

    Raw counters (like "total bytes sent") are useless because they always go up. We need the rate (bytes per second).

    rate(container_network_receive_bytes_total[5m])

    This calculates the per-second rate of network traffic received by every container, averaged over a 5-minute window.

    Query 3: Aggregation (Sum):

    The previous query returned 50+ lines (one for every container). Let's sum it up to see total cluster traffic.

    sum(rate(container_network_receive_bytes_total[5m]))

    Now you have a single line graph showing total cluster network load.

    Lab 4: Creating a PrometheusRule

    Objective: Define an alert using Kubernetes Custom Resources (CRDs). We will trigger an alert if a specific pod is missing.

    Deploy a Dummy App:
    kubectl create deployment web-app --image=nginx kubectl expose deployment web-app --port=80
    Define the Alert:

    Create a file named alert-rule.yaml. This tells Prometheus to fire if web-app has 0 replicas for 1 minute.

    apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1 kind: PrometheusRule metadata: name: web-app-down namespace: monitoring labels: release: monitoring # Critical: Matches the Prometheus Selector spec: groups: - name: web-app.rules rules: - alert: WebAppDown expr: kube_deployment_status_replicas_available{deployment="web-app"} == 0 for: 1m labels: severity: critical annotations: summary: "The Web App is down!" description: "Replicas available has been 0 for more than 1 minute."
    Apply the Alert:
    kubectl apply -f alert-rule.yaml
    Trigger the Alert:

    Scale the deployment to 0 to break the app.

    kubectl scale deployment web-app --replicas=0
    Observe in Prometheus UI:

    1. Port forward Prometheus (different port from Grafana):
    kubectl port-forward svc/monitoring-prometheus 9090:9090 -n monitoring
    2. Open http://localhost:9090.
    3. Go to Alerts.
    4. You will see WebAppDown in PENDING state (yellow) for 1 minute.
    5. After 1 minute, it turns FIRING (red).

    Cleanup:
    kubectl delete deployment web-app kubectl delete svc web-app kubectl delete prometheusrule web-app-down -n monitoring

    Lab Solutions & Troubleshooting Guide

    This section dissects the Observability labs. We delve into why we used specific PromQL functions, how the Prometheus Operator simplifies configuration via CRDs, and the architectural reasons for the component layout.

    Lab 1 Solution: The Kube-Prometheus Stack

    Component Breakdown

    The Helm chart installed a complex ecosystem. Here is what each piece does:

    • Prometheus Operator: The "Brain." It watches for Kubernetes Custom Resources (like ServiceMonitor and PrometheusRule) and auto-configures the Prometheus server. This means you never have to restart Prometheus manually to add a new target.
    • Node Exporter: Runs as a DaemonSet (one per node). It reads the host's /proc and /sys files to expose hardware metrics (Disk I/O, CPU Load, Network usage).
    • Kube State Metrics: Talks to the Kubernetes API server. It converts object states (e.g., "Pod is Pending", "Deployment has 3 replicas") into metrics. Without this, you wouldn't know how many pods are crashing.

    Lab 2 Solution: Grafana & Resource Analysis

    Understanding "Throttling"

    In the dashboard, you might have seen a graph for CPU Throttling.
    Scenario: You set a resource limit of cpu: "200m" (0.2 cores).
    Reality: The app tries to use 0.5 cores during startup.
    Result: The Linux Kernel (CFS Scheduler) forcibly pauses the process for a few milliseconds every period to enforce the 0.2 limit. This shows up as "Throttling" in Grafana and manifests as "Slowness" to the user. Monitoring this metric is critical for right-sizing limits.

    Lab 3 Solution: PromQL Mechanics

    Why `rate()`?

    Counter Metrics (like http_requests_total) only ever go UP. A graph of a raw counter is just a line climbing at a 45-degree angle. It tells you nothing about current load.

    The Derivative: rate(metric[5m]) calculates the per-second increase averaged over the last 5 minutes.
    If the counter went from 1000 to 2000 in 5 minutes (300 seconds):
    (2000 - 1000) / 300 = 3.33 requests/sec.

    ⚠️ Rate vs. Irate

    rate(): Averages over the whole time window. Smooth lines. Good for alerting and trending.
    irate(): Calculates rate based only on the last two data points. Very spiky. Good for detecting micro-bursts in high-frequency scraping, but bad for alerts (too much noise).

    Lab 4 Solution: Alerting Lifecycle

    Pending vs. Firing

    You defined for: 1m. This is the Dampening Interval.
    1. 00:00: Deployment scales to 0. Expression becomes true. Alert State: Pending.
    2. 00:00:30: Still true. State: Pending.
    3. 00:01:00: Still true. Threshold met. State: Firing.

    Why? To prevent "Flapping." If a pod restarts quickly (down for 10 seconds), you don't want to wake up the on-call engineer. You only want to alert if the issue persists.

    Where did the alert go?

    Prometheus generates the alert, but AlertManager handles it. In a real setup, you configure AlertManager to route "Critical" alerts to PagerDuty/OpsGenie and "Warning" alerts to Slack. AlertManager also handles Grouping (sending 1 email for 10 failing pods instead of 10 emails).

    Practice Questions

    These questions test your ability to reason about system behavior and select the right tool for the job.

    Which of the "Four Golden Signals" measures how "full" a service is (e.g., memory usage or thread pool capacity)?
    A. Latency
    B. Traffic
    C. Saturation
    D. Errors
    Answer: C. Saturation measures the constrained resources of a system (like CPU, Memory, or I/O). When Saturation hits 100%, performance usually degrades non-linearly (crashes or massive latency).
    Prometheus uses which data collection model?
    A. Pull (Scraping)
    B. Push (Agents sending data)
    C. Streaming
    D. Event-driven
    Answer: A. Prometheus actively connects to the targets (pods/services) to retrieve metrics. This prevents the monitoring server from being flooded by a DDoS of metrics from misbehaving apps.
    You want to calculate the average requests per second over the last 5 minutes. Which PromQL function is appropriate?
    A. increase()
    B. rate()
    C. sum()
    D. histogram_quantile()
    Answer: B. `rate()` calculates the per-second average. `increase()` calculates the total absolute change (e.g., "500 requests happened"), not the speed.
    What is the primary role of AlertManager in the Prometheus ecosystem?
    A. To scrape metrics
    B. To store long-term data
    C. To visualize graphs
    D. To group, dedup, and route alerts to receivers (Slack/PagerDuty)
    Answer: D. Prometheus evaluates the rules and fires the alert. AlertManager decides who to tell and how often to repeat the notification.
    Which component is responsible for exposing hardware metrics (CPU, Disk, Network) from the Kubernetes nodes?
    A. kube-state-metrics
    B. node-exporter
    C. cAdvisor
    D. metric-server
    Answer: B. Node Exporter runs as a DaemonSet on every node and translates the Linux kernel's syscalls/procfs data into Prometheus metrics.
    What is the difference between a Counter and a Gauge metric type?
    A. Gauges only go up; Counters go up and down
    B. Counters only go up (cumulative); Gauges go up and down
    C. Counters are for money; Gauges are for speed
    D. There is no difference
    Answer: B. A Counter tracks cumulative events (Total Requests, Total Errors) and resets only on restart. A Gauge tracks a current value (Memory Usage, Temperature, Number of Goroutines).
    You need to debug why a specific request from User A failed, but the metrics only show a 1% error rate globally. What observability tool do you need?
    A. Metrics (Prometheus)
    B. Distributed Tracing (Jaeger)
    C. Alerts (PagerDuty)
    D. Health Checks
    Answer: B. Metrics aggregate data, losing the specific context of individual requests (High Cardinality data). Traces capture the full journey of a single unique ID across the system.
    In a PromQL query, what does the syntax [5m] represent?
    A. An offset of 5 minutes
    B. A Range Vector selector (look back 5 minutes)
    C. A prediction for 5 minutes in the future
    D. An aggregation interval
    Answer: B. It tells Prometheus to select all data points collected for that metric within the last 5 minutes relative to the current evaluation time.
    Why is High Cardinality (e.g., putting User IDs or IP addresses in Metric Labels) dangerous for Prometheus?
    A. It isn't dangerous
    B. It creates a new Time Series for every unique ID, exploding memory usage
    C. It violates GDPR
    D. It slows down the network
    Answer: B. Prometheus stores a separate time series for every unique combination of label values. If you have 1 million users, adding `user_id` as a label creates 1 million time series, likely crashing the server. Use Logs or Traces for high-cardinality data.
    If a PrometheusRule evaluates to true, but the duration specified in the for: field has not passed yet, what is the state of the alert?
    A. Firing
    B. Pending
    C. Silenced
    D. Inhibited
    Answer: B. It enters the "Pending" state. If the condition clears before the duration expires, the alert is discarded and never reaches AlertManager.

    Week-Ending Assignment: Project "Watchtower"

    🎯 Assignment Objective

    You have been hired as the Lead SRE for a legacy application that is notorious for crashing at random times. Management is tired of users reporting outages before the engineering team knows about them.

    Your Mission: Implement a robust Observability Stack. You must deploy the application, configure Prometheus to scrape its metrics, build a "Golden Signals" dashboard in Grafana to visualize its behavior, and create an automated Alert that triggers when the Error Rate exceeds a critical threshold.

    Architecture Overview
