    The system consists of a "Blackbox" application that exposes a /metrics endpoint. You will build the surveillance system around it.

    🎯

    1. The Target

    The App: A simulation container that intentionally fluctuates CPU usage and returns HTTP 500 errors randomly.

    📊

    2. The Dashboard

    Grafana: A custom dashboard tracking Request Rate, Error Rate, and Latency (P99).

    🚨

    3. The Alarm

    PrometheusRule: An alert logic that fires when (Errors / Total) > 5%.

    Technical Requirements

    1. Application Deployment

    Deploy the image bad-app:v1 (we will use a generic prometheus-dummy-exporter for simulation).
    Expose it via a Service on port 8080.
    Ensure it has the correct labels for the ServiceMonitor.

    2. Metric Scraping Configuration

    Create a ServiceMonitor CRD. This is the Prometheus Operator way of saying "Scrape this service."
    Constraint: The ServiceMonitor must match the labels of your Service.

    3. Grafana Dashboard

    Create a dashboard with at least 3 panels:
    1. Traffic: Requests per second (Line graph).
    2. Reliability: Success vs. Error percentage (Pie chart or Graph).
    3. Saturation: Memory Usage of the pod.

    4. Alert Logic

    Create a PrometheusRule.
    Condition: HighErrorRate. Trigger if non-200 responses constitute > 5% of traffic for 1 minute.

    Step-by-Step Implementation Guide

    Phase 1: Deploy the "Bad App"

    We will use an image that generates fake metrics. Create a file app.yaml:

    apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: watchtower-app labels: app: watchtower spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: watchtower template: metadata: labels: app: watchtower spec: containers: - name: app image: prom/fake-prometheus-metric-data:0.1.0 ports: - name: web containerPort: 9090 --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: watchtower-svc labels: app: watchtower release: monitoring # Critical: Matches ServiceMonitor selector often used by helm chart spec: selector: app: watchtower ports: - name: web port: 80 targetPort: 9090

    Apply this to your cluster.

    Phase 2: The ServiceMonitor

    The app is running, but Prometheus doesn't know about it. Create monitor.yaml.
    Hint: Look at the serviceMonitorSelector in your Prometheus Operator values. Usually, it looks for the label release: monitoring.

    Phase 3: The Dashboard

    Log into Grafana. Create a new dashboard.
    Add a Panel. Use the query rate(http_requests_total[2m]) (note: metric name depends on the image used, check http://localhost:9090/metrics via port-forward to see real names).

    Phase 4: The Alert

    Create rule.yaml. Define an alert that calculates the percentage of errors.

    Phase 5: Verification

    Check Prometheus "Targets" page. Is watchtower-svc UP?
    Check Prometheus "Alerts" page. Is the alert inactive, pending, or firing?

    📋 Student Submission Checklist

    Before submitting, ensure you have:

    Teacher's Solution Guide

    Solution: Project Watchtower

    1. The ServiceMonitor (monitor.yaml)

    This connects the Service to Prometheus.

    apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1 kind: ServiceMonitor metadata: name: watchtower-monitor namespace: monitoring labels: release: monitoring # Must match the Prometheus Operator configuration spec: selector: matchLabels: app: watchtower # Matches the Service labels endpoints: - port: web interval: 15s

    2. The Alert Rule (rule.yaml)

    The math for Error Rate.

    apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1 kind: PrometheusRule metadata: name: watchtower-alerts namespace: monitoring labels: release: monitoring spec: groups: - name: watchtower rules: - alert: HighErrorRate # Query: (500s / Total Requests) * 100 > 5 expr: | (sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[1m])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total[1m]))) * 100 > 5 for: 1m labels: severity: critical annotations: summary: "High Error Rate detected" description: "Error rate is {{ $value }}%, which is above 5%."

    3. Debugging Tips

    "My Target is not showing up!"
    Check the labels on your ServiceMonitor. The Prometheus Operator is configured to only look for ServiceMonitors that have specific labels (usually release: monitoring or app: kube-prometheus-stack). If you miss this label, the operator ignores your file.

    "No Data in Grafana"
    Port-forward to the app directly (kubectl port-forward svc/watchtower-svc 8080:80) and visit localhost:8080/metrics. If you don't see text output, the app is broken. If you DO see text, check Prometheus Targets to see if the scrape failed (e.g., connection refused).

    🚀 Week 13 Complete

    You have now added "Eyes and Ears" to your infrastructure. You are no longer flying blind.

    Next Week (Week 14): We return to the pipeline. Now that we have monitoring, how do we make our CI/CD pipelines smarter? We will dive into Advanced CI/CD Engineering—building reusable pipeline libraries, caching strategies for speed, and testing infrastructure code before it deploys.

    Mentorship Review for Week 13

    Mentorship students: your Week 13 work (Monitoring, Logging & Observability) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    14

    Advanced CI/CD Pipeline Engineering

    Optimization, Templating, and Pipeline-as-Code

    Welcome to Week 14. You have already built basic pipelines that test and deploy code. That was the "Junior" phase. Now we enter the "Senior" phase.

    A pipeline that takes 45 minutes to run is a productivity killer. A pipeline that requires copying and pasting 50 lines of YAML for every new microservice is a maintenance nightmare. A pipeline that passes even when the tests fail is a liability.

    This week, we stop treating pipelines as simple scripts and start treating them as Software Engineering artifacts. We will apply software principles—DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself), Modularity, and Efficiency—to our CI/CD workflows. You will learn to optimize build times using Caching and Parallelism, and you will architect reusable Shared Libraries to standardize deployments across the entire organization.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Advanced CI/CD Pipeline Engineering: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Mechanisms: Define the difference between "Vertical Scaling" (bigger runners) and "Horizontal Scaling" (more runners) for pipelines. Identify the syntax for Matrix Builds.

    Understand

    Explain Architecture: Explain how Docker Layer Caching works inside a CI environment and why `npm ci` is preferred over `npm install` in pipelines.

    Apply

    Execute Refactoring: Refactor a monolithic 20-minute serial pipeline into a 5-minute parallel pipeline using stages and matrix strategies.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Bottlenecks: Analyze a build log to identify the "Critical Path" (the longest chain of dependent steps) and propose an optimization strategy.

    Evaluate

    Assess Tools: Evaluate the trade-offs between Jenkins (Self-hosted, infinite customizability, high maintenance) vs. GitHub Actions (SaaS, fast setup, lower control).

    Create

    Build Libraries: Architect a "Composite Action" (GitHub) or "Shared Library" (Jenkins) that encapsulates complex security scanning logic into a single line of config.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. This week focuses heavily on Jenkins and GitHub Actions. Budget approximately 1.5 hours.

    1Local Jenkins Environment

    To practice Jenkins pipelines without provisioning a massive server, we will run Jenkins in Docker.

    Task: Run the official Jenkins LTS image ("Docker in Docker" enabled is complex, so we will focus on the Controller logic).

    docker run -p 8080:8080 -p 50000:50000 --restart=on-failure \ -v jenkins_home:/var/jenkins_home \ jenkins/jenkins:lts-jdk11

    Action: Open `http://localhost:8080`, unlock using the initial password printed in the logs, and install the "Suggested Plugins."

    2Dependency Theory: `npm ci`

    Context: A common mistake in pipelines is using commands designed for humans (`npm install`) instead of commands designed for robots (`npm ci`).

    Task: Research the difference between `package.json` and `package-lock.json`.
    Question: Why does `npm install` sometimes result in different production builds even if the code hasn't changed? Why does `npm ci` fix this?

    3GitHub Actions Self-Hosted Runner (Optional but Recommended)

    Sometimes the free GitHub runners are too slow or need access to private VPC resources.

    Task: Go to your GitHub Repo > Settings > Actions > Runners. Follow the instructions to download and run the runner agent on your local machine (Linux/Mac/WSL). Verify that you can trigger a "Hello World" job on your own laptop from the cloud.

    4The Concept of "Artifacts"

    Research: In a multi-stage pipeline, Stage 1 (Build) runs on Server A, and Stage 2 (Test) might run on Server B.
    Problem: Server B does not have the binary Server A built.
    Solution: Look up "Pipeline Artifacts" or "Stashing". How do we pass files between stages?

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    In the early days of CI/CD, engineers clicked buttons in Jenkins UIs to configure jobs. This was fragile; if the server died, the configuration was lost. Today, we treat pipelines as Production Code. They are versioned, code-reviewed, and tested just like the application they deploy.

    This section covers the architecture of high-performance pipelines: defining them in code, optimizing their execution speed, and modularizing them for reuse across hundreds of microservices.

    14.1 Pipeline-as-Code Architectures

    The industry has converged on two dominant patterns: Jenkins (Groovy) and GitHub Actions/GitLab CI (YAML). While the syntax differs, the concepts of Stages, Steps, and Agents are universal.

    ☕

    Jenkins (Declarative)

    Language: Groovy (JVM-based).
    Strengths: Infinite extensibility. Massive plugin ecosystem. Capable of complex logic (loops, try/catch) inside the pipeline.
    Weaknesses: Heavyweight. Requires maintaining a master server. "Plugin Hell" (updates breaking pipelines).

    🐙

    GitHub Actions (YAML)

    Language: YAML (Declarative).
    Strengths: Zero maintenance (SaaS). Deep integration with Git events (PRs, Issues). Fast startup.
    Weaknesses: Harder to implement complex conditional logic. Reuse requires "Actions" rather than simple functions.

    # Jenkinsfile (Declarative Syntax) pipeline { agent any stages { stage('Build') { steps { sh 'npm ci' sh 'npm run build' } } stage('Test') { steps { sh 'npm test' } } } }

    14.2 Optimization: The Need for Speed

    A 20-minute pipeline allows a developer to deploy 3 times an hour. A 2-minute pipeline allows 30 times. Speed is a competitive advantage.

    Strategy A: Parallelism

    If you have 3 distinct test suites (Unit, Lint, Security), running them sequentially (A -> B -> C) takes Sum(A+B+C) time. Running them in parallel takes Max(A, B, C) time.

    Strategy B: Matrix Builds

    You need to verify your library works on Node 14, 16, and 18. Instead of writing 3 jobs, you define a Matrix. The CI system automatically spawns 3 containers.

    # GitHub Actions Matrix Example jobs: test: runs-on: ubuntu-latest strategy: matrix: node-version: [14.x, 16.x, 18.x] steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v2 - name: Use Node.js ${{ matrix.node-version }} uses: actions/setup-node@v1 with: node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }} - run: npm ci - run: npm test

    14.3 Caching & Artifacts

    Pipelines are usually ephemeral (containers start fresh every time). This means they re-download the internet (npm packages, apt packages) on every run.

    📦 Dependency Caching

    The Trick: Hash your lockfile (`package-lock.json`) to create a unique cache key.
    1. Pre-Run: Check if Cache Key `os-node-hash(package-lock.json)` exists.
    2. Hit: Download node_modules folder from S3/Cache (2 seconds).
    3. Miss: Run npm ci (2 minutes). Upload result to Cache.

    💾 Pipeline Artifacts

    Do not rebuild your Docker image in the "Deploy" stage if you already built it in the "Build" stage.
    Pattern: Build Once, Deploy Anywhere.
    Pass the binary or the Docker Image Digest between stages using Artifacts (or a registry).

    14.4 Reusability: Don't Repeat Yourself (DRY)

    If you have 50 microservices, you do not want 50 copies of the same Jenkinsfile. If you need to update the security scanner version, you'd have to edit 50 repos.

    Jenkins Shared Libraries:
    You write a global function standardPipeline() in a separate git repo. Your microservices call this one line.

    GitHub Composite Actions:
    You wrap a sequence of steps (Checkout -> Setup Node -> Install -> Build) into a single Action my-org/build-node-app@v1.

    ⚠️ The "BOM" (Bill of Materials) Problem

    When using shared libraries or actions, always pin versions (e.g., uses: my-action@v1.2.0). If you point to @main or @latest, a breaking change in the shared library will instantly break every pipeline in your company simultaneously. This is known as a "Blast Radius" event.

    Hands-On Labs

    In these labs, we will move away from simple "Hello World" builds and tackle real-world engineering challenges. You will optimize a slow pipeline using caching, parallelize testing using matrix builds, and refactor repetitive code into reusable modules.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    1. GitHub Account: We will focus on GitHub Actions as it is the most accessible platform for modern CI.
    2. Fork: You will need a repo to work on. Fork this sample repo: https://github.com/actions/starter-workflows or create a fresh repo with a basic package.json.

    Lab 1: Speeding Up Builds with Caching

    Objective: Reduce the installation time of NPM dependencies from minutes to seconds.

    Create the Baseline Workflow:

    Create .github/workflows/ci.yml. This is the "slow" version.

    name: Slow CI on: [push] jobs: build: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v3 - name: Use Node.js uses: actions/setup-node@v3 with: node-version: 16 - name: Install Dependencies run: npm ci # This downloads hundreds of MBs every time - name: Test run: npm test
    Run and Measure:

    Commit this file. Go to the "Actions" tab in GitHub. Note the duration of the "Install Dependencies" step (e.g., 45s).

    Implement Caching:

    Modify the workflow to check for a cache before installing.

    - name: Cache node modules uses: actions/cache@v3 id: npm-cache with: path: ~/.npm key: ${{ runner.os }}-node-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }} restore-keys: | ${{ runner.os }}-node- - name: Install Dependencies run: npm ci
    Verify Acceleration:

    1. Trigger the workflow again (Push a dummy change).
    2. The first run will still be slow (Cache Miss - creates the cache).
    3. Trigger it a third time.
    4. Check the logs. You should see "Cache restored from ..." and the install time drop drastically (e.g., 2s).

    Lab 2: Parallel Testing (The Matrix)

    Objective: Ensure your application works on Node 14, 16, and 18 simultaneously without writing three separate jobs.

    Define the Strategy:

    Modify your ci.yml to include a strategy block.

    jobs: test: runs-on: ubuntu-latest strategy: matrix: node-version: [14.x, 16.x, 18.x] steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v3 - name: Use Node.js ${{ matrix.node-version }} uses: actions/setup-node@v3 with: node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }} - run: npm ci - run: npm test
    Observe Parallelism:

    Push the change. Go to the Actions tab.
    You will see the test job split into 3 sub-jobs: test (14.x), test (16.x), test (18.x).
    They run at the same time. The total workflow time is determined by the slowest job, not the sum of all jobs.

    Lab 3: Creating a Reusable Workflow

    Objective: Abstract the "Build and Test" logic into a separate file so 10 different repos can use it.

    Create the Template:

    Create .github/workflows/reusable-build.yml.

    name: Reusable Build on: workflow_call: inputs: node-version: required: true type: string jobs: build: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v3 - uses: actions/setup-node@v3 with: node-version: ${{ inputs.node-version }} - run: npm ci - run: npm test
    Call the Template:

    Modify your main ci.yml to "call" the reusable workflow instead of defining steps.

    name: Main CI on: [push] jobs: call-workflow-1: uses: ./.github/workflows/reusable-build.yml with: node-version: '16.x' call-workflow-2: uses: ./.github/workflows/reusable-build.yml with: node-version: '18.x'
    Analyze the Benefit:

    If you need to add a "Security Scan" step later, you only add it to reusable-build.yml. Both call-workflow-1 and call-workflow-2 (and any other repos referencing it) get the update automatically.

    Lab 4: Accelerating Docker Builds

    Objective: Docker builds in CI are notoriously slow because they start from scratch. We will use the GitHub Actions Cache to store Docker layers.

    Setup Buildx:

    Standard `docker build` cannot easily export cache. We need `docker buildx`.

    - name: Set up Docker Buildx uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v2
    Configure Build with Cache:
    - name: Build and push uses: docker/build-push-action@v4 with: context: . push: false tags: user/app:latest # The Magic Part: cache-from: type=gha cache-to: type=gha,mode=max
    Verify:

    Run the build twice.
    Run 1: "Exporting cache" (Slow).
    Run 2: "Importing cache manifest" (Fast). You will see steps marked as CACHED in the logs, skipping the heavy lifting (like npm install inside the Dockerfile).

    Lab Solutions & Troubleshooting Guide

    This section provides the "answer key" for the Advanced CI/CD labs. It explains the architectural decisions behind caching strategies, how matrix builds actually execute on the backend, and why "Reusable Workflows" are the secret to scaling DevOps in large organizations.

    Lab 1 Solution: Caching Mechanics

    The Cache Key Strategy

    The most critical line in your caching configuration was:

    key: ${{ runner.os }}-node-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}

    Why is this specific format used?
    1. runner.os: node_modules often contain binary compilations (C++ bindings) that are OS-specific. You cannot restore a Linux cache onto a Windows runner.
    2. hashFiles('package-lock.json'): This generates a SHA-256 hash of your lockfile. If you add a single dependency to your project, the lockfile changes, the hash changes, and the cache key changes. This forces a "Cache Miss," ensuring you don't use stale dependencies.

    ⚠️ Cache Eviction

    GitHub Actions (free tier) has a 10GB total cache limit per repository. Once full, it evicts the least recently used cache. Optimizing cache size (e.g., ignoring `~/.npm/_cacache`) is a common advanced optimization.

    Lab 2 Solution: Matrix Builds

    "Fail-Fast" Behavior

    By default, GitHub Actions enables fail-fast: true.
    If your test suite fails on Node 14, GitHub will immediately cancel the running jobs for Node 16 and Node 18 to save you money (minutes).
    Pro Tip: When debugging cross-version compatibility, set fail-fast: false so you can see results for all versions, even if one fails.

    Lab 3 Solution: Reusable Workflows

    Inputs vs. Secrets

    Notice that we defined inputs in the reusable workflow.
    If your reusable workflow needs to deploy to AWS, it needs credentials. You cannot pass secrets via inputs (unsafe). You must use the secrets: inherit keyword in the caller workflow:

    uses: ./.github/workflows/deploy.yml secrets: inherit # Passes AWS_KEYS automatically

    Lab 4 Solution: Docker Layer Caching

    Inline Cache (`type=gha`)

    Before 2021, caching Docker layers in GitHub Actions required pushing them to a Docker Registry (using --cache-from user/app:cache). This was slow because it involved network uploads/downloads.

    The type=gha (GitHub Actions) cache driver stores the layers directly in the GitHub Actions Cache API (internal network). This is significantly faster and doesn't clutter your Docker Hub tags list with messy cache layers.

    💡 mode=max vs mode=min

    mode=min (Default): Only caches the final resulting image layers.
    mode=max: Caches intermediate layers (like the result of `RUN npm install`). This is what speeds up builds, but it uses more cache space.

    Practice Questions

    These questions mimic the format of the GitHub Actions Certification and DevOps Professional exams.

    What is the primary difference between `npm install` and `npm ci` in a CI environment?
    A. `npm ci` is slower but more secure
    B. `npm ci` updates the package-lock.json file
    C. `npm ci` deletes node_modules and installs exact versions from package-lock.json
    D. `npm ci` ignores devDependencies
    Answer: C. `npm ci` (Clean Install) is deterministic. It ensures that the build server uses the exact same dependency tree as your local machine. `npm install` might upgrade minor versions silently, leading to "works on my machine" bugs.
    Which GitHub Actions keyword is used to execute a job on multiple OS versions or Language versions in parallel?
    A. parallel
    B. strategy.matrix
    C. loop
    D. concurrent
    Answer: B. The Matrix strategy takes an array of inputs and creates a Cartesian product of jobs. E.g., `os: [ubuntu, windows]` and `node: [14, 16]` results in 4 jobs.
    You need to pass a compiled binary from the "Build" job to the "Deploy" job. What feature should you use?
    A. Git Commit
    B. Caching
    C. Artifacts (upload-artifact / download-artifact)
    D. SCP
    Answer: C. Artifacts are designed for passing data between jobs in the same workflow run. Caching is designed for passing data between different workflow runs (to speed them up).
    Why might you choose a Self-Hosted Runner over a GitHub-hosted runner?
    A. It is always cheaper
    B. It is easier to set up
    C. To access private resources inside your VPC (like a database)
    D. GitHub runners do not support Linux
    Answer: C. GitHub-hosted runners are in Azure public cloud. They cannot reach your private RDS instance or internal API unless you open firewalls (bad). A self-hosted runner sits inside your private network.
    In a Jenkinsfile, what does the `agent any` directive imply?
    A. The pipeline can run on any available executor in the cluster
    B. The pipeline runs on the Master node
    C. The pipeline requires no agent
    D. It is a syntax error
    Answer: A. It tells the Jenkins Controller to schedule the build on the first available agent (worker node) that has an idle slot.
    What triggers a "Cache Miss" in a dependency caching step?
    A. The branch name changing
    B. The calculated hash of the cache key changing (e.g., lockfile modified)
    C. A failed test
    D. Running the workflow manually
    Answer: B. If the key lookup fails (because the `package-lock.json` hash is new), the system reports a miss, runs the install step, and then saves the new cache at the end.
    Which YAML syntax correctly references a Reusable Workflow in GitHub Actions?
    A. `run: ./.github/workflows/deploy.yml`
    B. `uses: ./.github/workflows/deploy.yml`
    C. `import: deploy.yml`
    D. `call: deploy`
    Answer: B. The `uses` keyword is used for both Actions and Reusable Workflows. The path must start with `./` for local files or `owner/repo/path` for external files.
    What is the "Critical Path" of a pipeline?
    A. The most expensive step
    B. The longest chain of dependent steps that determines total duration
    C. The step where deployment happens
    D. The path to the Dockerfile
    Answer: B. If steps A and B run in parallel (A=5m, B=2m), and C runs after both, the Critical Path is A -> C. Optimizing B will not make the pipeline faster. Optimizing A will.
    When using Docker Layer Caching with `buildx`, why do we set `mode=max`?
    A. To maximize build speed
    B. To cache all intermediate layers, not just the final image
    C. To cache on the maximum number of runners
    D. To compress the image
    Answer: B. By default (`mode=min`), only the layers present in the final image are cached. `mode=max` caches intermediate build layers (like a builder stage where code is compiled), which is crucial for multi-stage Dockerfiles.
    If you use a "Composite Action" from the marketplace, what is the safest way to reference it version-wise?
    A. `uses: action@main`
    B. `uses: action@latest`
    C. `uses: action@v1.2.3` (Specific Tag or SHA)
    D. `uses: action`
    Answer: C. Referencing mutable tags like `main` or `latest` opens you up to Supply Chain Attacks or breaking changes. Pinning to a specific SHA or immutable tag is best practice.

    Week-Ending Assignment: Project "Velocity"

    🎯 Assignment Objective

    You have been brought in to fix a "broken" CI/CD culture at a startup. Their main application pipeline takes 25 minutes to run. Because it is so slow, developers are skipping tests and pushing directly to main, causing outages.

    The Legacy State: A single, serial workflow that downloads dependencies from scratch, runs linting, then unit tests, then integration tests, and finally builds the Docker image.

    Your Mission: Refactor this pipeline into a high-performance engineering artifact. You must reduce the runtime to under 5 minutes using Caching, Parallelism (Matrix), and Docker Layer Caching. Furthermore, you must clean up the code by extracting repeated logic into a Composite Action.

    Architecture Transformation

    We are moving from a "Serial Waterfall" to a "Parallel Mesh" architecture.

    ⚡

    1. Cache Strategy

    Dependency Cache: Store `node_modules` based on `package-lock.json`.
    Docker Cache: Store image layers using the GitHub Actions internal cache (`type=gha`).

    🔀

    2. Execution Strategy

    Matrix: Run Unit Tests on Node 16 and Node 18 simultaneously.
    Async: Run Linting and Security Scans in parallel with Tests.

    🧩

    3. Modularization

    Local Action: Extract the "Checkout + Setup Node + Cache + Install" sequence into a reusable local action ./.github/actions/setup-env.

    Technical Requirements

    1. The "Composite Action"

    Create a custom action in your repo at .github/actions/setup-app/action.yml.
    It must accept an input node-version.
    It must perform: Checkout -> Setup Node -> Cache Restore -> `npm ci`.

    2. The Optimized Workflow

    Create .github/workflows/optimized.yml.
    Job 1 (Quality): Runs Lint and Format. Uses your Composite Action.
    Job 2 (Test): Runs Unit Tests using a Matrix (Node 16, 18). Uses your Composite Action.
    Job 3 (Build): Builds Docker image using docker/build-push-action with cache-from/to: type=gha.

    3. Performance Benchmark

    You must run the pipeline twice.
    Run 1: Cold Cache (Slower).
    Run 2: Warm Cache (Fast).
    Success Criteria: The "Install Dependencies" step in Run 2 must take less than 5 seconds. The Docker Build step must show CACHED layers.

    Step-by-Step Implementation Guide

    Phase 1: Create the Composite Action

    Define .github/actions/setup-app/action.yml. Use the runs: using: "composite" syntax.
    Tip: Composite actions run steps on the host runner, just like a normal workflow.

    Phase 2: Build the Matrix Job

    In your workflow file, define the `test` job. Use strategy.matrix for node versions. Call your custom action: uses: ./.github/actions/setup-app.

    Phase 3: Add Docker Caching

    Add the `build` job. Ensure you use `docker/setup-buildx-action`. Configure the cache exporter to use GitHub Actions cache (`type=gha`).

    Phase 4: Run & Refine

    Push to GitHub. Watch the Action runs.
    Debug any path issues with your local action.
    Verify that the Matrix jobs are running in parallel rows in the UI.

    📋 Student Submission Checklist

    Before submitting, ensure you have:

    Teacher's Solution Guide

    Solution: Project Velocity

    1. The Composite Action (.github/actions/setup-app/action.yml)

    name: 'Setup App Environment' description: 'Sets up Node, caches dependencies, and installs' inputs: node-version: description: 'Node version to use' required: true default: '16' runs: using: "composite" steps: - name: Checkout uses: actions/checkout@v3 - name: Setup Node uses: actions/setup-node@v3 with: node-version: ${{ inputs.node-version }} # Centralized Caching Logic - name: Cache modules uses: actions/cache@v3 with: path: ~/.npm key: ${{ runner.os }}-node-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }} restore-keys: ${{ runner.os }}-node- - name: Install run: npm ci shell: bash # Required for composite actions

    2. The Optimized Workflow (.github/workflows/optimized.yml)

    name: High Performance CI on: [push] jobs: test: runs-on: ubuntu-latest strategy: matrix: node: [16, 18] steps: # Use the local action - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-app with: node-version: ${{ matrix.node }} - name: Run Tests run: npm test docker: runs-on: ubuntu-latest needs: test # Only build if tests pass steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v3 - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v2 - uses: docker/build-push-action@v4 with: context: . push: false tags: app:latest # Layer Caching cache-from: type=gha cache-to: type=gha,mode=max
    🚀 Week 14 Complete

    You have successfully engineered a pipeline that is fast, readable, and reusable. You are no longer just "writing scripts"; you are building the internal developer platform.

    Next Week (Week 15): We move to Advanced Deployment Strategies. How do you deploy code without anyone noticing? We will cover Blue-Green Deployments, Canary Releases with traffic shifting, and Feature Flags—techniques used by Netflix and Facebook to deploy thousands of times a day.

    Mentorship Review for Week 14

    Mentorship students: your Week 14 work (Advanced CI/CD Pipeline Engineering) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    15

    Advanced Deployment Strategies

    Blue-Green, Canary, and Progressive Delivery

    Welcome to Week 15. Up until now, we have relied on Kubernetes' standard Rolling Update strategy. While better than downtime, Rolling Updates have a fatal flaw: once the new pods are running, 100% of users are exposed to the new code immediately. If you have a bug that only affects checkout, you just broke checkout for everyone.

    This week, we learn how to deploy code like Netflix, Uber, and Facebook. We will decouple "Deployment" (installing the code) from "Release" (exposing it to traffic). We will implement Blue-Green Deployments for instant rollbacks and Canary Releases to test new features on just 1% of your users before rolling out to the rest. Finally, we will automate this entire process using Flagger for "Progressive Delivery."

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Advanced Deployment Strategies: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Strategies: Define the difference between Blue-Green (Traffic Switching), Canary (Traffic Splitting), and A/B Testing (User Segmentation).

    Understand

    Explain Mechanics: Explain how an Ingress Controller or Service Mesh weights traffic between two different Kubernetes Services.

    Apply

    Execute Traffic Shift: Use Argo Rollouts or Flagger to automatically shift traffic from 0% to 50% to 100% based on success metrics.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Failures: Analyze a failed Canary deployment where error rates spiked at 10% traffic, and determine why the automated rollback triggered.

    Evaluate

    Assess Cost vs. Risk: Evaluate the infrastructure cost of Blue-Green (requiring 2x capacity) versus the risk mitigation it provides.

    Create

    Build Strategy: Architect a "Progressive Delivery" pipeline that promotes code from Canary to Primary only if Prometheus latency metrics stay below 200ms.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Advanced deployments rely heavily on networking layers (Ingress/Mesh) to split traffic. Budget approximately 1.5 hours.

    1Install NGINX Ingress Controller

    To perform Canary releases (splitting traffic 90/10), we need a traffic manager. The standard Kubernetes `Service` object cannot do fractional weighting; it only does Round Robin.

    # Using Helm to install NGINX Ingress helm repo add ingress-nginx https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx helm repo update helm install my-ingress ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx \ --namespace ingress-nginx --create-namespace
    ⚠️ Cloud vs. Local

    If you are on Minikube, run `minikube addons enable ingress`.
    If you are on AWS EKS, the command above will create an AWS Network Load Balancer (NLB). This costs money (~$0.025/hour).

    2Cluster Capacity Check

    Context: Blue-Green deployment requires running two complete copies of your application simultaneously (the old version and the new version).
    Task: Check your cluster limits. If your app takes 2GB RAM, do you have 4GB available?

    # Check allocatable resources on nodes kubectl describe nodes | grep Allocatable -A 5

    3Install Argo Rollouts CLI

    We will use Argo Rollouts as our deployment controller. It extends Kubernetes with advanced deployment capabilities.

    # MacOS brew install argoproj/tap/kubectl-argo-rollouts # Linux curl -LO https://github.com/argoproj/argo-rollouts/releases/latest/download/kubectl-argo-rollouts-linux-amd64 chmod +x ./kubectl-argo-rollouts-linux-amd64 sudo mv ./kubectl-argo-rollouts-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/kubectl-argo-rollouts

    4Research: The "Blast Radius"

    Thought Experiment: You push a code change that causes a memory leak.
    Scenario A: You deploy to all 100 servers at once.
    Scenario B: You deploy to 1 server ("Canary"), watch it for 10 minutes, then deploy to the rest.
    Task: Read about the "Blast Radius" concept in SRE. How does Canary deployment reduce blast radius?

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    In traditional software delivery, "Deploying" and "Releasing" were the same event. You copied the files to the server, restarted Apache, and the users saw the new version immediately. In modern DevOps, we separate these concerns.

    Deployment: Installing the new version of the application onto the infrastructure (e.g., creating the Pods).
    Release: Switching production traffic to the new version.

    By separating them, we gain the ability to test the deployment in production before users see it (Dark Launching), or to expose it to only a tiny fraction of users (Canary).

    15.1 The Deployment Strategy Spectrum

    There are four main ways to update an application in Kubernetes. Each trades off between downtime, cost, and risk.

    ♻️

    Recreate

    Mechanism: Kill Version A. Then start Version B.
    Pros: Simple. No extra cost. No version incompatibility issues.
    Cons: Downtime (The system is down while B boots up).

    🔄

    Rolling Update (Default)

    Mechanism: Replace A with B one by one.
    Pros: Zero downtime. Minimal extra cost (usually +1 pod).
    Cons: No control. Blast Radius is 100% (once rolled out, everyone sees the bug). Hard to verify.

    🔵🟢

    Blue-Green

    Mechanism: Spin up a full cluster of B (Green) alongside A (Blue). Switch the Load Balancer instantly.
    Pros: Instant rollback. Testing on production infrastructure before switch.
    Cons: Expensive (Requires 2x capacity).

    🐤

    Canary

    Mechanism: Send 1% of traffic to B. If healthy, increase to 10%, 50%, 100%.
    Pros: Lowest risk. Errors only affect a small user subset.
    Cons: Complex. Requires advanced traffic routing (Ingress/Mesh) and automated analysis.

    15.2 Traffic Management: The "Router"

    Standard Kubernetes Services cannot do weighted traffic splitting (e.g., "90% here, 10% there"). They only do Round Robin (random). To implement Canary, we need a smarter router.

    🚦 The Ingress Controller Approach

    Advanced Ingress Controllers (like NGINX or ALB) support Annotations for splitting.
    Example Annotation: nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight: "10"
    This tells NGINX: "For every 100 requests hitting `example.com`, send 10 to the Service marked as Canary and 90 to the Primary."

    15.3 Automated Progressive Delivery (Argo Rollouts)

    Doing Canary manually is tedious (editing YAML to change weights from 10 to 20 to 50). Argo Rollouts is a Kubernetes controller that automates this. It introduces a new CRD: Rollout.

    apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1 kind: Rollout metadata: name: my-app spec: replicas: 5 strategy: canary: steps: - setWeight: 20 - pause: {duration: 1h} # Wait 1 hour to see if errors spike - setWeight: 50 - pause: {duration: 10m} - setWeight: 100 template: # (Standard Pod Template here)

    The "Analysis" Phase:
    The true power of Argo Rollouts is not just waiting for time (pause: 1h), but querying Prometheus.
    "Check Prometheus every minute. Is the HTTP 500 rate on the Canary pods > 1%? If yes, ABORT and rollback immediately."

    15.4 Feature Flags (Application-Level Deployment)

    Infrastructure deployment (Canary) controls traffic. Feature Flags control logic.

    Even if you deploy the code to 100% of servers, you can wrap the new feature in an if statement:

    if (LaunchDarkly.client.variation("new-checkout-flow", user_id) == true) { return renderNewCheckout(); } else { return renderOldCheckout(); }

    This decouples release from deployment entirely. You can turn features on/off instantly without touching Kubernetes or waiting for a pipeline.

    ⚠️ Technical Debt Warning

    Feature Flags are powerful but dangerous. If you don't clean them up, your code becomes a graveyard of dead if/else logic. A mature team has a process to remove flags once a feature is fully launched.

    Hands-On Labs

    In these labs, we will move beyond the standard kubectl apply. We will manually orchestrate a Canary release using NGINX Ingress to understand the networking mechanics, and then we will automate the process using Argo Rollouts, the industry-standard controller for progressive delivery in Kubernetes.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    1. Ingress Controller: You MUST have NGINX Ingress installed (from Pre-Class exercises).
    2. Argo Rollouts: You need the Controller installed in the cluster and the plugin installed in `kubectl`.
    3. Hosts File: We will use the domain `canary.local`. Add 127.0.0.1 canary.local (or your Minikube IP) to your /etc/hosts file.

    Lab 1: The "Poor Man's Canary" (NGINX Annotations)

    Objective: Use NGINX Ingress annotations to manually split traffic 90/10 between two Services without using any external deployment tools.

    Deploy V1 (Primary):

    Create a namespace `demo`. Deploy "Blue" (V1) application and Service.

    kubectl create ns demo # V1 Deployment & Service kubectl apply -f - <
    Deploy V1 Ingress:

    Create the main Ingress rule pointing to V1.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Deploy V2 (Canary):

    Deploy "Green" (V2) application and Service.

    # Similar to V1, but different text and name kubectl create deployment app-v2 --image=hashicorp/http-echo --replicas=1 --port=5678 -n demo -- -text="VERSION_2_GREEN" kubectl expose deployment app-v2 --name=service-v2 --port=80 --target-port=5678 -n demo
    Create the Canary Ingress:

    Here is the magic. We create a second Ingress resource with the same host but specific annotations.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Verify Traffic Split:

    Run a loop to hit the endpoint 10 times. You should see roughly 8 BLUEs and 2 GREENs.

    for i in {1..10}; do curl -s canary.local; echo; done
    VERSION_1_BLUE VERSION_1_BLUE VERSION_2_GREEN <-- There it is! VERSION_1_BLUE ...
    Cleanup Lab 1:
    kubectl delete ns demo

    Lab 2: The `Rollout` CRD

    Objective: Automate the deployment process. Instead of managing Deployments and Ingresses manually, we will define a `Rollout` resource that handles traffic splitting and pauses automatically between steps.

    Install Argo Rollouts Controller:

    We need to install the controller and the CRDs into the cluster.

    kubectl create namespace argo-rollouts kubectl apply -n argo-rollouts -f https://github.com/argoproj/argo-rollouts/releases/latest/download/install.yaml
    Deploy a Basic Rollout:

    Create a file named `basic-rollout.yaml`. Notice `kind: Rollout` (not Deployment).

    apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1 kind: Rollout metadata: name: rollout-demo spec: replicas: 5 strategy: canary: steps: - setWeight: 20 - pause: {} # Pauses indefinitely until manual approval - setWeight: 50 - pause: { duration: 10s } # Auto-promotes after 10s - setWeight: 100 revisionHistoryLimit: 2 selector: matchLabels: app: rollout-demo template: metadata: labels: app: rollout-demo spec: containers: - name: rollouts-demo image: argoproj/rollouts-demo:blue ports: - name: http containerPort: 8080 --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: rollout-demo spec: ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 8080 protocol: TCP name: http selector: app: rollout-demo
    kubectl apply -f basic-rollout.yaml
    Visualize (The CLI Dashboard):

    Argo provides a terminal UI to watch the pods and the traffic split in real-time.

    kubectl argo rollouts get rollout rollout-demo --watch
    Trigger an Update:

    Update the image to "Yellow" to start the canary process.

    kubectl argo rollouts set image rollout-demo rollouts-demo=argoproj/rollouts-demo:yellow

    Observe in the Dashboard:
    1. Argo creates 1 new pod (Yellow) and keeps 4 old pods (Blue) -> roughly 20% weight.
    2. It hits the `pause: {}` step and waits. Status becomes "Paused".

    Promote:

    Since the rollout is paused indefinitely, you must manually approve it to proceed to the next step.

    kubectl argo rollouts promote rollout-demo

    Observe:
    1. It scales Yellow to 50% (3 pods) and Blue to 50% (3 pods).
    2. It waits 10 seconds (as defined in `duration: 10s`).
    3. It automatically scales Yellow to 100% and terminates the Blue replica set.

    Cleanup Lab 2:
    kubectl delete rollout rollout-demo kubectl delete svc rollout-demo

    Lab 3: Blue-Green with Preview Service

    Objective: Configure a Blue-Green strategy where we can test the new version on a private "Preview" service before switching the public "Active" service. Unlike Canary (which is gradual), this switch is atomic.

    Define Services:

    We need two services: one for the public (Active) and one for QA (Preview). Both initially point to the same selector, but Argo will manage them.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Define Blue-Green Rollout:

    Create the Rollout manifest specifying the `blueGreen` strategy.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Trigger Update:

    Update the image to "Green".

    kubectl argo rollouts set image rollout-bluegreen rollouts-demo=argoproj/rollouts-demo:green

    What happens now?
    1. Argo spins up a new ReplicaSet (Green).
    2. It waits for the Green pods to become healthy.
    3. It updates the rollout-bluegreen-preview service to point to Green.
    4. The rollout-bluegreen-active service still points to Blue.

    Verify Preview:

    You can port-forward the preview service to test the new version without affecting users hitting the active service.

    # In Terminal A: kubectl port-forward svc/rollout-bluegreen-preview 9090:80 # In Terminal B: curl localhost:9090 # Output should show the "Green" version (different color/text)
    The Switch (Promotion):

    Once you are happy with the Preview, flip the switch.

    kubectl argo rollouts promote rollout-bluegreen

    Mechanism: Argo instantly updates the activeService selector to point to the Green ReplicaSet. Users are switched instantly. After a delay (default 30s), the Blue ReplicaSet is scaled down.

    Cleanup Lab 3:
    kubectl delete rollout rollout-bluegreen kubectl delete svc rollout-bluegreen-active rollout-bluegreen-preview

    Lab 4: Automated Rollback with Analysis

    Objective: The holy grail of progressive delivery. If the error rate increases during a canary step, the system should automatically abort and rollback without human intervention.

    Context:

    Normally, you would query Prometheus. Since that requires a complex setup from Week 13, we will use a Job-based Analysis for this lab. This runs a Kubernetes Job; if the job exits with code 0, it passes. If it exits with 1, it fails.

    Create AnalysisTemplate:

    This template defines "What is a success?". Here, we simulate a test that fails randomly.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Update Rollout to use Analysis:

    Modify your Canary Rollout (from Lab 2) to trigger this analysis during the steps. Update `basic-rollout.yaml` or patch it live.

    kubectl patch rollout rollout-demo --type='merge' -p '{"spec": {"strategy": {"canary": {"steps": [{"setWeight": 20}, {"pause": {"duration": "5s"}}, {"analysis": {"templates": [{"templateName": "success-rate"}], "args": [{"name": "service-name", "value": "rollout-demo"}]}}]}}}}'

    (Note: The patch adds an `analysis` step after the 20% weight step).

    Trigger a "Bad" Update:

    Change the image to trigger the rollout.

    kubectl argo rollouts set image rollout-demo rollouts-demo=argoproj/rollouts-demo:red
    Watch it Fail:

    Observation Sequence:
    1. Argo scales Canary to 20%.
    2. It runs the Analysis Job.
    3. The Job exits with 1 (Fail).
    4. Argo marks the analysis as Failed.
    5. Result: Argo automatically aborts the rollout. The status becomes Degraded. It scales the Canary back to 0 immediately to save users.

    Cleanup Lab 4:
    kubectl delete rollout rollout-demo kubectl delete analysistemplate success-rate

    Lab 5: Traffic Mirroring (The "Dark Launch")

    Objective: Test a new microservice version with real production traffic without the users knowing. The traffic is duplicated (mirrored) to the new service "out-of-band." The response from the mirrored service is discarded, so if it crashes, the user is unaffected.

    Concept:

    We will use NGINX Ingress to send 100% of traffic to V1 (User Facing) and a copy of 100% of traffic to V2 (Shadow).

    Deploy Shadow Service (V2):

    Reuse the `demo` namespace from Lab 1. If you deleted it, recreate the V1/V2 deployments and services quickly.

    # Ensure V1 (Main) exists kubectl create deployment app-v1 --image=hashicorp/http-echo --replicas=1 --port=5678 -n demo -- -text="MAIN_V1" kubectl expose deployment app-v1 --name=service-v1 --port=80 --target-port=5678 -n demo # Ensure V2 (Shadow) exists kubectl create deployment app-v2 --image=hashicorp/http-echo --replicas=1 --port=5678 -n demo -- -text="SHADOW_V2" kubectl expose deployment app-v2 --name=service-v2 --port=80 --target-port=5678 -n demo
    Configure Mirroring Ingress:

    Apply the mirror annotation to the main Ingress.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Generate Traffic:
    curl canary.local

    Output: You will ONLY see "MAIN_V1". The shadow response is discarded by NGINX.

    Verify the Shadow Received Traffic:

    Check the logs of the V2 pod. It should show it received the request.

    kubectl logs -l app=app-v2 -n demo
    "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 ... "curl/7.64.1"

    This proves V2 processed the request. This strategy is perfect for testing performance regressions or database load issues without risking user downtime.

    Cleanup Lab 5:
    kubectl delete ns demo

    🎉 Part 3 Conclusion

    You have now successfully orchestrated the four major patterns of modern deployment:

    • Canary (Manual): Using Ingress weights to verify networking logic.
    • Canary (Automated): Using Argo Rollouts to pause and promote based on time.
    • Blue-Green: Using atomic service switching for zero-downtime cutovers.
    • Traffic Mirroring: Using dark launching to test code safely.

    In Part 4, we will review the solutions and troubleshooting steps for when these rollouts get stuck (e.g., "Why is my rollout paused forever?").

    Lab Solutions & Troubleshooting Guide

    This section provides the "answer key" for the Advanced Deployment labs. It explains the mechanics behind NGINX's traffic splitting, the architecture of the Argo Rollouts controller, the critical differences between "Active" and "Preview" services, and how Traffic Mirroring allows for risk-free testing.

    Lab 1 Solution: NGINX Canary Mechanics

    How does NGINX split traffic?

    In Lab 1, you created two Ingress objects for the same host (canary.local). Normally, this would cause a conflict.
    However, the annotation nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary: "true" tells the Ingress Controller to treat the second object not as a conflict, but as an alternative backend.

    Under the hood, the Ingress Controller modifies the generated nginx.conf to include logic similar to:
    if (random(0, 100) < 20) { proxy_pass upstream_canary; }
    This logic is executed for every single request. This is why you saw roughly 2/10 requests go to the Green version in your curl loop.

    💡 Sticky Sessions

    In a real e-commerce site, you don't want a user to see the "New Checkout" on page 1 and the "Old Checkout" on page 2. To prevent this, you would add the annotation nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-by-cookie: "canary_user". This ensures that once a user is assigned to the Canary (via a set-cookie header), they stay on the Canary.

    Lab 2 Solution: Argo Rollouts Architecture

    ReplicaSet Management

    Did you notice that Argo created ReplicaSets with hash suffixes like rollout-demo-6f85d5c4b6?
    Just like a standard Kubernetes Deployment, a Rollout manages ReplicaSets. However, while a standard Deployment tries to scale the old RS down as fast as it scales the new RS up, a Rollout purposefully pauses this operation.

    The "Paused" State:
    When the rollout is paused at 20%, you effectively have:
    • Stable RS: 4 replicas (80%)
    • Canary RS: 1 replica (20%)
    Argo halts the controller reconciliation loop here until the `pause` duration expires or you manually promote it via the CLI.

    Lab 3 Solution: Blue-Green Services

    Active vs. Preview

    The core concept of Blue-Green in Kubernetes is Selector Swapping.
    1. Active Service: Initially selects color: blue (The old version).
    2. Preview Service: Selects color: green (The new version).

    When you run promote, Argo updates the Service definition so that Active Service now selects color: green. This switch is atomic (happens instantly). The users simply start hitting the new pods.

    ⚠️ Resource Double-Billing

    The biggest downside of Blue-Green is cost. If you have 100 pods in production, you need to spin up another 100 pods for the Green version before you switch. You need 200% capacity for a short time. If your cluster is full, the Green pods will stay Pending, and the deployment will fail.

    Lab 4 Solution: Analysis & Rollback

    The "Abort" Mechanism

    When the analysis job failed (exit code 1), Argo transitioned the Rollout to a Degraded state.
    Its immediate reaction was:
    1. Scale the Canary RS (Red/Bad) to 0.
    2. Ensure the Stable RS (Blue/Good) is at 100%.

    This automated safety net is why companies like Google can deploy thousands of times a day. They don't rely on humans watching dashboards; they rely on statistical analysis to catch bad deployments within seconds.

    Lab 5 Solution: Traffic Mirroring Mechanics

    Why didn't I see the Shadow Response?

    When using nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/mirror-target-service, NGINX operates in a "Fire and Forget" mode for the shadow request.

    1. Client Request: Comes in to NGINX.
    2. Primary Upstream: NGINX forwards request to V1. NGINX waits for V1's response.
    3. Shadow Upstream: NGINX copies the request to V2. NGINX ignores V2's response.

    This is why your `curl` command only showed "MAIN_V1". Even if V2 crashed or returned a 500 error, the client would receive a 200 OK from V1. This is what makes mirroring the safest possible way to test new code with real data.

    Practice Questions

    These questions test your ability to select the right deployment strategy for a given business scenario.

    Which deployment strategy has the highest infrastructure cost (requires the most resources)?
    A. Rolling Update
    B. Recreate
    C. Blue-Green
    D. Canary
    Answer: C. Blue-Green requires running two full copies of the application simultaneously (100% Blue + 100% Green) during the testing phase.
    You are using NGINX Ingress for Canary releases. What annotation controls the percentage of traffic sent to the new version?
    A. nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/weight
    B. nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/split
    C. nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight
    D. nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/traffic
    Answer: C. The standard annotation is `canary-weight`. It accepts an integer between 0 and 100.
    What is the primary disadvantage of the "Recreate" deployment strategy?
    A. Significant downtime
    B. High cost
    C. Complexity
    D. It requires a Service Mesh
    Answer: A. "Recreate" kills all old pods before starting any new ones. The application is completely unavailable during the boot-up time of the new pods.
    In Argo Rollouts, what is the purpose of the "Analysis" step?
    A. To scan the Docker image for vulnerabilities
    B. To compile the code
    C. To query metrics (like Prometheus) and determine if the rollout should proceed or abort
    D. To notify Slack users
    Answer: C. Analysis allows you to define success criteria (e.g., "Error rate < 1%"). If the criteria fail, the rollout automatically rolls back.
    You want to test a new UI feature only on internal employees. Which deployment pattern is most appropriate?
    A. Blue-Green
    B. Canary (Weighted)
    C. Canary (Header/Cookie based) or A/B Testing
    D. Rolling Update
    Answer: C. A weighted canary hits random users. Header/Cookie-based routing allows you to target specific users (e.g., those with a cookie `user_type=employee`).
    When performing a Blue-Green deployment, what actually changes to switch the traffic?
    A. The DNS record
    B. The Service Selector (Label Selector)
    C. The Pod IP addresses
    D. The Node affinity
    Answer: B. The Service object is updated to point its label selector from `version: v1` to `version: v2`. The Kubernetes endpoints controller then updates the routing tables instantly.
    Why does a standard Kubernetes Service not support weighted traffic splitting (e.g., 90/10)?
    A. It is a paid feature
    B. Kube-proxy (iptables/ipvs) is Layer 4 and only supports Round Robin or Random load balancing
    C. It requires IPv6
    D. It only supports UDP
    Answer: B. Standard Services operate at Layer 4 (TCP/UDP). Weighted splitting usually requires Layer 7 (HTTP) awareness, which is why we need an Ingress Controller or Service Mesh.
    What is "Blast Radius"?
    A. The memory usage of a pod
    B. The number of users or systems impacted by a defective change
    C. The network bandwidth used during deployment
    D. The cost of a failed deployment
    Answer: B. Canary deployments reduce the blast radius by exposing only a small percentage of users to the potential defect.
    If you need to perform a database schema migration that is not backward compatible (e.g., renaming a column), which deployment strategy is safest?
    A. Rolling Update
    B. Blue-Green
    C. Canary
    D. Maintenance Mode (Downtime)
    Answer: D. Tricky question! Most zero-downtime strategies (Rolling, Blue-Green) require the app to support both schema versions simultaneously. If the change is truly incompatible, you must take the system offline to migrate data safely.
    Which CRD replaces the standard `Deployment` object when using Argo Rollouts?
    A. StatefulSet
    B. DaemonSet
    C. Rollout
    D. CanarySet
    Answer: C. You simply change `kind: Deployment` to `kind: Rollout` and add the `strategy` block. The rest of the spec (containers, volumes) remains identical.

    Week-Ending Assignment: Project "Safety Net"

    🎯 Assignment Objective

    You have been hired as the Lead Platform Engineer for a high-frequency trading platform. A recent deployment bug caused 100% of transactions to fail for 30 minutes, costing the company millions. The CTO has issued a mandate: "No code goes to 100% traffic without first surviving 5 minutes at 10% traffic."

    Your Mission: Construct a fully automated Progressive Delivery Pipeline using Argo Rollouts. You must implement a "Self-Healing" deployment that automatically promotes itself if successful, but strictly aborts and rolls back if an automated "Smoke Test" fails.

    Architecture Overview

    You will build a pipeline that uses a "Job" to verify health. In a real world scenario, this would be a Prometheus query, but for this assignment, we will simulate health checks using a Kubernetes Job that runs `curl`.

    🚀

    1. The Rollout

    Strategy: Canary.
    Steps: 20% traffic -> Pause for Analysis -> 100% traffic.

    🕵️

    2. The Analyst

    AnalysisTemplate: A reusable definition that spawns a Kubernetes Job to curl the application and verify it returns HTTP 200.

    🛡️

    3. The Safety Net

    Behavior: If the Analysis Job fails (exit code 1), the Rollout must immediately scale the Canary ReplicaSet to 0.

    Technical Requirements

    1. The Rollout Manifest (rollout.yaml)

    Create a `Rollout` resource named `safetynet-app`.
    • Image: `argoproj/rollouts-demo:blue`
    • Replicas: 4
    • Strategy:
      1. Set Weight 25%.
      2. Pause 5 seconds (to allow pods to start).
      3. Run Analysis (Reference your template).
      4. Set Weight 100%.

    2. The Analysis Template (analysis.yaml)

    Create an `AnalysisTemplate` named `smoke-test`.
    • Provider: `job`.
    • Logic: Run a container (e.g., `curlimages/curl`) that attempts to connect to the service.
    • Argument: It should accept a `service-name` argument so it can be reused for other apps.

    3. The Stress Test (Proof of Work)

    You must verify two scenarios:
    1. The Happy Path: Deploy a working image. Verify it goes to 100% automatically.
    2. The Sad Path: Sabotage the Analysis (make the job `exit 1`). Deploy a new image. Verify the rollout stops and reverts.

    Step-by-Step Implementation Guide

    Phase 1: Setup Infrastructure

    Create a clean namespace `project-safetynet`.
    Ensure Argo Rollouts controller is running in the cluster.

    Phase 2: Define the "Test" (Analysis)

    Write the `AnalysisTemplate`.
    Hint: Use `restartPolicy: Never` and `backoffLimit: 0` so the job fails fast.

    Phase 3: Define the App (Rollout)

    Write the `Rollout`. Ensure you create a corresponding `Service` so the curl command has something to hit!

    Phase 4: Execution

    Apply the manifests.
    Run `kubectl argo rollouts get rollout safetynet-app --watch`.
    Trigger an update by changing the image to `yellow`.

    📋 Student Submission Checklist

    Before submitting, ensure you have:

    Teacher's Solution Guide

    Solution: Project Safety Net

    1. The Analysis Template (analysis.yaml)

    This template runs a ephemeral pod to curl the service.

    apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1 kind: AnalysisTemplate metadata: name: smoke-test namespace: project-safetynet spec: args: - name: service-name metrics: - name: connection-test interval: 5s count: 1 failureLimit: 1 provider: job: spec: template: spec: containers: - name: test image: curlimages/curl command: ["curl", "-f", "http://{{args.service-name}}"] restartPolicy: Never backoffLimit: 0

    2. The Rollout & Service (rollout.yaml)

    Notice how we pass the service-name argument to the analysis step.

    apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1 kind: Rollout metadata: name: safetynet-app namespace: project-safetynet spec: replicas: 4 selector: matchLabels: app: safetynet template: metadata: labels: app: safetynet spec: containers: - name: app image: argoproj/rollouts-demo:blue ports: - containerPort: 8080 strategy: canary: steps: - setWeight: 25 - pause: {duration: 5s} # Wait for pods to boot - analysis: templates: - templateName: smoke-test args: - name: service-name value: safetynet-svc - setWeight: 100 --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: safetynet-svc namespace: project-safetynet spec: ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 8080 selector: app: safetynet
    🚀 Week 15 Complete

    You have now mastered Progressive Delivery. You have moved beyond simple scripts and are now orchestrating intelligent, self-healing releases.

    Next Week (Week 16): We scale up. When you have 100 microservices communicating with each other, simple Services and Ingresses aren't enough. How do you secure traffic between pods? How do you visualize the mesh? We will dive into Service Mesh (Istio), mTLS, and advanced observability.

    Mentorship Review for Week 15

    Mentorship students: your Week 15 work (Advanced Deployment Strategies) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    16

    Service Mesh & Advanced Networking

    Istio, Envoy, and the Sidecar Pattern

    Welcome to Week 16. You have successfully broken your monolith into 50 microservices. Congratulations! You have also created 50 new problems.

    In a monolith, Module A calls Module B via a function call in memory. It is fast, secure, and never fails. In microservices, Service A calls Service B over the network. The network is unreliable, insecure, and slow. Service A needs to know: "Did B fail? Should I retry? Is the connection encrypted? Who is tracing this request?"

    Implementing this logic (retries, mTLS, tracing) inside every single application is a nightmare. Instead, we use a Service Mesh. We abstract the network into a dedicated infrastructure layer. We inject a tiny proxy (Envoy) next to every container, and these proxies handle the networking so your code doesn't have to.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Service Mesh & Advanced Networking: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Architecture: Define the "Control Plane" (Istiod) vs. the "Data Plane" (Envoy Proxies) in the context of a Service Mesh.

    Understand

    Explain Sidecars: Explain how the Sidecar Pattern works: how a request leaving your app is intercepted by iptables and redirected through the Envoy proxy.

    Apply

    Execute Injection: Enable automatic sidecar injection on a Kubernetes Namespace and verify that Pods start with 2 containers (App + Proxy).

    Analyze

    Diagnose Latency: Use Kiali (the Istio dashboard) to visualize the traffic graph and pinpoint exactly which microservice is causing a 500ms delay.

    Evaluate

    Assess Overhead: Evaluate the trade-offs of using a Service Mesh (CPU/Memory cost + added latency) versus the security/observability benefits.

    Create

    Build mTLS Policy: Architect a "Strict mTLS" policy that forbids any non-encrypted traffic between services, effectively creating a Zero Trust network.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Service Meshes are resource-intensive. Budget approximately 1.5 hours.

    1Install Istio CLI (`istioctl`)

    We will use Istio, the industry-standard mesh. You need the CLI tool to install and manage it.

    # Download latest version curl -L https://istio.io/downloadIstio | sh - # Add to path (replace with your version) cd istio-1.xx.x export PATH=$PWD/bin:$PATH # Verify istioctl version

    2Resource Audit

    Context: Istio injects a sidecar container into every pod. Each sidecar consumes roughly 50MB-100MB of RAM.
    Task: Ensure your Kubernetes cluster (Minikube or Cloud) has at least 4GB RAM and 2 CPUs available specifically for the mesh overhead. If using Minikube: minikube start --memory=8192 --cpus=4.

    3Research: The Sidecar Pattern

    Reading: Look up the "Sidecar Pattern" in Kubernetes.
    Question: If you have a Pod with two containers (Main App and Sidecar), they share the same Network Namespace (IP address). How does the Sidecar intercept traffic meant for the Main App? (Hint: Keywords are `iptables` and `traffic capture`).

    4HTTP Headers Refresher

    Context: Advanced routing (e.g., "Send iPhone users to V2") relies on Layer 7 (HTTP) inspection.
    Task: Use `curl -v google.com` and inspect the "User-Agent" header. Understand that Kubernetes Services (L4) cannot see this header, but Istio (L7) can.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    In the previous weeks, we treated the network as a simple pipe: Request goes in, Response comes out. As we scale to hundreds of microservices, this pipe becomes the most dangerous part of your infrastructure.

    The Problem: Developers write code that assumes the network is reliable, secure, and fast. It is none of these things.
    The Solution: A Service Mesh. It is a dedicated infrastructure layer that handles service-to-service communication. It extracts the complex logic of retries, encryption, and observability out of your application code and into a proxy.

    16.1 Architecture: The Sidecar Pattern

    Istio is split into two distinct planes: the Control Plane (The Brain) and the Data Plane (The Muscle).

    🧠

    Control Plane (Istiod)

    Role: Configuration Manager.
    It takes your high-level YAML (VirtualServices, DestinationRules) and converts it into low-level Envoy configuration. It pushes these updates to every proxy in the mesh. It does not touch any data packets.

    🏍️

    Data Plane (Envoy)

    Role: Traffic Interceptor.
    A tiny C++ proxy injected into every application Pod.
    Mechanism: When your app tries to send a request to `billing-service`, `iptables` rules force that packet into the local Envoy proxy first. Envoy then handles the routing, encryption, and telemetry.

    16.2 Traffic Management: Beyond Round Robin

    Kubernetes Services provide Layer 4 (TCP) load balancing. Istio provides Layer 7 (HTTP/gRPC) traffic management. This allows for vastly more sophisticated behavior.

    ⚡ Circuit Breaking

    Scenario: Service A calls Service B. Service B is overloaded and slow. Service A keeps waiting (holding connections open), eventually crashing itself.
    The Mesh Fix: You configure a Circuit Breaker on the Envoy proxy.
    "If Service B fails 3 times in a row, open the circuit. Fail immediately for the next 30 seconds without even trying to connect."
    This prevents Cascading Failure.

    🔁 Retries & Timeouts

    Scenario: A transient network blip causes a request to fail.
    The Mesh Fix: Envoy can automatically retry the request 3 times with a 20ms interval. Your application code never even knows the first attempt failed.

    16.3 Security: Mutual TLS (mTLS)

    In a standard cluster, traffic between pods is plain text. If a hacker compromises one pod, they can sniff all traffic in the cluster.

    The Solution: Mutual TLS.
    1. Identity: Every proxy is issued a robust identity (SPIFFE ID) encoded in a certificate.
    2. Encryption: Before Service A talks to Service B, their sidecars perform a TLS handshake.
    3. Zero Trust: Traffic is encrypted on the wire. The application doesn't need to manage certificates; the sidecar rotates them automatically every hour.

    16.4 Observability: "Free" Insights

    Because every single packet flows through an Envoy proxy, the mesh can generate a massive amount of telemetry data without you writing a single line of code.

    The Three Pillars of Mesh Observability

    • Metrics: Prometheus scrapes the sidecars. You get Request Rate, Error Rate, and Duration (RED) for every single link in your architecture.
    • Distributed Tracing: Envoy adds tracing headers (e.g., `x-b3-traceid`) to requests, allowing Jaeger/Zipkin to build full waterfalls of a transaction across 50 services.
    • Access Logs: A complete audit trail of who called whom, when, and with what result.

    16.5 The Cost of the Mesh

    Nothing is free. The Service Mesh tax is real.

    ⚠️ Performance & Complexity

    Latency: Every call now goes through two proxies (Sender Sidecar -> Network -> Receiver Sidecar). This adds roughly 2-5ms per hop. In a deep call chain, this adds up.
    Resources: If you have 1,000 pods, you now have 1,000 Envoy instances. That can easily consume 100GB of RAM across the cluster.
    Complexity: Debugging connection issues becomes harder. "Is it the app? Is it the firewall? Is it the Envoy config? Is it the Control Plane?"

    Hands-On Labs

    In these labs, we will install Istio, the world's most popular Service Mesh. We will deploy a microservices application ("Bookinfo") and observe how the mesh gives us instant observability, traffic control, and security without changing a single line of application code.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    1. Cluster Specs: Ensure your Kubernetes cluster has at least 4 CPUs and 8GB RAM. Istio is heavy.
    2. Tooling: You must have `istioctl` installed (from Pre-Class exercises).
    3. Clean State: It is recommended to use a fresh cluster or delete previous Ingress Controllers (like NGINX) to avoid port conflicts, although they can coexist.

    Lab 1: The Sidecar Injection

    Objective: Install the Istio Control Plane and enable "Automatic Sidecar Injection" for a namespace. Deploy an app and verify the proxy is running.

    Install Istio (Demo Profile):

    The "demo" profile installs the core components and high levels of logging suitable for learning.

    istioctl install --set profile=demo -y

    Verify: Check that `istiod` and `istio-ingressgateway` pods are running in the `istio-system` namespace.

    Create Namespace & Enable Injection:

    This is the critical step. We label the namespace. The Istio "Mutating Admission Webhook" looks for this label.

    kubectl create ns bookinfo kubectl label namespace bookinfo istio-injection=enabled
    Deploy the Sample App:

    We will use the famous "Bookinfo" app, composed of 4 microservices (Python, Java, Ruby, Node).

    # Deploy the app manifest directly from Istio's repo kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/istio/istio/release-1.18/samples/bookinfo/platform/kube/bookinfo.yaml -n bookinfo
    Verify the Sidecar:

    Wait for pods to start. Then look closely.

    kubectl get pods -n bookinfo
    NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS details-v1-558b8b4b76-2llld 2/2 Running 0 ratings-v1-b6994bb9-7t229 2/2 Running 0 reviews-v1-8ca98894-nz899 2/2 Running 0

    Observation: Notice READY 2/2.
    1 container is the App.
    1 container is the Envoy Proxy (injected automatically).
    If you see 1/1, you forgot to label the namespace before deploying!

    Lab 2: Visualizing the Mesh (Kiali)

    Objective: Use the Istio Dashboard (Kiali) to see the live traffic map of our microservices.

    Install Addons:

    Istio comes with integrations for Prometheus, Grafana, Jaeger, and Kiali.

    kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/istio/istio/release-1.18/samples/addons/prometheus.yaml kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/istio/istio/release-1.18/samples/addons/kiali.yaml
    Generate Traffic:

    The dashboard will be empty if there is no traffic. Let's curl the internal service loop.

    # Create a temporary curl pod inside the mesh kubectl run curl-pod --image=curlimages/curl -n bookinfo --restart=Never -- sleep 3600 # Send 100 requests to productpage kubectl exec -n bookinfo curl-pod -- sh -c 'for i in $(seq 1 100); do curl -s -o /dev/null http://productpage:9080/productpage; done'
    Open Kiali:
    istioctl dashboard kiali

    Action: Go to the "Graph" tab. Select namespace `bookinfo`.
    Observation: You will see a map: `productpage` -> `reviews` -> `ratings`.
    You can see the Request/Sec throughput and the percentage of errors on every single arrow (edge). This is generated purely from the sidecars; the app didn't send any data.

    Lab 3: Advanced Traffic Shaping

    Objective: Unlike NGINX (Layer 4/basic Layer 7), Istio allows routing based on deep Layer 7 attributes like HTTP Headers. We will route traffic based on a user login.

    Understand the App:

    The `reviews` service has 3 versions:
    • v1: No stars.
    • v2: Black stars.
    • v3: Red stars.
    Currently, Istio round-robins between all three.

    Define Destinations (Subsets):

    Istio needs to know which pods belong to v1, v2, v3. We use a `DestinationRule`.

    kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/istio/istio/release-1.18/samples/bookinfo/networking/destination-rule-all.yaml -n bookinfo
    Route Everything to V1:

    Let's stop the flickering versions. Force all traffic to v1.

    apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1alpha3 kind: VirtualService metadata: name: reviews namespace: bookinfo spec: hosts: - reviews http: - route: - destination: host: reviews subset: v1

    Save as `vs-v1.yaml` and apply.

    The "Jason" Rule:

    Requirement: If the user is logged in as "jason" (header `end-user: jason`), show them v2 (Black stars). Everyone else sees v1.

    apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1alpha3 kind: VirtualService metadata: name: reviews namespace: bookinfo spec: hosts: - reviews http: - match: - headers: end-user: exact: jason route: - destination: host: reviews subset: v2 - route: - destination: host: reviews subset: v1

    Verification:
    1. Open the Product Page in browser (you'll need the Gateway for this, or use port-forward).
    2. Sign in as "jason" (password is irrelevant). You see Black Stars.
    3. Sign out. You see No Stars.

    Lab 4: Enforcing Zero Trust

    Objective: Prove that traffic is encrypted. Then, enforce a policy that strictly forbids any unencrypted traffic.

    Verify Permissive Mode:

    By default, Istio is "Permissive". It accepts both TLS and Plaintext.
    Let's verify we can still curl from outside the mesh (plaintext).

    # Run a pod in the 'default' namespace (not injected) kubectl run hacker --image=curlimages/curl -n default --restart=Never -- sleep 3600 # Try to access productpage from hacker kubectl exec -n default hacker -- curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" http://productpage.bookinfo:9080/productpage # Output: 200 (It works! This is bad for Zero Trust)
    Apply Strict mTLS Policy:

    Lock it down. Only proxies with valid certificates can talk.

    apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1beta1 kind: PeerAuthentication metadata: name: default namespace: bookinfo spec: mtls: mode: STRICT

    Save as `mtls-strict.yaml` and apply.

    Verify Blocking:

    Try the hacker pod again.

    kubectl exec -n default hacker -- curl -s -v http://productpage.bookinfo:9080/productpage
    * Recv failure: Connection reset by peer # or exit code 56

    Success: The Envoy proxy on `productpage` saw the request came without a client certificate and immediately cut the connection. The application never even saw the request.

    Lab Solutions & Troubleshooting Guide

    This section provides the "answer key" for the Service Mesh labs. It clarifies the often-confusing relationship between VirtualServices and DestinationRules, explains the magic behind automatic sidecar injection, and details exactly why the hacker pod was blocked by mTLS.

    Lab 1 Solution: The Injection Mechanism

    How does `kubectl label` trigger injection?

    When you ran kubectl label namespace bookinfo istio-injection=enabled, you didn't change any pods immediately.

    However, when you subsequently deployed the app, the Kubernetes API Server sent the "Pod Creation Request" to Istio's Mutating Admission Webhook.
    1. The Webhook saw the namespace label.
    2. It modified the YAML on-the-fly, adding the istio-proxy container and an init-container that sets up iptables.
    3. The API Server persisted this modified Pod definition.

    Troubleshooting: If you don't see 2/2 containers, delete the pod (`kubectl delete pod ...`). The Deployment controller will create a new one, triggering the webhook again.

    Lab 2 Solution: Kiali & Telemetry

    Where does Kiali get its data?

    Kiali does not talk to your pods directly.
    1. Envoy: Generates stats (e.g., `istio_requests_total`) and exposes them on port 15020.
    2. Prometheus: Scrapes these metrics every 15 seconds.
    3. Kiali: Queries Prometheus to build the graph.

    If Kiali says "Empty Graph," it usually means either your app isn't receiving traffic, or Prometheus isn't scraping the sidecars correctly.

    Lab 3 Solution: VirtualService vs. DestinationRule

    This is the #1 confusion point in Istio.

    VirtualService (The Router)

    "How do we get there?"
    Defines routing logic (Traffic splitting, Header matching, Rewrites).
    Example: "If header is 'jason', go to subset v2. Otherwise, go to v1."

    DestinationRule (The Target)

    "What happens when we arrive?"
    Defines the subsets (v1 labels vs v2 labels) and policies (Load Balancing algorithm, Circuit Breakers, TLS settings).
    Example: "Subset v1 implies `version: v1` label. Max connections = 100."

    You typically need both. The VirtualService directs traffic to a "subset" defined in the DestinationRule.

    Lab 4 Solution: Strict mTLS

    Why "Connection Reset"?

    When you applied mode: STRICT, you told the sidecar: "Reject any connection that does not have a valid Istio Client Certificate."
